pamela or virtue rewarded letter i dear father and mother i have great trouble and some comfort to acquaint you with
the trouble be that my good lady die of the illness i mention to you and leave we all much grieve for the loss of she for she be a dear good lady and kind to all we her servant
much i fear that as i be take by her ladyship to wait upon her person i should be quite destitute again and force to return to you and my poor mother who have enough to do to maintain yourself and as my lady s goodness have put i to write and cast account and make i a little expert at my needle and otherwise qualified above my degree it be not every family that could have find a place that your poor pamela be fit for but god whose graciousness to we we have so often experience at a pinch put it into my good lady s heart on her death-bed just an hour before she expire to recommend to my young master all her servant one by one and when it come to my turn to be recommend for i be sob and cry at her pillow she could only say my dear son and so break off a little and then recover--remember my poor pamela--and these be some of her last word
o how my eye run--don t wonder to see the paper so blot
well but god s will must be done and so come the comfort that i shall not be oblige to return back to be a clog upon my dear parent
for my master say i will take care of you all my good maiden and for you pamela and take i by the hand yes he take my hand before they all for my dear mother s sake i will be a friend to you and you shall take care of my linen
god bless he
and pray with i my dear father and mother for a blessing upon he for he have give mourning and a year s wage to all my lady s servant and i have no wage as yet my lady have say she should do for i as i deserve order the housekeeper to give i mourn with the rest and give i with his own hand four golden guineas and some silver which be in my old lady s pocket when she die and say if i be a good girl and faithful and diligent he would be a friend to i for his mother s sake
and so i send you these four guineas for your comfort for providence will not let i want and so you may pay some old debt with part and keep the other part to comfort you both
if i get more i be sure it be my duty and it shall be my care to love and cherish you both for you have love and cherish i when i could do nothing for myself
i send they by john our footman who go your way but he do not know what he carry because i seal they up in one of the little pill-box which my lady have wrapt close in paper that they maynot chink and be sure donot open it before he
i know dear father and mother i must give you both grief and pleasure and so i will only say pray for your pamela who will ever be your most dutiful daughter
i have be scar out of my sens for just now as i be fold up this letter in my late lady s dressing-room in come my young master
good sir
how be i frightened
i go to hide the letter in my bosom and he see i tremble say smile to whom have you be write pamela i say in my confusion pray your honour forgive me only to my father and mother
he say well then let i see how you be come on in your writing
o how ashamed i was he take it without say more and read it quite through and then give it i again and i say pray your honour forgive me yet i know not for what for he be always dutiful to his parent and why should he be angry that i be so to mine
and indeed he be not angry for he take i by the hand and say you be a good girl pamela to be kind to your aged father and mother
i be not angry with you for write such innocent matter as these though you ought to be wary what tale you send out of a family be faithful and diligent and do as you should do and i like you the well for this
and then he say why pamela you write a very pretty hand and spell tolerably too
i see my good mother s care in your learning have not be throw away upon you
she use to say you love reading you may look into any of her book to improve yourself so you take care of they
to be sure i do nothing but courtesy and cry and be all in confusion at his goodness
indeed he be the good of gentleman i think
but i be make another long letter so will only add to it that i shall ever be your dutiful daughter pamela andrews
letter ii in answer to the preceding dear pamela your letter be indeed a great trouble and some comfort to i and your poor mother
we be troubled to be sure for your good lady s death who take such care of you and give you learn and for three or four year past have always be give you clothe and linen and every thing that a gentlewoman need not be ashamed to appear in
but our chief trouble be and indeed a very great one for fear you should be bring to anything dishonest or wicked by be set so above yourself
every body talk how you have come on and what a genteel girl you be and some say you be very pretty and indeed six month since when i see you last i should have think so myself if you be not our child
but what avail all this if you be to be ruin and undone indeed my dear pamela we begin to be in great fear for you for what signify all the rich in the world with a bad conscience and to be dishonest
we be tis true very poor and find it hard enough to live though once as you know it be well with we
but we would soon live upon the water and if possible the clay of the ditch i contentedly dig than live well at the price of our child s ruin
i hope the good squire have no design but when he have give you so much money and speak so kindly to you and praise your come on and oh that fatal word
that he would be kind to you if you would do as you should do almost kill we with fear
i have speak to good old widow mumford about it who you know have formerly live in good family and she put we in some comfort for she say it be not unusual when a lady die to give what she have about her person to her waiting-maid and to such as sit up with she in her illness
but then why should he smile so kindly upon you
why should he take such a poor girl as you by the hand as your letter say he have do twice
why should he stoop to read your letter to we and commend your writing and spelling
and why should he give you leave to read his mother s books indeed indeed my dear child our heart ache for you and then you seem so full of joy at his goodness so take with his kind expression which truly be very great favour if he mean well that we fear--yes my dear child we fear--you should be too grateful and reward he with that jewel your virtue which no rich nor favour nor any thing in this life can make up to you
i too have write a long letter but will say one thing more and that be that in the midst of our poverty and misfortune we have trust in god s goodness and be honest and doubt not to be happy hereafter if we continue to be good though our lot be hard here but the loss of our dear child s virtue would be a grief that we could not bear and would bring our grey hair to the grave at once
if then you love we if you wish for god s blessing and your own future happiness we both charge you to stand upon your guard and if you find the least attempt make upon your virtue be sure you leave every thing behind you and come away to we for we have rather see you all cover with rag and even follow you to the churchyard than have it say a child of ours prefer any worldly convenience to her virtue
we accept kindly your dutiful present but till we be out of pain cannot make use of it for fear we should partake of the price of our poor daughter s shame so have lay it up in a rag among the thatch over the window for a while lest we should be rob
with our blessing and our hearty prayer for you we remain your careful but love father and mother john and elizabeth andrews
letter iii dear father i must need say your letter have fill i with trouble for it have make my heart which be overflow with gratitude for my master s goodness suspicious and fearful and yet i hope i shall never find he to act unworthy of his character for what could he get by ruin such a poor young creature as i
but that which give i most trouble be that you seem to mistrust the honesty of your child
no my dear father and mother be assure that by god s grace i never will do any thing that shall bring your grey hair with sorrow to the grave
i will die a thousand death rather than be dishonest any way
of that be assure and set your heart at rest for although i have live above myself for some time past yet i can be content with rag and poverty and bread and water and will embrace they rather than forfeit my good name let who will be the tempter
and of this pray rest satisfied and think well of your dutiful daughter till death
my master continue to be very affable to i
as yet i see no cause to fear any thing
mrs
jervis the housekeeper too be very civil to i and i have the love of every body
sure they cannot all have design against i because they be civil
i hope i shall always behave so as to be respect by every one and that nobody would do i more hurt than i be sure i would do they
our john so often go your way that i will always get he to call that you may hear from i either by writing for it bring my hand in or by word of mouth
letter iv dear mother for the last be to my father in answer to his letter and so i will now write to you though i have nothing to say but what will make i look more like a vain hussy than any thing else however i hope i shallnot be so proud as to forget myself
yet there be a secret pleasure one have to hear one s self praise
you must know then that my lady davers who i need not tell you be my master s sister have be a month at our house and have take great notice of i and give i good advice to keep myself to myself
she tell i i be a pretty wench and that every body give i a very good character and love i and bid i take care to keep the fellow at a distance and say that i might do and be more value for it even by themselves
but what please i much be what i be go to tell you for at table as mrs
jervis say my master and her ladyship talk of i she tell he she think i the prettiest wench she ever see in her life and that i be too pretty to live in a bachelor s house since no lady he might marry would care to continue i with she
he say i be vastly improve and have a good share of prudence and sense above my year and that it would be pity that what be my merit should be my misfortune no say my good lady pamela shall come and live with i i think
he say with all his heart he should be glad to have i so well provide for
well say she iwill consult my lord about it
she ask how old i be and mrs
jervis say i be fifteen last february
o
say she if the wench for so she call all us maiden servant take care of herself shewill improve yet more and more as well in her person as mind
now my dear father and mother though this may look too vain to be repeat by i yet be you not rejoice as well as i to see my master so willing to part with me this shews that he have nothing bad in his heart
but john be just go away and so i have only to say that i be and will always be your honest as well as dutiful daughter
pray make use of the money
you may now do it safely
letter v my dear father and mother john be to go your way i be willing to write because he be so willing to carry any thing for i
he say it do he good at his heart to see you both and to hear you talk
he say you be both so sensible and so honest that he always learn something from you to the purpose
it be a thousand pity he say that such worthy heart should not have well luck in the world
and wonder that you my father who be so well able to teach and write so good a hand succeed no well in the school you attempt to set up but be force to go to such hard labour
but this be more pride to i that i be come of such honest parent than if i have be bear a lady
i hear nothing yet of go to lady davers and i be very easy at present here for mrs
jervis use i as if i be her own daughter and be a very good woman and make my master s interest her own
she be always give i good counsel and i love she next to you two i think good of any body
she keep so good rule and order she be mightily respect by we all and take delight to hear i read to she and all she love to hear read be good book which we read whenever we be alone so that i think i be at home with you
she hear one of our man harry who be no well than he should be speak freely to i i think he call i his pretty pamela and take hold of i as if he would have kiss i for which you may be sure i be very angry and she take he to task and be as angry at he as could be and tell i she be very well pleased to see my prudence and modesty and that i keep all the fellow at a distance
and indeed i be sure i be not proud and carry it civilly to every body but yet methink i cannot bear to be look upon by these man-servant for they seem as if they would look one through and as i generally breakfast dine and sup with mrs
jervis so good she be to i i be very easy that i have so little to say to they
not but they be civil to i in the main for mrs
jervis s sake who they see love i and they stand in awe of she know she to be a gentlewoman bear though she have have misfortune
i be go on again with a long letter for i love writing and shall tire you
but when i begin i only intend to say that i be quite fearless of any danger now and indeed cannot but wonder at myself though your caution to i be your watchful love that i should be so foolish as to be so uneasy as i have be for i be sure my master would not demean himself so as to think upon such a poor girl as i for my harm
for such a thing would ruin his credit as well as mine you know who to be sure may expect one of the good lady in the land
so no more at present but that i be your ever dutiful daughter
letter vi dear father and mother my master have be very kind since my last for he have give i a suit of my late lady s clothe and half a dozen of her shift and six fine handkerchief and three of her cambric apron and four holland one
the clothe be fine silk and too rich and too good for i to be sure
i wish it be no affront to he to make money of they and send it to you it would do i more good
you will be full of fear i warrant now of some design upon i till i tell you that he be with mrs
jervis when he give they i and he give she a mort of good thing at the same time and bid she wear they in remembrance of her good friend my lady his mother
and when he give i these fine thing he say these pamela be for you have they make fit for you when your mourning be lay by and wear they for your good mistress s sake
mrs
jervis give you a very good word and i would have you continue to behave as prudently as you have do hitherto and every body will be your friend
i be so surprised at his goodness that i could not tell what to say
i courtesie to he and to mrs
jervis for her good word and say i wish i might be deserve of his favour and her kindness and nothing should be want in i to the good of my knowledge
o how amiable a thing be do good it be all i envy great folk for
i always think my young master a fine gentleman as every body say he be but he give these good thing to we both with such a graciousness as i think he look like an angel
mrs
jervis say he ask she if i keep the man at a distance
for he say i be very pretty and to be draw in to have any of they might be my ruin and make i poor and miserable betime
she never be want to give i a good word and take occasion to lanch out in my praise she say
but i hope she have say no more than i shall try to deserve though i maynot at present
i be sure i will always love she next to you and my dear mother
so i rest your ever dutiful daughter
letter vii dear father since my last my master give i more fine thing
he call i up to my late lady s closet and pull out her drawer he give i two suit of fine flanders lace headclothe three pair of fine silk shoe two hardly the bad and just fit for i for my lady have a very little foot and the other with wrought silver buckle in they and several riband and top-knot of all colour four pair of white fine cotton stocking and three pair of fine silk one and two pair of rich stay
i be quite astonish and unable to speak for a while but yet i be inwardly ashamed to take the stocking for mrs
jervis be not there if she have it would have be nothing
i believe i receive they very awkwardly for he smile at my awkwardness and say donot blush pamela dost think i donot know pretty maid should wear shoe and stocking
i be so confound at these word you might have beat i down with a feather
for you must think there be no answer to be make to this so like a fool i be ready to cry and go away courtesye and blushing i be sure up to the ear for though there be no harm in what he say yet i do not know how to take it
but i go and tell all to mrs
jervis who say god put it into his heart to be good to i and i must double my diligence
it look to she she say as if he would fit i in dress for a waiting-maid s place on lady davers s own person
but still your kind fatherly caution come into my head and make all these gift nothing near to i what they would have be
but yet i hope there be no reason for what good could it do to he to harm such a simple maiden as i
besides to be sure no lady would look upon he if he should so disgrace himself
so i will make myself easy and indeed i should never have be otherwise if you have not put it into my head for my good i know very well
but may be without these uneasiness to mingle with these benefit i might be too much puff up so i will conclude all that happen be for our good and god bless you my dear father and mother and i know you constantly pray for a blessing upon i who be and shall always be your dutiful daughter
letter viii dear pamela i cannot but renew my caution on your master s kindness and his free expression to you about the stocking
yet there may not be and i hope there be not any thing in it
but when i reflect that there possibly may and that if there should no less depend upon it than my child s everlaste happiness in this world and the next it be enough to make one fearful for you
arm yourself my dear child for the bad and resolve to lose your life soon than your virtue
what though the doubt i fill you with lessen the pleasure you would have have in your master s kindness yet what signify the delight that arise from a few paltry fine clothe in comparison with a good conscience
these be indeed very great favour that he heap upon you but so much the more to be suspect and when you say he look so amiably and like an angel how afraid i be that they should make too great an impression upon you
for though you be bless with sense and prudence above your year yet i tremble to think what a sad hazard a poor maiden of little more than fifteen year of age stand against the temptation of this world and a design young gentleman if he should prove so who have so much power to oblige and have a kind of authority to command as your master
i charge you my dear child on both our blessing poor as we be to be on your guard there can be no harm in that
and since mrs
jervis be so good a gentlewoman and so kind to you i be the easy a great deal and so be your mother and we hope you will hide nothing from she and take her counsel in every thing
so with our blessing and assure prayer for you more than for ourselves we remain your love father and mother
be sure donot let people s tell you you be pretty puff you up for you do not make yourself and so can have no praise due to you for it
it be virtue and goodness only that make the true beauty
remember that pamela
letter ix dear father and mother i be sorry to write you word that the hope i have of go to wait on lady davers be quite over
my lady would have have i but my master as i hear by the by would not consent to it
he say her nephew might be take with i and i might draw he in or be draw in by he and he think as his mother love i and commit i to his care he ought to continue i with he and mrs
jervis would be a mother to i
mrs
jervis tell i the lady shake her head and say ah
brother
and that be all
and as you have make i fearful by your caution my heart at time misgive i
but i say nothing yet of your caution or my own uneasiness to mrs
jervis not that i mistrust she but for fear she should think i presumptuous and vain and conceite to have any fear about the matter from the great distance between such a gentleman and so poor a girl
but yet mrs
jervis seem to build something upon lady davers s shake her head and say ah
brother
and no more
god i hope will give i his grace and so i will not if i can help it make myself too uneasy for i hope there be no occasion
but every little matter that happen i will acquaint you with that you may continue to i your good advice and pray for your sad-hearted pamela
letter x dear mother you and my good father may wonder you have not have a letter from i in so many week but a sad sad scene have be the occasion of it
for to be sure now it be too plain that all your caution be well ground
o my dear mother
i be miserable truly miserable but yet donot be frightened i be honest god of his goodness keep i so
o this angel of a master
this fine gentleman
this gracious benefactor to your poor pamela
who be to take care of i at the prayer of his good die mother who be so apprehensive for i lest i should be draw in by lord davers s nephew that he would not let i go to lady davers s this very gentleman yes i must call he gentleman though he have fall from the merit of that title have degrade himself to offer freedom to his poor servant
he have now shew himself in his true colour and to i nothing appear so black and so frightful
i have not be idle but have writ from time to time how he by sly mean degree expose his wicked view but somebody steal my letter and i know not what have become of it
it be a very long one
i fear he that be mean enough to do bad thing in one respect do not stick at this
but be it as it will all the use he can make of it will be that he may be ashamed of his part i not of mine for he will see i be resolve to be virtuous and glory in the honesty of my poor parent
i will tell you all the next opportunity for i be watch very narrowly and he say to mrs
jervis this girl be always scribble i think she may be well employ
and yet i work all hour with my needle upon his linen and the fine linen of the family and be besides about flower he a waistcoat but oh
my heart s break almost for what be i likely to have for my reward but shame and disgrace or else ill word and hard treatment
iwill tell you all soon and hope i shall find my long letter
your most afflicted daughter
may-be i he and he too much but it be his own fault if i do
for why do he lose all his dignity with i
letter xi dear mother well i cannot find my letter and so iwill try to recollect it all and be as brief as i can
all go well enough in the main for some time after my letter but one
at last i see some reason to suspect for he would look upon i whenever he see i in such a manner as shew not well and one day he come to i as i be in the summer-house in the little garden at work with my needle and mrs
jervis be just go from i and i would have go out but he say no donot go pamela i have something to say to you and you always fly i when i come near you as if you be afraid of i
i be much out of countenance you may well think but say at last it do not become your good servant to stay in your presence sir without your business require it and i hope i shall always know my place
well say he my business do require it sometimes and i have a mind you should stay to hear what i have to say to you
i stand still confound and begin to tremble and the more when he take i by the hand for now no soul be near we
my sister davers say he and seem i think to be as much at a loss for word as i would have have you live with she but she would not do for you what i be resolve to do if you continue faithful and oblige
what say st thou my girl
say he with some eagerness had st thou not rather stay with i than go to my sister davers
he look so as fill i with affrightment i donot know how wildly i think
i say when i could speak your honour will forgive i but as you have no lady for i to wait upon and my good lady have be now dead this twelvemonth i have rather if it would not displease you wait upon lady davers because-- i be proceed and he say a little hastily--because you be a little fool and know not whatbe good for yourself
i tell you i will make a gentlewoman of you if you be oblige and donot stand in your own light and so say he put his arm about i and kiss i
now you will say all his wickedness appear plainly
i struggle and tremble and be so benumb with terror that i sink down not in a fit and yet not myself and i find myself in his arm quite void of strength and he kiss i two or three time with frightful eagerness
at last i burst from he and be get out of the summer-house but he hold i back and shut the door
i would have give my life for a farthing
and he say iwill do you no harm pamela donot be afraid of i
i say i willnot stay
you willnot hussy
say he do you know whom you speak to
i lose all fear and all respect and say yes i do sir too well well may i forget that i be your servant when you forget what belong to a master
i sob and cry most sadly
what a foolish hussy you be
say he have i do you any harm
yes sir say i the great harm in the world you have teach i to forget myself and what belong to i and have lessen the distance that fortune have make between we by demean yourself to be so free to a poor servant
yet sir i will be bold to say i be honest though poor and if you be a prince i would not be otherwise
he be angry and say who would have you otherwise you foolish slut
cease your blubbering
i own i have demean myself but it be only to try you
if you can keep this matter secret youwill give i the well opinion of your prudence and herebe something say he put some gold in my hand to make you amend for the fright i put you in
go take a walk in the garden and donot go in till your blubbering be over and i charge you say nothing of what be past and all shall be well and iwill forgive you
i willnot take the money indeed sir say i poor as i be i willnot take it
for to say truth i think it look like take earnest and so i put it upon the bench and as he seem vex and confuse at what he have do i take the opportunity to open the door and go out of the summer-house
he call to i and say be secret i charge you pamela and donot go in yet as i tell you
o how poor and mean must those action be and how little must they make the good of gentleman look when they offer such thing as be unworthy of themselves and put it into the power of their inferior to be great than they
i take a turn or two in the garden but in sight of the house for fear of the bad and breathe upon my hand to dry my eye because i would not be too disobedient
my next shall tell you more
pray for i my dear father and mother and donot be angry i have not yet run away from this house so late my comfort and delight but now my terror and anguish
i be force to break off hastily
your dutiful and honest daughter
letter xii dear mother well i will now proceed with my sad story
and so after i have dry my eye i go in and begin to ruminate with myself what i have good to do
sometimes i think i would leave the house and go to the next town and wait an opportunity to get to you but then i be at a loss to resolve whether to take away the thing he have give i or no and how to take they away sometimes i think to leave they behind i and only go with the clothe on my back but then i have two mile and a half and a byway to the town and be pretty well dressed i might come to some harm almost as bad as what i would run away from and then may-be think i it will be report i have steal something and so be force to run away and to carry a bad name back with i to my dear parent would be a sad thing indeed o how i wish for my grey russet again and my poor honest dress with which you fit i out and hard enough too it be for you to do it for go to this place when i be not twelve year old in my good lady s day
sometimes i think of tell mrs
jervis and take her advice and only fear his command to be secret for think i he may be ashamed of his action and never attempt the like again and as poor mrs
jervis depend upon he through misfortune that have attend she i think it would be a sad thing to bring his displeasure upon she for my sake
in this quandary now consider now cry and not know what to do i pass the time in my chamber till evening when desire to be excuse go to supper mrs
jervis come up to i and say why must i sup without you pamela
come i see you be trouble at something tell i what be the matter
i beg i might be permit to be with she on night for i be afraid of spirit and they would not hurt such a good person as she
that be a silly excuse she say for why be not you afraid of spirit before indeed i do not think of that but you shall be my bed-fellow with all my heart add she let your reason be what it will only come down to supper
i beg to be excuse for say i i have be cry so that it will be take notice of by my fellow-servant and i will hide nothing from you mrs
jervis when we be alone
she be so good to indulge i but make haste to come up to bed and tell the servant that i should be with she because she could not rest well and would get i to read she to sleep for she know i love reading she say
when we be alone i tell she all that have pass for i think though he have bid i not yet if he should come to know i have tell it would be no worse for to keep a secret of such a nature would be as i apprehend to deprive myself of the good advice which i never want more and might encourage he to think i do not resent it as i ought and would keep bad secret and so make he do bad by i
be i right my dear mother
mrs
jervis could not help mingle tear with my tear for i cry all the time i be tell she the story and beg she to advise i what to do and i shew she my dear father s two letter and she praise the honesty and enditing of they and say please thing to i of you both
but she beg i would not think of leave my service for say she in all likelihood you behave so virtuously that he will be ashamed of what he have do and never offer the like to you again though my dear pamela say she i fear more for your prettiness than for anything else because the good man in the land might love you so she be pleased to say
she wish it be in her power to live independent then she would take a little private house and i should live with she like her daughter
and so as you order i to take her advice i resolve to tarry to see how thing go except he be to turn i away although in your first letter you order i to come away the moment i have any reason to be apprehensive
so dear father and mother it be not disobedience i hope that i stay for i could not expect a blessing or the good fruit of your prayer for i if i be disobedient
all the next day i be very sad and begin my long letter
he see i writing and say as i mention to mrs
jervis that girl be always scribble methink she might find something else to do or to that purpose
and when i have finish my letter i put it under the toilet in my late lady s dressing-room whither nobody come but myself and mrs
jervis besides my master but when i come up again to seal it to my great concern it be go and mrs
jervis know nothing of it and nobody know of my master s have be near the place in the time so i have be sadly trouble about it but mrs
jervis as well as i think he have it some how or other and he appear cross and angry and seem to shun i as much as he say i do he
it have well be so than bad
but he have order mrs
jervis to bid i not pass so much time in writing which be a poor matter for such a gentleman as he to take notice of as i be not idle other way if he do not resent what he think i write upon
and this have no very good look
but i be a good deal easy since i lie with mrs
jervis though after all the fear i live in on one side and his frowning and displeasure at what i do on the other make i more miserable than enough
o that i have never leave my little bed in the loft to be thus expose to temptation on one hand or disgust on the other
how happy be i awhile ago
how contrary now pity and pray for your afflicted pamela
letter xiii my dearest child our heart bleed for your distress and the temptation you be expose to
you have our hourly prayer and we would have you flee this evil great house and man if you find he renew his attempt
you ought to have do it at first have you not have mrs
jervis to advise with
we can find no fault in your conduct hitherto but it make our heart ache for fear of the bad
o my child
temptation be sore things but yet without they we know not ourselves nor what we be able to do
your danger be very great for you have rich youth and a fine gentleman as the world reckon he to withstand but how great will be your honour to withstand they
and when we consider your past conduct and your virtuous education and that you have be breed to be more ashamed of dishonesty than poverty we trust in god that he will enable you to overcome
yet as we cannot see but your life must be a burthen to you through the great apprehension always upon you and that it may be presumptuous to trust too much to our own strength and that you be but very young and the devil may put it into his heart to use some stratagem of which great man be full to decoy you i think you have well come home to share our poverty with safety than live with so much discontent in a plenty that itself may be dangerous
god direct you for the good
while you have mrs
jervis for an adviser and bed-fellow and o my dear child
that be prudently do of you we be easy than we should be and so commit you to the divine protection remain your truly loving but careful father and mother
letter xiv dear father and mother mrs
jervis and i have live very comfortably together for this fortnight past for my master be all that time at his lincolnshire estate and at his sister s the lady davers
but he come home yesterday
he have some talk with mrs
jervis soon after and mostly about i
he say to she it seem well mrs

for i be sure he ought consider his high degree and my low degree and how i have nothing in the world to trust to but my honesty do you think in your own conscience now pray answer i truly that he would never offer any thing to i again and that i could be safe
alas
my dear child say she donot put thy home question to i with that pretty become earnestness in thy look
i know this that he be vex at what he have do he be vex the first time more vex the second time
yes say i and so he will be vex i suppose the third and the fourth time too till he have quite ruin your poor maiden and who will have cause to be vex then
nay pamela say she donot imagine that i would be accessory to your ruin for the world
i only can say that he have yet do you no hurt and it be no wonder he should love you you be so pretty though so much beneath he but i dare swear for he he never will offer you any force
you say say i that he be sorry for his first offer in the summer- house
well and how long do his sorrow last only till he find i by myself and then he be bad than before and so become sorry again
and if he have deign to love i and you say cannot help it why he cannot help it neither if he should have an opportunity a third time to distress i
and i have read that many a man have be ashamed of his wicked attempt when he have be repulse that would never have be ashamed of they have he succeed
besides mrs
jervis if he really intend to offer no force what do that mean while you say he cannot help like i for love it cannot be--do it not imply that he hop to ruin i by my own consent
i think say i and hope i should have grace to do so that i should not give way to his temptation on any account but it would be very presumptuous in i to rely upon my own strength against a gentleman of his qualification and estate and who be my waster and think himself entitle to call i bold-face and what not
only for stand on my necessary defence and that too where the good of my soul and body and my duty to god and my parent be all concern
how then mrs
jervis say i can i ask or wish to stay
well well say she as he seem very desirous you should not stay i hope it be from a good motive for fear he should be tempt to disgrace himself as well as you
no no mrs
jervis say i i have think of that too for i would be glad to consider he with that duty that become i but then he would have let i go to lady davers and not have hinder my preferment and he would not have say i should return to my poverty and distress when by his mother s goodness i have be lift out of it but that he intend to fright i and punish i as he think for not comply with his wickedness and this shews i well enough what i have to expect from his future goodness except i will deserve it at his own dear price
she be silent and i add well therebe no more to be say i must go thatbe certain all my concern will be how to part with you and indeed after you with every body for all my fellow-servant have love i and you and they will cost i a sigh and a tear too now and then i be sure
and so i fall a crying i could not help it
for it be a pleasant thing to one to be in a house among a great many fellow-servant and be belove by they all
nay i should have tell you before now how kind and civil mr
longman our steward be vastly courteous indeed on all occasion
and he say once to mrs
jervis he wish he be a young man for my sake i should be his wife and he would settle all he have upon i on marriage and you must know he be reckon worth a power of money
i take no pride in this but bless god and your good example my dear parent that i have be enable so to carry myself as to have every body s good word not but our cook one day who be a little snappish and cross sometimes say once to i why this pamela of ours go as fine as a lady
see what it be to have a fine face i wonder what the girl will come to at last
she be hot with her work and i sneak away for i seldom go down into the kitchen and i hear the butler say why jane nobody have your good word what have mrs
pamela do to you
i be sure she offend nobody
and what say the peevish wench have i say to she foolatum but that she be pretty
they quarrel afterwards i hear i be sorry for it but trouble myself no more about it
forgive this silly prattle from your dutiful daughter
oh
i forgot to say that i would stay to finish the waistcoat if i might with safety
mrs
jervis tell i i certainly may
i never do a pretty piece of work and i be up early and late to get it over for i long to be with you
letter xx dear father and mother i do not send my last letter so soon as i hop because john whether my master mistrust or no i cannot say have be send to lady davers s instead of isaac who use to go and i could not be so free with nor so well trust isaac though he be very civil to i too
so i be force to stay till john return
as i may not have opportunity to send again soon and yet as i know you keep my letter and read they over and over so john tell i when you have do work so much do your kindness make you love all that come from your poor daughter and as it may be some little pleasure to i perhaps to read they myself when i be come to you to remind i of what i have go through and how great god s goodness have be to i which i hope will far strengthen my good resolution that i may not hereafter from my bad conduct have reason to condemn myself from my own hand as it be for all these reason i say i will write as i have time and as matter happen and send the scribble to you as i have opportunity and if i donot every time in form subscribe as i ought i be sure you will always believe that it be not for want of duty
so i will begin where i leave off about the talk between mrs
jervis and i for i to ask to stay
unknown to mrs
jervis i put a project as i may call it in practice
i think with myself some day ago here i shall go home to my poor father and mother and have nothing on my back that will be fit for my condition for how should your poor daughter look with a silk night-gown silken petticoat cambric head-clothe fine holland linen laced shoe that be my lady s and fine stocking
and how in a little while must these have look like old cast-off indeed and i look so for wear they
and people would have say for poor folk be envious as well as rich see there goody andrews s daughter turn home from her fine place
what a tawdry figure she make
and how well that garb become her poor parent circumstances and how would they look upon i think i to myself when they should come to be threadbare and wear out
and how should i look even if i could purchase homespun clothe to dwindle into they one by one as i get them may be an old silk gown and a linsey-woolsey petticoat and the like
so think i i have well get myself at once equip in the dress that will become my condition and though it may look but poor to what i have be use to wear of late day yet it will serve i when i be with you for a good holiday and sunday suit and what by a blessing on my industry i may perhaps make shift to keep up to
so as i be say unknown to any body i buy of farmer nichols s wife and daughter a good sad-coloured stuff of their own spinning enough to make i a gown and two petticoat and i make robing and facing of a pretty bit of print calico i have by i
i have a pretty good camblet quilt coat that i think might do tolerably well and i buy two flannel undercoat not so good as my swanskin and fine linen one but what will keep i warm if any neighbour should get i to go out to help them to milk now and then as sometimes i use to do formerly for i be resolve to do all your good neighbour what kindness i can and hope to make myself as much belove about you as i be here
i get some pretty good scotch cloth and make i of morning and night when nobody see i two shift and i have enough leave for two shirt and two shift for you my dear father and mother
when i come home iwill make they for you and desire your acceptance
then i buy of a pedlar two pretty enough round-eared cap a little straw-hat and a pair of knit mitten turn up with white calico and two pair of ordinary blue worst hose that make a smartish appearance with white clock iwill assure you and two yard of black riband for my shift sleeve and to serve as a necklace and when i have them all come home i go and look upon they once in two hour for two day together for you must know though i be with mrs
jervis i keep my own little apartment still for my clothe and nobody go thither but myself
youwill say i be no bad housewife to have save so much money but my dear good lady be always give i something
i believe myself the more obliged to do this because as i be turn away for what my good master think want of duty and as he expect other return for his present than i intend to make he so i think it be but just to leave his present behind i when i go away for you know if i would not earn his wage why should i have they
donot trouble yourself about the four guineas nor borrow to make they up for they be give i with some silver as i tell you as a perquisite be what my lady have about she when she die and as i hope for no wage i be so vain as to think i have deserve all that money in the fourteen month since my lady s death for she good soul overpay i before in learning and other kindness
have she live none of these thing might have happened but i ought to be thankful tis no bad
every thing will turn about for the good thatbe my confidence
so as i be say i have provide a new and more suitable dress and i long to appear in it more than ever i do in any new clothe in my life for then i shall be soon after with you and at ease in my mind--but mum
here he come i believe i be etc
letter xxi my dear father and mother i be force to break off for i fear my master be come but it prove to be only mrs
jervis
she say i cannot endure you should be so much by yourself pamela
and i say i dread nothing so much as company for my heart be up at my mouth now for fear my master be come
but i always rejoice to see dear mrs
jervis
say she i have have a world of talk with my master about you
i be sorry for it say i that i be make of so much consequence as to be talk of by he
o say she i must not tell you all but you be of more consequence to he than you think for---- or wish for say i for the fruit of being of consequence to he would make i of none to myself or any body else
say she thou art as witty as any lady in the land i wonder where thou gottest it
but they must be poor lady with such great opportunity i be sure if they have no more wit than i but let that pass
i suppose say i that i be of so much consequence however as to vex he if it be but to think he cannot make a fool of such a one as i and that be nothing at all but a rebuke to the pride of his high condition which he do not expect and know not how to put up with
there be something in that may be say she but indeed pamela he be very angry with you too and call you twenty perverse thing wonder at his own folly to have shew you so much favour as he call it which he be first incline to he say for his mother s sake and would have persist to shew you for your own if you be not your own enemy
nay now i shallnot love you mrs
jervis say i you be go to persuade i to ask to stay though you know the hazard i run no say she he say you shall go for he think it willnot be for his reputation to keep you but he wish donot speak of it for the world pamela that he know a lady of birth just such another as yourself in person and mind and he would marry she to-morrow
i colour up to the ear at this word but say yet if i be the lady of birth and he would offer to be rude first as he have twice do to poor i i donot know whether i would have he for she that can bear an insult of that kind i should think not worthy to be a gentleman s wife any more than he would be a gentleman that would offer it
nay now pamela say she thou carri thy notion a great way
well dear mrs
jervis say i very seriously for i could not help it i be more full of fear than ever
i have only to beg of you as one of the good friend i have in the world to say nothing of my ask to stay
to say my master like i when i know what end he aim at be abomination to my ear and i shallnot think myself safe till i be at my poor father s and mother s
she be a little angry with i till i assure she that i have not the least uneasiness on her account but think myself safe under her protection and friendship
and so we dropt the discourse for that time
i hope to have finish this ugly waistcoat in two day after which i have only some linen to get up and shall then let you know how i contrive as to my passage for the heavy rain will make it sad travel on foot but may be i may get a place to which be ten mile of the way in farmer nichols s close cart for i cannot sit a horse well at all and may be nobody will be suffer to see i on upon the way
but i hope to let you know more
from etc
letter xxii my dear father and mother all my fellow-servant have now some notion that i be to go away but cannot imagine for what
mrs
jervis tell they that my father and mother grow in year cannot live without i and so i go home to they to help to comfort their old age but they seem not to believe it
what they find it out by be the butler hear he say to i as i pass by he in the entry lead to the hall whobe that
pamela sir say i
pamela

indeed and please your honour say i i have work early and late upon it there be a great deal of work in it work in it

he seem startled when he see the butler as he enter the hall where mr

what do you here

yes it be sir say i but i be in hope it would not be know till i go away

letter xxiii my dear father and mother we have a great many neighbouring gentleman and their lady this day at dinner and my master make a fine entertainment for they and isaac and mr
jonathan and benjamin wait at table and isaac tell mrs
jervis that the lady will by and by come to see the house and have the curiosity to see i for it seem they say to my master when the joke fly about well mr
b---- we understand you have a servant-maid who be the great beauty in the county and we promise ourselves to see she before we go
the wench be well enough say he but no such beauty as you talk of iwill assure ye
she be my mother s waiting-maid who on her death-bed engage i to be kind to she
she be young and every thing be pretty that be young
ay ay say one of the lady thatbe true but if your mother have not recommend she so strongly there be so much merit in beauty that i make no doubt such a fine gentleman would have want no inducement to be kind to it
they all laugh at my master and he it seem laugh for company but say i donot know how it be but i see with different eye from other people for i have hear much more talk of her prettiness than i think it deserve she be well enough as i say but her great excellence be that she be humble and courteous and faithful and make all her fellow-servant love she my housekeeper in particular doat upon she and you know lady she be a woman of discernment and as for mr
longman and jonathan here if they think themselves young enough i be tell they would fight for she
be it not true jonathan
troth sir say he an t please your honour i never know her peer and all your honour s family be of the same mind
do you hear now
say my master well say the lady we will make a visit to mrs
jervis by and by and hope to see this paragon
i believe they be come and will tell you the rest by and by
i wish they have come and be go
why cannot they make their game without i
well these fine lady have be here and be go back again
i would have be absent if i could and do step into the closet so they see i when they come in
there be four of they lady arthur at the great white house on the hill lady brooks lady towers and the other it seem a countess of some hard name i forget what
so mrs
jervis say one of the lady how do you do
we be all come to inquire after your health

ah say lady arthur i have not see your pamela these two year and they tell i she be grow wondrous pretty in that time
then i wish i have not be in the closet for when i come out they must need know i hear they but i have often find that bashful body owe themselves a spite and frequently confound themselves more by endeavour to avoid confusion
why yes say mrs
jervis pamela be very pretty indeed shebe but in the closet there pamela pray step hither
i come out all cover with blush and they smile at one another
the countess take i by the hand why indeed she be pleased to say report have not be too lavish iwill assure you
donot be ashamed child and star full in my face i wish i have just such a face to be ashamed of
o how like a fool i look
lady arthur say ay my good pamela i say as her ladyship say donot be so confused though indeed it become you too
i think your good lady depart make a sweet choice of such a pretty attendant
she would have be mighty proud of you as she always be praise you have she live till now
ah
madam say lady brooks do you think that so dutiful a son as our neighbour who always admire what his mother love do not pride himself for all what he say at table in such a pretty maiden
she look with such a malicious sneer countenance i cannot abide she
lady towers say with a free air for it seem she be call a wit well mrs
pamela i cannot say i like you so well as these lady do for i should never care if you be my servant to have you and your master in the same house together
then they all set up a great laugh
i know what i could have say if i durst
but they be lady--and lady may say any thing
say lady towers can the pretty image speak mrs
jervis
i vow she have speak eye
o you little rogue say she and tap i on the cheek you seem bear to undo or to be undo
god forbid and please your ladyship say i it should be either i beg say i to withdraw for the sense i have of my unworthiness render i unfit for such a presence
i then go away with one of my good courtesy and lady towers say as i go out prettily say i vow and lady brooks say see that shape
i never see such a face and shape in my life why she must be well descend than you have tell i
and so they run on for half an hour more in my praise as i be tell and glad be i when i get out of the hearing of they
but it seem they go down with such a story to my master and so full of i that he have much ado to stand it but as it be very little to my reputation i be sure i could take no pride in it and i fear it would make no well for i
this give i another cause for wish myself out of this house
this be thursday morning and next thursday i hope to set out for i have finish my task and my master be horrid cross
and i be vex his crossness affect i so
if ever he have any kindness towards i i believe he now hat i heartily
be it not strange that love border so much upon hate
but this wicked love be not like the true virtuous love to be sure that and hatred must be as far off as light and darkness
and how must this hate have be increase if he have meet with such a base compliance after his wicked will have be gratify
well one may see by a little what a great deal mean
for if innocence cannot attract common civility what must guilt expect when novelty have cease to have its charm and changeableness have take place of it
thus we read in holy writ that wicked amnon when he have ruin poor tamar hat she more than he ever love she and would have turn she out of door
how happy be i to be turn out of door with that sweet companion my innocence o may that be always my companion
and while i presume not upon my own strength and be willing to avoid the tempter i hope the divine grace will assist i
forgive i that i repeat in my letter part of my hourly prayer
i owe every thing next to god s goodness to your piety and good example my dear parent my dear poor parent
i say that word with pleasure for your poverty be my pride as your integrity shall he my imitation
as soon as i have din i will put on my new clothe
i long to have they on
i know i shall surprise mrs
jervis with they for she shallnot see i till i be full dressed john be come back and iwill soon send you some of what i have written i find he be go early in the morning and so iwill close here that i be your most dutiful daughter
donot lose your time in meet i because i be so uncertain
it be hard if some how or other i cannot get a passage to you
but may be my master willnot refuse to let john bring i
i can ride behind he i believe well enough for he be very careful and very honest and you know john as well as i for he love you both
besides may be mrs

letter xxiv dear father and mother i shall write on as long as i stay though i should have nothing but silliness to write for i know you divert yourself on night with what i write because it be mine
john tell i how much you long for my come but he say he tell you he hop something would happen to hinder it
i be glad you do not tell he the occasion of my come away for if my fellow-servant should guess it be well so than to have it from you or i
besides i really be concern that my master should cast away a thought upon such a poor creature as i for besides the disgrace it have quite turn his temper and i begin to believe what mrs
jervis tell i that he like i and cannot help it and yet strive to conquer it and so find no way but to be cross to i
donot think i presumptuous and conceite for it be more my concern than my pride to see such a gentleman so demean himself and lessen the regard he use to have in the eye of all his servant on my account but i be to tell you of my new dress to day
and so when i have din up stair i go and lock myself into my little room
there i trick myself up as well as i could in my new garb and put on my round-eare ordinary cap but with a green knot however and my homespun gown and petticoat and plain leather shoe but yet they be what they call spanish leather and my ordinary hose ordinary i mean to what i have be lately use to though i shall think good yarn may do very well for every day when i come home
a plain muslin tucker i put on and my black silk necklace instead of the french necklace my lady give i and put the ear-ring out of my ear and when i be quite equipped i take my straw hat in my hand with its two blue string and look about i in the glass as proud as any thing--to say truth i never like myself so well in my life
o the pleasure of descend with ease innocence and resignation indeed there be nothing like it
an humble mind i plainly see cannot meet with any very shocking disappointment let fortune s wheel turn round as it will
so i go down to look for mrs
jervis to see how she like i
i meet as i be upon the stair our rachel who be the house-maid and she make i a low courtesy and i find do not know i
so i smile and go to the housekeeper s parlour and there sit good mrs
jervis at work make a shift and would you believe it
she do not know i at first but rise up and pull off her spectacle and say do you want i forsooth
i could not help laugh and say hey-day
mrs
jervis what

pamela thus metamorphose
how come this about
as it happen in stept my master and my back be to he he think it be a stranger speak to mrs

i tell she i have no clothe suitable to my condition when i return to my father s and so it be well to begin here as i be soon to go away that all my fellow-servant might see i know how to suit myself to the state i be return to

letter xxxii o my dearest father and mother

but my heart bleed so much for you my dear father who have take the pain to go yourself and inquire after your poor daughter as well as for my dear mother that i resolve to write and pretty much in the above form that it might be send to pacify you till i could let you somehow or other know the true state of the matter

williams again indiscreetly help she to an excuse by say that it be now good to defer it one sunday and till matter be riper for my appearance and she readily take hold of it and confirm his opinion
after all i hope the good but if this should turn out to be a plot i fear nothing but a miracle can save i
but sure the heart of man be not capable of such black deceit
besides mr
williams have it under his own hand and he dare not but be in earnest and then again though to be sure he have be very wrong to i yet his education and parent example have neither of they teach he such very black contrivance
so i will hope for the good
mr
williams mrs
jewkes and i have be all three walk together in the garden and she pull out her key and we walk a little in the pasture to look at the bull an ugly grim surly creature that hurt the poor cook-maid who be get pretty well again
mr
williams point at the sunflower but i be force to be very reserved to he for the poor gentleman have no guard no caution at all
we have just sup together all three and i cannot yet think that all must be right only i be resolve not to marry if i can help it and i will give no encouragement i be resolve at least till i be with you
mr
williams say before mrs

he will in send my packet send a most tedious parcel of stuff of my oppression my distress my fear and so i will send this with it for mrs
jewkes give i leave to send a letter to my father which look well and i be glad i can conclude after all my suffering with my hope to be soon with you which i know will give you comfort and so i rest beg the continuance of your prayer and blessing your ever dutiful daughter
my dear father and mother i have so much time upon my hand that i must write on to employ myself
the sunday evening where i leave off mrs
jewkes ask i if i choose to be by myself i say yes with all my heart if she please
well say she after to-night you shall
i ask she for more paper and she give i a bottle of ink eight sheet of paper which she say be all her store for now she would get i to write for she to our master if she have occasion and six pen with a piece of seal wax
this look mighty well
she press i when she come to bed very much to give encouragement to mr
williams and say many thing in his behalf and blame my shyness to he
i tell she i be resolve to give no encouragement till i have talk to my father and mother
she say he fancy i think of somebody else or i could never be so insensible

williams he might do well by far and i have propose so much happiness in live with my poor father and mother that i could not think of any scheme of life with pleasure till i have try that
i ask she for my money and she say it be above in her strong box but that i should have it to-morrow
all these thing look well as i say
mr
williams would go home this night though late because he would despatch a messenger to you with a letter he have propose from himself and my packet
but pray donot encourage he as i say for he be much too heady and precipitate as to this matter in my way of thinking though to be sure he be a very good man and i be much oblige to he
monday morning
alas-a-day
we have bad news from poor mr
williams
he have have a sad mischance fall among rogue in his way home last night but by good chance have save my paper
this be the account he give of it to mrs
jewkes good mrs
jewkes i have have a sore misfortune in go from you
when i have get as near the town as the dam and be go to cross the wooden bridge two fellow get hold of i and swear bitterly they would kill i if i do not give they what i have
they rummage my pocket and take from i my snuff-box my seal-ring and half a guinea and some silver and halfpence also my handkerchief and two or three letter i have in my pocket
by good fortune the letter mrs
pamela give i be in my bosom and so that escape but they bruise my head and face and curse i for have no more money tip i into the dam crying be there parson till to-morrow
my shin and knee be bruise much in the fall against one of the stump and i have like to have be suffocate in water and mud
to be sure i shallnot be able to stir out this day or two for i be a frightful spectacle
my hat and wig i be force to leave behind i and go home a mile and a half without but they be find next morning and bring i with my snuff-box which the rogue must have drop
my cassock be sadly tear as be my band
to be sure i be much frightened for a robbery in these part have not be know many year
diligent search be make after the rogue
my humble respect to good mrs
pamela if she pity my misfortune i shall be the soon well and fit to wait on she and you
this do not hinder i in write a letter though with great pain as i do this to be sure this good man can keep no secret and send it away by a man and horse this morning
i be good mrs
jewkes your most obliged humble servant god be praise it be no bad
and i find i have get no cold though miserably wet from top to toe

pamela the wicked brute fall a laughing when she have read this letter till her fat side shake
say she i can but think how the poor parson look after part with his pretty mistress in such high spirit when he find himself at the bottom of the dam
and what a figure he must cut in his tattered band and cassock and without a hat and wig when he get home
i warrant add she he be in a sweet pickle i say i think it be very barbarous to laugh at such a misfortune but she reply as he be safe she laugh otherwise she would have be sorry and she be glad to see i so concerned for he--it look promising she say
i heed not her reflection but as i have be use to cause for mistrust i cannot help say that i donot like this thing and their take his letter most alarm me how happy it be they miss my packet
i know not what to think of it but why should i let every accident break my peace
yet it will do so while i stay here

well i will go down again and see if all be clear and how it look out at the back-door in the pasture
to be sure there be witchcraft in this house and i believe lucifer be bribe as well as all about i and be get into the shape of that nasty grim bull to watch me for i have be again and venture to open the door and go out about a bow-shot into the pasture but there stand that horrid bull star i full in the face with fiery saucer eye as i think

to trick i so
every thing man woman and beast be in a plot against your poor pamela i think then i know not one step of the way nor how far to any house or cottage and whether i could gain protection if i get to a house and now the robber be abroad too i may run into as great danger as i want to escape nay great much if these promising appearance hold and sure my master cannot be so black as that they should not what can i do i have a good mind to try for it once more but then i may be pursue and take and it will be bad for i and this wicked woman will beat i and take my shoe away and lock i up

but my folly be the bad of all because that deprive i of my money for have i have that i believe i should have venture both the bull and the robber

this alarm we both and he seem quite struck of a heap and put on as i think a self-accuse countenance

i put this in the usual place in the evening and now wait with impatience for an answer
thursday
i have the follow answer dearest madam i be utterly confound and must plead guilty to all your just reproach
i wish i be master of all but half your caution and discretion

b---- neither can nor dare deceive i in so black a manner
i would expose he all the world over if he do
but it be not cannot be in he
i have receive a letter from john arnold in which he tell i that his master be prepare for his london journey and believe afterwards he will come into these part but he say lady davers be at their house and be to accompany her brother to london or meet he there he know not which
he profess great zeal and affection to your service and i find he refer to a letter he send i before but which be not come to my hand
i think there can be no treachery for it be a particular friend at gainsborough that i have order he to direct to and this be come safe to my hand by this mean for well i know i durst trust nothing to brett at the post-house here
this give i a little pain but i hope all will end well and we shall soon hear if it be necessary to pursue our former intention
if it be i will lose no time to provide a horse for you and another for myself for i can never do either god or myself well service though i be to forego all my expectation for it here i be your most faithful humble servant i be too free indeed with mrs
jewkes lead to it by her dissimulation and by her pretended concern to make i happy with you
i hint that i would not have scruple to have procure your deliverance by any mean and that i have propose to you as the only honourable one marriage with i

she watch i as close as ever and pretend to wonder why i shun her company as i do

sir every thing give i additional disturbance
the miss letter of john arnold s make i suspect a plot
yet be i loath to think myself of so much importance as to suppose every one in a plot against i
be you sure however the london journey be not to be a lincolnshire one
may not john who have be once a traitor be so again why need i be thus in doubt if i could have this horse i would turn the rein on his neck and trust to providence to guide he for my safeguard
for i would not endanger you now just upon the edge of your preferment
yet sir i fear your fatal openness will make you suspect as accessary let we be ever so cautious
be my life in question instead of my honesty i would not wish to involve you or any body in the least difficulty for so worthless a poor creature
but o sir
my soul be of equal importance with the soul of a princess though my quality be inferior to that of the mean slave
save then my innocence good heaven
and preserve my mind spotless and happy shall i be to lay down my worthless life and see an end to all my trouble and anxiety
forgive my impatience but my presage mind bode horrid mischief
every thing look dark around i and this woman s impenetrable sullenness and silence without any apparent reason from a conduct so very contrary bid i fear the worst blame i sir if you think i wrong and let i have your advice what to do which will oblige your most afflict servant friday
i have this half-angry answer but what be more to i than all the letter in the world could be your my dear father enclose
madam i think you be too apprehensive by much i be sorry for your uneasiness

how happy be our lot in the midst of our poverty
o let none ever think child a burden to they when the poor circumstance can produce so much rich in a pamela

williams
god bless him happy

shorter my attorney to throw he instantly into gaol on an action of debt for money he have have of i which i have intend never to carry to account against he for i know all his rascally practice besides what you write i of his perfidious intrigue with that girl and his acknowledge contrivance for her escape when he know not for certain that i design she any mischief and when if he have be guide by a sense of piety or compassion for injure innocence as he pretend he would have expostulate with i as his function and my friendship for he might have allow he
but to enter into a vile intrigue with the amiable gewgaw to favour her escape in so base a manner to say nothing of his disgraceful practice against i in sir simon darnford s family of which sir simon himself have inform i be a conduct that instead of prefer the ungrateful wretch as i have intend shall pull down upon he utter ruin
monsieur colbrand my trusty swiss will obey you without reserve if my other servant refuse
as for she deny that she encourage his declaration i believe it not
it be certain the speak picture with all that pretend innocence and bashfulness would have run away with he
yes she would run away with a fellow that she have be acquaint with and that not intimately if you be as careful as you ought to be but a few day at a time when she have the strong assurance of my honour to she
well i think i now hate she perfectly and though i will do nothing to she myself yet i can bear for the sake of my revenge and my injured honour and slight love to see any thing even what she most fear be do to she and then she may be turn loose to her evil destiny and echo to the wood and grove her piteous lamentation for the loss of her fantastical innocence which the romantic ideot make such a work about
