why this say she be a sad letter indeed i be sorry for it but i fear you would carry your nicety too far leave i leave i mrs
jewkes say i for a while i cannot speak nor talk poor heart
say she well iwill come up again presently and hope to find you well
but here take your own letter i wish you well but this be a sad mistake
and so she put down by i that which be intend for i but i have no spirit to read it at present
o man
man
hard- hearted cruel man
what mischief art thou not capable of unrelenting persecutor as thou art
i sit ruminate when i have a little come to myself upon the term of this wicked letter and have no inclination to look into my own
the bad name fool s play-thing artful creature paint bauble gewgaw speak picture be hard word for your poor pamela
and i begin to think whether i be not indeed a very naughty body and have not do vile thing but when i think of his have discover poor john and of sir simon s base officiousness in tell he of mr
williams with what he have resolve against he in revenge for his goodness to i i be quite dispirit and yet still more about that fearful colbrand and what he could see do to i for then i be ready to gasp for breath and my heart quite fail i
then how dreadful be the word that he will decide my fate in three week
gracious heaven say i strike i dead before that time with a thunderbolt or provide some way for my escape these threaten mischief
god forgive i if i sin
at last i take up the letter direct for mrs
jewkes but design for i and i find that little well than the other
these be the hard term it contain well have you do perverse forward artful yet foolish pamela to convince i before it be too late how ill i have do to place my affection on so unworthy an object i have vow honour and love to your unworthiness believe you a mirror of bashful modesty and unspotted innocence and that no perfidious design lurk in so fair a bosom
but now i have find you out you specious hypocrite
and i see that though you could not repose the least confidence in one you have know for year and who under my good mother s misplaced favour for you have grow up in a manner with you when my passion in spite of my pride and the difference of our condition make i stoop to a meanness that now i despise myself for yet you could enter into an intrigue with a man you never know till within these few day past and resolve to run away with a stranger whom your fair face and insinuate art have bewitch to break through all the tie of honour and gratitude to i even at a time when the happiness of his future life depend upon my favour
henceforth for pamela s sake whenever i see a lovely face will i mistrust a deceitful heart and whenever i hear of the great pretence to innocence will i suspect some deep-lay mischief
you be determined to place no confidence in i though i have solemnly over and over engage my honour to you
what though i have alarm your fear in send you one way when you hop to go another yet have i not to convince you of my resolution to do justly by you although with great reluctance such then be my love for you engage not to come near you without your own consent
be not this a voluntary demonstration of the generosity of my intention to you
yet how have you requite i
the very first fellow that your charming face and insinuate address could influence you have practise upon corrupt too i may say and even ruin as the ungrateful wretch shall find and throw your forward self upon he
as therefore you would place no confidence in i my honour owe you nothing and in a little time you shall find how much you have err in treating as you have do a man who be once your affectionate and kind friend mrs
jewkes have direction concern you and if your lot be now hard than you might wish you will bear it the easy because your own rash folly have bring it upon you alas
for i what a fate be mine to be thus think artful and forward and ungrateful when all i intend be to preserve my innocence and when all the poor little shift which his superior wicked wit and cunning have render ineffectual be force upon i in my own necessary defence
when mrs
jewkes come up to i again she find i bath in tear
she seem as i think to be move to some compassion and find myself now entirely in her power and that it be not for i to provoke she i say it be now i see in vain for i to contend against my evil destiny and the superior art of my barbarous master
i will resign myself to the divine will and prepare to expect the bad
but you see how this poor mr
williams be draw in and undone i be sorry i be make the cause of his ruin
poor poor man to be thus involve and for my sake too but if youwill believe i say i i give no encouragement to what he propose as to marriage nor would he have propose it i believe but as the only honourable way he think be leave to save i and his principal motive to it at all be virtue and compassion to one in distress
what other view could he have
you know i be poor and friendless
all i beg of you be to let the poor gentleman have notice of my master s resentment and let he fly the country and not be throw into gaol
this will answer my master s end as well for it will as effectually hinder he from assist i as if he be in a prison
ask i say she to do any thing that be in my power consistent with my duty and trust and i will do it for i be sorry for you both
but to be sure i shall keep no correspondence with he nor let you
i offer to talk of a duty superior to that she mention which would oblige she to help distressed innocence and not permit she to go the length enjoin by lawless tyranny but she plainly bid i be silent on that head for it be in vain to attempt to persuade she to betray her trust all i have to advise you say she be to be easy lie aside all your contrivance and art to get away and make i your friend by give i no reason to suspect you for i glory in my fidelity to my master and you have both practise some strange sly art to make such a progress as he have own there be between you so seldom as i think you see one another and i must be more circumspect than i have be
this double my concern for i now apprehend i should be much close watch than before
well say i since i have by this strange accident discover my hard destiny let i read over again that fearful letter of your that i may get it by heart and with it feed my distress and make calamity familiar to i
then say she let i read yours again
i give her mine and she lend i her and so i take a copy of it with she leave because as i say i would by it prepare myself for the bad
and when i have do i pin it on the head of the couch this say i be the use i shall make of this wretched copy of your letter and here you shall always find it wet with my tear
she say she would go down to order supper and insist upon my company to it
i would have excuse myself but she begin to put on a commanding air that i durst not oppose
and when i go down she take i by the hand and present i to the most hideous monster i ever see in my life
here monsieur colbrand say she here be your pretty ward and mine let we try to make her time with we easy
he bow and put on his foreign grimace and seem to bless himself and in broken english tell i i be happy in de affection of de fine gentleman in de varld i be quite frightened and ready to drop down and i will describe he to you my dear father and mother if now you will ever see this and you shall judge if i have not reason especially not know he be to be there and be apprise as i be of his hat employment to watch i close
he be a giant of a man for stature taller by a good deal than harry mowlidge in your neighbourhood and large boned and scraggy and have a hand i never see such an one in my life
he have great star eye like the bull s that frighten i so vast jaw-bone stick out eyebrow hang over his eye two great scar upon his forehead and one on his left cheek and two large whisker and a monstrous wide mouth blubber lip long yellow tooth and a hideous grin
he wear his own frightful long hair tie up in a great black bag a black crape neckcloth about a long ugly neck and his throat stick out like a wen
as to the rest he be dress well enough and have a sword on with a nasty red knot to it leather garter buckle below his knee and a foot--near as long as my arm i verily think
he say he fright de lady and offer to withdraw but she bid he not and i tell mrs
jewkes that as she know i have be cry she should not have call i to the gentleman without let i know he be there
i soon go up to my closet for my heart ache all the time i be at table not be able to look upon he without horror and this brute of a woman though she see my distress before this addition to it no doubt do it on purpose to strike more terror into i
and indeed it have its effect for when i go to bed i could think of nothing but his hideous person and my master s more hideous action and think they too well pair and when i dropt asleep i dream they be both come to my bedside with the bad design and i jump out of my bed in my sleep and frighten mrs
jewkes till wake with the terror i tell she my dream and the wicked creature only laugh and say all i fear be but a dream as well as that and when it be over and i be well awake i should laugh at it as such
and now i be come to the close of wednesday the 27 th day of my distress
poor mr
williams be actually arrest and carry away to stamford
so there be an end of all my hope from he poor gentleman
his over- security and openness have ruin we both
i be but too well convinced that we ought not to have lose a moment s time but he be half angry and think i too impatient and then his fatal confession and the detestable artifice of my master but one might well think that he who have so cunningly and so wickedly contrive all his stratagem hitherto that it be impossible to avoid they would stick at nothing to complete they
i fear i shall soon find it so
but one stratagem i have just invent though a very discouraging one to think of because i have neither friend nor money nor know one step of the way if i be out of the house
but let bull and bear and lion and tiger and what be bad false treacherous deceitful man stand in my way i cannot be in more danger than i be and i depend nothing upon his three week for how do i know now he be in such a passion and have already begin his vengeance on poor mr
williams that he will not change his mind and come down to lincolnshire before he go to london
my stratagem be this i will endeavour to get mrs
jewkes to go to bed without i as she often do while i sit lock up in my closet and as she sleep very sound in her first sleep of which she never fail to give notice by snore if i can but then get out between the two bar of the window for you know i be very slender and i find i can get my head through then i can drop upon the lead underneath which be little more than my height and which lead be over a little summer-parlour that jut out towards the garden and as i be light i can easily drop from they for they be not high from the ground then i shall be in the garden and then as i have the key of the back-door i will get out
but i have another piece of cunning still good heaven succeed to i my dangerous but innocent devices i have read of a great captain who be in danger leap overboard into the sea and his enemy as he swam shoot at he with bow and arrow he unloose his upper garment and take another course while they stick that full of their dart and arrow and so he escape and live to triumph over they all
so what will i do but strip off my upper petticoat and throw it into the pond with my neckhandkerchief
for to be sure when they miss i they will go to the pond first think i have drown myself and so when they see some of my clothe float there they will be all employ in drag the pond which be a very large one and as i shall not perhaps be miss till the morning this will give i opportunity to get a great way off and i be sure i will run for it when i be out
and so i trust that providence will direct my step to some good place of safety and make some worthy body my friend for sure if i suffer ever so i cannot be in more danger nor in bad hand than where i be and with such avow bad design
o my dear parent
donot be frighten when you come to read this but all will be over before you can see it and so god direct i for the good
my writing for fear i should not escape i will bury in the garden for to be sure i shall be search and use dreadfully if i cannot get off
and so i will close here for the present to prepare for my plot
prosper thou o gracious protector of oppressed innocence
this last effort of thy poor handmaid
that i may escape the crafty device and snare that have begin to entangle my virtue and from which but by this one trial i see no way of escape
and oh
whatever become of i bless my dear parent and protect poor mr
williams from ruin
for he be happy before he know i
just now just now
i hear mrs

williams be a contrivance of her and execute by the groom and a helper in order to seize my letter upon he which they miss
they be now both laugh at the dismal story which they little think i overheard--o how my heart ache
for what be not such wretch capable of

past eleven o clock
mrs
jewkes be come up and go to bed and bid i not stay long in my closet but come to bed
o for a dead sleep for the treacherous brute
i never see she so tipsy and that give i hope

thursday friday saturday sunday the 28 th 29 th 30 th and 31 st day of my distress
and distress indeed
for here i be still and every thing have be bad and bad
oh
the poor unhappy pamela without any hope leave and ruin in all my contrivance
but oh
my dear parent rejoice with i even in this low plunge of my distress for your poor pamela have escape from an enemy bad than any she ever meet with an enemy she never think of before and be hardly able to stand against i mean the weakness and presumption both in one of her own mind which have well nigh have not the divine grace interpose sink she into the low last abyss of misery and perdition
i will proceed as i have opportunity with my sad relation for my pen and ink in my now doubly-secure closet be all i have to employ myself with and indeed i have be so weak that till yesterday evening i have not be able to hold a pen
i take with i but one shift besides what i have on and two handkerchief and two cap which my pocket hold for it be not for i to encumber myself and all my stock of money which be but five or six shilling to set out for i know not where and get out of the window not without some difficulty stick a little at my shoulder and hip but i be resolve to get out if possible
and it be far from the lead than i think and i be afraid i have sprain my ancle and when i have dropt from the lead to the ground it be still far off but i do pretty well there at least
i get no hurt to hinder i from pursue my intention
so be now on the ground i hide my paper under a rose-bush and cover they with mould and there they still lie as i hope
then i hie away to the pond the clock strike twelve just as i get out and it be a dark misty night and very cold but i feel it not then
when i come to the pond-side i fling in my upper-coat as i have design and my neckhandkerchief and a round-eared cap with a knot and then with great speed run to the door and take the key out of my pocket my poor heart beat all the time against my bosom as if it would have force its way through it and beat it well might
for i then too late find that i be most miserably disappoint for the wicked woman have take off that lock and put another on so that my key would not open it
i try and try and feel about i find a padlock besides on another part of the door
o then how my heart sunk i dropt down with grief and confusion unable to stir or support myself for a while
but my fear awaken my resolution and know that my attempt would be as terrible for i as any other danger i could then encounter i clamber up upon the ledge of the door and upon the lock which be a great wooden one and reach the top of the door with my hand then little thinking i could climb so well i make shift to lay hold on the top of the wall with my hand but alas for i
nothing but ill luck no escape for poor pamela
the wall be old the brick i hold by give way just as i be take a spring to get up and down come i and receive such a blow upon my head with one of the brick that it quite stun i and i break my shin and my ancle besides and beat off the heel of one of my shoe
in this dreadful way flat upon the ground lie poor i for i believe five or six minute and then try to get up i sink down again two or three time and my left hip and shoulder be very stiff and full of pain with bruise and besides my head bleed and ache grievously with the blow i have with the brick
yet these hurt i value not but creep a good way upon my foot and hand in search of a ladder i just recollect to have see against the wall two day before on which the gardener be nail a nectarine branch that be loosen from the wall but no ladder could i find and the wall be very high
what now think i must become of the miserable pamela then i begin to wish myself most heartily again in my closet and to repent of my attempt which i now censure as rash because it do not succeed
god forgive i
but a sad thought come just then into my head i tremble to think of it
indeed my apprehension of the usage i should meet with have like to have make i miserable for ever
o my dear dear parent forgive your poor child but be then quite desperate i creep along till i could raise myself on my staggering foot and away limp i what to do but to throw myself into the pond and so put a period to all my grief in this world but o
to find they infinitely aggravate have i not by the divine grace be withhold in a miserable eternity
as i have escape this temptation bless be god for it i will tell you my conflict on this dreadful occasion that the divine mercy may be magnify in my deliverance that i be yet on this side the dreadful gulf from which there could have be no return
it be well for i as i have since thought that i be so maim as make i the long before i get to the water for this give i time to consider and abate the impetuousness of my passion which possibly might otherwise have hurry i in my first transport of grief on my see no way to escape and the hard usage i have reason to expect from my dreadful keeper to throw myself in
but my weakness of body make i move so slowly that it give time as i say for a little reflection a ray of grace to dart in upon my benighted mind and so when i come to the pond-side i sit myself down on the slop bank and begin to ponder my wretched condition and thus i reason with myself
pause here a little pamela on what thou art about before thou takest the dreadful leap and consider whether there be no way yet leave no hope if not to escape from this wicked house yet from the mischief threaten thee in it
i then consider and after i have cast about in my mind every thing that could make i hope and see no probability a wicked woman devoid of all compassion
a horrid helper just arrive in this dreadful colbrand
an angry and resent master who now hat i and threaten the most afflicting evil
and that i should in all probability be deprive even of the opportunity i now have before i to free myself from all their persecutions what hast thou to do distressed creature say i to myself but throw thyself upon a merciful god who know how innocently i suffer to avoid the merciless wickedness of those who be determined on my ruin
and then think i and oh
that thought be surely of the devil s instigation for it be very soothing and powerful with i these wicked wretch who now have no remorse no pity on i will then be move to lament their misdoing and when they see the dead corpse of the unhappy pamela drag out to these dewy bank and lie breathless at their foot they will find that remorse to soften their obdurate heart which now have no place there and my master my angry master will then forget his resentment and say o this be the unhappy pamela
that i have so causelessly persecute and destroy
now do i see she prefer her honesty to her life will he say and be no hypocrite nor deceiver but really be the innocent creature she pretend to be
then think i will he perhaps shed a few tear over the poor corpse of his persecute servant and though he may give out it be love and disappointment and that perhaps in order to hide his own guilt for the unfortunate mr
williams yet will he be inwardly grieve and order i a decent funeral and save i or rather this part of i from the dreadful stake and the highway interment and the young man and maiden all around my dear father s will pity poor pamela
but o
i hope i shall not be the subject of their ballad and elegy but that my memory for the sake of my dear father and mother may quickly slide into oblivion
i be once rise so indulgent be i to this sad way of thinking to throw myself in but again my bruise make i slow and i think what art thou about to do wretche pamela
how know thou though the prospect be all dark to thy short-sighted eye what god may do for thee even when all human mean fail
god almighty would not lay i under these sore affliction if he have not give i strength to grapple with they if i will exert it as i ought and who know but that the very presence i so much dread of my angry and design master for he have have i in his power before and yet i have escape may be well for i than these persecuting emissary of his who for his money be true to their wicked trust and be harden by that and a long habit of wickedness against compunction of heart
god can touch his heart in an instant and if this should not be do i can then but put an end to my life by some other mean if i be so resolved
but how do i know think i that even these bruise and maim that i have get while i pursue only the laudable escape i have meditate may not kindly have furnish i with the opportunity i be now tempt with to precipitate myself and of surrender up my life spotless and unguilty to that merciful being who give it
then think i who give thee presumptuous as thou art a power over thy life
who authorise thee to put an end to it when the weakness of thy mind suggest not to thee a way to preserve it with honour
how know thou what purpose god may have to serve by the trial with which thou art now exercise
art thou to put a bound to the divine will and to say thus much will i bear and no more
and wilt thou dare to say that if the trial be augment and continue thou wilt soon die than bear it
this act of despondency think i be a sin that if i pursue it admit of no repentance and can therefore hope no forgiveness and wilt thou to shorten thy transitory grief heavy as they be and weak as thou fanciest thyself plunge both body and soul into everlasting misery
hitherto pamela think i thou art the innocent the suffering pamela and wilt thou to avoid thy suffering be the guilty aggressor
and because wicked man persecute thee wilt thou fly in the face of the almighty and distrust his grace and goodness who can still turn all these suffering to benefit
and how do i know but that god who see all the lurk vileness of my heart may have permit these suffering on that very score and to make i rely solely on his grace and assistance who perhaps have too much pride myself in a vain dependence on my own foolish contrivance
then again think i wilt thou suffer in one moment all the good lesson of thy poor honest parent and the benefit of their example who have persist in do their duty with resignation to the divine will amidst the extreme degree of disappointment poverty and distress and the persecution of an ungrateful world and merciless creditor to be throw away upon thee and bring down as in all probability this thy rashness will their grey hair with sorrow to the grave when they shall understand that their beloved daughter slight the tender of divine grace despair of the mercy of a protect god have blemish in this last act a whole life which they have hitherto approve and delight in
what then presumptuous pamela dost thou here
think i quit with speed these perilous bank and fly from these curling water that seem in their meaning murmur this still night to reproach thy rashness
tempt not god s goodness on the mossy bank that have be witness of thy guilty purpose and while thou hast power leave thee avoid the tempting evil lest thy grand enemy now repulse by divine grace and due reflection return to the assault with a force that thy weakness may not be able to resist
and let one rash moment destroy all the conviction which now have awe thy rebellious mind into duty and resignation to the divine will
and so say i arise but be so stiff with my hurt so cold with the moist dew of the night and the wet grass on which i have sit as also with the damp arise from so large a piece of water that with great pain i get from this pond which now i think of with terror and bend my limping step towards the house take refuge in the corner of an outhouse where wood and coal be lay up for family use till i should be find by my cruel keeper and consign to a more wretched confinement and bad usage than i have hitherto experience and there behind a pile of firewood i creep and lie down as you may imagine with a mind just break and a heart sensible to nothing but the extreme woe and dejection
this my dear father and mother be the issue of your poor pamela s fruitless enterprise and who know if i have get out at the back-door whether i have be at all in a well case moneyless friendless as i be and in a strange place but blame not your poor daughter too much nay if ever you see this miserable scribble all bath and blot with my tear let your pity get the well of your reprehension
but i know it will--and i must leave off for the present for oh
my strength and my will are at this time very far unequal to one another but yet i will add that though i should have praise god for my deliverance have i be free from my wicked keeper and my design master yet i have more abundant reason to praise he that i have be deliver from a bad enemy myself
i will conclude my sad relation
it seem mrs
jewkes awake not till day-break and not find i in bed she call i and no answer be return she relate that she get out of bed and run to my closet and miss i search under the bed and in another closet find the chamber-door as she have leave it quite fast and the key as usual about her wrist
for if i could have get out of the chamber-door there be two or three passage and door to they all double-locked and barred to go through into the great garden so that to escape there be no way but out of the window and of that window because of the summer-parlour under it for the other window be a great way from the ground
she say she be excessively frightened and instantly raise the swiss and the two maid who lie not far off and find every door fast she say i must be carry away as st
peter be out of prison by some angel
it be a wonder she have not a bad thought
she say she weep and wrung her hand and take on sadly run about like a mad woman little thinking i could have get out of the closet window between the iron bar and indeed i donot know whether i could do so again
but at last finding that casement open they conclude it must be so and run out into the garden and find my footstep in the mould of the bed which i dropt down upon from the lead and so speed away all of they that be to say mrs
jewkes colbrand and nan towards the back-door to see if that be fast while the cook be send to the out-office to raise the man and make they get horse ready to take each a several way to pursue i
but it seem find that door double-locked and padlock and the heel of my shoe and the broken brick they verily conclude i be get away by some mean over the wall and then they say mrs
jewkes seem like a distracted woman till at last nan have the thought to go towards the pond and there see my coat and cap and handkerchief in the water cast almost to the bank by the agitation of the wave she think it be i and scream out run to mrs
jewkes and say o madam madam
herebe a piteous thing mrs
pamela lie drown in the pond
thither they all run and find my clothe doubt not i be at the bottom and they all swiss among the rest beat their breast and make most dismal lamentation and mrs
jewkes send nan to the man to bid they get the drag-net ready and leave the horse and come to try to find the poor innocent
as she it seem then call i beat she breast and lament my hard hap but most what would become of they and what account they should give to my master
while every one be thus differently employ some weeping and wail some run here and there nan come into the wood-house and there lie poor i so weak so low and deject and withal so stiff with my bruise that i could not stir nor help myself to get upon my foot

so the maid take i up between they and carry i to my chamber and when the wretch see how bad i be she begin a little to relent--while every one wonder at which i have neither strength nor inclination to tell they how all this come to pass which they impute to sorcery and witchcraft
i be so weak when i have get up stair that i faint away with dejection pain and fatigue and they undress i and get i to bed and mrs
jewkes order nan to bathe my shoulder and arm and ancle with some old rum warm and they cut the hair a little from the back part of my head and wash that for it be clot with blood from a pretty long but not a deep gash and put a family plaister upon it for if this woman have any good quality it be it seem in a readiness and skill to manage in case where sudden misfortune happen in a family
after this i fall into a pretty sound and refreshing sleep and lie till twelve o clock tolerably easy consider i be very feverish and aguishly inclined and she take a deal of care to fit i to undergo more trial which i have hop would have be happily end but providence do not see fit
she would make i rise about twelve but i be so weak i could only sit up till the bed be make and go into it again and be as they say delirious some part of the afternoon
but have a tolerable night on thursday i be a good deal well on friday and on saturday get up and eat a little spoon-meat and my feverishness seem to be go and i be so mend by evening that i beg her indulgence in my closet to be leave to myself which she consent to it be double-bar the day before and i assure she that all my contrivance as she call they be at an end
but first she make i tell the whole story of my enterprise which i do very faithfully know now that nothing could stand i in any stead or contribute to my safety and escape and she seem full of wonder at my resolution but tell i frankly that i should have find it a hard matter to get quite off for that she be provide with a warrant from my master who be a justice of peace in this county as well as in the other to get i apprehend if i have get away on suspicion of wrong he let i have be where i would
o how deep-lay be the mischief design to fall on my devoted head surely surely i cannot be worthy of all this contrivance
this too well shews i the truth of what be hint to i formerly at the other house that my master swear he would have i
o preserve i heaven
from be his in his own wicked sense of the adjuration
i must add that now the woman see i pick up so fast she use i bad and have abridge i of paper all but one sheet which i be to shew she write or unwritten on demand and have reduce i to one pen yet my hidden store stand i in stead
but she be more and more snappish and cross and tauntingly call i mrs
williams and any thing she think will vex i
sunday afternoon
mrs
jewkes have think fit to give i an airing for three or four hour this afternoon and i be a good deal well and should be much more so if i know for what i be reserve
but health be a blessing hardly to be covet in my circumstance since that but expose i to the calamity i be in continual apprehension of whereas a weak and sickly state might possibly move compassion for i
o how i dread the come of this angry and incense master though i be sure i have do he no harm
just now we hear that he have like to have be drown in cross the stream a few day ago in pursue his game
what be the matter that with all his ill usage of i i cannot hate he
to be sure i be not like other people
he have certainly do enough to make i hate he but yet when i hear his danger which be very great i could not in my heart forbear rejoice for his safety though his death would have end my affliction
ungenerous master
if you know this you surely would not be so much my persecutor
but for my late good lady s sake i must wish he well and o what an angel would he be in my eye yet if he would cease his attempt and reform
well i hear by mrs
jewkes that john arnold be turn away be detect in write to mr
williams and that mr
longman and mr
jonathan the butler have incur his displeasure for offer to speak in my behalf
mrs
jervis too be in danger for all these three probably go together to beg in my favour for now it be know where i be
mrs
jewkes have with the news about my master receive a letter but she say the content be too bad for i to know
they must be bad indeed if they be bad than what i have already know
just now the horrid creature tell i as a secret that she have reason to think he have find out a way to satisfy my scruple it be by marry i to this dreadful colbrand and buy i of he on the wedding day for a sum of money was ever the like heard she say it will be my duty to obey my husband and that mr
williams will be force as a punishment to marry we and that when my master have pay for i and i be surrender up the swiss be to go home again with the money to his former wife and child for she say it be the custom of those people to have a wife in every nation
but this to be sure be horrid romancing
yet abominable as it be it may possibly serve to introduce some plot now hatching with what strange perplexity be my poor mind agitated
perchance some sham- marriage may be design on purpose to ruin i but can a husband sell his wife against her own consent and will such a bargain stand good in law
monday tuesday wednesday the 32 d 33 d and 34 th day of my imprisonment
nothing offer these day but squabbling between mrs
jewkes and i
she grow bad and bad to i
i vex she yesterday because she talk nastily and tell she she talk more like a vile london prostitute than a gentleman s housekeeper and she think she cannot use i bad enough for it
bless i
she curse and storm at i like a trooper and can hardly keep her hand off i
you may believe she must talk sadly to make i say such harsh word indeed it cannot be repeat as she be a disgrace to her sex
and then she ridicule i and laugh at my notion of honesty and tell i impudent creature as she be
what a fine bed-fellow i shall make for my master and such- like with such whimsical notion about me do you think this be to be bear
and yet she talk bad than this if possible
quite filthily
o what vile hand be i put into
thursday
i have now all the reason that can be to apprehend my master will be here soon for the servant be busy in set the house to right and a stable and coach-house be clean out that have not be use some time
i ask mrs
jewkes but she tell i nothing nor will hardly answer i when i ask she a question
sometimes i think she put on these strange wicked air to i purposely to make i wish for what i dread most of all thing my master s come down
he talk of love if he have any the least notion of regard for i to be sure he would not give this naughty body such power over me and if he do come where be his promise of not see i without i consent to it
but it seem his honour owe i nothing
so he tell i in his letter
and why
because i be willing to keep mine
but indeed he say he hat i perfectly but it be plain he do or i should not be leave to the mercy of this woman and what be bad to my woful apprehension
friday the 36 th day of my imprisonment
i take the liberty yesterday afternoon find the gate open to walk out before the house and ere i be aware have get to the bottom of the long row of elm and there i sit myself down upon the step of a sort of broad stile which lead into the road and go towards the town
and as i sit muse upon what always busy my mind i see a whole body of folk run towards i from the house man and woman as in a fright
at first i wonder what be the matter till they come near and i find they be all alarmed think i have attempt to get off
there be first the horrible colbrand run with his long leg well nigh two yard at a stride then there be one of the groom poor mr
williams s robber then i spy nan half out of breath and the cook- maid after she
and lastly come waddle as fast as she could mrs
jewkes exclaim most bitterly as i find against i
colbrand say o how have you fright we all and go behind i lest i should run away as i suppose
i sit still to let they see i have no view to get away for besides the improbability of succeed my last sad attempt have cure i of enterprise again
and when mrs
jewkes come within hearing i find she terribly incense and rave about my contrivance
why say i should you be so concerned
here i have sit a few minute and have not the least thought of get away or go far but to return as soon as it be duskish
she would not believe i and the barbarous creature strike at i with her horrid fist and i believe would have fell i have not colbrand interpose and say he see i sit still look about i and not seem to have the least inclination to stir
but this would not serve she order the two maid to take i each by an arm and lead i back into the house and up stair and there have i be lock up ever since without shoe
in vain have i plead that i have no design as indeed i have not the least and last night i be force to be between she and nan and i find she be resolve to make a handle of this against i and in her own behalf indeed what with her usage and my own apprehension of still bad i be quite weary of my life
just now she have be with i and give i my shoe and have lay she imperious command upon i to dress myself in a suit of clothe out of the portmanteau which i have not see lately against three or four o clock for she say she be to have a visit from lady darnford s two daughter who come purposely to see i and so she give i the key of the portmanteau
but i will not obey she and i tell she i would not be make a show of nor see the lady
she leave i say it would be bad for i if i do not

seven o clock

i can hardly write yet as i can do nothing else i know not how to forbear yet i cannot hold my pen--how crooked and tremble the line
i must leave off till i can get quieter fingers why should the guiltless tremble so when the guilty can possess their mind in peace
saturday morning
now let i give you an account of what pass last night for i have no power to write nor yet opportunity till now
this vile woman hold my master till half an hour after seven and he come hither about five in the afternoon
and then i hear his voice on the stair as he be come up to i
it be about his supper for he say i shall choose a boil chicken with butter and parsley and up he come
he put on a stern and majestic air and he can look very majestic when he please
well perverse pamela ungrateful runaway say he for my first salutation you do well donot you to give i all this trouble and vexation
i could not speak but throw myself on the floor hid my face and be ready to die with grief and apprehension he say well may you hide your face
well may you be ashamed to see i vile forward one as you are i sob and weep but could not speak
and he let i lie and go to the door and call mrs
jewkes there say he take up that fall angel once i think she as innocent as an angel of light but i have now no patience with she
the little hypocrite prostrate herself thus in hope to move my weakness in her favour and that iwill raise she from the floor myself
but i shall not touch she no say he cruel gentleman as he be
let such fellow as williams be take in by her artful wile
i know she now and see she be for any fool s turn that will be catch by she
i sigh as if my heart would break and mrs
jewkes lift i up upon my knee for i tremble so i could not stand
come say she mrs
pamela learn to know your good friend confess your unworthy behaviour and beg his honour s forgiveness of all your fault
i be ready to faint and he say she be mistress of art iwill assure you and will mimic a fit ten to one in a minute
i be strike to the heart at this but could not speak presently only lift up my eye to heaven and at last make shift to say--god forgive you sir he seem in a great passion and walk up and down the room cast sometimes an eye upon i and seem as if he would have speak but check himself--and at last he say when she have act this her first part over perhaps i will see she again and she shall soon know what she have to trust to
and so he go out of the room and i be quite sick at heart surely say i i be the wicked creature that ever breathe
well say the impertinent not so wicked as that neither but i be glad you begin to see your fault
nothing like be humble come iwill stand your friend and plead for you if youwill promise to be more dutiful for the future come come add the wretch this may be all make up by to- morrow morning if you be not a fool begone hideous woman
say i and let not my affliction be add to by thy inexorable cruelty and unwomanly wickedness
she give i a push and go away in a violent passion and it seem she make a story of this and say i have such a spirit there be no bearing it
i lay i down on the floor and have no power to stir till the clock strike nine and then the wicked woman come up again
you must come down stair say she to my master that be if you please spirit said i i believe i cannot stand
then say she iwill send mons
colbrand to carry you down
i get up as well as i could and tremble all the way down stair and she go before i into the parlour and a new servant that he have wait on he instead of john withdraw as soon as i come in and by the way he have a new coachman too which look as if bedfordshire robin be turn away
i think say he when i come down you should have sit at table with i when i have not company but when i find you cannot forget your original but must prefer my menial to i i call you down to wait on i while i sup that i may have some talk with you and throw away as little time as possible upon you
sir say i you do i honour to wait upon you and i never shall i hope forget my original
but i be force to stand behind his chair that i might hold by it
fill i say he a glass of that burgundy
i go to do it but my hand shake so that i could not hold the plate with the glass in it and spill some of the wine

he take it and say stand behind i out of my sight
why mrs

no say she not so much as will keep life and soul together and be always cry you say too
yes sir answer she i think she be for one thing or another
ay say he your young wench will feed upon their tear and their obstinacy will serve they for meat and drink

she deny it herself say she but it have all the appearance of one

that you be here little hypocrite as you be plead your cause before i be owe to her care of you else you have be with the parson wicked girl
say he to tempt a man to undo himself as you have do he at a time i be on the point of make he happy for his life
i arise but say with a deep sigh i have do sir i have done i have a strange tribunal to plead before
the poor sheep in the fable have such an one when it be try before the vulture on the accusation of the wolf
so mrs

you donot know how well this innocent be read in reflection
she have wit at will when she have a mind to display her own romantic innocence at the price of other people s character
well say the aggravate creature this be nothing to what she have call i i have be a jezebel a london prostitute and what not but i be content with her ill name now i see it be her fashion and she can call your honour a vulture
say i i have no thought of compare my master--and be go to say on but he say donot prate girl no say she it donot become you i be sure
well say i since i must not speak i will hold my peace but there be a righteous judge who know the secret of all heart and to he i appeal
see there

o she can curse most heartily in the spirit of christian meekness iwill assure you come saucy-face give i another glass of wine
so i do as well as i could but weep so that he say i suppose i shall have some of your tear in my wine

you have be tell i suppose that you be most beautiful in your tears did you ever say he to she who all this while be stand in one corner of the parlour see a more charming creature than this
be it to be wonder at that i demean myself thus to take notice of her see say he and take the glass with one hand and turn i round with the other what a shape

what a hand
and what a bloom on that lovely face but who can describe the trick and artifice that lie lurk in her little plotting guileful heart
tis no wonder the poor parson be infatuate with her i blame he less than i do she for who could expect such artifice in so young a sorceress
i go to the farther part of the room and hold my face against the wainscot and in spite of all i could do to refrain crying sob as if my heart would break
he say i be surprised mrs
jewkes at the mistake of the letter you tell i of
but you see i be not afraid any body should read what i write
i donot carry on private correspondence and reveal every secret that come to my knowledge and then corrupt people to carry my letter against their duty and all good conscience
come hither hussy
say he you and i have a dreadful reckoning to make
why donot you come when i bid you fie upon it mrs
pamela say she
what
not stir when his honour command you to come to him who know but his goodness will forgive you
he come to i for i have no power to stir and put his arm about my neck and would kiss i and say well mrs
jewkes if it be not for the thought of this cursed parson i believe in my heart so great be my weakness that i could not forgive this intriguing little slut and take she to my bosom
o say the sycophant you be very good sir very forgiving indeed but come add the profligate wretch i hope you will be so good as to take she to your bosom and that by to-morrow morning youwill bring she to a well sense of her duty

i have no patience but yet grief and indignation choake up the passage of my word and i could only stammer out a passionate exclamation to heaven to protect my innocence
but the word be the subject of their ridicule
be ever poor creature worse beset
he say as if he have be consider whether he could forgive i or not no i cannot yet forgive her neither she have give i great disturbance have bring great discredit upon i both abroad and at home have corrupt all my servant at the other house have despise my honourable view and intention to she and seek to run away with this ungrateful parson and surely i ought not to forgive all this yet with all this wretched grimace he kiss i again and would have put his hand into my bosom but i struggle and say i would die before i would be use thus consider pamela say he in a threatening tone consider where you be
and donot play the fool if you do a more dreadful fate await you than you expect
but take she up stair mrs
jewkes and iwill send a few line to she to consider of and let i have your answer pamela in the morning
till then you have to resolve and after that your doom be fixed so i go up stair and give myself up to grief and expectation of what he would send but yet i be glad of this night s reprieve
he send i however nothing at all
and about twelve o clock mrs
jewkes and nan come up as the night before to be my bed-fellow and i would go to bed with some of my clothe on which they mutter at sadly and mrs
jewkes rail at i particularly
indeed i would have sit up all night for fear if she would have let i
for i have but very little rest that night apprehend this woman would let my master in
she do nothing but praise he and blame i but i answer she as little as i could
he have sir simon tell-tale alias darnford to dine with he to-day whose family send to welcome he into the country and it seem the old knight want to see i so i suppose i shall be send for as samson be to make sport for him here i be and must bear it all
twelve o clock saturday noon
just now he have send i up by mrs
jewkes the follow proposal
so here be the honourable intention all at once lay open
they be my dear parent to make i a vile keep mistress which i hope i shall always det the thought of
but youwill see how they be accommodate to what i should have most desire could i have honestly promote it your welfare and happiness
i have answer they as i be sure youwill approve and i be prepare for the bad for though i fear there will be nothing omit to ruin i and though my poor strength will not be able to defend i yet i will be innocent of crime in my intention and in the sight of god and to he leave the avenging of all my wrong time and manner
i shall write to you my answer against his article and hope the good though i fear the bad
but if i should come home to you ruin and undo and may not be able to look you in the face yet pity and inspirit the poor pamela to make her little remnant of life easy for long i shall not survive my disgrace and you may be assure it shall not be my fault if it be my misfortune
to mrs
pamela andrews
the following articles be propose to your serious consideration and let i have an answer in writing to they that i may take my resolution accordingly
only remember that i will not be trifle with and what you give for answer will absolutely decide your fate without expostulation or farth trouble
this be my answer
forgive sir the spirit your poor servant be about to show in her answer to your article
not to be warm and in earnest on such an occasion as the present would shew a degree of guilt that i hope my soul abhor
i will not trifle with you nor act like a person doubtful of her own mind for it want not one moment s consideration with i and i therefore return the answer following let what will be the consequence
i
if you can convince i that the hat parson have have no encouragement from you in his address and that you have no inclination for he in preference to i then i will offer the follow proposal to you which i will punctually make good
i
as to the first article sir it may behove i that i may not deserve in your opinion the opprobrious term of forward and artful and such like to declare solemnly that mr
williams never have the least encouragement from i as to what you hint and i believe his principal motive be the apprehend duty of his function quite contrary to his apparent interest to assist a person he think in distress
you may sir the rather believe i when i declare that i know not the man breathe i would wish to marry and that the only one i could honour more than another be the gentleman who of all other seek my everlaste dishonour
ii
i will directly make you a present of 500 guinea for your own use which you may dispose of to any purpose you please and will give it absolutely into the hand of any person you shall appoint to receive it and expect no favour in return till you be satisfied in the possession of it
ii
as to your second proposal let the consequence be what it will i reject it with all my soul
money sir be not my chief good may god almighty desert i whenever it be
and whenever for the sake of that i can give up my title to that bless hope which will stand i in stead at a time when million of gold will not purchase one happy moment of reflection on a past misspent life
iii
i will likewise directly make over to you a purchase i lately make in kent which bring in 250 l
per annum clear of all deduction
this shall be make over to you in full property for your life and for the life of any child to perpetuity that you may happen to have and your father shall be immediately put into possession of it in trust for these purpose and the management of it will yield a comfortable subsistence to he and your mother for life and i will make up any deficiency if such should happen to that clear sum and allow he 50 l
per annum besides for his life and that of your mother for his care and management of this your estate
iii
your third proposal sir i reject for the same reason and be sorry you could think my poor honest parent would enter into their part of it and be concerned for the management of an estate which would be owe to the prostitution of their poor daughter
forgive sir my warmth on this occasion but you know not the poor man and the poor woman my ever-dear father and mother if you think that they would not much rather choose to starve in a ditch or rot in a noisome dungeon than accept of the fortune of a monarch upon such wicked term
i dare not say all that my full mind suggest to i on this grievous occasion--but indeed sir you know they not nor shall the terror of death in its most frightful form i hope through god s assist grace ever make i act unworthy of such poor honest parent
iv
i will moreover extend my favour to any other of your relation that you may think worthy of it or that be value by you
iv
your fourth proposal i take upon i sir to answer as the third
if i have any friend that want the favour of the great may they ever want it if they be capable of desire it on unworthy term
v
i will besides order pattern to be send you for choose four complete suit of rich clothe that you may appear with reputation as if you be my wife
and will give you the two diamond ring and two pair of ear-ring and diamond necklace that be buy by my mother to present to miss tomlins if the match that be propose between she and i have be bring to effect and i will confer upon you still other gratuity as i shall find myself oblige by your good behaviour and affection
v
fine clothe sir become not i nor have i any ambition to wear they
i have great pride in my poverty and meanness than i should have in dress and finery
believe i sir i think such thing less become the humble-bear pamela than the rag your good mother raise i from
your ring sir your necklace and your ear-ring will well befit lady of degree than i and to lose the good jewel my virtue would be poorly recompense by those you propose to give i
what should i think when i look upon my finger or see in the glass those diamond on my neck and in my ear but that they be the price of my honesty and that i wear those jewel outwardly because i have none inwardly
vi
now pamela will you see by this what a value i set upon the free-will of a person already in my power and who if these proposal be not accept shall find that i have not take all these pain and risk my reputation as i have do without resolve to gratify my passion for you at all adventure and if you refuse without make any term at all
vi
i know sir by woful experience that i be in your power i know all the resistance i can make will be poor and weak and perhaps stand i in little stead i dread your will to ruin i be as great as your power yet sir will i dare to tell you that i will make no free-will offering of my virtue
all that i can do poor as it be i will do to convince you that your offer shall have no part in my choice and if i cannot escape the violence of man i hope by god s grace i shall have nothing to reproach myself for not do all in my power to avoid my disgrace and then i can safely appeal to the great god my only refuge and protector with this consolation that my will bore no part in my violation
vii
you shall be mistress of my person and fortune as much as if the foolish ceremony have pass
all my servant shall be yours and you shall choose any two person to attend yourself either male or female without any control of mine and if your conduct be such that i have reason to be satisfied with it i know not but will not engage for this that i may after a twelvemonth s cohabitation marry you for if my love increase for you as it have do for many month past it will be impossible for i to deny you any thing
and now pamela consider well it be in your power to oblige i on such term as will make yourself and all your friend happy but this will be over this very day irrevocably over and you shall find all you would be think to fear without the least benefit arise from it to yourself
and i beg youwill well weigh the matter and comply with my proposal and i will instantly set about securing to you the full effect of they and let i if you value yourself experience a grateful return on this occasion and iwill forgive all thatbe past vii
i have not once dare to look so high as to such a proposal as your seventh article contain
hence have proceed all my little abortive artifice to escape from the confinement you have put i in although you promise to be honourable to i
your honour well i know would not let you stoop to so mean and so unworthy a slave as the poor pamela all i desire be to be permit to return to my native meanness unviolate
what have i do sir to deserve it should be otherwise
for the obtaining of this though i would not have marry your chaplain yet would i have run away with your mean servant if i have think i could have get safe to my beloved poverty
i hear you once say sir that a certain great commander who could live upon lentil might well refuse the bribe of the great monarch and i hope as i can contentedly live at the mean rate and think not myself above the low condition that i be also above make an exchange of my honesty for all the rich of the indies
when i come to be proud and vain of gaudy apparel and outside finery then which i hope will never be may i rest my principal good in such vain trinket and despise for they the more solid ornament of a good fame and a chastity inviolate
give i leave to say sir in answer to what you hint that you may in a twelvemonth s time marry i on the continuance of my good behaviour that this weigh less with i if possible than any thing else you have say for in the first place there be an end of all merit and all good behaviour on my side if i have now any the moment i consent to your proposal and i should be so far from expect such an honour that i will pronounce that i should be most unworthy of it
what sir would the world say be you to marry your harlot
that a gentleman of your rank in life should stoop not only to the base-bear pamela but to a base-bear prostitute little sir as i know of the world i be not to be catch by a bait so poorly cover as this
yet after all dreadful be the thought that i a poor weak friendless unhappy creature be too full in your power
but permit i sir to pray as i now write on my bended knee that before you resolve upon my ruin you will weigh well the matter
hitherto sir though you have take large stride to this cry sin yet be you on this side the commission of it when once it be do nothing can recall it
and where will be your triumph what glory will the spoil of such a weak enemy yield you
let i but enjoy my poverty with honesty be all my prayer and i will bless you and pray for you every moment of my life
think o think
before it be yet too late
what sting what remorse will attend your die hour when you come to reflect that you have ruin perhaps soul and body a wretched creature whose only pride be her virtue
and how pleased you will be on the contrary if in that tremendous moment you shall be able to acquit yourself of this foul crime and to plead in your own behalf that you suffer the earnest supplication of an unhappy wretch to prevail with you to be innocent yourself and let she remain so may god almighty whose mercy so lately save you from the peril of perish in deep water on which i hope you will give i cause to congratulate you touch your heart in my favour and save you from this sin and i from this ruin and to he do i commit my cause and to he will i give the glory and night and day pray for you if i may be permit to escape this great evil your poor oppress broken spirited servant
i take a copy of this for your perusal my dear parent if i shall ever be so happy to see you again for i hope my conduct will be approve of by you and at night when sir simon be go he send for i down
well say he have you consider my proposal
yes sir say i i have and there be my answer but pray let i not see you read it
be it your bashfulness say he or your obstinacy that make you not choose i should read it before you
i offer to go away and he say donot run from i i willnot read it till you be go
but say he tell i pamela whether you comply with my proposal or not
sir say i you will see presently pray donot hold i for he take my hand
say he do you well consider before you answered i do sir say i
if it be not what you think will please i say he dear girl take it back again and reconsider it for if i have this as your absolute answer and i donot like it you be undo for i will not sue meanly where i can command
i fear say he it be not what i like by your manner and let i tell you that i cannot bear denial
if the term i have offer be not sufficient i will augment they to two-third of my estate for say he and swear a dreadful oath i cannot live without you and since the thing be go so far i will not
and so he clasp i in his arm in such a manner as quite frighten i and kiss i two or three time
i get from he and run up stair and go to the closet and be quite uneasy and fearful
in an hour s time he call mrs
jewkes down to he
and i hear he very high in passion and all about i
and i hear she say it be his own fault there would be an end of all my complaining and perverseness if he be once resolve and other most impudent aggravation
i be resolve not to go to bed this night if i can help it lie still lie still my poor flutter heart what will become of i
almost twelve o clock saturday night
he send mrs
jewkes about ten o clock to tell i to come to he
where
say i
iwill shew you say she
i go down three or four step and see she make to his chamber the door of which be open so i say i cannot go there don t be foolish say she but come no harm will be do to you well say i if i die i cannot go there

sunday morning
a thought come into my head i mean no harm but it be a little bold
for see my master dress to go to church and his chariot get ready i go to my closet and i writ the prayer of this congregation be earnestly desire for a gentleman of great worth and honour who labour under a temptation to exert his great power to ruin a poor distressed worthless maiden and also the prayer of this congregation be earnestly desire by a poor distressed creature for the preservation of her virtue and innocence

sunday evening

tuesday night
for the future i will always mistrust most when appearance look fairest
o your poor daughter
what have she not suffer since what i write on sunday night my bad trial and my fearfull danger
o how i shudder to write you an account of this wicked interval of time

and so the poor maid go mutter up stair

jervis in this manner here say i be my poor honest parent they take care to instill good principle into my mind till i be almost twelve year of age and teach i to prefer goodness and poverty to the high condition of life and they confirm their lesson by their own practice for they be of late year remarkably poor and always as remarkably honest even to a proverb for as honest as goodman andrews be a byeword
well then say i come my late dear good lady and take a fancy to i and say she would be the making of i if i be a good girl and she put i to sing to dance to play on the spinnet in order to divert she melancholy hour and also teach i all manner of fine needle-work but still this be her lesson my good pamela be virtuous and keep the man at a distance
well so i be i hope and so i do and yet though i say it they all love i and respect i and would do any thing for i as if i be a gentlewoman
but then what come next why it please god to take my good lady and then come my master and what say he why in effect it be be not virtuous pamela
so here i have live about sixteen year in virtue and reputation and all at once when i come to know what be good and what be evil i must renounce all the good all the whole sixteen year innocence which next to god s grace i owe chiefly to my parent and my lady s good lesson and example and choose the evil and so in a moment s time become the vilest of creature
and all this for what i pray
why truly for a pair of diamond ear-ring a necklace and a diamond ring for my finger which would not become i for a few paltry fine clothe which when i wear they would make but my former poverty more ridiculous to every body that see i especially when they know the base term i wear they upon
but indeed i be to have a great parcel of guineas beside i forget how many for have there be ten time more they would have be not so much to i as the honest six guineas you trick i out of mrs
jewkes

yes thank he for nothing truly
and what pray be all these violent misdeeds why they be for dare to adhere to the good lesson that be teach i and not learn a new one that would have reverse all my former for not be content when i be run away with in order to be ruin but contrive if my poor wit have be able to get out of danger and preserve myself honest
then be he once jealous of poor john though he know john be his own creature and help to deceive i
then be he outrageous against poor parson williams

why truly for that be a divine and a good man he have the fear of god before his eye and be willing to forego all his expectation of interest and assist an oppressed poor creature

and to what purpose

wednesday morning
i find i be watch and suspect still very close and i wish i be with you but that must not be it seem this fortnight

my master just now send for i down to take a walk with he in the garden but i like he not at all nor his way for he would have all the way his arm about my waist and say abundance of fond thing to i enough to make i proud if his design have not be apparent

jewkes and do not think i hear he and which though always uppermost with i i do not mention before because i do not know how to bring it in in my writing

but i trust that that god who have deliver i from the paw of the lion and the bear that be his and mrs
jewkes s violence will soon deliver i from this philistine that i may not defy the command of the live god
but as i be say this expression come into my thought i be of opinion i could not be too much on my guard at all time more especially when he take such liberty for he profess honour all the time with his mouth while his action do not correspond

but indeed i will not talk with you sir say i any more
do you know say he whom you talk to and where you be

why tell i say he what answer you would make
it will only make you angry say i and so i shall fare bad if possible
i willnot be angry say he
why then sir say i you cannot be my late good lady s son for she love i and teach i virtue

he put his arm round i and his other hand on my neck which make i more angry and bold and he say what then be i
why say i struggle from he and in a great passion to be sure you be lucifer himself in the shape of my master or you could not use i thus
these be too great liberty say he in anger and i desire that you will not repeat they for your own sake for if you have no decency towards i iwill have none towards you
i be run from he and he say come back when i bid you so know every place be alike dangerous to i and i have nobody to run to i come back at his call and see he look displeased i hold my hand together and weep and say pray sir forgive i
no say he rather say pray lucifer forgive i
and now since you take i for the devil how can you expect any good from me how rather can you expect any thing but the bad treatment from me you have give i a character pamela and blame i not that i act up to it
sir say i let i beg you to forgive i i be really sorry for my boldness but indeed you donot use i like a gentleman and how can i express my resentment if i mince the matter while you be so indecent
precise fool

but begone

begone
say he again with a haughty air
indeed sir say i i cannot go till you pardon i which i beg on my bended knee
i be truly sorry for my boldness but i see how you go on you creep by little and little upon i and now soothe i and now threaten i and if i should forbear to shew my resentment when you offer incivility to i would not that be to be lose by degree
would it not shew that i could bear any thing from you if i do not express all the indignation i could express at the first approach you make to what i dread
and have you not as good as avow my ruin and have you once make i hope you will quit your purpose against i
how then sir can i act but by shew my abhorrence of every step that make towards my undoing
and what be leave i but words and can these word be other than such strong one as shall shew the detestation which from the bottom of my heart i have for every attempt upon my virtue
judge for i sir and pardon i
pardon you
say he what
when you donot repent when you have the boldness to justify yourself in your fault
why donot you say you never will again offend i
i will endeavour sir say i always to preserve that decency towards you which become i
but really sir i must beg your excuse for say that when you forget what belong to decency in your action and when word be all that be leave i to shew my resentment of such action i will not promise to forbear the strong expression that my distressed mind shall suggest to i nor shall your angriest frown deter i when my honesty be in question
what then say he do you beg pardon for
where be the promise of amendment for which i should forgive you
indeed sir say i i own that must absolutely depend on your usage of i for i will bear any thing you can inflict upon i with patience even to the laying down of my life to shew my obedience to you in other case but i cannot be patient i cannot be passive when my virtue be at stake
it would be criminal in i if i be
he say he never see such a fool in his life
and he walk by the side of i some yard without say a word and seem vex and at last walk in bid i attend he in the garden after dinner
so have a little time i go up and write thus far
wednesday night
if my dear parent i be not destine more surely than ever for ruin i have now more comfort before i than ever i yet know and be either nearer my happiness or my misery than ever i be
god protect i from the latter if it be his blessed will
i have now such a scene to open to you that i know will alarm both your hope and your fear as it do mine

ah mrs

and it be by the side of this pond and not far from the place where i have that dread conflict that my present hop if i be not to be deceive again begin to dawn which i presume to flatter myself with be a happy omen for i as if god almighty would shew your poor sinful daughter how well i do to put my affiance in his goodness and not to throw away myself because my ruin seem inevitable to my short-sight apprehension
so he be pleased to say well pamela i be glad you be come of your own accord as i may say give i your hand
i do so and he look at i very steadily and press my hand all the time at last say i will now talk to you in a serious manner
you have a good deal of wit a great deal of penetration much beyond your year and as i think your opportunity
you be possess of an open frank and generous mind and a person so lovely that you excel all your sex in my eye
all these accomplishment have engage my affection so deeply that as i have often say i cannot live without you and i would divide with all my soul my estate with you to make you mine upon my own term
these you have absolutely reject and that though in saucy term enough yet in such a manner as make i admire you the more
your pretty chit-chat to mrs
jewkes the last sunday night so innocent and so full of beautiful simplicity half disarm my resolution before i approach your bed and i see you so watchful over your virtue that though i hop to find it otherwise i cannot but confess my passion for you be increase by it

consider the pride of my condition
i cannot endure the thought of marriage even with a person of equal or superior degree to myself and have decline several proposal of that kind how then with the distance between we in the world s judgment can i think of make you my wife yet i must have you i cannot bear the thought of any other man supplant i in your affection and the very apprehension of that have make i hate the name of williams and use he in a manner unworthy of my temper
now pamela judge for i and since i have tell you thus candidly my mind and i see yours be big with some important meaning by your eye your blush and that sweet confusion which i behold struggle in your bosom tell i with like openness and candour what you think i ought to do and what you would have i do
it be impossible for i to express the agitation of my mind on this unexpected declaration so contrary to his former behaviour

charming creature
lovely pamela

a handsome man as you be in your person so happy in the gift of your mind that every body court your company and possess of such a noble and clear estate and very rich in money besides leave you by the good of father and mother with such ancient blood in your vein untainted

so that i beg you will restore she to her parent and give her 100 l
or so to make she happy in some honest fellow of her own degree and that will be do something and will also oblige and pacify your much grieve sister if i have write too sharply consider it be my love to you and the shame you be bring upon yourself and i wish this may have the effect upon you intend by your very love sister this be a sad letter my dear father and mother and one may see how poor people be despise by the proud and the rich
and yet we be all on a foot originally and many of these gentry that brag of their ancient blood would be glad to have it as wholesome and as really untainted as ours surely these proud people never think what a short stage life be and that with all their vanity a time be come when they shall be oblige to submit to be on a level with we and true say the philosopher when he look upon the skull of a king and that of a poor man that he see no difference between they
besides do they not know that the rich of prince and the poor of beggar be to have one great and tremendous judge at the last day who will not distinguish between they accord to their circumstance in life but on the contrary may make their condemnation the great as their neglect opportunity be the great
poor soul
how do i pity their pride o keep i heaven

the breakfasting-time the preparation for dinner and sometimes to entertain your choose friend and the company you shall bring home with you gentleman if not lady and the suppering will fill up a great part of the day in a very necessary manner
