an ample recompense for all her suffering do i think this sweet conversation only
a hundred tender thing he express besides that though they never can escape my memory yet would be too tedious to write down
oh how i bless god and i hope ever shall for all his gracious favour to his unworthy handmaid
what a happy change be this
and who know but my kind my generous master may put it in my power when he shall see i not quite unworthy of it to be a mean without injure he to dispense around i to many person the happy influence of the condition to which i shall be by his kind favour exalt
doubly blest shall i be in particular if i can return the hundredth part of the obligation i owe to such honest good parent to whose pious instruction and example under god i owe all my present happiness and future prospects o the joy that fill my mind on these proud hope
on these delightful prospects it be too mighty for i and i must sit down to ponder all these thing and to admire and bless the goodness of that providence which have through so many intricate maze make i tread the path of innocence and so amply reward i for what it have itself enable i to do
all glory to god alone be ever give for it by your poor enraptured daughter i will now continue my most pleasing relation
as the chariot be return home from this sweet airing he say from all that have pass between we in this pleasing turn my pamela will see and will believe that the trial of her virtue be all over from i but perhaps there will be some few yet to come of her patience and humility
for i have at the earnest importunity of lady darnford and her daughter promise they a sight of my beloved girl and so i intend to have their whole family and lady jones and mrs
peters s family to dine with i once in a few day
and since i believe you would hardly choose at present to grace the table on the occasion till you can do it in your own right i should be glad you would not refuse come down to we if i should desire it for i would preface our nuptial say the dear gentleman
o what a sweet word be that with their good opinion of your merit and to see you and your sweet manner will be enough for that purpose and so by degree prepare my neighbour for what be to follow and they already have your character from i and be disposed to admire you
sir say i after all that have pass i should be unworthy if i could not say that i can have no will but your and however awkwardly i shall behave in such company weigh down with a sense of your obligation on one side and my own unworthiness with their observation on the other i will not scruple to obey you
i be oblige to you pamela say he and pray be only dress as you be for since they know your condition and i have tell they the story of your present dress and how you come by it one of the young lady beg it as a favour that they may see you just as you be and i be the rather pleased it should be so because they will perceive you owe nothing to dress but make a much well figure with your own native stock of loveliness than the great lady array in the most splendid attire and adorn with the most glittering jewel
o sir say i your goodness behold your poor servant in a light greatly beyond her merit
but it must not be expect that other lady especially will look upon i with your favourable eye but nevertheless i should be well pleased to wear always this humble garb till you for your own sake shall order it otherwise for oh sir say i i hope it will be always my pride to glory most in your goodness
and it will be a pleasure to i to shew every one that with respect to my happiness in this life i be entirely the work of your bounty and to let the world see from what a lowly original you have raise i to honours that the great lady would rejoice in
admirable pamela
say he excellent girl surely thy sentiment be superior to those of all thy sex i might have address a hundred fine lady but never surely could have have reason to admire one as i do you
as my dear father and mother i repeat these generous saying only because they be the effect of my master s goodness be far from presume to think i deserve one of they so i hope you will not attribute it to my vanity for i do assure you i think i ought rather to be more humble as i be more oblige for it must be always a sign of a poor condition to receive obligation one cannot repay as it be of a rich mind when it can confer they without expect or need a return
it be on one side the state of the human creature compare on the other to the creator and so with due deference may his beneficence be say to be godlike and that be the high that can be say
the chariot bring we home at near the hour of two and bless be god my master be pure well and cheerful and that make i hope he do not repent he of his late generous treatment of i
he hand i out of the chariot and to the parlour with the same goodness that he shew when he put i into it before several of the servant
mrs
jewkes come to inquire how he do
quite well mrs
jewkes say he quite well i thank god and this good girl for it i be glad of it say she but i hope you be not the bad for my care and my doctoring of you no but the well mrs
jewkes say he you have much oblige i by both
then he say mrs
jewkes you and i have use this good girl very hardly i be afraid sir say she i should be the subject of her complaints i assure you say he she have not open her lip about you
we have have a quite different subject to talk of and i hope she will forgive we both you especially she must because you have do nothing but by my order
but i only mean that the necessary consequence of those order have be very grievous to my pamela and now come our part to make her amend if we can
sir say she i always say to madam as she call i that you be very good and very forgiving
no say he i have be stark naught and it be she i hope will be very forgiving
but all this preamble be to tell you mrs
jewkes that now i desire youwill study to oblige she as much as to obey i you be force to disoblige she before
and youwill remember that in every thing she be to be her own mistress
yes say she and mine too i suppose sir
ay say the generous gentleman i believe it will be so in a little time then say she i know how it will go with i
and so put she handkerchief to her eyes pamela say my master comfort poor mrs
jewkes
this be very generous already to seem to put she in my power and i take she by the hand and say i shall never take upon i mrs
jewkes to make a bad use of any opportunity that may be put into my hand by my generous master nor shall i ever wish to do you any disservice if i might for i shall consider that what you have do be in obedience to a will which it will become i also to submit to and so if the effect of our obedience may be different yet as they proceed from one cause that must be always reverence by i
see there mrs
jewkes say my master we be both in generous hand and indeed if pamela do not pardon you i should think she but half forgave i because you act by my instructions well say she god bless you both together since it must be so and i will double my diligence to oblige my lady as i find she will soon be
o my dear father and mother
now pray for i on another score for fear i should grow too proud and be giddy and foolish with all these promising thing so soothing to the vanity of my year and sex
but even to this hour can i pray that god would remove from i all these delightful prospect if they be likely so to corrupt my mind as to make i proud and vain and not acknowledge with thankful humility the bless providence which have so visibly conduct i through the dangerous path i have trod to this happy moment
my master be pleased to say that he think i might as well dine with he since he be alone but i beg he would excuse i for fear as i say such excess of goodness and condescension all at once should turn my head and that he would by slow degree bring on my happiness lest i should not know how to bear it
person that doubt themselves say he seldom do amiss and if there be any fear of what you say you could not have it in your thought for none but the presumptuous the conceited and the thoughtless err capitally
but nevertheless say he i have such an opinion of your prudence that i shall generally think what you do right because it be you that do it
sir say i your kind expression shall not be throw away upon i if i can help it for they will task i with the care of endeavour to deserve your good opinion and your approbation as the good rule of my conduct
be then about to go up stair permit i sir say i look about i with some confusion to see that nobody be there thus on my knee to thank you as i often want to do in the chariot for all your goodness to i which shall never i hope be cast away upon i
and so i have the boldness to kiss his hand
i wonder since how i come to be so forward
but what could i do my poor grateful heart be like a too full river which overflow its bank and it carry away my fear and my shamefacedness as that do all before it on the surface of its water
he clasp i in his arm with transport and condescendingly kneel by i and kiss i say o my dear oblige good girl on my knee as you on your i vow to you everlaste truth and fidelity
and may god but bless we both with half the pleasure that seem to be before we and we shall have no reason to envy the felicity of the great princes o sir say i how shall i support so much goodness
i be poor indeed in every thing compare to you
and how far very far do you in every generous way leave i behind you
he raise i and as i bent towards the door lead i to the stair foot and salute i there again leave i to go up to my closet where i throw myself on my knee in rapture of joy and bless that gracious god who have thus change my distress to happiness and so abundantly reward i for all the suffering i have pass through and oh how light how very light do all those suffering now appear which then my repine mind make so grievous to me hence in every state of life and in all the change and chance of it for the future will i trust in providence who know what be good for we and frequently turn the very evil we most dread to be the cause of our happiness and of our deliverance from greater my experience young as i be as to this great point of reliance on god be strong though my judgment in general may be weak and uninformed but youwill excuse these reflection because they be your beloved daughter s and so far as they be not amiss derive themselves from the benefit of yours and my late good lady s example and instruction
i have write a vast deal in a little time and shall only say to conclude this delightful wednesday that in the afternoon my good master be so well that he ride out on horseback and come home about nine at night and then step up to i and see i with pen and ink before i in my closet say i come only to tell you i be very well my pamela and since i have a letter or two to write i will leave you to proceed in your as i suppose that be your employment for i have put by my paper at his come up and so he salute i bid i good night and go down and i finish up to this place before i go to bed
mrs
jewkes tell i if it be more agreeable to i she would he in another room but i say no thank you mrs
jewkes pray let i have your company
and she make i a fine courtesy and thank me how time be alter
thursday
this morning my master come up to i and talk with i on various subject for a good while together in the most kind manner
among other thing he ask i if i choose to order any new clothe against my marriage
o how my heart flutter when he mention this subject so freely i say i leave every thing to his good pleasure only repeat my request for the reason aforegiven that i might not be too fine
he say i think my dear it shall be very private i hope you be not afraid of a sham-marriage and pray get the service by heart that you may see nothing be omit
i glow between shame and delight
o how i feel my cheek burn
i say i fear nothing i apprehend nothing but my own unworthiness
say he i think it shall be do within these fourteen day from this day at this house
o how i tremble
but not with grief you may believe--what say my girl
have you to object against any day of the next fourteen because my affair require i to go to my other house and i think not to stir from this till i be happy with you
i have no will but your say i all glow like the fire as i could feel but sir do you say in the house
ay say he for i care not how privately it be do and it must be very public if we go to church
it be a holy rite sir say i and would be well methink in a holy place
i see say he most kindly my lovely maid s confusion and your trembling tenderness shews i ought to oblige you all i may
therefore i will order my own little chapel which have not be use for two generation for any thing but a lumber-room because our family seldom reside here long together to be clear and clean and get ready for the ceremony if you dislike your own chamber or mine
sir say i that will be well than the chamber and i hope it will never be lumber again but keep to the use for which as i presume it have be consecrate
o yes say he it have be consecrate and that several age ago in my great great grandfather s time who build that and the good old house together
but now my good girl if i do not too much add to your sweet confusion shall it be in the first seven day or the second of this fortnight
i look down quite out of countenance
tell i say he
in the second if you please sir say i a you please say he most kindly but i should thank you pamela if you would choose the first
iwould rather sir if you please say i have the second
well say he be it so but donot defer it till the last day of the fourteen
pray sir say i since you embolden i to talk on this important subject may i not send my dear father and mother word of my happiness you may say he but charge they to keep it secret till you or i direct the contrary
and i tell you i would see no more of your paper but i mean i would not without your consent but if you will shew they to i and now i have no other motive for my curiosity but the pleasure i take in read what you write i shall acknowledge it as a favour
if sir say i you will be pleased to let i write over again one sheet i will though i have rely upon your word and not write they for your perusal
what be that
say he though i cannot consent to it beforehand for i more desire to see they because they be your true sentiment at the time and because they be not write for my perusal
sir say i what i be loath you should see be very severe reflection on the letter i receive by the gipsy when i apprehend your design of the sham-marriage though there be other thing i would not have you see but that be the bad
it cannot be bad say he my dear sauce- box than i have see already and i will allow your treat i in ever so black a manner on that occasion because it must have a very black appearance to you well sir say i i think i will obey you before night
but donot alter a word say he
i willnot sir reply i since you order it
while we be talk mrs
jewkes come up and say thomas be return
o say my master let he bring up the paper for he hop and so do i that you have send they by he
but it be a great balk when he come up and say sir mr
andrews do not care to deliver they and would have it that his daughter be force to write that letter to he and indeed sir say he the old gentleman take on sadly and would have it that his daughter be undo or else he say she would not have turn back when on her way as i tell he she do say thomas instead of come to they
i begin to be afraid now that all would be bad for i again
well tom say he donot mince the matter tell i before mrs
andrews what they say
why sir both he and goody andrews after they have confer together upon your letter madam come out weep bitterly that grieve my very heart and they say now all be over with their poor daughter and either she have write that letter by compulsion or have yield to your honour so they say and be or would be ruin
my master seem vex as i fear
and i say pray sir be so good as to excuse the fear of my honest parent
they cannot know your goodness to i
and so say he without answer i they refuse to deliver the paper
yes and please your honour say thomas though i tell they that you madam of your own accord on a letter i have bring you very cheerfully write what i carry but the old gentleman say why wife there be in these paper twenty thing nobody should see but ourselves and especially not the squire
o the poor girl have have so many stratagem to struggle with
and now at last she have meet with one that have be too hard for she
and can it be possible for we to account for she set out to come to we and in such post haste and when she have get above half-way to send we this letter and to go back again of her own accord as you say when we know that all her delight would have be to come to we and to escape from the peril she have be so long contend with
and then and please your honour he say he could not bear this for his daughter be ruin to be sure before now
and so say thomas the good old couple sit themselves down and hand-in-hand lean upon each other s shoulder do nothing but lament i be piteously grieve say he but all i could say could not comfort they nor would they give i the paper though i tell they i should deliver they only to mrs
andrews herself
and so and please your honour i be force to come away without they
my good master see i all bath in tear at this description of your distress and fear for i and he say i would not have you take on so
i be not angry with your father in the main he be a good man and i would have you write out of hand and it shall be send by the post to mr
atkins who live within two mile of your father and iwill enclose it in a cover of mine in which iwill desire mr
atkins the moment it come to his hand to convey it safely to your father or mother and say nothing of their send their paper that it may not make they uneasy for i want not now to see they on any other score than that of mere curiosity and that will do at any time
and so say he salute i before thomas and with his own handkerchief wipe my eye and say to thomas the good old folk be not to be blame in the main
they donot know my honourable intention by their dear daughter who tom will in a little time be your mistress though i shall keep the matter private some day and would not have it speak of by my servant out of my house
thomas say god bless your honour
you know well
and i say o sir you be all goodness how kind be this to forgive the disappointment instead of be angry as i fear you would
thomas then withdraw
and my master say i need not remind you of write out of hand to make the good folk easy and i will leave you to yourself for that purpose only send i down such of your paper as you be willing i should see with which i shall entertain myself for an hour or two
but one thing add he i forget to tell you the neighbouring gentry i mention will be here tomorrow to dine with i and i have order mrs
jewkes to prepare for they
and must i sir say i be shewn to they
o yes say he thatbe the chief reason of their coming
and youwill see nobody equal to yourself donot be concern
i open my paper as soon as my master have leave i and lay out those beginning on the thursday morning he set out for stamford with the morning visit he make i before i be up and the injunction of watchfulness etc
to mrs
jewkes the next day s gipsy affair and my reflection in which i call he truly diabolical and be otherwise very severe on the strong appearance the matter have then against he
his return on saturday with the dread he put i in on the offering to search i for my paper which follow those he have get by mrs
jewkes s mean
my be force to give they up
his carriage to i after he have read they and question to i
his great kindness to i on see the danger i have escape and the trouble i have undergo
and how i unseasonably in the midst of his goodness express my desire of be send to you have the intelligence of a sham-marriage from the gipsy in my thought
how this enrage he and make he turn i that very sunday out of his house and send i on my way to you
the particular of my journey and my grief at part with he and my free acknowledgment to you that i find unknown to myself i have begin to love he and could not help it
his send after i to beg my return but yet generously leave i at my liberty when he might have force i to return whether i be willing or not
my resolution to oblige he and fatigue journey back
my concern for his illness on my return
his kind reception of i and shew i his sister davers s angry letter against his behaviour to i desire he to set i free and threaten to renounce he as a brother if he should degrade himself by marry i
my serious reflection on this letter etc all which i hope with the other you will shortly see and this carry matter down to tuesday night last
all that follow be so kind on his side be our chariot conference as above on wednesday morning and how good he have be ever since that i think i would go no far for i be a little ashamed to be so very open on that tender and most grateful subject though his great goodness to i deserve all the acknowledgment i can possibly make
and when i have look these out i carry they down myself into the parlour to he and say put they into his hand your allowance good sir as heretofore and if i have be too open and free in my reflection or declaration let my fear on one side and my sincerity on the other be my excuse
you be very obliging my good girl say he
you have nothing to apprehend from my thought any more than from my action
so i go up and write the letter to you briefly acquaint you with my present happiness and my master s goodness and express the gratitude of heart which i owe to the kind gentleman in the world and assure you that i should soon have the pleasure of send back to you not only those paper but all that succeed they to this time as i know you delight to amuse yourself in your leisure hour with my scribble and i say carry it down to my master before i seal it will you please sir to take the trouble of read what i write to my dear parent
thank you pamela say he and set i on his knee while he read it and seem much pleased with it and give it i again you be very happy say he my beloved girl in your style and expression and the affectionate thing you say of i be inexpressibly oblige and again with this kiss say he do i confirm for truth all that you have promise for my intention in this letter o what halcyon day be these
god continue them a change would kill i quite
he go out in his chariot in the afternoon and in the evening return and send i word he would be glad of my company for a little walk in the garden and down i go that very moment
he come to meet i
so say he how do my dear girl do now whom do you think i have see since i have be out i donot know sir say i
why say he there be a turning in the road about five mile off that go round a meadow that have a pleasant foot-way by the side of a little brook and a double row of lime on each side where now and then the gentry in the neighbourhood walk and angle and divert themselves iwill shew it you next opportunity and i stept out of my chariot to walk across this meadow and bid robin meet i with it on the further part of it and whom should i spy there walk with a book in his hand reading but your humble servant mr
williams
donot blush pamela say he
as his back be towards i i think i would speak to the man and before he see i i say how do you old acquaintance
for say he you know we be of one college for a twelvemonth i think the man would have jump into the brook he give such a start at hear my voice and see i
poor man
say i
ay say he but not too much of your poor man in that soft accent neither pamela said i i be sorry my voice be so startling to you mr
williams
what be you read
sir say he and stammer with the surprise it be the french telemachus for i be about perfect myself if i can in the french tongue thought i i have rather so than perfect my pamela in it you do well reply i donot you think that yonder cloud may give we a small shower
and it do a little begin to wet he say he believe not much
if say i you be for the village iwill give you a cast for i shall call at sir simon s in my return from the little round i be take
he ask i if it be not too great a favour no say i donot talk of that let we walk to the further opening there and we shall meet my chariot
so pamela continue my master we fall into conversation as we walk
he say he be very sorry he have incur my displeasure and the more as he have be tell by lady jones who have it from sir simon s family that i have a more honourable view than at first be apprehend
i say we fellow of fortune mr
williams take sometimes a little more liberty with the world than we ought to do wanton very probably as you contemplative folk would say in the sunbeam of a dangerous affluence and cannot think of confine ourselves to the common path though the safe and most eligible after all
and you may believe i could not very well like to be supplant in a view that lie next my heart and that by an old acquaintance whose good before this affair i be studious to promote
i would only say sir say he that my first motive be entirely such as become my function and very politely say my master he add and i be very sure that however inexcusable i might seem in the progress of the matter yourself sir would have be sorry to have it say you have cast your thought on a person that nobody could have wish for but yourself
well mr
williams say i i see you be a man of gallantry as well as religion but what i take most amiss be that if you think i do a wrong thing you do not expostulate with i upon it as your function might have allow you to do but immediately determine to counterplot i and attempt to secure to yourself a prize you would have rob i of and that from my own house
but the matter be at an end and i retain not any malice upon it though you do not know but i might at last do honourably by she as i actually intend
i be sorry for myself sir say he that i should so unhappily incur your displeasure but i rejoice for her sake in your honourable intention give i leave only to say that if you make miss andrews your lady she will do credit to your choice with every body that see she or come to know she and for person and mind both you may challenge the county
in this manner say my master do the parson and i confabulate and i set he down at his lodging in the village
but he keep your secret pamela and would not own that you give any encouragement to his address
indeed sir say i he could not say that i do and i hope you believe i
i do i do say he but tis still my opinion that if when i see plot set up against my plot i have not discover the parson as i do the correspondence between you might have go to a length that would have put our present situation out of both our power
sir say i when you consider that my utmost presumption could not make i hope for the honour you now seem to design i that i be so hardly use and have no prospect before i but dishonour you will allow that i should have seem very little in earnest in my profession of honesty if i have not endeavour to get away but yet i resolve not to think of marriage for i never see the man i could love till your goodness embolden i to look up to you
i should my dear pamela say he make a very ill compliment to my vanity if i do not believe you though at the same time justice call upon i to say that it be some thing consider beyond my merit
there be a sweet noble expression for your poor daughter my dear father and mother and from my master too
i be glad to hear this account of the interview between mr
williams and himself but i dare not to say so
i hope in time he will be reinstate in his good grace
he be so good as to tell i he have give order for the chapel to be clear
o how i look forward with inward joy yet with fear and tremble
friday
about twelve o clock come sir simon and his lady and two daughter and lady jones and a sister-in-law of her and mr
peters and his spouse and niece
mrs
jewkes who be more and more obliging be much concerned i be not dress in some of my good clothe and make i many compliment
they all go into the garden for a walk before dinner and i understand be so impatient to see i that my master take they into the large alcove after they have walk two or three turn and stept himself to i
come my pamela say he the lady cannot be satisfy without see you and i desire youwill come
i say i be ashamed but i would obey he
say he the two young lady be dress out in their good attire but they make not such an appearance as my charming girl in this ordinary garb sir say i shallnot i follow you thither
for i cannot bear you should do i so much honour
well say he iwill go before you
and he bid mrs
jewkes bring a bottle of sack and some cake
so he go down to they
this alcove front the long gravel-walk in the garden so that they see i all the way i come for a good way and my master tell i afterwards with pleasure all they say of i
will you forgive the little vain slut your daughter if i tell you all as he be pleased to tell i
he say spy i first look there lady come my pretty rustic they all i see which dash i stand at the window and in the door-way look full at i
my master tell i that lady jones say she be a charming creature i see that at this distance
and sir simon it seem who have be a sad rake in his young day swear he never see so easy an air so fine a shape and so graceful a presence the lady darnford say i be a sweet girl
and mrs
peters say very handsome thing
even the parson say i should be the pride of the county
o dear sir
all this be owe to the light my good master s favour place i in which make i shine out in their eye beyond my desert
he say the young lady blush and envy i
when i come near he see i in a little confusion and be so kind as to meet i give i your hand say he my poor girl you walk too fast for indeed i want to be out of their gazing
i do so with a courtesy and he lead i up the step of the alcove and in a most gentleman-like manner present i to the lady and they all salute i and say they hop to be well acquaint with i and lady darnford be pleased to say i should be the flower of their neighbourhood
sir simon say good neighbour by your leave and salute i add now will i say that i have kiss the lovely maiden in england
but for all this methought i owe he a grudge for a tell-tale though all have turn out so happily
mr
peters very gravely follow his example and say like a bishop god bless you fair excellence
say lady jones pray dear madam sit down by i and they all sit down but i say i would stand if they please
no pamela say my master pray sit down with these good lady my neighbours they will indulge it to you for my sake till they know you well and for your own when they be acquaint with you
sir say i i shall be proud to deserve their indulgence
they all so gazed at i that i could not look up for i think it be one of the distinction of person of condition and well-breed people to put bashful body out of countenance
well sir simon say my master what say you now to my pretty rustic he swear a great oath that he should well know what to say to i if he be as young as himself
lady darnford say you will never leave sir simon
say my master you be a little confused my good girl and out of breath but i have tell all my kind neighbour here a good deal of your story and your excellence
yes say lady darnford my dear neighbour as i will call you we that be here present have all hear of your uncommon story
madam say i you have then hear what must make your kind allowance for i very necessary
no say mrs
peters we have hear what will always make you value as an honour to our sex and as a worthy pattern for all the young lady in the county
you be very good madam say i to make i able to look up and to be thankful for the honour you be pleased to do i
mrs
jewkes come in with the canary bring by nan to the alcove and some cake on a silver salver and i say mrs
jewkes let i be your assistant i will serve the lady with the cake
and so i take the salver and go round to the good company with it end with my master
the lady jones say she never be serve with such a grace and it be give i too much trouble
o madam say i i hope my good master s favour will never make i forget that it be my duty to wait upon his friend
master sweet one
say sir simon i hope you willnot always call mr
b---- by that name for fear it should become a fashion for all our lady to do the like through the county
i sir say i shall have many reason to continue this style which cannot affect your good lady
sir simon say lady jones you be very arch upon we but i see very well that it will be the interest of all the gentleman to bring their lady into an intimacy with one that can give they such a good example
i be sure then madam say i it must be after i have be polished and improve by the honour of such an example as your
they all be very good and affable and the young lady darnford who have wish to see i in this dress say i beg your pardon dear miss as she call i but i have hear how sweetly this garb become you and be tell the history of it and i beg it as a favour that you might oblige we with your appearance in it
i be much oblige to your ladyship say i that your kind prescription be so agreeable to my choice
why say she be it your choice then i be glad of that though i be sure your person must give and not take ornament from any dress
you be very kind madam say i but there will be the less reason to fear i should forget the high obligation i should have to the kind of gentleman when i can delight to shew the humble degree from which his goodness have raise me my dear pamela say my master if you proceed at this rate i must insist upon your first seven day
you know what i mean

he put on a clean shirt and neckcloth which he bring in his pocket at an alehouse there and get shave and so after he have eat some bread and cheese and drink a can of ale he set out for my master s house with a heavy heart dread for i and in much fear of be brow-beat

tuesday
mr

sir say i if you can account to your own mind i shall always be easy in whatever you do
but our great happiness here sir continue i be of very short duration and this life at the long be a poor transitory one and i hope we shall be so happy as to be enable to look forward with comfort to another where our pleasure will be everlaste
you say well pamela and i shall by degree be more habituated to this way of thinking as i more and more converse with you but at present you must not be over serious with i all at once though i charge you never forbear to mingle your sweet divinity in our conversation whenever it can be bring in a propos and with such a cheerfulness of temper as shall not throw a gloomy cloud over our innocent enjoyment
i be abash at this and silent fear i have offend but he say if you attend rightly to what i say i need not tell you again pamela not to be discourage from suggest to i on every proper occasion the pious impulse of your own amiable mind
sir say i you will be always indulgent i make no doubt to my imperfection so long as i mean well
my master make i dine with he and would eat nothing but what i help he to and my heart be every hour more and more enlarge with his goodness and condescension
but still what ail i i wonder
a strange sort of weight hang upon my mind as thursday draw on which make i often sigh involuntarily and damp at time the pleasure of my delightful prospects i hope this be not ominous but only the foolish weakness of an over-thoughtful mind on an occasion the most solemn and important of one s life next to the last scene which shut up all
i could be very serious but i will commit all my way to that bless providence which hitherto have so wonderfully conduct i through real evil to this hopeful situation
i only fear and surely i have great reason that i shall be too unworthy to hold the affection of so dear a gentleman god teach i humility and to know my own demerit
and this will be next to his grace my sure guard in the state of life to which though most unworthy i be go to be exalt
and donot cease your prayer for i my dear parent for perhaps this new condition may be subject to still bad hazard than those i have escape as would be the case be conceitedness vanity and pride to take hold of my frail heart and if i be for my sin to be leave to my own conduct a frail bark in a tempestuous ocean without ballast or other pilot than my own inconsiderate will
but my master say on another occasion that those who doubt most always err least and i hope i shall always doubt my own strength my own worthiness
i will not trouble you with twenty sweet agreeable thing that pass in conversation with my excellent benefactor nor with the civility of m
colbrand mrs
jewkes and all the servant who seem to be highly pleased with i and with my conduct to they and as my master hitherto find no fault that i go too low nor they that i carry it too high i hope i shall continue to have every body s good-will but yet will i not seek to gain any one s by little meanness or debasement
but aim at an uniform and regular conduct willing to conceal involuntary error as i would have my own forgive and not too industrious to discover real one or to hide such if any such should appear as might encourage bad heart or unclean hand in material case where my master should receive damage or where the moral of the transgressor should appear wilfully and habitually corrupt
in short i will endeavour as much as i can that good servant shall find in i a kind encourager indifferent one be make well by inspire they with a laudable emulation and bad one if not too bad in nature and quite irreclaimable reform by kindness expostulation and even proper menace if necessary but most by a good example all this if god please
wednesday
now my dear parent i have but this one day between i and the most solemn rite that can be perform
my heart cannot yet shake off this heavy weight
sure i be ungrateful to the divine goodness and the favour of the good of benefactors yet i hope i be not for at time my mind be all exultation with the prospect of what good to-morrow s happy solemnity may possibly by the leave of my generous master put it in my power to do
o how shall i find word to express as i ought my thankfulness for all the mercy before i
wednesday evening
my dear master be all love and tenderness
he see my weakness and generously pity and comfort i
i beg to be excuse supper but he bring i down himself from my closet and place i by he bid abraham not wait
i could not eat and yet i try for fear he should be angry

oh sir
say i all be owe to the sense i have of my own unworthiness to be sure it cannot be any thing else

all i have not time to repeat some i will
and oh
indulge your foolish daughter who trouble you with her weak nonsense because what she have to say be so affect to she and because if she go to bed instead of scribble she could not sleep
this sweet confusion and thoughtfulness in my beloved pamela say the kind man on the near approach of our happy union when i hope all doubt be clear up and nothing of dishonour be apprehend shew i most abundantly what a wretch i be to attempt such purity with a bad intention--no wonder that one so virtuous should find herself desert of life itself on a violence so dreadful to her honour and seek a refuge in the shadow of death but now my dear pamela that you have see a purity on my side as nearly imitate your own as our sex can shew to your and since i have all the day long suppress even the least intimation of the come day that i might not alarm your tender mind why all this concern why all this affect yet sweet confusion
you have a generous friend my dear girl in i a protector now not a violator of your innocence why then once more i ask this strange perplexity this sweet confusion

revive my heart then say he with a more cheerful aspect and let that over-anxious solicitude which appear in the most charming face in the world be chase from it have you my dear girl any fear that i can dissipate any doubt that i can obviate any hope that i can encourage any request that i can gratify speak my dear pamela and if i have power but speak and to purchase one smile it shall be do
i cannot sir say i have any fear any doubt but that i shall never be able to deserve all your goodness
i have no hope but that my future conduct may be agreeable to you and my determined duty well accept
nor have i any request to make but that you will forgive all my imperfection and among the rest this foolish weakness that make i seem to you after all the generous thing that have pass to want this further condescension and these kind assurance
but indeed sir i be oppress by your bounty my spirit sink under the weight of it and the oppression be still the great as i see not how possibly in my whole future life by all i can do to merit the least of your favour
i know your grateful heart say he but remember my dear what the lawyer tell we that marriage be the high consideration which the law know
and this my sweet bride have make you mine and i your and you have the good claim in the world to share my fortune with i
but set that consideration aside what be the obligation you have to i
your mind be pure as that of an angel and as much transcend mine
your wit and your judgment to make you no compliment be more than equal to mine you have all the grace that education can give a woman improve by a genius which make those grace natural to you
you have a sweetness of temper and a noble sincerity beyond all comparison and in the beauty of your person you excel all the lady i ever see
where then my dearest be the obligation if not on my side to you but to avoid these comparison let we talk of nothing henceforth but equality although if the rich of your mind and your unblemished virtue be set against my fortune which be but an accidental good as i may call it and all i have to boast of the condescension will be your and i shall not think i can possibly deserve you till after your sweet example my future life shall become nearly as blameless as your
o sir say i what comfort do you give i that instead of my be in danger of be ensnare by the high condition to which your goodness have exalt i you make i hope that i shall be confirm and approve by you and that we may have a prospect of perpetuate each other s happiness till time shall be no more but sir i will not as you once caution i be too serious

he kiss i very tenderly and thank i for this kind assurance as he call it
and so we enter the house together
eight o clock at night
now these sweet assurance my dear father and mother you will say must be very consolatory to i and be voluntary on his side be all that could be wish for on mine and i be resolve if possible to subdue my idle fear and apprehension
ten o clock at night
as we sit at supper he be generously kind to i as well in his action as expression
he take notice in the most delicate manner of my endeavour to conquer my foible and say i see with pleasure my dear girl strive to comport herself in a manner suitable to my wish i see even through the sweet tender struggle of your over-nice modesty how much i owe to your intention of oblige i
as i have once tell you that i be the conquest more of your virtue than your beauty so not one alarming word or look shall my beloved pamela hear or see to give her reason to suspect the truth of what i aver
you may the rather believe i continue he as you may see the pain i have to behold any thing that concern you even though your concern be causeless
and yet i will indulge my dear girl s bashful weakness so far as to own that so pure a mind may suffer from apprehension on so important a change as this and i can therefore be only displeased with such part of your conduct as may make your suffering great than my own when i be resolve through every stage of my future life in all event to study to make they less
after supper of which with all his sweet persuasion i could hardly taste he make i drink two glass of champaign and afterwards a glass of sack which he kindly force upon i by name your health and as the time of retire draw on he take notice but in a very delicate manner how my colour go and come and how foolishly i tremble
nobody surely in such delightful circumstance ever behave so silly and he say my dear girl i fear you have have too much of my company for so many hour together and would well recollect yourself if you retire for half an hour to your closet
i wish for this but durst not say so much lest he should be angry for as the hour grow on i find my apprehension increase and my silly heart be the unquieter every time i could lift up my eye to his dear face so sweetly terrible do he appear to my apprehension
i say you be all goodness dear sir and i boldly kiss his dear hand and press it to my lip with both mine
and salute i very fervently he give i his hand see i hardly able to stand and lead i to my chamber-door and then most generously withdraw
i go to my closet and the first thing i do on my knee again thank god for the blessing of the day and besought his divine goodness to conduct my future life in such a manner as should make i a happy instrument of his glory
after this be now leave to my own recollection i grow a little more assured and lightsome and the pen and paper be before i i amuse myself with write thus far
eleven o clock thursday night

no light frothy jest drop from his lip no alarming raillery no offensive expression nor insult air reproach or wound the ear of your happy thrice happy daughter

williams be here yesterday to ask leave to go to see his new living and to provide for take possession of it and seem so pleased with my master s kindness and fondness for i as well as his generous deportment to himself that he leave we in such a disposition as shew he be quite happy
i be very glad of it for it would rejoice i to be an humble mean of make all mankind so and oh
what return ought i not to make to the divine goodness
and how ought i to strive to diffuse the blessing i experience to all in my knowledge
for else what be it for such a worm as i to be exalt
what be my single happiness if i suffer it niggard-like to extend no far than to myself but then indeed do god almighty s creature act worthy of the blessing they receive when they make or endeavour to make the whole creation so far as be in the circle of their power happy
great and good god
as thou hast enlarge my opportunity enlarge also my will and make i delight in dispense to other a portion of that happiness which i have myself so plentifully receive at the hand of thy gracious providence
then shall i not be useless in my generation then shall i not stand a single mark of thy goodness to a poor worthless creature that in herself be of so small account in the scale of being a mere cipher on the wrong side of a figure but shall be place on the right side and though nothing worth in myself shall give signification by my place and multiply the blessing i owe to thy goodness which have distinguish i by so fair a lot
this as i conceive be the indispensable duty of a high condition and how great must be the condemnation of poor creature at the great day of account when they shall be ask what use they have make of the opportunity put into their hand
and be able only to say we have live but to ourselves we have circumscribe all the power thou hast give we into one narrow selfish compass we have heap up treasure for those who come after we though we know not whether they would not make a still bad use of they than we ourselves do
and how can such poor selfish pleader expect any other sentence than the dreadful depart ye curse
but sure my dear father and mother such person can have no notion of the exalt pleasure that flow from do good be there to be no after-account at all
there be something so satisfactory and pleasing to reflect on the being able to administer comfort and relief to those who stand in need of it as infinitely of itself reward the beneficent mind
and how often have i experience this in my good lady s time though but the second- hand dispenser of her benefit to the poor and sickly when she make i her almoner how have i be affect with the blessing which the miserable have heap upon she for her goodness and upon i for be but the humble conveyer of her bounty to them and how delighted have i be when the moving report i have make of a particular distress have augment my good lady s first intention in relief of it
this i recall with pleasure because it be now by the divine goodness become my part to do those good thing she be willnot to do and oh
let i watch myself that my prosperous state do not make i forget to look up with due thankfulness to the providence which have entrust i with the power that so i may not incur a terrible woe by the abuse or neglect of it
forgive i these reflection my dear parent and let i have your prayer that i may not find my present happiness a snare to i but that i may consider that more and more will be expect from i in proportion to the power give i and that i may not so unworthily act as if i believe i ought to set up my rest in my mean self and think nothing further to be do with the opportunity put into my hand by the divine favour and the good of man
saturday seven o clock in the evening

williams be go as i say to his new living but believe he could for the afternoon and so he promise to give we his company to dinner and to read afternoon service and this make my master invite all the rest as well as he to dinner and not to church and he make they promise to come and tell mr
peters he would send his coach for he and his family
miss darnford tell he pleasantly she would not come unless he would promise to let she be at his wedding by which i find mr
peters have keep the secret as my master desire
he be pleased to give i an airing after dinner in the chariot and renew his kind assurance to i and if possible be kind than ever
this be sweetly comfortable to i because it shews i he do not repent of his condescension to i and it encourage i to look up to he with more satisfaction of mind and less doubtfulness
i beg leave to send a guinea to a poor body in the town that i hear by mrs
jewkes lie very ill and be very destitute
he say send two my dear if you please
say i sir i will never do any thing of this kind without let you know what i do
he most generously answer i shall then perhaps have you do less good than you would otherwise do from a doubt of i though i hope your discretion and my own temper which be not avaricious will make such doubt causeless
now my dear continue he iwill tell you how we will order this point to avoid even the shadow of uneasiness on one side or doubt on the other
as to your father and mother in the first place they shall be quite out of the question for i have already determine in my mind about they and it be thus they shall go down if they and you think well of it to my little kentish estate which i once mention to you in such a manner as make you reject it with a nobleness of mind that give i pain then but pleasure since
there be a pretty little farm and house untenante upon that estate and tolerably well stock and i will far stock it for they for such industrious folk willnot know how to live without some employment and it shall be theirs for both their life without pay any rent and i will allow they 50 l
per annum besides that they may keep up the stock and be kind to any other of their relation without be beholden to you or i for small matter and for great where needful you shall always have it in your power to accommodate they for i shall never question your prudence
and we will so long as god spar our life go down once a year to see they and they shall come up as often as they please it cannot be too often to see we for i mean not this my dear to send they from us before i proceed do my pamela like this
o sir say i the english tongue afford not word or at least i have they not to express sufficiently my gratitude
teach i dear sir continue i and press his dear hand to my lip teach i some other language if there be any that abound with more grateful term that i may not thus be choke with meaning for which i can find no utterance
my charmer
say he your language be all wonderful as your sentiment and you most abound when you seem most to want all that i wish be to find my proposal agreeable to you and if my first be not my second shall be if i can but know what you wish
do i say too much my dear parent when i say he be if possible kinder and kinder o the blessed man
how my heart be overwhelmed with his goodness
well say he my dearest let i desire you to mention this to they to see if they approve it
but if it be your choice and theirs to have they near to you or even under the same roof with you i will freely consent to it
o no sir say i and i fear almost sin in my grateful flight i be sure they would not choose that they could not perhaps serve god so well if they be to live with you for so constantly see the hand that bless they they would it may be as must be my care to avoid be tempt to look no far in their gratitude than to the dear dispenser of such innumerable benefit
excellent creature
say he my beloved want no language nor sentiment neither and her charming thought so sweetly express would grace any language and this be a blessing almost peculiar to my fairest your so kind acceptance my pamela add he repay the benefit with interest and leave i under obligation to your goodness
but now my dearest i will tell you what we will do with regard to point of your own private charity for far be it from i to put under that name the subject we have be mention because that and more than that be duty to person so worthy and so nearly related to my pamela and as such to myself o how the sweet man outdo i in thought word power and every thing
and this say he lie in very small compass for i will allow you two hundred pound a year which longman shall constantly pay you at fifty pound a quarter for your own use and of which i expect no account to commence from the day you enter into my other house i mean say he that the first fifty pound shall then be due because you shall have something to begin with
and add the dear generous man if this be pleasing to you let it since you say you want word be signify by such a sweet kiss as you give i yesterday
i hesitate not a moment to comply with these oblige term and throw my arm about his dear neck though in the chariot and bless his goodness to i
but indeed sir say i i cannot bear this generous treatment
he be pleased to say donot be uneasy my dear about these trifle god have bless i with a very good estate and all of it in a prosperous condition and generally well tenant
i lie up money every year and have besides large sum in government and other security so that you will find what i have hitherto promise be very short of that proportion of my substance which as my dear wife you have a right to
in this sweet manner do we pass our time till evening when the chariot bring we home and then our supper succeed in the same agreeable manner
and thus in a rapturous circle the time move on every hour bring with it something more delightful than the past sure nobody be ever so blest as i
sunday the fourth day of my happiness
not go to chapel this morning the reason of which i tell you i bestow the time from the hour of my beloved s rise to breakfast in prayer and thanksgiving in my closet and now i begin to be quite easy cheerful and free in my spirit and the rather as i find myself encourage by the tranquillity and please vivacity in the temper and behaviour of my beloved who thereby shews he do not repent of his goodness to i
i attend he to breakfast with great pleasure and freedom and he seem quite pleased with i and say now do my dearest begin to look upon i with an air of serenity and satisfaction it shall be always add he my delight to give you occasion for this sweet become aspect of confidence and pleasure in me my heart dear sir say i be quite easy and have lose all its foolish tumult which combat with my gratitude might give an unacceptable appearance to my behaviour but now your goodness sir have enable it to get the well of its uneasy apprehension and my heart be all of one piece and devote to you and grateful tranquillity
and could i be so happy as to see you and my good lady davers reconcile i have nothing in this world to wish for more but the continuance of your favour
he say i wish this reconciliation my dearest as well as you and i do assure you more for your sake than my own and if she would behave tolerably i would make the term easy to she for that reason
he say i will lay down one rule for you my pamela to observe in your dress and i will tell you every thing i like or dislike as it occur to i and i would have you do the same on your part that nothing may be upon either of our mind that may occasion the least reservedness
i have often observe in married folk that in a little while the lady grow careless in her dress which to i look as if she would take no pain to secure the affection she have gain and shews a slight to her husband that she have not to her lover
now you must know this have always give i great offence and i should not forgive it even in my pamela though she would have this excuse for herself that thousand could not make that she look lovely in every thing
so my dear i shall expect of you always to be dress by dinner-time except something extraordinary happen and this whether you be to go abroad or stay at home
for this my love will continue to you that sweet ease in your dress and behaviour which you be so happy a mistress of and whomsoever i bring home with i to my table youwill be in readiness to receive they and will not want to make those foolish apology to unexpected visitor that carry with they a reflection on the conduct of those who make they and besides will convince i that you think yourself oblige to appear as graceful to your husband as you would to person less familiar to your sight
this dear sir say i be a most obliging injunction and i most heartily thank you for it and will always take care to obey it why my dear say he you may well do this than half your sex because they too generally act in such a manner as if they seem to think it the privilege of birth and fortune to turn day into night and night into day and be seldom stir till it be time to sit down to dinner and so all the good old family rule be reverse for they breakfast when they should dine dine when they should sup and sup when they should go to bed and by the help of dear quadrille sometimes go to bed when they should rise in all thing but these my dear continue he i expect you to be a lady
and my good mother be one of this oldfashioned cut and in all other respect as worthy a lady as any in the kingdom
and so you have not be use to the new way and may the easy practise the other
dear sir say i pray give i more of your sweet injunction
why then continue he i shall in the usual course and generally if not hinder by company like to go to bed with my dearest by eleven and if i donot shallnot hinder you
i ordinarily now rise by six in summer
i will allow you to be half an hour after i or so
then youwill have some time you may call your own till you give i your company to breakfast which may be always so as that we may have do at a little after nine
then will you have several hour again at your disposal till two o clock when i shall like to sit down at table
you will then have several useful hour more to employ yourself in as you shall well like and i would generally go to supper by eight and when we be resolve to stick to these oldfashione rule as near as we can we shall have our visitor conform to they too and expect they from we and suit themselves accordingly for i have always observe that it be in every one s power to prescribe rule to himself
it be only stand a few ridiculous jest at first and that too from such generally as be not the most worthy to be mind and after a while they will say it signify nothing to ask he he will have his own way
there be no put he out of his bias
he be a regular piece of clock- work they will joke and all that and why my dear should we not be so
for man be as frail a piece of machinery as any clock-work whatever and by irregularity be as subject to be disorder
then my dear continue the charming man when they see they be receive at my own time with an open countenance and cheerful heart when they see plenty and variety at my board and meet a kind and hearty welcome from we both they will not offer to break in upon my condition nor grudge i my regular hour and as most of these people have nothing to do except to rise in a morning they may as well come to breakfast with we at half an hour after eight in summer as at ten or eleven to dinner at two as at four five or six and to supper at eight as at ten or eleven
and then our servant too will know generally the time of their business and the hour of their leisure or recess and we as well as they shall reap the benefit of this regularity
and who know my dear but we may revive the good oldfashion in our neighbourhood by this means at least it will be do our part towards it and answer the good lesson i learn at school every one mend one
and the bad that will happen will be that when some of my brother rake such as those who break in upon we so unwelcomely last thursday be get out of the way if that can ever be and begin to consider who they shall go to dine with in their ramble they will only say we must not go to he for his dinner-time be over and so theywill reserve i for another time when they happen to suit it well or perhaps they will take a supper and a bed with i instead of it
now my dearest continue the kind man you see here be more of my injunction as you call they and though i will not be so set as to quarrel if they be not always exactly comply with yet as i know you willnot think they unreasonable i shall be glad they may as often as they can and you will give your order accordingly to your mrs
jervis who be a good woman and will take pleasure in obey you
o dearest dear sir say i have you nothing more to honour i with
you oblige and improve i at the same time what a happy lot be mine
why let i see my dearest say he--but i think of no more at present for it would be needless to say how much i value you for your natural sweetness of temper and that open cheerfulness of countenance which adorn you when nothing have give my fair apprehension for her virtue a sweetness and a cheerfulness that prepossess in your favour at first sight the mind of every one that behold you i need not i hope say that i would have you diligently preserve this sweet appearance let no thwarting accident no cross fortune for we must not expect to be exempt from such happy as we now be in each other deprive this sweet face of this its principal grace and when any thing unpleasing happen in a quarter of an hour at farth begin to mistrust yourself and apply to your glass and if you see a gloom arising or arisen banish it instantly smooth your dear countenance resume your former composure and then my dearest whose heart must always be see in her face and cannot be a hypocrite will find this a mean to smooth her passion also and if the occasion be too strong for so sudden a conquest she will know how to do it more effectually by repair to her closet and beg that gracious assistance which have never yet fail she and so shall i my dear who as you once but too justly observe have be too much indulge by my good mother have an example from you as well as a pleasure in you which will never be pal
one thing continue he i have frequently observe at the house of many a gentleman that when we have unexpectedly visit or break in upon the family order lay down by the lady and especially if any of we have lain under the suspicion of have occasionally seduce our married companion into bad hour or give indifferent example the poor gentleman have be oddly affect at our coming though the good breeding of the lady have make she just keep up appearance
he have look so conscious have be so afraid as it be to disoblige have make so many excuse for some of we before we have be accuse as have always shew i how unwelcome we have be and how much he be oblige to compound with his lady for a tolerable reception of we and perhaps she too in proportion to the honest man s concern to court her smile have be more reserved stiff and formal and have behave with an indifference and slight that have often make i wish myself out of her house for too plainly have i see that it be not his
this my dear you will judge by my description have afford i subject for animadversion upon the married life for a man may not though in the main he be willing to flatter himself that he be master of his house and will assert his prerogative upon great occasion when it be strongly invade be always willing to contend and such woman as those i have describe be always ready to take the field and be bad enemy than the old parthians who annoy most when they seem to retreat and never fail to return to the charge again and carry on the offensive war till they have tired out resistance and make the husband willing like a vanquish enemy to compound for small matter in order to preserve something
at least the poor man do not care to let his friend see his case and so will not provoke a fire to break out that he see and so do his friend too the meek lady have much ado to smother and which very possibly burn with a most comfortable ardour after we be go
you smile my pamela say he at this whimsical picture and i be sure i never shall have reason to include you in these disagreeable outline but yet i will say that i expect from you whoever come to my house that you accustom yourself to one even uniform complaisance that no frown take place on your brow that however ill or well provide we may be for their reception you shew no flutter or discomposure that whoever you may have in your company at the time you signify not by the least reserve look that the stranger be come upon you unseasonably or at a time you wish he have not
but be facetious kind oblige to all and if to one more than another to such as have the least reason to expect it from you or who be most inferior at the table for thus will you my pamela cheer the doubt mind quiet the uneasy heart and diffuse ease pleasure and tranquillity around my board
and be sure my dear continue he let no little accident ruffle your temper
i shall never forget once that i be at lady arthur s and a footman happen to stumble and let fall a fine china dish and break it all to piece it be grievous to see the uneasiness it give the poor lady and she be so sincere in it that she suffer it to spread all over the company and it be a pretty large one too and not a person in it but turn either her consoler or fall into story of the like misfortune and so we all become for the rest of the evening nothing but blunder footman and careless servant or be turn into broken jar plate glass tea-cup and such like brittle substance
and it affect i so much that when i come home i go to bed and dream that robin with the handle of his whip break the fore glass of my chariot and i be so solicitous methought to keep the good lady in countenance for her anger that i break his head in revenge and stab one of my coach-horse
and all the comfort i have when it be do methought be that i have not expose myself before company and there be no sufferer but guilty robin and one innocent coach-horse
i be exceedingly divert with the facetious hint and the pleasant manner in which he give they and i promise to improve by the excellent lesson contain in they
i then go up and dress myself as like a bride as i could in my good clothe and on inquiry hear my dear master be go to walk in the garden i go to find he out
he be read in the little alcove and i say sir be i license to intrude upon you no my dear say he because you cannot intrude
i be so wholly your that wherever i be you have not only a right to join i but you do i a very acceptable favour at the same time
i have sir say i obey your first kind injunction as to dress myself before dinner but may be you be busy sir
he put up the paper he be read and say i can have no business or pleasure of equal value to your company my dear
what be you go to say only sir to know if you have any more kind injunction to give me i could hear you talk a whole day together you be very obliging pamela say he but you be so perfectly what i wish that i might have spar those i give you but i be willing you should have a taste of my freedom with you to put you upon the like with i for i be confident there can be no friendship last without freedom and without communicate to one another even the little caprice if my pamela can have any such which may occasion uneasiness to either
now my dear say he be so kind as to find some fault with i and tell i what you would wish i to do to appear more agreeable to you
o sir say i and i could have kiss he but for shame to be sure i shall grow a sad fond hussy i have not one single thing to wish for no not one he salute i very kindly and say he should be sorry if i have and forbore to speak it
do you think my dear sir say i that your pamela have no conscience
do you think that because you so kindly oblige she and delight in oblige she that she must rack her invention for trial of your goodness and know not when shebe happy o my dearest sir add i less than one half of the favour you have so generously confer upon i would have exceed my utmost wish
my dear angel say he and kiss i again i shall be troublesome to you with my kiss if you continue thus sweetly oblige in your action and expression
o sir say i i have be think as i be dress myself what excellent lesson you teach i
when you command i at your table to cheer the doubt mind and comfort the uneasy heart and to behave most kindly to those who have least reason to expect it and be most inferior how sweetly in every instance that could possibly occur have you do this yourself by your poor unworthy pamela till you have diffuse in your own dear word ease pleasure and tranquillity around my glad heart
then again sir when you bid i not be disturb by little accident or by stranger come in upon i unexpectedly how noble an instance do you give i of this when on our happy wedding-day the coming of sir charles hargrave and the other two gentleman for which you be quite unprovided and which hinder our happiness of dining together on that choose day do not so disturb you but that you entertain the gentleman pleasantly and part with they civilly and kindly
what charming instance be these i have be recollect with pleasure of your pursue the doctrine you deliver
my dear say he these observation be very kind in you and much to my advantage but if i do not always for i fear these be too much accident so well pursue the doctrine i lie down my pamela must not expect that my imperfection will be a plea for her nonobservance of my lesson as you call they for i doubt i shall never be half so perfect as you and so i cannot permit you to recede in your goodness though i may find myself unable to advance as i ought in my duty
i hope sir say i by god s grace i never shall
i believe it say he but i only mention this know my own defect lest my future lesson should not be so well warrant by my practice as in the instance you have kindly recollect
he be pleased to take notice of my dress and span my waist with his hand say what a sweet shape be here
it would make one regret to lose it and yet my beloved pamela i shall think nothing but that loss wanting to complete my happiness i put my bold hand before his mouth and say hush hush
o fie sir the free thing you have ever yet say since i have be yours he kiss my hand and say such an innocent wish my dearest may be permit i because it be the end of the institution but say would such a case be unwelcome to my pamela i will say sir say i and hide my blushing face on his bosom that your wish in every thing shall be mine but pray sir say no more
he kindly salute i and thank i and change the subject i be not too free i hope
thus we talk till we hear the coach and then he say stay here in the garden my dear and iwill bring the company to you
and when he be go i pass by the back-door kneel down against it and bless god for not permit my then so much desire escape
i go to the pond and kneel down on the mossy bank and again bless god there for his mercy in my escape from myself my then bad enemy though i think i have none but enemy and no friend near i
and so i ought to do in almost every step of this garden and every room in this house and i be bend my step to the dear little chapel to make my acknowledgment there but i see the company come towards i

dear heart
i wish think i you would spare these compliment for i shall have some joke i doubt pass on i by-and-by that will make i suffer for all these fine thing

well i think a young rake be hardly tolerable but an old rake and an old beau be two very sad things and all this before daughter woman-grown i whisper my dearest a little after and say i fear i shall suffer much from sir simon s rude joke by-and-by when you reveal the matter tis his way my dear say he you must now grow above these things miss nanny darnford say to i with a sort of half grave ironical air well miss andrews if i may judge by your easy deportment now to what it be when i see you last i hope you will let my sister if you willnot i see the happy knot tie

peters to answer for he to the ringer at the town if they should hear of it till our return into this country and that then he would be bountiful to they because he would not publicly declare it till he have first do so in bedfordshire
monday the fifth day
i have have very little of my dear friend s company this day for he only staid breakfast with i and ride out to see a sick gentleman about eighteen mile off who beg by a man and horse on purpose to speak with he believe he should not recover and upon part of whose estate my master have a mortgage
he say my dearest i shall be very uneasy if i be oblige to tarry all night from you but lest you should be alarm if i donot come home by ten donot expect i for poor mr
carlton and i have pretty large concern together and if he should be very ill and would be comfort by my presence as i know he love i and his family will be more in my power if he die than i wish for charity will not let i refuse
it be now ten o clock at night and i fear he will not return
i fear for the sake of his poor sick friend who i doubt be bad
though i know not the gentleman i be sorry for his own sake for his family s sake and for my dear master s sake who by his kind expression i find love he and methink i should be sorry any grief should touch his generous heart though yet there be no living in this world without too many occasion for concern even in the most prosperous state
and it be fit it should be so or else poor wretch as we be
we should look no far but be like sensual traveller on a journey homeward who meet with good entertainment at some inn on the way put up their rest there and never think of pursue their journey to their proper home this i remember be often a reflection of my good lady s to whom i owe it

but poor wretch

by this we may see my dear father and mother of what force example be and what be in the power of the head of family to do and this shews that evil example in superior be doubly pernicious and doubly culpable because such person be bad themselves and not only do no good but much harm to other and the condemnation of such must to be sure be so much the greater and how much the great still must my condemnation be who have have such a religious education under you and be so well nurture by my good lady if i should forget with all these mercy heap upon i what belong to the station i be prefer to o how i long to be do some good

to mrs
andrews my dearest pamela monday night
i hope my not come home this night will not frighten you
you may believe i cannot help it
my poor friend be so very ill that i doubt he cannot recover
his desire to have i stay with he be so strong that i shall sit up all night with he as it be now near one o clock in the morning for he cannot bear i out of his sight and i have make he and his distressed wife and child so easy in the kind assurance i could give he of my consideration for he and they that i be look upon as the poor disconsolate widow as she i doubt will soon be tell i as their good angel
i could have wish we have not engage to the good neighbourhood at sir simon s for to-morrow night but i be so desirous to set out on wednesday for the other house that as well as in return for the civility of so many good friend who will be there on purpose i would not put it off
what i beg of you therefore my dear be that you would go in the chariot to sir simon s the soon in the day the well because you will be divert with the company who all so much admire you and i hope to join you there by your tea-time in the afternoon which will be well than go home and return with you as it will be six mile difference to i and i know the good company will excuse my dress on the occasion
i count every hour of this little absence for a day for i be with the utmost sincerity my dearest love for ever your etc if you could go to dine with they it will be a freedom that would be very pleasing to they and the more as they donot expect it i begin to have a little concern lest his fatigue should be too great and for the poor sick gentleman and family but tell mrs
jewkes that the least intimation of his choice should be a command to i and so i would go to dinner there and order the chariot to be get ready to carry i when a messenger come up just as i be dress to tell she she must come down immediately

she say she know the world as much at thirty-two as i do at sixteen and bid i remember that
i take the liberty to say but i get a good way off that i scorn her ladyship s word and be as much married as her ladyship

when the royal family of stuart ally itself into the low family of hyde comparatively low i mean do any body scruple to call the lady royal highness and duchess of york
and do any body think she daughter the late queen mary and queen anne less royal for that

and who scruple to call she lady duchess or countess

and be she not effectually degrade
and will any duchess or countess rank with she
now lady davers do you not see a difference between my marry my dear mother s beloved and deserve waiting-maid with a million of excellency about she and such grace of mind and person as would adorn any distinction and your marry a sordid groom whose constant train of education conversation and opportunity could possibly give he no other merit than that which must proceed from the vilest low taste in his sordid dignifier

and do not the difference strike you
for what lady of quality ought to respect another who have make so sordid a choice and set a groom above she
for would not that be to put that groom upon a par with themselves call this palliation or what you will but if you see not the difference you be blind and a very unfit judge for yourself much more unfit to be a censurer of i
iwould have you say she publish your fine reason to the world and they will be sweet encouragement to all the young gentleman who read they to cast themselves away on the servant-wench in their family

people say i mrs
