and even the great though at present angry they be not obey will afterwards have no ill opinion of a person for withstand they in their unlawful command
mrs
jewkes seem a little concerned at this and i say i speak chiefly from my own experience for that i might say as they both know my story that i have not want both for menace and temptation and have i comply with the one or be intimidate by the other i should not have be what i be
ah madam
reply mrs
jewkes i never know any body like you and i think your temper sweeter since the happy day than before and that if possible you take less upon you
why a good reason say i may be assign for that i think myself in danger i look upon every one as my enemy and it be impossible that i should not be fretful uneasy jealous
but when my dear friend have take from i the ground of my uneasiness and make i quite happy i should have be very blamable if i have not shew a satisfied and easy mind and a temper that should engage every one s respect and love at the same time if possible and so much the more as it be but justify in some sort the honour i have receive for the few enemy i make myself the more i engage every one to think that my good benefactor have be less to blame in descend as he have do
this way of talk please they both very much and they make i many compliment upon it and wish i always to be happy as they say i so well deserve
we be thus engage when my master and his sister and her nephew come in and they make i quite alive in the happy humour in which they all return

he tell i that they rally he on the stateliness of his temper and say they see he would make an exceeding good husband where he be but it must be owe to my meekness more than to his complaisance for say miss darnford i could see well enough when your ladyship detain she though he have but hint his desire of find she at our house he be so out of humour at her suppose noncompliance that mine and my sister s pity for she be much more engaged than our envy

you know you do sauce-box

and i own i be displeased to be break in upon after your provocation by either of you and she must learn that lesson never to come near i when i be in those humour which shall be as little as possible for after a while if let alone i always come to myself and be sorry for the violence of a temper so like my dear sister s here and for this reason think it be no matter how few witness i have of its intemperance while it last especially since every witness whether they merit it or not as you see in my pamela s case must be a sufferer by it if unsent for they come in my way
he repeat the same lesson to i again and enforce it and own that he be angry with i in earnest just then though more with himself afterwards for be so but when pamela say he you want to transfer all my displeasure upon yourself it be so much brave i with your merit as if i must soon end my anger if place there or it be make it so light to you that i be truly displeased for continue he i cannot bear that you should wish on any occasion whatever to have i angry with you or not to value my displeasure as the heavy misfortune that could befall you
but sir say i you know that what i do be to try to reconcile my lady and as she herself observe it be pay she a high regard

sir say i i hope i shall always comport myself so as not wilfully to disoblige you for the future and the rather do i hope this as i be sure i shall want only to know your pleasure to obey it
but this instance shews i that i may much offend without design it in the least
now pamela reply he donot be too serious i hope i shallnot be a very tyrannical husband to you yet do i not pretend to be perfect or to be always govern by reason in my first transport and i expect from your affection that you will bear with i when you find i wrong
i have no ungrateful spirit and can when cool enter as impartially into myself as most man and then i be always kind and acknowledge in proportion as i have be out of the way
but to convince you my dear continue he of your fault i mean with regard to the impetuosity of my temper for there be no fault in your intention that i acknowledge iwill observe only that you meet when you come to i while i be so out of humour a reception you do not expect and a harsh word or two that you do not deserve
now have you not break in upon i while my anger last but staid till i have come to you or send to desire your company youwould have see none of this but that affectionate behaviour which i doubt not youwill always merit and i shall always take pleasure in express and in this temper shall you always find a proper influence over i but you must not suppose whenever i be out of humour that in oppose yourself to my passion you oppose a proper butt to it but when you be so good like the slender reed to bend to the hurricane rather than like the sturdy oak to resist it you will always stand firm in my kind opinion while a contrary conduct would uproot you with all your excellency from my soul
sir say i i will endeavour to conform myself in all thing to your will
i make no doubt but you will and iwill endeavour to make my will as conformable to reason as i can
and let i tell you that this belief of you be one of the inducement i have have to marry at all for nobody be more averse to this state than myself and now we be upon this subject iwill tell you why i be so averse
we people of fortune or such as be bear to large expectation of both sex be generally educate wrong
you have occasionally touch upon this pamela several time in your journal so justly that i need say the less to you
we be usually so headstrong so violent in our will that we very little bear control
humour by our nurse through the fault of our parent we practise first upon they and shew the gratitude of our disposition in an insolence that ought rather to be check and restrain than encourage
next we be to be indulge in every thing at school and our master and mistress be reward with further grateful instance of our boisterous behaviour
but in our wise parent eye all look well all be forgive and excuse and for no other reason but because we be theirs
our next progression be we exercise our spirit when bring home to the torment and regret of our parent themselves and torture their heart by our undutiful and perverse behaviour to they which however ungrateful in we be but the natural consequence of their culpable indulgence to we from infancy upwards
and then next after we have perhaps half break their heart a wife be look out for convenience or birth or fortune be the first motif affection the last if it be at all consult and two people thus educate thus train up in a course of unnatural ingratitude and who have be headstrong torment to every one who have have a share in their education as well as to those to whom they owe their being be bring together and what can be expect but that they should pursue and carry on the same comfortable conduct in matrimony and join most heartily to plague one another
and in some measure indeed this be right because hereby they revenge the cause of all those who have be aggrieve and insult by they upon one another
the gentleman have never be control the lady have never be contradict
he cannot bear it from one whose new relation he think should oblige she to shew a quite contrary conduct
she think it very barbarous now for the first time to be oppose in her will and that by a man from whom she expect nothing but tenderness
so great be the difference between what they both expect from one another and what they both find in each other that no wonder misunderstanding happen that these ripen to quarrel that act of unkindness pass which even have the first motive to their union be affection as usually it be not would have efface all manner of tender impression on both side
appeal to parent or guardian often ensue
if by mediation of friend a reconciliation take place it hardly ever hold for why
the fault be in the mind of both and neither of they will think so so that the wound not permit to be probe be but skin over and rankle still at the bottom and at last break out with more pain and anguish than before
separate bed be often the consequence perhaps elopement if not an unconquerable indifference possibly aversion
and whenever for appearance-sake they be oblige to be together every one see that the yawn husband and the vapourish wife be truly insupportable to one another but separate have free spirit and can be tolerable company
now my dear i would have you think and i hope you will have no other reason that have i marry the first lady in the land i would not have treat she well than i will my pamela
for my wife be my wife and i be the long in resolve on the state because i know its requisite and doubt my conduct in it
i believe i be more nice than many gentleman but it be because i have be a close observer of the behaviour of wed folk and hardly ever have see it to be such as i could like in my own case
i shall possibly give you instance of a more particular nature of this as we be long and perhaps i might say well acquaint
have i marry with the view of many gentleman and with such as my good sister supply the place of my father and mother would have recommend i have wed a fine lady bring up pretty much in my own manner and use to have she will in every thing
some gentleman can come into a compromise and after a few struggle sit down tolerably content
but have i marry a princess i could not have do so
i must have love she exceedingly well before i have consent to knit the knot with she and prefer she to all her sex for without this pamela indifference if not disgust will arise in every wed life that could not have make i happy at home and there be few instance i believe of man s love well after matrimony than of woman s the reason of which tis not my present purpose to account for
then i must have be morally sure that she prefer i to all man and to convince i of this she must have lessen not aggravate my failing she must have bear with my imperfection she must have watch and study my temper and if ever she have any point to carry any desire of overcome it must have be by sweetness and complaisance and yet not such a slavish one as should make she condescension seem to be rather the effect of her insensibility than judgment or affection
she should not have give cause for any part of my conduct to she to wear the least aspect of compulsion or force
the word command on my side or obedience on her i would have blot from my vocabulary
for this reason i should have think it my duty to have desire nothing of she that be not significant reasonable or just and that then she should on her have shew no reluctance uneasiness or doubt to oblige i even at half a word
i would not have excuse she to let i twice enjoin the same thing while i take so much care to make her compliance with i reasonable and such as should not destroy her own free agency in point that ought to be allow she and if i be not always right that yet she would bear with i if she see i set upon it and expostulate with i on the right side of compliance for that would shew i suppose small point in dispute from which the great quarrel among friend generally arise that she differ from i not for contradiction-sake but desire to convince i for my own and that i should another time take well resolution
this would be so oblige a conduct that i should in justice have double my esteem for one who to humour i could give up her own judgment and i should see she could have no other view in her expostulation after her compliance have pass than to rectify my motion for the future and it would have be impossible then but i must have pay the great deference to her opinion and advice in more momentous matter
in all company she must have shewn that she have whether i deserve it altogether or not a high regard and opinion of i and this the rather as such a conduct in she would be a reputation and security to herself for if we rake attempt a married lady our first encouragement exclusive of our own vanity arise from the indifferent opinion slight or contempt she express of her husband
i should expect therefore that she should draw a kind veil over my fault that such as she could not hide she would extenuate that she would place my well action in an advantageous light and shew that i have her good opinion at least whatever libertie the world take with my character
she must have value my friend for my sake be cheerful and easy whomsoever i have bring home with i and whatever fault she have observe in i have never blame i before company at least with such an air of superiority as should have shew she have a well opinion of her own judgment than of mine
now my pamela this be but a faint sketch of the conduct i must have expect from my wife let her quality have be what it would or have live with she on bad term
judge then if to i a lady of the modish taste could have be tolerable
the perverseness and contradiction i have too often see in some of my visit even among people of sense as well as condition have prejudice i to the married state and as i know i could not bear it surely i be in the right to decline it and you see my dear that i have not go among this class of people for a wife nor know i indeed where in any class i could have seek one or have one suitable to my mind if not you for here be my misfortune i could not have be content to have be but moderately happy in a wife
judge you from all this if i could very well bear that you should think yourself so well secured of my affection that you could take the fault of other upon yourself and by a suppose supererogatory merit think your interposition sufficient to atone for the fault of other
yet be i not perfect myself no i be greatly imperfect

they once live creditably and bring up a great family of which i be the young but have misfortune through their do beyond their power for two unhappy brother who be both dead and whose debt they stand bind for and so become reduced and by harsh creditor where most of the debt be not of their own contracting turn out of all and have without success try to set up a little country-school for my father understand a little of account and write a pretty good hand force to take to hard labour but honest all the time content never repine and love to one another and in the midst of their poverty and disappointment above all temptation and all their fear be that i should be wicked and yield to temptation for the sake of worldly rich and to god s grace and their good lesson and those i imbibe from my dear good lady your ladyship s mother it be that i owe the preservation of my innocence and the happy station i be exalt to
she be pleased to kiss i again and say there be such a noble simplicity in thy story such an honest artlessness in thy mind and such a sweet humility in thy deportment notwithstanding thy present station that i believe i shall be force to love thee whether i will or not and the sight of your paper i dare say will crown the work will disarm my pride banish my resentment on lady betty s account and justify my brother s conduct and at the same time redound to your own everlasting honour as well as to the credit of our sex and so i make no doubt but my brother will let i see they

he be pleased afterwards to lead i up stair and give i possession of my lady s dressing-room and cabinet and her fine repeat-watch and equipage and in short of a complete set of diamond that be his good mother s as also of the two pair of diamond ear-ring the two diamond ring and diamond necklace he mention in his naughty article which her ladyship have intend for present to miss tomlins a rich heiress that be propose for his wife when he be just come from his travel but which go off after all be agree upon on both the friend side because he approve not her conversation and she have as he tell his mother too masculine an air and he never could be bring to see she but once though the lady like he very well
he present i also with her ladyship s book picture linen lace etc
that be in her apartment and bid i call those apartment mine
o give i my good god
humility and gratitude
sunday night
this day as matter could not be ready for our appearance at a well place we staid at home and my dear master employ himself a good deal in his library and i have be take up pretty much i hope as i ought to be in thankfulness prayer and meditation in my newly-present closet and i hope god will be pleased to give a blessing to i for i have the pleasure to think i be not puff up with this great alteration and yet be i not want to look upon all these favour and blessing in the light wherein i ought to receive they both at the hand of heaven and my dear benefactor
we din together with great pleasure and i have in every word and action all the instance of kindness and affection that the most indulge heart could wish
he say he would return to his closet again and at five o clock would come and take a walk with i in the garden and so retire as soon as he have din and i go up to mine
about six he be pleased to come up to i and say now my dear i will attend you for a little walk in the garden and i give he my hand with great pleasure
this garden be much well cultivate than the lincolnshire one but that be large and have nobler walk in it and yet there be a pretty canal in this and a fountain and cascade
we have a deal of sweet conversation as we walk and after we have take a turn round i bent towards the little garden and when i come near the summer-house take the opportunity to slip from he and just whipt up the step of this once frightful place and kneel down and say i bless thee o god
for my escape and for thy mercy
o let i always possess a grateful humble heart
and i whipt down again and join he and he hardly miss i
several of the neighbouring gentry send their compliment to he on his return but not a word about his marriage particularly mr
arthur mr
towers mr
brooks and mr
martin of the grove
monday
i have a good deal of employment in choose pattern for my new clothe
he think nothing too good but i think every thing i see be and he be so kind to pick out six of the rich for i to choose three suit out of say we would furnish ourselves with more in town when we go thither
one be white flower with silver most richly and he be pleased to say that as i be a bride i should make my appearance in that the follow sunday
and so we shall have in two or three day from several place nothing but mantua-maker and tailor at work
bless i
what a chargeable and what a worthless hussy i be to the dear gentleman but his fortune and station require a great deal of it and his value for i will not let he do less than if he have marry a fortune equal to his own and then as he say it would be a reflection upon he if he did and so i doubt it will be as it be for either way the world will have something to say
he make i also choose some very fine lace and linen and have send a message on purpose with his order to hasten all down what can be do in town as the millinery matter etc
to be complete there and send by particular messenger as do
all to be here and finish by saturday afternoon without fail
i send away john this morning with some more of my paper to you and with the few he will give you separate
my desire be that you will send i all the paper you have do with that i may keep my word with lady davers to beg the continuance of your prayer and blessing to hope you will give i your answer about my dear benefactor s proposal of the kentish farm to beg you to buy two suit of clothe each of the fine cloth for you my dear father and of a creditable silk for my dear mother and good linen and every thing answerable and that you will as my good friend bid i say let we see you here as soon as possible and he will have his chariot come for you when you tell john the day
oh
how i long to see you both my dear good parent and to share with you my felicity
you will have ibe sure the goodness to go to all your creditor which be chiefly those of my poor unhappy brother and get an account of all you be bind for and every one shall be pay to the utmost farthing and interest besides though some of they have be very cruel and unrelenting but they be entitle to their own and shall be thankfully pay
now i think of it john shall take my paper down to this place that you may have something to amuse you of your dear child s instead of those you part with and i will continue write till i be settle and you be determine and then i shall apply myself to the duty of the family in order to become as useful to my dear benefactor as my small ability will let i
if you think a couple of guineas will be of use to mrs
mumford who i doubt have not much aforehand pray give they to she from i and i will return they to you as for a pair of glove on my nuptial and look through your poor acquaintance and neighbour and let i have a list of such honest industrious poor as may be true object of charity and have no other assistance particularly such as be blind lame or sickly with their several case and also such poor family and housekeeper as be reduce by misfortune as ours be and where a great number of child may keep they from rise to a state of tolerable comfort and i will choose as well as i can for i long to be make a beginning with the kind quarterly benevolence my dear good benefactor have bestow upon i for such good purpose
i be resolve to keep account of all these matter and mr
longman have already furnish i with a vellum book of white paper some side of which i hope soon to fill with the name of proper object and though my dear master have give i all this without account yet shall he see but nobody else how i lie it out from quarter to quarter and i will if any be leave carry it on like an accomptant to the next quarter and strike a balance four time a year and a general balance at every year s end and i have write in it humble return for divine mercy and lock it up safe in my newly-present cabinet
i intend to let lady davers see no farther of my paper than to her own angry letter to her brother for i would not have she see my reflection upon it and shewill know down to that place all thatbe necessary for her curiosity as to my suffering and the stratagem use against i and the honest part i have be enable to act and i hope when she have read they all she will be quite reconcile for she will see it be all god almighty s doing and that a gentleman of his part and knowledge be not to be draw in by such a poor young body as i
i will detain john no long
he will tell you to read this last part first and while he stay
and so with my humble duty to you both and my dear mr
b---- s kind remembrance i rest your ever-dutiful and gratefully happy daughter

honoured father and mother

thursday
we set out at about half an hour after six accordingly and drive pretty smartly get at this truly neat house at half an hour after eight and i be much pleased with the neatness of the good woman and her daughter and maid and he be so good as to say he would now and then take a turn with i to the same place and on the same occasion as i seem to like it for that it would be a pretty exercise and procure we appetite to our breakfast as well as our return would to our dinner

the governess of it keep a chaise and pair which be to be make a double chaise at pleasure and in summer time when the miss perform their task to satisfaction she favour they with an airing to this place three or four at a time and after they have breakfast they be carry back
and this serve both for a reward and for exercise and the miss who have this favour be not a little proud of it and it bring they forward in their respective task
a very good method sir say i
and just as we be talk the chaise come in with four miss all pretty much of a size and a maid-servant to attend they
they be shew another little neat apartment that go through our and make their honour very prettily as they pass by we

that after this design to break off all correspondence with the whole family and miss too she find mean to engage he to give she a meeting at woodstock in order to clear herself that poor lady
she be there oblige naughty creature as he be

and this my dear say he be the story in brief
and i do assure you pamela add he i be far from make a boast of or take a pride in this affair but since it have happen i cannot say but i wish the poor child to live and be happy and i must endeavour to make she so

and so you see pamela that in the whole story on both side the truth be as much preserve as possible
poor lady
say i how her story move i

indeed indeed sir say i i bleed for what her distress must be in this case i be grieve for her poor mind s remorse through her childbed terror which could have so great and so worthy an effect upon she afterwards and i honour her resolution and would rank such a return dear lady in the class of those who be most virtuous and doubt not god almighty s mercy to she and that her present happiness be the result of his gracious providence bless her penitence and reformation but sir say i do you not once see the poor lady after her lie-in

one would have think sir it be enough to reclaim you for ever
all your naughty purpose i make no doubt be quite change
why my dear say he i be much move you may be sure when i come to reflect but at first i be so assured of be a successful tempter and spoil her voyage that i be vex and much out of humour but when i come to reflect as i say i be quite overcome with this instance of her prudence her penitence and her resolution and more admire she than i ever have do
yet i could not bear she should so escape i neither so much overcome i as it be in an heroical bravery and i hasten away and get a bill of credit of lord davers upon his banker in london for five hundred pound and set out for that place have call at oxford and get what light i could as to where i might hear of she there
when i arrive in town which be not till monday morning i go to a place call crosby-square where the friend of the two lady live
she have set out in the fly-coach on tuesday get to the two lady that very night and on saturday have set out with they for gravesend much about the time i be expect she at woodstock
you may suppose that i be much affected my dear with this
however i get my bill of credit convert into money and i set out with my servant on monday afternoon and reach gravesend that night and there i understand that she and the two lady have go on board from the very inn i put up at in the morning and the ship wait only for the wind which then be turn about in its favour
i get a boat directly and go on board the ship and ask for mrs
godfrey
but judge you my dear pamela her surprise and confusion when she see i
she have like to have faint away
i offer any money to put off the sailing till next day but it would not be comply with and fain would i have get she on shore and promise to attend she if she would go over land to any part of england the ship would touch at
but she be immovable
every one conclude i her humble servant and be touch at the moving interview the young lady and their female attendant especially
with great difficulty upon my solemn assurance of honour she trust herself with i in one of the cabin and there i try what i could to prevail upon she to quit her purpose but all in vain she say i have make she quite unhappy by this interview
she have difficulty enough upon her mind before but now i have embitter all her voyage and give she the deep distress
i could prevail upon she but for one favour and that with the great reluctance which be to accept of the five hundred pound as a present from i and she promise at my earnest desire to draw upon i for a great sum as a person that have her effect in my hand when she arrive if she should find it convenient for she
in short this be all the favour i could procure for she would not promise so much as to correspond with i and be determine on go and i believe if i would have marry she which yet i have not in my head she would not have deviate from her purpose
but how sir say i do you part
i would have sail with she answer he and be land at the first port in england or ireland i care not which they should put in at but she be too full of apprehension to admit it and the rough fellow of a master captain they call he but in my mind i could have throw he overboard would not stay a moment the wind and tide be quite fair and be very urgent with i to go a-shore or to go the voyage and be impetuous in my temper spoil you know my dear by my mother and not use to control i think it very strange that wind or tide or any thing else should be prefer to i and my money but so it be i be force to go and so take leave of the lady and the other passenger wish they a good voyage give five guineas among the ship s crew to be good to the lady and take such a leave as you may well imagine than i express
she recommend once more to i the dear guest as she call she the lady be present and thank i for all these instance of my regard which she say would leave a strong impression on her mind and at part she throw her arm about my neck and we take such a leave as affect every one present man as well as lady
so with a truly heavy heart i go down the ship s side to my boat and stand up in it look at she as long as i could see she and she at i with her handkerchief at her eye and then i gaze at the ship till and after i have land as long as i could discern the least appearance of it for she be under sail in a manner when i leave she and so i return highly disturbed to my inn
i go to bed but rest not return to london the next morning and set out that afternoon again for the country
and so much my dear for poor sally godfrey she send i understand by all opportunity with the knowledge of her husband to learn how her child by her first husband do and have the satisfaction to know she be happily provide for
and about half a year ago her spouse send a little negro boy of about ten year old as a present to wait upon she
but he be take ill of the small-pox and die in a month after he be land
sure sir say i your generous mind must have be long affect with this melancholy case and all its circumstance
it hang upon i indeed some time say he but i be full of spirit and inconsideration
i go soon after to travel a hundred new object dance before my eye and keep reflection from i
and you see i have five or six year afterwards and even before that so thoroughly lose all the impression you talk of that i doubt not to make my pamela change her name without either act of parliament or wedlock and be sally godfrey the second
o you dear naughty man
say i this seem but too true
but i bless god that it be not so i bless god for your reformation and that for your own dear sake as well as mine
well my dear say he and i bless god for it too i do most sincerely and tis my great pleasure because i have as i hop see my error so early and that with such a stock of youth and health on my side in all appearance i can truly abhor my past liberty and pity poor sally godfrey from the same motif that i admire my pamela s virtue and resolve to make myself as worthy of they as possible and i will hope my dear your prayer for my pardon and my perseverance will be of no small efficacy on this occasion
these agreeable reflection on this melancholy but instructive story bring we in view of his own house and we alight and take a walk in the garden till dinner be ready
and now we be so busy about make ready for our appearance that i shall hardly have time to write till that be over
monday morning
yesterday we set out attend by john abraham benjamin and isaac in fine new livery in the good chariot which have be new clean and line and new harness so that it look like a quite new one
but i have no arm to quarter with my dear lord and master s though he jocularly upon my taking notice of my obscurity say that he have a good mind to have the olive-branch which would allude to his hope quarter for mine
i be dress in the suit i mention of white flower with silver and a rich head-dress and the diamond necklace ear-ring etc
i also mention before and my dear sir in a fine laced silk waistcoat of blue paduasoy and his coat a pearl-colour fine cloth with gold button and button-hole and line with white silk and he look charmingly indeed

it seem the neighbouring gentry have expect we and there be a great congregation for against my wish we be a little of the late so that as we walk up the church to his seat we have abundance of gazer and whisperer but my dear master behave with so intrepid an air and be so cheerful and complaisant to i that he do credit to his kind choice instead of shew as if he be ashamed of it and as i be resolve to busy my mind entirely with the duty of the day my intentness on that occasion and my thankfulness to god for his unspeakable mercy to i so take up my thought that i be much less concern than i should otherwise have be at the gazing and whispering of the lady and gentleman as well as of the rest of the congregation whose eye be all turn to our seat
when the sermon be end we staid the long because the church should be pretty empty but we find great number at the church-door and in the church-porch and i have the pleasure of hear many commendation as well of my person as my dress and behaviour and not one reflection or mark of disrespect

tuesday
my generous master have give i this morning a most considerate but yet from the nature of it melancholy instance of his great regard for my unworthiness which i never could have wish hop for or even think of
he take a walk with i after breakfast into the garden and a little shower fall he lead i for shelter into the little summer-house in the private garden where he formerly give i apprehension and sit down by i he say i have now finish all that lie on my mind my dear and be very easy for have you not wonder that i have so much employ myself in my library
be so much at home and yet not in your company no sir say i i have never be so impertinent as to wonder at any thing you please to employ yourself about nor would give way to a curiosity that should be troublesome to you and besides i know your large possession and the method you take of look yourself into your affair must need take up so much of your time that i ought to be very careful how i intrude upon you
well say he but iwill tell you what have be my last work i have take it into my consideration that at present my line be almost extinct and that the chief part of my maternal estate in case i die without issue will go to another line and great part of my personal will fall into such hand as i shall not care my pamela should he at the mercy of
i have therefore as human life be uncertain make such a disposition of my affair as will make you absolutely independent and happy as will secure to you the power of do a great deal of good and live as a person ought to do who be my relict and shall put it out of any body s power to molest your father and mother in the provision i design they for the remainder of their day and i have finish all this very morning except to name trustee for you and if you have any body you would confide in more than another i would have you speak
i be so touch with this mournful instance of his excessive goodness to i and the thought necessarily flow from the solemn occasion that i be unable to speak and at last relieve my mind by a violent fit of weeping and could only say clasp my arm around the dear generous man how shall i support this
so very cruel yet so very kind
donot my dear say he be concern at what give i pleasure

and there be many important point to be think of when life be draw to its utmost verge and the mind may be so agitated and unfit that it be a most sad thing to put off to that time any of those concern which more especially require a considerate and compose frame of temper and perfect health and vigour to give direction about
my poor friend mr
carlton who die in my arm so lately and have a mind disturb by worldly consideration on one side a weakness of body through the violence of his distemper on another and the concern of still as much more moment as the soul be to the body on a third make so great an impression upon i then that i be the more impatient to come to this house where be most of my writing in order to make the disposition i have now perfect and since it be grievous to my dear girl i will myself think of such trustee as shall be most for her benefit
i have only therefore to assure you my dear that in this instance as i will do in any other i can think of i have study to make you quite easy free and independent
and because i shall avoid all occasion for the future which may discompose you i have but one request to make which be that if it please god for my sin to separate i from my dear pamela you will only resolve not to marry one person for i would not be such a herod as to restrain you from a change of condition with any other however reluctantly i may think of any other person s succeed i in your esteem
i could not answer and think my heart would have burst and he continue to conclude at once a subject that be so grievous to you i will tell you my pamela that this person be mr
williams
and now i will acquaint you with my motive for this request which be wholly owe to my niceness and to no dislike i have for he or apprehension of any likelihood that it will be so but methink it would reflect a little upon my pamela if she be to give way to such a conduct as if she have marry a man for his estate when she have rather have have another have it not be for that and that now the world will say she be at liberty to pursue her inclination the parson be the man and i cannot bear even the most distant apprehension that i have not the preference with you of any man live let i have be what i would as i have shew my dear life that i have prefer she to all her sex of whatever degree
i could not speak might i have have the world and he take i in his arm and say i have now speak all my mind and expect no answer and i see you too much move to give i one
only forgive i the mention since i have tell you my motive which as much affect your reputation as my niceness and offer not at an answer only say you forgive i and i hope i have not one discompose thing to say to my dearest for the rest of my life which i pray god for both our sake to lengthen for many happy year
grief still choake up the passage of my word and he say the shower be over my dear let we walk out again he lead i out and i would have speak but he say i will not hear my dear creature say any thing
to hearken to your assurance of comply with my request would look as if i doubt you and want it
i be confident i need only to speak my mind to be observe by you and i shall never more think on the subject if you donot remind i of it
he then most sweetly change the discourse
donot you with pleasure my dear say he take in the delightful fragrance that this sweet shower have give to these bank of flower
your presence be so enliven to i that i could almost fancy that what we owe to the shower be owe to that and all nature methink bloom around i when i have my pamela by my side
you be a poetess my dear and i will give you a few line that i make myself on such an occasion as this i be speak of the presence of a sweet companion and the fresh verdure that after a shower succeed a long drought shew itself throughout all vegetable nature
and then in a sweet and easy accent with his dear arm about i as we walk he sing i the follow verse of which he afterwards favour i with a copy i
all nature bloom when you appear the field their rich liv rie wear oak elm and pine blest with your view shoot out fresh green and bud anew

oh
what a poor thing be human life in its good enjoyment
subject to imaginary evil when it have no real one to disturb it and that can be make as effectually unhappy by its apprehension of remote contingency as if it be struggle with the pang of a present distress
this duly reflect upon methink should convince every one that this world be not a place for the immortal mind to be confine to and that there must be an hereafter where the whole soul shall be satisfied
but i shall get out of my depth my shallow mind cannot comprehend as it ought these weighty subject let i only therefore pray that after have make a grateful use of god s mercy here i may with my dear benefactor rejoice in that happy state where be no mixture no unsatisfiedness and where all be joy and peace and love for evermore

friday
we be yesterday favour with the company of almost all the neighbouring gentleman and their lady who by appointment with one another meet to congratulate our happiness
nothing could be more obliging more free and affectionate than the lady nothing more polite than the gentleman

but i know as my lady davers say though in anger yet in truth that i be but a poor bit of paint dirt
all that i value myself upon be that god have raise i to a condition to be useful in my generation to well person than myself
this be my pride and i hope this will be all my pride

god the all-gracious the all-good the all-bountiful the all-mighty the all-merciful god be the first to he therefore be all the glory
as i expect the happiness the unspeakable happiness my ever-dear and ever-honour father and mother of enjoy you both here under this roof so soon and pray let it be as soon as you can i will not enter into the particular of the last agreeable evening for i shall have a thousand thing as well as that to talk to you upon
i fear you will be tired with my prattle when i see you
i be to return these visit singly and there be eight lady here of different family
dear heart

o what a happy creature be i may i be always thankful to god and grateful to he

lady davers send this morning her compliment to we both very affectionately and her lord s good wish and congratulation and she desire my writing per bearer and say she will herself bring they to i again with thank as soon as she have read they and she and her lord will come and be my guest that be her particularly kind word for a fortnight

b---- have intend
methink i want sadly your list of the honest and worthy poor for the money lie by i and bring i no interest

i be provide a power of pretty thing for she against i see she next that i may make her love i if i can

o how i long for it

here end at present the letter of pamela to her father and mother
they arrive at their daughter s house on tuesday evening in the follow week and be receive by she with the utmost joy and duty and with great goodness and complaisance by mr
b----
and have reside there till every thing be put in order for they at the kentish estate they be carry down thither by himself and their daughter and put into possession of the pretty farm he have design for they
the reader will here indulge we in a few brief observation which naturally result from the story and character and which will serve as so many application of its most material incident to the mind of youth of both sexes
first then in the character of the gentleman may be see that of a fashionable libertine who allow himself in the free indulgence of his passion especially to the fair sex and find himself support in his daring attempt by an affluent fortune in possession a personal bravery as it be call ready to give than take offence and an imperious will yet as he betime see his error and reform in the bloom of youth an edify lesson may be draw from it for the use of such as be bear to large fortune and who may be teach by his example the inexpressible difference between the hazard and remorse which attend a profligate course of life and the pleasure which flow from virtuous love and benevolent action
in the character of lady daver let the proud and the high-bear see the deformity of unreasonable passion and how weak and ridiculous such person must appear who suffer themselves as be usually the case to be hurry from the height of violence to the most abject submission and subject themselves to be outdo by the humble virtue they so much despise
let good clergymen in mr
williams see that whatever displeasure the doing of their duty may give for a time to their proud patron providence will at last reward their piety and turn their distress to triumph and make they even more value for a conduct that give offence while the violence of passion last than if they have meanly stoop to flatter or soothe the vice of the great
in the example of good old andrews and his wife let those who be reduce to a low estate see that providence never fail to reward their honesty and integrity and that god will in his own good time extricate they by mean unforeseen out of their present difficulty and reward they with benefit unhoped for
the upper servant of great family may from the odious character of mrs
jewkes and the amiable one of mrs
jervis mr
longman etc
learn what to avoid and what to choose to make themselves value and esteem by all who know they
and from the double conduct of poor john the lower servant may learn fidelity and how to distinguish between the lawful and unlawful command of a superior
the poor deluded female who like the once unhappy miss godfrey have give up her honour and yield to the allurement of her designing lover may learn from her story to stop at the first fault and by resolve to repent and amend see the pardon and blessing which await her penitence and a kind providence ready to extend the arm of its mercy to receive and reward her return duty while the prostitute pursue the wicked course into which perhaps she be at first inadvertently draw hurry herself into filthy disease and an untimely death and too probably into everlasting perdition
let the despond heart be comfort by the happy issue which the trouble and trial of pamela meet with when they see in her case that no danger nor distress however inevitable or deep to their apprehension can be out of the power of providence to obviate or relieve and which as in various instance in her story can turn the most seemingly grievous thing to its own glory and the reward of suffering innocence and that too at a time when all human prospect seem to fail
let the rich and those who be exalt from a low to a high estate learn from she that they be not promote only for a single good but that providence have raise they that they should dispense to all within their reach the blessing it have heap upon they and that the great the power be to which god hath raise they the great be the good that will be expect from they
from the low opinion she every where shews of herself and she attribute all her excellency to pious education and her lady s virtuous instruction and bounty let person even of genius and piety learn not to arrogate to themselves those gift and grace which they owe least of all to themselves since the beauty of person be frail and it be not in our power to give they to ourselves or to be either prudent wise or good without the assistance of divine grace
from the same good example let child see what a blessing await their duty to their parent though ever so low in the world and that the only disgrace be to be dishonest but none at all to be poor
from the economy she purpose to observe in her elevation let even lady of condition learn that there be family employment in which they may and ought to make themselves useful and give good example to their inferior as well as equal and that their duty to god charity to the poor and sick and the different branch of household management ought to take up the most considerable portion of their time
from her signal veracity which she never forfeit in all the hardship she be try with though her answer as she have reason to apprehend would often make against she and the innocence she preserve throughout all her stratagem and contrivance to save herself from violation person even sorely tempt may learn to preserve a sacred regard to truth which always beget a reverence for they even in the corrupt mind
in short her oblige behaviour to her equal before her exaltation her kindness to they afterwards her forgiving spirit and her generosity her meekness in every circumstance where her virtue be not concern her charitable allowance for other as in the case of miss godfrey for fault she would not have forgive in herself her kindness and prudence to the offspring of that melancholy adventure her maiden and bridal purity which extend as well to her thought as to her word and action her signal affiance in god her thankful spirit her grateful heart her diffusive charity to the poor which make she bless by they whenever she appear abroad the cheerful ease and freedom of her deportment her parental conjugal and maternal duty her social virtue be all so many signal instance of the excellency of her mind which may make her character worthy of the imitation of her sex
and the editor of these sheet will have his end if it inspire a laudable emulation in the mind of any worthy person who may thereby entitle themselves to the reward the praise and the blessing by which pamela be so deservedly distinguish
the end volume ii by samuel richardson author s original preface to volume ii the first part of pamela meet with a success greatly exceed the most sanguine expectation and the editor hop that the letter which compose this part will be find equally write to nature avoid all romantic night improbable surprise and irrational machinery and the passion be touch where requisite and rule equally new and practicable inculcate throughout the whole for the general conduct of life and therefore he flatter himself that they may expect the good fortune which few continuation have meet with to be judge not unworthy the first part nor disproportione to the more exalt condition in which pamela be destine to shine as an affectionate wife a faithful friend a polite and kind neighbour an indulgent mother and a beneficent mistress after have in the former part support the character of a dutiful child a spotless virgin and a modest and amiable bride
the reader will easily see that in so great a choice of material as must arise from a multitude of important subject in a married life to such genius and friendship as those of mr
and mrs
b
the editor s great difficulty be how to bring they within the compass which he be determine not to exceed
and it have be leave to his own choice in what manner to digest and publish the letter and where to close the work he have intend at first in regard to his other avocation to have carry the piece no far than the first part
it may be expect therefore that he should enter into an explanation of the reason whereby he be provoke into a necessity of alter his intention
but he be willing to decline say any thing upon so well-know a subject
the editor have be much press with importunity and conjecture in relation to the person and family of the gentleman who be the principal person in the work all he think himself at liberty to say or be necessary to be say be only to repeat what have already be hint that the story have its foundation in truth and that there be a necessity for obvious reason to vary and disguise some fact and circumstance as also the name of person place c
letter i my dear father and mother we arrive here last night highly pleased with our journey and the occasion of it
may god bless you both with long life and health to enjoy your sweet farm and pretty dwelling which be just what i wish it to be
and donot make your grateful heart too uneasy in the possession of it by your modest diffidence of your own unworthiness for at the same time that it be what will do honour to the good of man it be not so very extraordinary consider his condition as to cause any one to censure it as the effect of a too partial and injudicious kindness for the parent of one whom he delighteth to honour
my dear master why should i not still call he so bind to reverence he as i be in every light he can shine in to the most obliging and sensible heart still propose to fit up the large parlour and three apartment in the commodious dwelling he call your for his entertainment and mine when i pay my duty to you both for a few happy day and he have actually give order to that effect and that the three apartment be so fit up as to be rather suitable to your condition than his own for he say the plain simple elegance which he will have observe in the room as well as the furniture will be a variety in his retirement to this place that will make he return to his own with the great pleasure and at the same time when we be not there will be of use for the reception of any of your friend and so he shall not as he kindly say rob the good couple of any of their accommodation
the old bow-window he will have preserve but will not have they sashe nor the woodbine jessamine and vine that run up against they destroy only he will have large pane of glass and more convenient casement to let in the sweet air and light and make amend for that obstruct by the shade of those fragrant climber
for he have mention three or four time how gratefully they dispense their intermingle odour to we when the last evening we stand at the window to hear the responsive song of two warbling nightingale one at a distance the other near which delight we for above two hour and the more as we think their season have be over
and when they have do he make i sing he one for which he reward i with a kiss say how greatly do the innocent pleasure i now hourly taste exceed the guilty tumult that use formerly to agitate my unequal mind never talk my pamela as you frequently do of obligation to i one such hour as i now enjoy be an ample reward for all the benefit i can confer on you and your in my whole life the parlour will indeed be more elegant though that be to be rather plain than rich as well in its wainscot as furniture and to be new-floored
the dear gentleman have already give order and you will soon have workman to put they in execution
the parlour-door be to have brass-hinge and lock and to shut as close he tell they as a watch-case for who know say he my dear but we shall have still add blessing in two or three charming boy and girl to place there in their infancy before they can be of age to be benefit by your lesson and example
and besides i shall no doubt entertain there some of my choose friend in their excursion for a day or two how be i every hour of my life overwhelm with instance of god almighty s goodness and his
o spare bless father of mercies the precious life of this excellent man increase my thankfulness and my worthiness and then--but what shall i say only that i may continue to be what i be for more blessed and happy in my own mind i cannot be
the bed he will have of cloth as he think the situation a little cold especially when the wind be easterly and purpose to be down in the early spring season now and then as well as in the latter autumn and the window curtain of the same in one room red in the other green but plain lest you should be afraid to use they occasionally
the carpet for they will be send with the other furniture for he will not alter the old oaken floor of the bed-chamber nor the little room he intend for my use when i choose not to join in such company as may happen to fall in which my dear say he shall be as little as be possible only particular friend who may be dispose once in a year or two to see when i be there how i live with my pamela and her parent and how i pass my time in my retirement as i shall call this or perhaps they will be apt to think i ashamed of company i shall always be pleased with
nor be you my dear to take this as a compliment to yourself but a piece of requisite policy in i for who will offer to reproach i with marrying as the world think below i when they shall see that i not only pride myself in my pamela but take pleasure in own her relation as mine and visit they and receive visit from they and yet offer not to set they up in such a glaring light as if i would have the world forget who in that case would always take the more pleasure in remembering what they be
and how will it anticipate low reflection when they shall see i can bend my mind to partake with they the pleasure of their humble but decent life ay continue he and be reward for it too with well health well spirit and a well mind so that my dear add he i shall reap more benefit by what i propose to do than i shall confer in this generous manner do this good of man endeavour to disclaim though i must be very ungrateful if with i it do not enhance the proper merit of a beneficence natural to he and which indeed as i tell he may be in one respect deprecate inasmuch as so excellent be his nature he cannot help it if he would
o that it be in my power to recompense he for it
but i be poor as i have often say in every thing but will--and that be wholly his and what a happiness be it to i a happiness i could not so early have hop for that i can say so without reserve since the dear object of it require nothing of i but what be consistent with my duty to the supreme benefactor the first mover and cause of all his own happiness of my happiness and that of my dear my ever dear parent
your dutiful and happy daughter letter ii my dearest daughter i need not repeat to you the sense your good mother and i have of our happiness and of our obligation to your honoured spouse you both be pleased witness of it every hour of the happy fortnight you pass with we
yet my dear we hardly know how to address ourselves even to you much less to the squire with the freedom he so often invite we to take for i donot know how it be but though you be our daughter and so far from be lift up by your high condition that we see no difference in your behaviour to we your poor parent yet view you as the lady of so fine a gentleman we cannot forbear have a kind of respect and--i donot know what to call it--that lay a little restraint upon we
and yet we should not methink let our mind be run away with the admiration of worldly grandeur so as to set too much by it
but your merit and prudence be so much above all we could ever have any notion of and to have gentry come only to behold and admire you not so much for your gentleness and amiableness or for your behaviour and affability to poor as well as rich and to hear every one call you an angel and say you deserve to be what you be make we hardly know how to look upon you but as an angel indeed
i be sure you have be a good angel to we since for your sake god almighty have put it into your honour husband s heart to make we the happy couple in the world
but little less we should have be have we only in some far distant land hear of our dear child s happiness and never partake of the benefit of it ourselves
but thus to be provide for
thus kindly to be own and call father and mother by such a brave gentleman
and so place as to have nothing to do but to bless god he and you and hourly pray for you both be a providence too mighty to be bear by we with equalness of temper we kneel together every morning noon and night and weep and rejoice and rejoice and weep to think how our unworthiness be distinguish and how god have provide for we in our latter day when all our fear be that as we grow old and more infirm and wear out by hard labour we should be troublesome where not our pride but our industrious will would have make we wish not to be so but to be entitle to a happy lot for this would have grieve we the more for the sake of you my dear child and your unhappy brother s child for it be well know that though we pretend not to boast of our family and indeed have no reason yet none of we be ever sink so low as i be to be sure partly by my own fault for have it be for your poor aged mother s sake only i ought not to have do what i do for john and william for so unhappy be they poor lad
that what i could do be but as a drop of water to a bucket
you command i--let i as write to mr
b s lady say command though as to my dear daughter i will only say desire and indeed i will not as you wish i not to do let the one condition which be accidental put the other which be natural out of my thought you speak it in well word but this be the sense
but you have the gift of utterance and education be a fine thing where it meet with such talent to improve upon as god have give you
yet let i not forget what i be go to say--you command or if you please--you desire i to write long letter and often--and how can i help it if i would
for when here in this happy dwelling and this well-stock farm in these rich meadow and well-cropt acre we look around we and which way soever we turn our head see blessing upon blessing and plenty upon plenty see barn well store poultry increase the kine lowing and crowd about we and be bid to call they our own
then think that all be the reward of our child s virtue o my dear daughter who can bear these things excuse i
i must break off a little
for my eye be as full as my heart and i will retire to bless god and your honoured husband
so my dear child i now again take up my pen but read what i have write in order to carry on the thread i can hardly forbear again be in one sort affect
but do you think i will call all these thing my own do you think i would live rent-free
can the honour squire believe that have such a generous example before i if i have no gratitude in my temper before i could help be touch by such an one as he set i
if this goodness make he know no mean in giving shall i be so greedy as to know none in receive
come come my dear child your poor father be not so sordid a wretch neither
he will shew the world that all these benefit be not throw away upon one who will disgrace you as much by his temper as by his condition
what though i cannot be as worthy of all these favour as i wish i will be as worthy as i can
and let i tell you my dear child if the king and his royal family god bless them be not ashamed to receive taxis and duty from his subject if duke and earl and all the top gentry cannot support their bravery without have their rent pay i hope i shall not affront the squire to pay to his steward what any other person would pay for his noble stock and improve farm and i will do it if it please god to bless i with life and health
i should not be worthy to crawl upon the earth if i do not
and what do i say to mr
longman the faithful mr
longman
sure no gentleman have ever a more worthy steward than he it be as we be walk over the ground together and observe in what good order every thing be he be praise some little contrivance of my own for the improvement of the farm and say how comfortably he hop we might live upon it
ay mr
longman say i comfortably indeed but do you think i could be properly say to live if i be not to pay as much rent for it as another i can tell you say he the squire will not receive any thing from you goodman andrews
why man he have no occasion for it hebe worth a power of money besides a noble and clear estate in land
ad heartliken you must not affront he i can tell you that hebe as generous as a prince where he take but he be hasty and will have his own way why for that reason mr
longman say i i be think to make you my friend make i your friend
you have not a well in the world to my power i can tell you that nor your dame neither for i love such honest heart i wish my own brother would let i love he as well but let that pass
what i can do for you i will and herebe my hand upon it well then say i it be this let i account to you at the rent farmer dickens offer and let i know what the stock cost and what the crop be value at and pay the one as i can and the other quarterly and not let the squire know it till you cannot choose and i shall be as happy as a prince for i doubt not by god s blessing to make a comfortable livelihood of it besides why dost believe goodman andrews say he that i would do such a thing
would not his honour think if i hide one thing from he i might hide another
go to honest heart i love thee dearly but can mr

do too much for his lady think st thou
come come and he jeer i so i know not what to say i wish at bottom there be not some pride in this
what i warrant you would not be too much beholden to his honour would you no say i it be not that ibe sure
if i have any pride it be only in my dear child--to whom under god all this be owe
but some how or other it shall be so and so my dear daughter i resolve it shall and it will be over and above one of the great pleasure to i to do the good squire service as well as to be so much benefit and oblige by he
our eld grandson thomas desire to come and live with we the boy be honest and i hear industrious
and cousin borroughs want i to employ his son roger who understand the business of a farm very well
it be no wonder that all one s relation should wish to partake of our happy lot and if they can and will do their business as well as other i see not why relationship should be an objection but yet i think one should not beleaguer as one may say your honour husband with one s relation
you my good child will give i always your advice as to my carriage in this my new lot for i would not for the world be think an encroacher
and you have so follow than your

letter iii my dear father and mother i have shew your letter to my beloved
donot be uneasy that i have for you need not be ashamed of it since it be my pride to have such honest and grateful parent and iwill tell you what he say to it as the good argument i can use why you should not be uneasy but enjoy without pain or anxiety all the benefit of your happy lot
dear good soul say he now every thing they say and write manifest the worthiness of their heart
no wonder pamela you love and revere such honest mind for that you would do be they not your parent and tell they that i be so far from have they believe what i have do for they be only from my affection for their daughter that let them find out another couple as worthy as they be and i will do as much for they
i would not place they he continue in the same county because i would wish two county to be bless for their sake
tell they my dear that they have a right to what they enjoy on the foot of their own proper merit and bid they enjoy it as their patrimony and if any thing arise that be more than they themselves can wish for in their way of life let they look among their own relation where it may be acceptable and communicate to they the like solid reason for rejoice in the situation they be pleased with and do you my dear still far enable they as you shall judge proper to gratify their enlarge heart for fear they should deny any comfort to themselves in order to do good to other i could only fly to his generous bosom for this be a subject which most affect i and with my eye swim in tear of grateful joy and which overflow as soon as my bold lip touch his dear face bless god and bless he with my whole heart for speak i could not
but almost chok d with my joy sobb d to he my grateful acknowledgment
he clasp i in his arm and say how my dearest do you overpay i for the little i have do for your parent
if it be thus to be bless d for confer benefit so insignificant to a man of my fortune what joy be it not in the power of rich man to give themselves whenever they please foretaste indeed of those we be bid to hope for which can surely only exceed these as then we shall be all intellect and well fit to receive they tis too much too much say i in broken accent how be i oppress with the pleasure you give me o sir bless i more gradually and more cautiously--for i cannot bear it and indeed my heart go flutter flutter flutter at his dear breast as if it want to break its too narrow prison to mingle still more intimately with his own
surely my beloved parent nobody s happiness be so great as mine if it proceed thus from degree to degree and be to be augment by the charming hope that the dear second author of our blessing be the uniformly good as well as the partially kind man to we what a felicity will this be
and if our prayer shall be hear and we shall have the pleasure to think that his advance in piety be owe not a little to they and to the example god shall give we grace to set then indeed may we take the pride to think we have repay his goodness to we and that we have satisfy the debt which nothing less can discharge
forgive i my worthy parent if my style on this subject be raise above the natural simplicity more suited to my humble talent
but how can i help it
for when the mind be elevate ought not the sense we have of our happiness to make our expression soar equally
can the affection be so highly raise as mine be on these occasion and the thought creep grovel like one s ordinary self
no indeed call not this therefore the gift of utterance if it should appear to you in a well light than it deserve
it be the gift of gratitude a gift which make you and i to speak and write as i hope it will make we act above ourselves
thus will our gratitude be the inspirer of joy to our common benefactor and his joy will heighten our gratitude and so we shall proceed as cause and effect to each other s happiness to bless the dear man who bless we
and will it be right then to say you be uneasy under such at least as to your will return and discharged obligation
god almighty require only a thankful heart for all the mercy he heap upon the child of man my dear mr
b who in these particular imitate divinity desire no more
you have this thankful heart and that to such a high degree of gratitude that nobody can exceed you
but yet when your worthy mind would be too much affect with your gratitude so as to lay under the restraint you mention to the dear gentleman and for his sake to your dependent daughter let i humbly advise you with more particular more abstracted aspiration than at other time to raise your thought upwards and consider who it be that give he the opportunity and pray for he and for i for he that all his future action may be of a piece with this noble disposition of mind for i that i may continue humble and consider myself blest for your sake and in order that i may be in some sort a rewarder in the hand of providence of this its dear excellent agent and then we shall look forward all of we with pleasure indeed to that state where there be no distinction of degree and where the humble cottager shall be upon a par with the proud monarch
o my dear parent how can you as in your postscript say may we not be favour now-and-then with a letter call i your daughter your pamela--i be no lady to you
i have more pleasure to be call your comfort and think to act worthy of the sentiment with which your example and instruction have inspire i than in any other thing in this life my determined duty to our common benefactor the good of gentleman and husband except
god have bless i for your sake and have thus answer for i all your prayer nay more than answer all you or i could have wish or hop for
we only pray only hop that god would preserve you honest and i virtuous and o see my excellent parent how we be crown with blessing upon blessing till we be the talk of all that know we
hence my dear parent i mean from the delight i have in write to you which transport i far above my own sphere youwill see that i must write and cannot help it if i would
and will it be a great joy to you and be there any thing that can add to your joy think you in the power of your pamela that she would not do
o that the life and health of my dearest mr
b
and you my parent may be continue to i
and who can then be so blest as your pamela
i will write depend upon it on every occasion--and you augment my joy to think it be in my power to add to your comfort
nor can you conceive my pleasure in hop that this your new happy lot may by relieve you from corroding care and the too weary effect of hard labour add in these your advanced year to both your day
for so happy be i i can have no grief no pain in look forward but from reflect that one day we must be separate
but it be fit that we so comport ourselves as not to embitter our present happiness with prospect too gloomy--but bring our mind to be cheerfully thankful for the present wisely to enjoy that present as we go along--and at last when all be to be wind up--lie down and say not mine but thy will be do i have write much yet have still more to say relate to other part of your kind acceptable letter and so will soon write again for i must think every opportunity happy whereby i can assure you how much i be and will ever be without any addition to my name if it will make you easy your dutiful pamela
letter iv my dearest father and mother i now write again as i tell you i should in my last but i be half afraid to look at the copy of it for your worthy heart so visible in your letter and my beloved s kind deportment upon shew it to he raise i into a frame of mind border on ecstasy yet i write my heart
but you must not my dear father write to your pamela so affectingly
your steady mind could hardly bear your own moving strain and you be force to lay down your pen and retire how then could i who love you so dearly if you have not increase that love by fresh and strong instance of your worthiness forbear being affect and raise above myself
but i will not again touch upon this subject
you must know then that my dear spouse command i with his kind respect to tell you he have think of a method to make your worthy heart easy those be his word and this be say he by put that whole estate with the new purchase under your father s care as i at first intend he shall receive and pay and order every thing as he please and longman who grow in year shall be ease of that burden
your father write a very legible hand and shall take what assistant he please and do you pamela see that this new task be make as easy and pleasant to he as possible
he shall make up his account only to you my dear
and there will be several pleasure arise to i upon it first that it will be a relief to honest longman who have business enough on his hand
next it will make the good couple easy to have an opportunity of enjoy that as their due which now their too grateful heart give they so many causeless scruple about
thirdly it will employ your father s time more suitably to your like and mine because with more ease to himself for you see his industrious will cannot be satisfied without do something
in the fourth place the management of this estate will gain he more respect and reverence among the tenant and his neighbour and yet be all in his own way
for add he youwill see that it be always one point in view with i to endeavour to convince every one that i esteem and value they for their own intrinsic merit and want not any body to distinguish they in any other light than that in which they have be accustom to appear so my dear father the instrument will be draw and bring you by honest mr
longman who will be with you in a few day to put the last hand to the new purchase and to give you possession of your new commission if you accept it as i hope you will and the rather for my dear mr
b s third reason and know that this trust will be discharge as worthily and as sufficiently after you be use to it as if mr
longman himself be in it--and well it cannot be

will leave every thing to you as to rent where not already fix and likewise as to act of kindness and favour to be do where you think proper and he say that with his bad quality he be ever deem a kind landlord and that i can confirm in fifty instance to his honour so that the old gentleman say he need not be afraid of be put upon severe or harsh method of proceeding where thing will do without and he can always befriend an honest man by which mean the province will be entirely such a one as suit with his inclination
