be pleased to mention and what a charming contrivance be here
god for ever bless his considerate heart for it
to make you useful to he and easy to yourself as well as respect by and even a benefactor to all around you
what can one say to all thing
but what signify exult on one s gratitude for one benefit every hour the dear man heap new one upon we and we can hardly thank he for one but a second and a third and so on to countless degree confound one and throw back our word upon our heart before they be well form and oblige we to sit down under all with profound silence and admiration
as to the desire of cousin thomas and roger to live with you i endeavour to sound what our dear benefactor s opinion be
he be pleased to say i have no choice in this case my dear
your father be his own master he may employ whom he please and if they shew respect to he and your mother i think as he rightly observe relationship should rather have the preference and as he can remedy inconvenience if he find any by all mean to let every branch of your family have reason to rejoice with he but i have think of this matter a good deal since i have the favour of your letter and i hope since you condescend to ask my advice you will excuse i if i give it freely yet entirely submit all to your liking
first then i think it well to have any body than relation and for these reason one be apt to expect more regard from they and they more indulgence than stranger can hope for
that where there be such a difference in the expectation of both uneasiness cannot but arise
that this will subject you to bear it or to resent it and to part with they
if you bear it you will know no end of imposition if you dismiss they it will occasion ill-will
they will call you unkind and you they ungrateful and as your prosperous lot may raise you envier such will be apt to believe they rather than you
then the world will be incline to think that we be crowd upon a generous gentleman a numerous family of indigent people and it will be say the girl be fill every place with her relation and beleaguer as you significantly express it a worthy gentleman should one s kindre behave ever so worthily
so in the next place one would not for their sake that this should be do who may live with less reproach and equal benefit any where else for i would not wish any one of they to be lift out of his station and make independent at mr
b s expense if their industry will not do it although i would never scruple to do any thing reasonable to promote or assist that industry in the way of their calling
then my dear father i apprehend that our honoured benefactor would be under some difficulty from his natural politeness and regard for you and i
you see how kindly on all occasion he treat you both not only as the parent of his pamela but as if you be his own and if you have any body as your servant there who call you cousin or grandfather or uncle he would not care when he come down to treat they on the foot of common servant though they might think themselves honour as they would be and as i shall always think myself with his command
and would it not if they be modest and worthy be as great a difficulty upon they to be thus distinguish as it would be to he and to i for his sake
for otherwise believe i i hope you will my dear father and mother i could sit down and rejoice with the mean and remotest relation i have
but in the world s eye to every body but my good of parent i must if ever so reluctant to it appear in a light that may not give discredit to his choice
then again as i hint you will be able without the least injury to our common benefactor to do kind thing by any of our relation when not with you than you can do if they live with you
you may lend they a little money to put they in a way if any thing offer that you think will be to their advantage
you can fit out my she-cousin to good reputable place
the young you can put to school or when fit to trade accord to their talent and so they will be of course in a way to get an honest and creditable livelihood
but above all thing one would discourage such a proud and ambitious spirit in any of they as should want to raise itself by favour instead of merit and this the rather for undoubtedly there be many more happy person in low than in high life take number for number all the world over
i be sure although four or five year of different life have pass with i i have so much pride and pleasure in the thought of work for my live with you if i could but get honest to you that it make my confinement the more grievous and if possible aggravate the apprehension attend it
but i beg of you not to think these my reason proceed from the bad motif of a heart taint with pride on its high condition
indeed there can be no reason for it to one who think after this manner--the great family on earth have some among they who be unhappy and low in life and shall such a one reproach i with have twenty low relation because they have peradventure not above five
let we then my dear parent endeavour to judge of one another as god at the last day will judge of we all and then the honest peasant will stand fair in our esteem than the guilty peer
in short this shall be my own rule--every one who act justly and honestly i will look upon as my relation whether so or not and the more he want my assistance the more entitled to it he shall be as well as to my esteem while those who deserve it not must expect only compassion from i and my prayer be they my brother or sister
tis true have i not be poor and lowly i might not have think thus but if it be a right way of thinking it be a blessing that i be so and that shall never be matter of reproach to i which one day will be matter of justification
upon the whole i should think it advisable my dear father and mother to make such kind excuse to the offer service of my cousin as your well reason shall suggest to you and to do any thing else for they of more value as their circumstance may require or occasion offer to serve they
but if the employing and have they about you will add comfort to your life i give up entirely my own opinion and doubt not every thing will be think well of that you shall think fit to do
and so i conclude with assure you that i be my ever-dear parent your dutiful and happy daughter
the copy of this letter i will keep to myself till i have your answer that you may be under no difficulty how to act in either of the case mention in it
letter v my dearest daughter how shall i do to answer as they deserve your two last letter
sure no happy couple ever have such a child as we have
but it be in vain to aim at word like your and equally in vain for we to offer to set forth the thankfulness of our heart on the kind office your honour husband have give we for no reason but to favour we still more and to quiet our mind in the notion of be useful to he
god grant i may be able to be so happy shall i be if i can
but i see the generous drift of his proposal it be only to make i more easy from the nature of my employment and in my mind too over-loaded as i may say with benefit and at the same time to make i more respected in my new neighbourhood

longman shall with still great pleasure do all i can in it
but i doubt i shall want ability but i will be just and honest however
that by god s grace will be within my own capacity and that i hope i may answer for

longman with this and take his advice for i will not be too troublesome either to you my dear child or to your spouse if i could act so for his interest as not to be a burden what happy creature should we both be in our own minds we find ourselves more and more respected by every one and so far as shall be consistent with our new trust we will endeavour to deserve it that we may interest as many as know we in our own good wish and prayer for the happiness of you both
but let i say how much convinced i be by your reason for not take to we any of our relation
every one of those reason have its force with we
how happy be we to have so prudent a daughter to advise with
and i think myself oblige to promise this that whatever i do for any of they above the amount of--forty shilling at one time i will take your direction in it that your wise hint of make every one continue their industry and not to rely upon favour instead of merit may be follow
i be sure this be the way to make they happy as well as well man and woman for as i have often think if one be to have a hundred pound a year it would not do without industry and with it one may do with a quarter of it and less
in short my dear child your reason be so good that i wonder they come not into my head before and then i need not to have trouble you about the matter but yet it run in my own thought that i could not like to be an encroacher for i hate a dirty thing and in the midst of my distress never could be guilty of one
thank god for it
you rejoice our heart beyond expression at the hope you give we of receive letter from you now-and-then it will be the chief comfort of our life next to see you as we expect we sometimes shall
but yet my dear child donot let we inconvenience you neither
pray donot youwill have enough upon your hand without--to be sure you will

letter vi from lady davers to mrs
b my dear pamela i intend to have be with you before this but my lord have be a little indisposed with the gout and jackey have have an intermitting fever but they be pretty well recover and it shall not be long before i see you now i understand you be return from your kentish expedition

indeed pamela you be a charming creature and an ornament to your sex
we want to have have you among we a hundred time as we read that we might have love and kiss and thank you

i can assure you my lord long to see you and will accompany i for he say he have but a faint idea of your person

letter vii my good dear lady you have do i great honour in the letter your ladyship have be pleased to send i and it be a high pleasure to i now all be so happily over that my poor paper in the least divert you and such honourable and worthy person as your ladyship mention

letter viii from lady davers in reply my dear pamela you have give we all a great disappointment in decline to oblige i with the sequel of your paper
i be a little out of humour with you at first i must own i was for i cannot bear denial when my heart be set upon any thing
but lady betty become your advocate and say she think you very excusable since no doubt there might be many tender thing circumstance as you be well enough for your parent to see but for nobody else and relation of our side the least of all whose future intimacy and frequent visit might give occasion for raillery and remark not otherwise agreeable
i regard she apology for you the more because i know it be a great baulk to she that you do not comply with my request
but now child when you know i more youwill find that if i be oblige to give up one point i always insist on another as near it as i can in order to see if it be only one thing i be to be refuse or every thing in which last case i know how to take my measure and resent
now this be what i insist upon that you correspond with i the same as you do with your parent and acquaint i with every passage that be of concern to you begin with your account how both of you spend your time when in kent for you must know we be all take with your duty to your parent and the discretion of the good couple and think you have give a very edify example of filial piety to all who shall hear your story for if so much duty be owe to parent where nothing can be do for one how much more be it to be expect where there be power to add to the natural obligation all the comfort and convenience of life
we people in upper life love to hear how gratitude and unexpected benefit operate upon honest mind who have little more than plain artless nature for their guide and we flatter ourselves with the hope of many a delightful hour by your mean in this our solitary situation if oblige to pass the next winter in it as my lord and the earl threaten i and the countess and lady betty that we shall
then let we hear of every thing that give you joy or trouble and if my brother carry you to town for the winter while he attend parliament the advice you can give we of what pass in london and of the public entertainment and diversion he will take you to relate in your own artless and natural observation will be as divert to we as if at they ourselves
for a young creature of your good understanding to whom all these thing will be quite new will give we perhaps a well taste of they their beauty and defect than we might have before for we people of quality go to those place dress out and adorn in such a manner outvye one another as if we consider ourselves as so many part of the public entertainment and be too much pleased with ourselves to be able so to attend to what we see as to form a right judgment of it but some of we behave with so much indifference to the entertainment as if we think ourselves above be divert by what we come to see and as if our view be rather to trifle away our time than improve ourselves by attend to the story of the action
see pamela i shall not make an unworthy correspondent altogether for i can get into thy grave way and moralize a little now and then and if youwill promise to oblige i by your constant correspondence in this way and divest yourself of all restraint as if you be write to your parent and i can tell you youwill write to one who will be as candid and as favourable to you as they can be then i be sure we shall have truth and nature from you and these be thing which we be generally so much lift above by our condition that we hardly know what they be
but i have write enough for one letter and yet have more to say i will after this send another without wait for your answer which you may give to both together and be yours c

letter ix dear pamela i be very glad thy honest man have let thee into the affair of sally godfrey
but pr ythee pamela tell we how he do it and thy thought upon it for that be a critical case and as he have represent it so shall i know what to say of it before you and he for i would not make mischief between you for the world

now pamela i have two view in this
one be to see how a man of my brother s spirit who have not deny himself any genteel liberty for it must be own he never be a common town rake and have always a dignity in his roguery will behave himself to you and in wedlock which use to be freely sneer at by he the next that i may love you more and more as by your letter i shall be more and more acquainted with you as well as by conversation so that you cannot be off if you would

you be lift up to the rank of a lady and you must act up to it and not think of set such an example as will draw upon you the ill-will and censure of other lady
for will any of our sex visit one who be continually employ herself in such work as either must be a reproach to herself or to them you ll have nothing to do but to give order
you will consider yourself as the task-mistress and the common herd of female servant as so many negro direct themselves by your nod or yourself as the master-wheel in some beautiful piece of mechanism whose dignified grave motion be to set a-go all the under-wheel with a velocity suitable to their respective part
let your servant under your direction do all that relate to household management they cannot write to entertain and instruct as you can so what will you have to do i ll answer my own question in the first place endeavour to please your sovereign lord and master and let i tell you any other woman in england be her quality ever so high would have find enough to do to succeed in that
secondly to receive and pay visit in order for his credit as well as your own to make your fashionable neighbour fond of you
then thirdly you will have time upon your hand as your monarch himself rise early and be tolerably regular for such a brazen face as he have be to write to i in the manner i have mention and expect and i see plainly by your style nothing can be easy for you than to do this

besides child be not these thing write in confidence have not recite all you could recite would there not have be room for any one who see what you write to imagine they have be still bad
and how could the terror be suppose to have have such effect upon you as to endanger your life without imagine you have undergo the bad a vile man could offer unless you have tell we what that be which he do offer and so put a bound as it be to one s fear of what you suffer which otherwise must have be injurious to your purity though you could not help it
moreover pamela it be but do justice to the libertine himself to tell your mother the whole truth that she might know he be not so very abandon but he could stop short of the execution of his wicked purpose which he apprehend if pursue would destroy the life that of all life he would choose to preserve and you owe also thus much to your parent peace of mind that after all their distracting fear for you they might see they have reason to rejoice in an uncontaminated daughter
and one cannot but reflect now he have make you his wife that it must be satisfaction to the wicked man as well as to yourself that he be not more guilty than he be nor take more liberty than he do
for my own part i must say that i could not have account for your fit by any description short of those you give and have you be less particular in the circumstance i should have judge he have be still bad and your person though not your mind less pure than his pride would expect from the woman he should marry for this be the case of all rake that though they indulge in all manner of libertinism themselves there be no class of man who exact great delicacy from the person they marry though they care not how bad they make the wife the sister and daughter of other
i will only add and send all my three letter together that we all blame you in some degree for bear the wicked jewkes in your sight after her most impudent assistance in his lewd attempt much less we think ought you to have leave she in her place and reward she for her vileness could hardly be equal by the bad action of the most abandon procuress

she would she say have she be in your case have have one struggle for her dismission let it have be take as it would and he that be so well pleased with your virtue must have think this a natural consequence of it if he be in earnest to reclaim
i know not whether you shew he all i write but i have write this last part in the cover as well for want of room as that you may keep it from he if you please
though if you think it will serve any good end i be not against shew to he all i write
for i must ever speak my mind though i be to smart for it and that nobody can or have the heart to make i do but my bold brother
so pamela for this time adieu
letter xi my good lady i be honour with your ladyship s three letter the content of which be highly oblige to i and i should be inexcusable if i do not comply with your injunction and be very proud and thankful for your ladyship s condescension in accept of my poor scribble and promise such a rich and valuable return of which you have already give such ample and delightful instance
i will not plead my defect to excuse my obedience
i only fear that the awe which will be always upon i when i write to your ladyship will lay i under so great a restraint that i shall fall short even of the merit my paper have already make for i through your kind indulgence yet shelter myself under your goodness i will cheerfully comply with every thing your ladyship expect from i that it be in my power to do
you will give i leave madam to put into some little method the particular of what you desire of i that i may speak to they all for since you be so good as to excuse i from send the rest of my paper which indeed would not bear in many place i will omit nothing that shall tend to convince you of my readiness to obey you in every thing else
first then your ladyship would have the particular of the happy fortnight we pass in kent on one of the most agreeable occasion that could befall i
secondly an account of the manner in which your dear brother acquaint i with the affect story of miss godfrey and my behaviour upon it

letter xii from lady davers to mrs
b my dear pamela you very much oblige i by your cheerful compliance with my request i leave it entirely to you to write as you shall be in the humour when you take up your pen and then i shall have you write with less restraint for you must know that what we admire in you be truth and nature not study or elaborate epistle

to take such a method to shew your good daughter that because she do not come soon enough to you she come too soon
and do ever papa before you put a good book for such i doubt not it be because you be in affliction though so little affect by its precept to such a bad use
as parent example be so prevalent suppose your daughter have take it and fling it at her sister miss nancy at she waiting-maid and so it have go through the family would it not have be an excuse for every one to say that the father and head of the family have set the example
you almost wish my dear miss tell i that i would undertake you this be very good of you
sir simon i might would his patience have suffer i to run on thus have add but i hope since you be so sensible that you want to be undertake and since this peevish rashness convince i that you do that you will undertake yourself that you will not when your indisposition require the attendance and duty of your dear lady and daughter make it more uncomfortable to they by add a difficulty of be please and an impatience of spirit to the concern their duty and affection make they have for you and at least resolve never to take a book into your hand again if you cannot make a well use of it than you do then but sir simon will say i have already undertake he be he to see this
yet my lady darnford once beg i would give he a hint or two on this subject which she be pleased to say would be well receive from i than from any body and if it be a little too severe it be but a just reprisal make by one whose ear he know he have cruelly wound more than once twice or thrice besides by what he call his innocent double entendre and who if she have not resent it when an opportunity offer must have be believe by he to be neither more nor less than a hypocrite
therebe for you sir simon and so here end all my malice for now i have speak my mind
yet i hope your dear papa will not be so angry as to deny i for this my freedom the request i make to he to your mamma and to your dear self for your beloved company for a month or two in bedfordshire and at london and if you might be permit to winter with we at the latter how happy should i be
it will be half do the moment you desire it

o my dear miss damford i how happy be it in your power to make i

i see my dearest miss darnford how useful in every condition of life a virtuous and a serious turn of mind may be

as to i it become i to forgive you as i most cordially do since your usage of i as it prove be but a necessary mean in the hand of providence to exalt i to that state of happiness in which i have every day more and more cause give i to rejoice by the kind and most generous of gentleman

and when you can taste the all-surpass pleasure that fill the worthy breast on be place in a station where your example may be of advantage to the soul of other as well as to your own--a pleasure that every good mind glory in and none else can truly relish then may you be assure that nothing but your perseverance and the consequential improvement result from it be want to convince you that you be in a right way and that the woe that be pronounce against the presumptuous sinner belong not to you
let i therefore dear mrs
jewkes for now indeed you be dear to i caution you against two thing the one that you return not to your former way and wilfully err after this repentance for the divine goodness will then look upon itself as mock by you and will withdraw itself from you and more dreadful will your state then be than if you have never repent the other that you donot despair of the divine mercy which have so evidently manifest itself in your favour and have awaken you out of your deplorable lethargy without those sharp medicine and operation which other and perhaps not more faulty person have suffer
but go on cheerfully in the same happy path
depend upon it you be now in the right way and turn not either to the right hand or to the left for the reward be before you in reputation and a good fame in this life and everlaste felicity beyond it
your letter be that of a sensible woman as i always think you and of a truly contrite one as i hope you will prove yourself to be and i the rather hope it as i shall be always desirous then of take every opportunity that offer of do you real service as well with regard to your present as future life for i be good mrs
jewkes as i now hope i may call you your love friend to serve you p b

our trouble will be quickly overblown and we shall be happy in a well i make no doubt then my dear mother throw her arm about his neck and say with tear god s will be do my dear love
all cannot be rich and happy
i be content and have rather say i have a poor honest husband than a guilty rich one

for a farth proof that honest poverty be not such a deplorable thing as some people imagine let i ask what pleasure can those over-happy person know who from the luxury of their taste and their affluent circumstance always eat before they be hungry and drink before they be thirsty
this may be illustrate by the instance of a certain eastern monarch who as i have read march at the head of a vast army through a wide extended desert which afford neither river nor spring for the first time find himself in common with his soldier overtake by a craving thirst which make he pant after a cup of water
and when after diligent search one of his soldier find a little dirty puddle and carry he some of the filthy water in his nasty helmet the monarch greedily swallow it cry out that in all his life he never taste so sweet a draught
but when i talk or write of my worthy parent how i run on excuse i my good lady and donot think i in this respect too much like the cat in the fable turn into a fine lady for though i would never forget what i be yet i would be think to know how gratefully to enjoy my present happiness as well with regard to my obligation to god as to your dear brother

letter xxii from sir simon darnford to mr
b
sir little do i think i should ever have occasion to make a formal complaint against a person very dear to you and who i believe deserve to be so but donot let she be so proud and so vain of oblige and please you as to make she not care how she affront every body else
the person be no other than the wife of your bosom who have take such liberty with i as ought not to be take and seek to turn my own child against i and make a dutiful girl a rebel
if people will set up for virtue and all that let them be uniformly virtuous or i would not give a farthing for their pretence
here i have be plague with gout rheumatism and nameless disorder ever since you leave we which have make i call for a little more attendance than ordinary and i have reason to think myself slight where an indulgent father can least bear to be so that is where he most love and that by young upstart who be grow up to the enjoyment of those pleasure which have run away from i fleeting rascal as they be
before i be willing to part with they
and i rung and rung and wherebe polly for i honour the slut with too much of my notice wherebe polly be all my cry to every one who come up to ask what i rung for
and at last in burst the pert baggage with an air of assurance as if she think all must be well the moment she appear with do you want i papa do i want you confidence
yes i do
where have you be these two hour that you never come near i when you know twa my time to have my foot rub which give i mortal pain for you must understand mr
b that nobody s hand s so soft as polly s
she give i a saucy answer as i be disposed to think it because i have just then a twinge that i could scarce bear for pain be a plaguy thing to a man of my lively spirit
she give i i say a careless answer and turn upon her heel and not come to i at my first word i fling a book which i have in my hand at her head
and this fine lady of your s this paragon of meekness and humility in so many word bid i or which be bad tell my own daughter to bid i never to take a book into my hand again if i willnot make a well use of it and yet what well use can an offended father make of the good book than to correct a rebellious child with they and oblige a saucy daughter to jump into her duty all at once
mrs
b
reflect upon i for make her blush formerly and say thing before my daughter that truly i ought to be ashamed of
then avow malice and revenge
why neighbour be these thing to be borne do you allow your lady to set up for a general corrector of every body s moral but your own do you allow she to condemn the only instance of wit that remain to this generation that dear polite double entendre which keep alive the attention and quicken the apprehension of the good company in the world and be the salt the sauce which give a poignancy to all our genteeler entertainment
very fine truly
that more than half the world shall be shut out of society shall be preclude their share of conversation amongst the gay and polite of both sex be your lady to have her will
let her first find people who can support a conversation with wit and good sense like her own and then something may be say but till then i positively say and will swear upon occasion that double entendre shall not be banish from our table and where this willnot raise a blush or create a laugh we will if we please for all mrs
b
and her new-fangle notion force the one and the other by still plainer hint and let she help herself how she can

and dear sir be it not a sad thing for a young lady who love and honour her papa to observe that he be discredit himself and want the example he ought to give and pardon i sir for smile on so serious an occasion but be it not a fine sight to see a gentleman as we have often see sir simon when he have think proper to read a passage in some bad book pull off his spectacle to talk filthily upon it
methink i see he now add the bold slut split his arch face with a broad laugh shew a mouth with hardly a tooth in it and make obscene remark upon what he have read and then the dear saucy-face laugh out to bear i company for i could not for the soul of i avoid laugh heartily at the figure she bring to my mind which i have see my old friend more than once make with his dismounted spectacle arch mouth and gum of shin jet succeed those of polished ivory of which he often boast as one ornament of his youthful days and i the rather in my heart sir simon give you up because when i be a sad fellow it be always my maxim to endeavour to touch a lady s heart without wound her ear
and indeed i find my account sometimes in observe it
but resume my gravity-- hussy say i do you think i will have my old friend thus make the object of your ridicule suppose a challenge should have ensue between we on your account--what might have be the issue of it
to see an old gentleman stumping as he say on crutch to fight a duel in defence of his wound honour very bad sir to be sure i see that and be sorry for it for have you carry off sir simon s crutch as a trophy he must have lain sigh and groan like a wound soldier in the field of battle till another have be bring he to have stump home with but dear sir simon i have bring this matter to an issue that will i hope make all easy miss polly and my pamela shall both be punish as they deserve if it be not your own fault
i be tell that the sin of your youth donot sit so heavily upon your limb as in your imagination and i believe change of air and the gratification of your revenge a fine help to such lively spirit as your will set you up
you shall then take coach and bring your pretty criminal to mine and when we have they together they shall humble themselves before we and you can absolve or punish they as you shall see proper
for i cannot bear to have my worthy friend insult in so heinous a manner by a couple of saucy girl who if not take down in time may proceed from fault to fault till there will be no living with they
if to be still more serious your lady and you will lend miss darnford to my pamela s wish whose heart be set upon the hope of she winter with we in town you will lay an obligation upon we both which will be acknowledge with great gratitude by dear sir your affectionate and humble servant
letter xxiv from sir simon darnford in reply hark ye mr
b a word in your ear to be plain i like neither you nor your wife well enough to trust my polly with you
but here s war declare against my poor gum it seem
well i will never open my mouth before your lady as long as i live if i can help it
i have for these ten year avoid to put on my cravat and for what reason do you think why because i could not bear to see what ruin a few year have make in a visage that use to inspire love and terror as it please
and here your--what-shall-i-call-she of a wife with all the insolence of youth and beauty on her side follow i with a glass and would make i look in it whether i will or not
ibe a plaguy good-humour old fellow--if i be an old fellow--or i should not bear the insult contain in your letter

but here i have be use to cut a joke and toss the squib about and as far as i know it have help to keep i alive in the midst of pain and ache and with two woman-grow girl and the rest of the mortification that will attend on advanced year for i willnot hang i if i will give it up as absolute old age but now it seem i must leave all this off or i must be mortify with a look glass hold before i and every wrinkle must be make as conspicuous as a furrow--and what pray be to succeed to this reformation i can neither fast nor pray i doubt and besides if my stomach and my jest depart from i farewell sir simon darnford
but cannot i pass as one necessary character do you think as a foil as by-the-bye some of your own action have be to your lady s virtue to set off some more edify example where variety of character make up a feast in conversation
well i believe i might have trust you with my daughter under your lady s eye rake as you have be yourself and fame say wrong if you have not be for your time a bold sinn than ever i be with your maxim of touching lady heart without wound their ear which make surer work with they that be all though tis to be hop you be now reform and if you be the whole country round you east west north and south owe great obligation to your fair reclaimer
but here be a fine prim young fellow come out of norfolk with one estate in one county another in another and jointure and settlement in his hand and more wit in his head as well as more money in his pocket than he can tell what to do with to visit our polly though i tell she i much question the former quality his wit if he be for marry

letter xxv my honoured and dear parent i hope you will excuse my long silence which have be owe to several cause and have have nothing new to entertain you with and yet this last be but a poor excuse to you who think every trifling subject agreeable from your daughter
i daily expect here my lord and lady davers
this give i no small pleasure and yet it be mingle with some uneasiness at time lest i should not when view so intimately near behave myself answerably to her ladyship s expectation
but i resolve not to endeavour to move out of the sphere of my own capacity in order to emulate her ladyship
she must have advantage by conversation as well as education which it would be arrogance in i to assume or to think of imitating
all that i will attempt to do therefore shall be to shew such a respectful obligingness to my lady as shall be consistent with the condition to which i be raise so that she may not have reason to reproach i of pride in my exaltation nor her dear brother to rebuke i for meanness in condescending and as to my family arrangement i be the less afraid of inspection because by the natural bias of my own mind i bless god i be above dark reserve and have not one selfish or sordid view to make i wish to avoid the most scrutinising eye
i have begin a correspondence with miss darnford a young lady of uncommon merit
but yet you know her character from my former writing
she be very solicitous to hear of all that concern i and particularly how lady davers and i agree together
i love she from the moment i see she first for she have the least pride and the most benevolence and solid thought i ever know in a young lady and do not envy any one
i shall write to she often and as i shall have so many avocation besides to fill up my time i know you will excuse i if i procure from this lady the return of my letter to she for your perusal and for the entertainment of your leisure hour
this will give you from time to time the account you desire of all that happen here
but as to what relate to our own particular i beg you will never spare writing as i shall not answer for it be one of my great delight that i have such worthy parent as i hope in god i long shall to bless i and to correspond with i
the paper i send herewith will afford you some diversion particularly those relate to sir simon darnford and i must desire that when you have peruse they as well as what i shall send for the future you will return they to i
mr
longman greatly please i on his last return in his account of your health and the satisfaction you take in your happy lot and i must recite to you a brief conversation on this occasion which i dare say will please you as much as it do i

my dear say he seat i by he and make the good old gentleman sit down for he will always rise at my approach mr
longman and i have settle in two hour some account which would have take up as many month with some person for never be there an exacter or more methodical accomptant

letter xxvi my dearest child it may not be improper to mention ourselves what the nature of the kindness be which we confer on our poor neighbour and the labour people lest it should be surmise by any body that we be lavish away wealth that be not our own
not that we fear either your honoured husband or you will suspect so or that the worthy mr
longman would insinuate as much for he see what we do and be highly pleased with it and say he would make such a report of it as you write he do
what we do be in small thing though the good we hope from they be not small perhaps and if a very distressful case should happen among our poor neighbour require any thing considerable and the object be deserve we would acquaint you with it and leave it to you to do as god should direct you
my dear child you be very happy and if it can be may you be happy still
yet i verily think you cannot be more happy than your father and mother except in this one thing that all our happiness under god proceed from you and as other parent bless their child with plenty and benefit you have bless your parent or your honoured husband rather for your sake with all the good thing this world can afford
your paper be the joy of our leisure hour and you be kind beyond all expression in take care to oblige we with they
we know how your time be take up and ought to be very well content if but now and then you let we hear of your health and welfare

we be really so enraptured with your writing that when our spirit flag through the infirmity of year which hath begin to take hold of we we have recourse to some of your papers come my dear cry i what say you to a banquet now she know what i mean
with all my heart say she
so i read although it be on a sunday so good be your letter and you must know i have copy of many and after a little while we be as much alive and brisk as if we have no nagging at all and return to the duty of the day with double delight

well but what shall i say more
and yet how shall i end only with my prayer that god will continue to you the blessing and comfort you be in possession of and pray now be not over-thoughtful about london for why should you let the dread of future evil lessen your present joys there be no absolute perfection in this life thatbe true but one would make one s self as easy as one could
tis time enough to be trouble when trouble come-- sufficient unto the day be the evil thereof rejoice then as you have often say you would in your present blessing and leave the event of thing to the supreme disposer of all event

letter xxvii my dearest miss darnford the interest i take in everything that concern you make i very importunate to know how you approve the gentleman whom some of your good friend and well-wisher have recommend to your favour
i hope he will deserve your good opinion and then he must excel most of the unmarried gentleman in england
your papa in his humourous manner mention his large possession and rich but be he as rich as croesus he should not have my consent if he have no great merit though that be what the generality of parent look out for first and indeed an easy fortune be so far from be to be disregard that when attend with equal merit i think it ought to have a preference give to it suppose affection disengage
for tis certain that a man or woman may stand as good a chance for happiness in marriage with a person of fortune as with one who have not that advantage and notwithstanding i have neither rich nor descent to boast of i must be of opinion with those who say that they never know any body despise either that have they
but to permit rich to be the principal inducement to the neglect of superior merit that be the fault which many a one smart for whether the choice be their own or impose upon they by those who have a title to their obedience
here be a saucy body might some who have not miss darnford s kind consideration for her friend be apt to say who be thus meanly descend nevertheless presume to give her opinion in these high case unasked but i have this to say that i think myself so entirely divest of partiality to my own case that as far as my judgment shall permit i will never have that in view when i be presume to hint my opinion of general rule
for most surely the honour i have receive and the debasement to which my good friend have subject himself have for their principal excuse that the gentleman be entirely independent have no question to ask and have a fortune sufficient to make himself as well as the person he choose happy though she bring he nothing at all and that he have moreover such a character for good sense and knowledge of the world that nobody could impute to he any other inducement but that of a noble resolution to reward a virtue he have so frequently and i will say so wickedly try and could not subdue
my dear miss let i as a subject very pleasing to i touch upon your kind mention of the worthy mr
peters s sentiment to that part of his conduct to i which oppress by the terror and apprehension to which i be subject once i censure and the readier as i have so great an honour for his cloth that i think to be a clergyman and all that be compassionate good and virtuous be the same thing
but when i come to know mr
peters i have a high opinion of his worthiness and as no one can be perfect in this life thus i think to myself how hard be then my lot to be the cause of stumble to so worthy a heart
to be sure a gentleman one who know and practise so well his duty in every other instance and preach it so efficaciously to other must have be one day sensible that it would not have mis-become his function and character to have afford that protection to oppress innocence which be request of he and how would it have grieve his considerate mind have my ruin be complete that he do not
but as he have once a namesake as one may say that fail in a much great instance let not my want of charity exceed his fault but let i look upon it as an infirmity to which the most perfect be liable i be a stranger to he a servant girl carry off by her master a young gentleman of violent and lawless passion who in this very instance shew how much in earnest he be set upon effecting all his vile purpose and whose heart although god might touch it be not probable any less influence could
then he be not sure that though he might assist my escape i might not afterwards fall again into the hand of so determine a violator and that difficulty would not with such an one enhance his resolution to overcome all obstacle
moreover he might think that the person who be move he to this worthy measure possibly seek to gratify a view of his own and that while endeavour to save to outward appearance a virtue in danger he be in reality only help another to a wife at the hazard of expose himself to the vindictiveness of a violent temper and a rich neighbour who have power as well as will to resent for such be his apprehension entirely groundless as it be though not improbable as it might seem to he
for all these consideration i must pity rather than too rigorously censure the worthy gentleman and i will always respect he
and thank he a thousand time my dear in my name for his goodness in condescend to acknowledge by your hand his infirmity as such for this give an excellent proof of the natural worthiness of his heart and that it be beneath he to seek to extenuate a fault when he think he have commit one
indeed my dear friend i have so much honour for the clergy of all degree that i never forget in my prayer one article that god will make they shin light to the world since so much depend on their ministry and example as well with respect to our public as private duty
nor shall the fault of a few make impression upon i to the disadvantage of the order for i be afraid a very censorious temper in this respect be too generally the indication of an uncharitable and perhaps a profligate heart level character in order to cover some inward pride or secret enormity which they be ashamed to avow and will not be instruct to amend
forgive my dear this tedious scribble i cannot for my life write short letter to those i love
and let i hope that you will favour i with an account of your new affair and how you proceed in it and with such of your conversation as may give i some notion of a polite courtship

letter xxix my dear miss darnford at your desire and to oblige your honour mamma and your good neighbour i will now acquaint you with the arrival of lady davers and will occasionally write what pass among we i will not say worthy of notice for be i only to do so i should be more brief perhaps by much than you seem to expect

so i go to france a second time and pass a year there in the good of company and with some improvement both to my moral and understanding and have a very few sally consider my love of intrigue and the ample mean i have to prosecute successfully all the desire of my heart

she use in vain therefore to plead family reason to me like most young fellow i be too much a self-lover to pay so great a regard to posterity and to say truth have little solicitude at that time whether my name be continued or not in my own descendant
however i look upon my mother s pamela with no small pleasure and i find she so much improve as well in person as behaviour that i have the less inducement either to renew my intriguing life or to think of a married state
yet as my mother have all her eye about she as the phrase be i affect great shyness both before she and to the girl for i doubt not my very look would be watch by they both and what the one discover would not be a secret to the other and lay myself open too early to a suspicion i think would but ice the girl over and make her lady more watchful
so i use to go into my mother s apartment and come out of it without take the least notice of she but put on stiff air and as she always withdraw when i come in i never make any pretence to keep she there

madam my eye sparkle at such a girl as that

she may be your favourite as a waiting-maid but i see nothing but clumsy curtsey and awkward air about she
a little rustic affectation of innocence that to such as cannot see into she may pass well enough nay my dear reply my mother donot say that of all thing
she have no affectation i be sure yes she have in my eye madam and iwill tell you how it be you have teach she to assume the air of a gentlewoman to dance and to enter a room with a grace and yet bid she keep her low birth and family in view and between the one character which she want to get into and the other she dare not get out of she trip up and down mincingly and know not how to set her foot so tis the same in every gesture her arm she know not whether to swim with or to hold before she nor whether to hold her head up or down and so do neither but hang it on one side a little awkward piece of one-and-t other i think she
and indeed youwould do the girl more kindness to put she into your dairy than to keep she about your person for shewill be utterly spoil i doubt for any useful purpose ah son say she i fear by your description you have mind she too much in one sense though not enough in another
tis not my intention to recommend she to your notice of all man and i doubt not if it please god i live and she continue a good girl but she will make a man of some middling genteel business very happy pamela come in just then with an air so natural so humble and yet so much above herself that i be force to turn my head from she lest my mother should watch my eye again and i be inclined to do she that justice which my heart assent to but which my lip have just before deny she
all my difficulty in apprehension be my good mother the effect of whose lesson to her girl i be not so much afraid of as her vigilance
for think i i see by the delicacy of her person the brilliancy of her eye and the sweet apprehensiveness that play about every feature of her face she must have tinder enough in her constitution to catch a well-strike spark and iwill warrant i shall know how to set she in a blaze in a few month more yet i want as i pass to catch her attention too i expect she to turn after i and look so as to shew a liking towards i for i have a great opinion of my person and air which have be fortunately distinguish by the lady whom of course my vanity make i allow to be very good judge of these outward advantage
but to my great disappointment pamela never by any favourable glance give the least encouragement to my vanity
well think i this girl have certainly nothing ethereal in her mould all unanimated clay but the dancing and sing air my mother be teach she will well qualify she in time and another year will ripen she into my arm no doubt of it
let i only go on thus and make she fear i that will enhance in her mind every favour i shall afterwards vouchsafe to shew she and never question old humdrum virtue think i but the tempter without and the tempter within will be too many for the perverse nicety that ever the sex boast yet though i could not once attract her eye towards i she never fail to draw mine after she whenever she go by i or wherever i see she except as i say in my mother s presence and particularly when she have pass i and could not see i look at she without turn her head as i expect so often from she in vain
you will wonder lord davers who i suppose be once in love or youwould never have marry such an hostile spirit as my sister s there- go on sauce--box say she i willnot interrupt you you will wonder how i could behave so coolly as to escape all discovery so long from a lady so watchful as my mother and from the apprehensiveness of the girl
but to say nothing of her tender year and that my love be not of this bashful sort i be not absolutely determined so great be my pride that i ought to think she worthy of be my mistress when i have not much reason as i think to despair of prevail upon person of high birth be i disposed to try to live with i upon my own term
my pride therefore keep my passion at bay as i may say so far be i from imagine i should ever be bring to what have since happen
but to proceed hitherto my mind be take up with the beauty of her person only
my eye have draw my heart after it without give myself any trouble about that sense and judgment which my mother be always praise in her pamela as exceed her year and opportunity but an occasion happen which though slight in itself take the head into the party and i think of she young as she be with a distinction that before i have not for she
it be this be with my mother in her closet who be talk to i on the old subject matrimony i see pamela s commonplace book as i may call it in which by her lady s direction from time to time she have transcribe from the bible and other good book such passage as most impress she as she read--a method i take it my dear turn to i of great service to you as it initiate you into write with that freedom and ease which shine in your saucy letter and journal and to which my present fetter be not a little owe just as pedlar catch monkey in the baboon kingdom provoke the attentive fool by their own example to put on shoe and stocking till the ape of imitation try to do the like entangle their foot and so cannot escape upon the bough of the tree of liberty on which before they be willnot to hop and skip about and play a thousand puggish trick
i observe the girl write a pretty hand and very swift and free and affix her point or stop with so much judgment she year consider that i begin to have an high opinion of her understanding
some observation likewise upon several of the passage be so just and solid that i could not help be tacitly surprised at they
my mother watch my eye and be silent i seem not to observe that she do and after a while lay down the book shut it with great indifference and talk of another subject
upon this my mother say donot you think pamela write a pretty hand son i do not mind it much say i with a careless air
this be her writing be it take the book and open it again at a place of scripture
the girl be mighty pious say i
i wish you be so child i wish so too madam if it would please you i wish so for your own sake child so do i madam and down i lay the book again very carelessly
look once more in it say she and see if you cannot open it upon some place that may strike you i open it at-- train up a child in the way it should go c
i fancy say i when i be of pamela s age i be pretty near as good as she never never say my mother i be sure i take great pain with you but alas i to very little purpose
you have always a violent headstrong will some allowance for boy and girl i hope madam but you see i be as good for a man as my sister for a woman no indeed you be not i do assure you i be sorry for that
madam you give i a sad opinion of myself brazen wretch say my lady but go on turn to one of the girl s observation on some text say my mother
i do and be pleased with it more than i would own
the girl s well enough say i for what she be but letus see what shewill be a few year hence
then will be the trial shewill be always good i doubt not so much the well for she
but cannot we talk of any other subject
you complain how seldom i attend you and when you be always talk of matrimony or of this low-bear raw girl it must need lessen the pleasure of approach you but now as i hint to you lady and my lord i have a still high opinion of pamela and esteem she more worthy of my attempt
for think i the girl have good sense and it will be some pleasure to watch by what gradation she may be make to rise into love and into a high life than that to which she be bear and so i begin to think she would be worthy in time of be my mistress which till now as i say before i have be a little scrupulous about
i take a little tour soon after this in company of some friend with whom i have contract an intimacy abroad into scotland and ireland they have a curiosity to see those country and we spend six or eight month on this expedition and when i have land they in france i return home and find my good mother in a very indifferent state of health but her pamela arrive to a height of beauty and perfection which exceed all my expectation
i be so take with her charm when i first see she which be in the garden with a book in her hand just come out of a little summer-house that i then think of oblige she to go back again in order to begin a parley with she but while i be resolve she tript away with her curtesie and reverence and be out of my sight before i could determine
i be resolve however not to be long without she and mrs
jewkes have be recommend to i a little before by a brother-rake as a woman of try fidelity i ask she if she would be faithful if i have occasion to commit a pretty girl to her care
she hop she say it would be with the lady s own consent and she should make no scruple in obey i
so i think i would way-lie the girl and carry she first to a little village in northamptonshire to an acquaintance of mrs
jewkes s
and when i have bring she to be easy and pacify a little i design that jewkes should attend she to lincolnshire for i know there be no come at she here under my mother s wing by her own consent and that to offer term to she would be to blow up my project all at once
besides i be sensible that mrs
jervis would stand in the way of my proceeding as well as my mother
the method i have contrive be quite easy as i imagine and such as could not have fail to answer my purpose as to carry she off and i doubt not of make she well satisfied in her good fortune very quickly for have a notion of her affectionate duty to her parent i be not displeased that i could make the term very easy and happy to they all
what most stand in my way be my mother s fondness for she but supposing i have get she favourite in my hand which appear to i as i say a task very easy to be conquer i have actually form a letter for she to transcribe acknowledge a love-affair and lay she withdraw herself so privately to an implicit obedience to her husband s command to whom she be married that morning and who be a young gentleman of genteel family and dependent on his friend be desirous of keep it all a profound secret and beg on that account her lady not to divulge it so much as to mrs
jervis
and to prepare for this and make she escape the more probable when matter be ripe for my plot i come in one night and examine all the servant and mrs
jervis the latter in my mother s hearing about a genteel young man whom i pretend to find with a pillion on the horse he ride upon wait about the back door of the garden for somebody to come to he and who ride off when i come up to the door as fast as he could
nobody know any thing of the matter and they be much surprised at what i tell they but i beg pamela might be watch and that no one would say any thing to she about it
my mother say she have two reason not to speak of it to pamela one to oblige i the other and chief because it would break the poor innocent girl s heart to be suspect
poor dear child say she whither can she go to be so happy as with i
would it not be inevitable ruin to she to leave i
there be nobody come after she she receive no letter but now-and-then one from her father and mother and those she shews i well reply i i hope she can have no design twould be strange if she have form any to leave so good a mistress but you cannot be sure all the letter she receive be from her father and her shew to you those he write look like a cloak to other she may receive from another hand
but it can be no harm to have an eye upon she
you donot know madam what trick there be in the world not i indeed but only this i know that the girl shall be under no restraint if she be resolve to leave i well as i love she mrs
jervis say she would have an eye upon pamela in obedience to my command but she be sure there be no need nor would she so much wound the poor child s peace as to mention the matter to she
this i suffer to blow off and seem to my mother to have so good an opinion of her pamela that i be sorry as i tell she i have such a surmise say that though the fellow and the pillion be odd circumstance yet i dare to say there be nothing in it for i doubt not the girl s duty and gratitude would hinder she from do a foolish or rash thing
this my mother hear with pleasure although my motive be but to lay pamela on the thick to she when she be to be tell she have escape
she be glad i be not an enemy to the poor child
pamela have no friend but i continue she and if i donot provide for she i shall have do she more harm than good as you and your aunt b
have often say in the accomplishment i have give she and yet the poor girl i see that add she would not be backward to turn her hand to any thing for the sake of an honest livelihood be she put to it which if it please god to spare i and she continue good she never shall be i wonder not pamela at your tear on this occasion
your lady be an excellent woman and deserve this tribute to her memory
all my pleasure now be that she know not half my wicked prank and that i do not vex her worthy heart in the prosecution of this scheme which would have give i a severe sting inasmuch as i might have apprehend with too much reason that i have shorten her day by the knowledge of the one and the other
i have thus every thing ready for the execution of my project but my mother s ill state of health give i too much concern to permit i to proceed
and now-and-then as my frequent attendance in her illness give i an opportunity of observe more and more of the girl her affectionate duty and continual tear find she often on her knee pray for her mistress i be move to pity she and while those scene of my mother s illness and decline be before i i would resolve to conquer if possible my guilty passion as those scene teach i while their impression hold justly to call it and i be much concerned to find it so difficult a task for till now i think it principally owe to my usual enterprise temper and a love of intrigue and that i have nothing to do but to resolve against it and to subdue it
but i be greatly mistake for i have insensibly bring myself to admire she in every thing she say or do and there be so much gracefulness humility and innocence in her whole behaviour and i see so many melt scene between her lady and she that i find i could not master my esteem for she
my mother s illness increase beyond hope of recovery and have settle all her great affair she talk to i of her servant i ask what she would have do for pamela and mrs
jervis
make mrs
jervis my dear son as happy as you can she be a gentlewoman bear you know let she always be treat as such but for your own sake donot make her independent for then youwill want a faithful manager
yet if you marry and your lady should not value she as she deserve allow she a competency for the rest of her life and let she live as she please
as for pamela i hope you will be her protector she be a good girl i love she next to you and your dear sister
she be just arrive at a try time of life
i donot know what to say for she
what i have design be that if any man of a genteel calling should offer i would give she a little pretty portion have god spar my life till then
but be she make independent some idle fellow might snap she up for she be very pretty or if she should carry what you give she to her poor parent as her duty would lead she to do they be so unhappily involve that a little matter would be nothing to they and the poor girl might be to seek again
perhaps lady davers will take she
but i wish she be not so pretty
she may be the bird for which some wicked fowler will spread his snare or it may be every lady will not choose to have such a waiting-maid
you be a young gentleman and i be sorry to say not well than i wish you to be--though i hope my pamela would not be in danger from her master who owe all his servant protection as much as the king do to his subject
yet i donot know how to wish she to stay with you for your own reputation s sake my dear son for the world will censure as it lists would to god say she the dear girl have the small-pox in a mortifying manner shewould be lovely though in the genteelness of her person and the excellency of her mind and more out of danger of suffer from the transcient beauty of countenance
yet i think add she she might be safe and happy under mrs
