jervis let them go together and live as they like
i think that will be the good for both
and you have a generous spirit enough i will not direct you in the quantum
but my dear son remember that i be the less concerned that i have not do for the poor girl myself because i depend upon you the manner how fitly to provide for she have make i defer it till now that i have so much more important concern on my hand life and strength ebb so fast that i be hardly fit for any thing or to wish for any thing but to receive the last releasing stroke here he stop be under some concern himself and we in much more
at last he resume the subject
you will too naturally think my lord--and you my good lady--that the mind must be truly diabolical that could break through the regard due to the solemn injunction of a die parent
they do hold i a good while indeed and as fast as i find any emotion of a contrary nature rise in my breast i endeavour for some time to suppress they and to think and act as i ought but the dear bewitching girl every day rise in her charm upon i and find she still continue the use of her pen and ink i could not help entertain a jealousy that she be write to somebody who stand well in her opinion and my love for she and my own spirit of intrigue make it a sweetheart of course
and i could not help watch her emotion and see she once put a letter she have just fold up into her bosom at my entrance into my mother s dressing-room i make no doubt of detect she and her correspondent and so i take the letter from her stay she tremble and curtsey with a sweet confusion and highly pleased i be to find it contain only innocence and duty to the deceased mistress and the love parent express her joy that in the midst of her grief for lose the one she be not oblige to return to be a burden to the other and i give it she again with word of encouragement and go down much well satisfied than i have be with her correspondence
but when i reflect upon the innocent simplicity of her style i be still more in love with she and form a stratagem and succeed in it to come at her other letter which i send forward after i have read they all but three or four which i keep back when my plot begin to ripen for execution although the little slut be most abominably free with my character to her parent
you will censure i no doubt that my mother s injunction make not a more lasting impression
but really i struggle hard with myself to give they their due force and the dear girl as i say every day grow lovelier and more accomplished
her letter be but so many link to the chain in which she have bind i and though once i have resolve to part with she to lady davers and you madam have an intention to take she i could not for my life give she up and think more honourably then of the state of a mistress than i have do since i could not persuade myself since i intend to do as handsomely by she as ever man do to a lady in that situation but that i should do well for she than my mother have wish i to do and so more than answer all her injunction as to the providing for she and i could not imagine i should meet with a resistance i have seldom encounter from person much her superior as to descent and be amazed at it for it confound i in all the notion i have of her sex which like a true libertine i suppose want nothing but importunity and opportunity a bold attempter and a mind not ungenerous
sometimes i admire she for her virtue at other time impetuous in my temper and unused to control i could have beat she
she well i remember describe the tumult of my soul repeat what once pass between we in word like these take the little witch from i mrs
jervis i can neither bear nor forbear she--but stay-you shallnot go--yet be gone no come back again she think i be mad she say in her paper
indeed i be little less
she say i take her arm and grip it black and blue to bring she back again and then sit down and look at she as silly as such a poor girl as she well do she describe the passion i struggle with and no one can conceive how much my pride make i despise myself at time for the little action my love for she put i upon and yet to find that love increase every day as her charm and her resistance increased i have catch myself in a rag fit sometimes vow i would have she and at other jealous that to secure herself from my attempt she would throw herself into the arm of some menial or inferior whom otherwise she would not have think of
sometimes i soothe sometimes threaten she but never be such courage when her virtue seem in danger mix with so much humility when her fear give way to her hope of a juster treatment then i would think it impossible so slight an opinion have i of woman s virtue that such a girl as this cottage-bear who owe every thing to my family and have an absolute dependence upon my pleasure myself not despicable in person or mind as i suppose she unprejudice in any man s favour at an age susceptible of impression and a frame and constitution not ice or snow surely think i all this frost must be owe to the want of fire in my attempt to thaw it i use to dare more and succeed well
shall such a girl as this awe i by her rigid virtue
no she shall not then i would resolve to be more in earnest
yet my love be a traitor that be more faithful to she than to i it have more honour in it at bottom than i have design
awe by her unaffected innocence and a virtue i have never before encounter so uniform and immovable the moment i see her i be half disarm and i court her consent to that which though i be not likely to obtain yet it go against i to think of extorting by violence
yet marriage be never in my thought i scorn so much as to promise it
to what numberless mean thing do not this unmanly passion subject me i use to watch for her letter though mere prittle-prattle and chit-chat receive they with delight though myself be accuse in they and stigmatize as i deserve
i would listen meanly at her chamber-door try to overhear her little conversation in vain attempt to suborn mrs
jervis to my purpose inconsistently talk of honour when no one step i take or action i attempt shew any thing like it lose my dignity among my servant make a party in her favour against i of every body but whom my money corrupt and that hardly sufficient to keep my partisan steady to my interest so greatly do the virtue of the servant triumph over the vice of the master when confirm by such an example
i have be very tedious lady and my lord davers in my narration but i be come within view of the point for which i now be upon my trial at your dread tribunal bow to we all
after several endeavour of a smooth and rough nature in which my devil constantly fail i and her good angel prevail i have talk to mrs
jervis to seduce the girl to whom in hope of frighten she i have give warning but which she reject to take to my great disappointment to desire to stay and suspect mrs
jervis play me booty and rather confirm she in her coyness and her desire of leave i i be mean enough to conceal myself in the closet in mrs
jervis s room in order to hear their private conversation but really not design to make any other use of my concealment than to tease she a little if she should say any thing i do not like which would give i a pretence to treat she with great freedom than i have ever yet do and would be an introduction to take off from her unprecedented apprehensiveness another time
but the dear prattler not know i be there as she undress herself begin such a bewitching chit-chat with mrs
jervis who i find but ill keep my secret that i never be at such a loss what to resolve upon
one while i wish myself unknown to they out of the closet into which my inconsiderate passion have meanly lead i another time i be incense at the freedom with which i hear myself treat but then rigidly consider that i have no business to hearken to their private conversation and it be such as become they while i ought to have be ashamed to give occasion for it i excuse they both and admire still more and more the dear prattler
in this suspense the undesigned rustling of my night-gown from change my posture alarm the watchful pamela she in a fright come towards the closet to see who be there
what could i then do but bolt out upon the apprehensive charmer and have so do and she run to the bed scream to mrs
jervis would not any man have follow her thither detect as i be
but yet i say if she forbear she scream i would do she no harm but if not she should take the consequence
i find by their exclamation that this would pass with both for an attempt of the bad kind but really i have no such intention as they fear
when i find myself detect when the dear frightened girl run to the bed when mrs
jervis throw herself about she when they would not give over their hideous squalling when i be charge by mrs
jervis with the bad design it be enough to make i go far than i design and could i have prevail upon mrs
jervis to go up and quiet the maid who seem to be rise upon the other screaming i believe have pamela keep out of her fit i should have be a little free with she than ever i have be but as it be i have no thought but of make as honourable a retreat as i could and to save myself from be expose to my whole family and i be not guilty of any freedom that her modesty unaffrighted could reproach herself with have suffer and the dear creature s fainting fit give i almost as great apprehension as i could give she
thus lady--and my lord--have i tediously and little enough to my own reputation give you my character and tell you more against myself than any one person could accuse i of
whatever redound to the credit of my pamela redound in part to my own and so i have the less regret to accuse myself since it exalt she
but as to a form intention to hide myself in the closet in order to attempt the girl by violence and in the presence of a good woman as mrs
jervis be which you impute to i bad as i be i be not so vile so abandon as that
love as i say before subject its inconsiderate votary to innumerable meanness and unlawful passion to many more
i could not live without this dear girl
i hat the thought of matrimony with any body and to be bring to the state by my mother s waiting-maid forbid it pride think i forbid it example
forbid it all my past sneer and constant ridicule both on the estate and on those who descend to inequality in it
and lastly forbid it my family spirit so visible in lady davers as well as in myself to whose insult and those of all the world i shall be obnoxious if i take such a step all this tend to demonstrate the strength of my passion i could not conquer my love so i conquer a pride which every one thought unconquerable and since i could not make an innocent heart vicious i have the happiness to follow so good an example and by this mean a vicious heart be become virtuous
i have the pleasure of rejoice in the change and hope i shall do so still more and more for i really view with contempt my past folly and it be now a great wonder to i how i could act as i do than that i should detest those action which make i a curse instead of a benefit to society
i be not yet so pious as my pamela but that be to come and it be one good sign that i can truly say i delight in every instance of her piety and virtue and now i will conclude my tedious narration thus he end his affect relation which in the course of it give i a thousand different emotion and make i often pray for he that god will entirely convert a heart so generous and worthy as his be on most occasion
and if i can but find he not deviate when we go to london i shall greatly hope that nothing will affect his moral again
i have just read over again the forego account of himself
as near as i remember and my memory be the good faculty i have it be pretty exact only he be full of beautiful similitude and speak in a more flowery style as i may say
yet donot you think miss if i have not do injustice to his spirit that the beginning of it especially be in the saucy air of a man too much alive to such notion
for so the lady observe in his narration is it very like the style of a true penitent but indeed he go on well and conclude good of all
but donot you observe what a dear good lady i have
a thousand blessing on her beloved memory
be i to live to see my child s child they should be all teach to lisp her praise before they could speak
my gratitude should always be renew in their mouth and god and my dear father and mother my lady and my master that be my good friend that be but principally as most due the first who inspire all the rest should have their morning their noontide and their evening praise as long as i live
i will only observe far as to this my third conversation-piece that my lord davers offer to extenuate some part of his dear brother-in-law s conduct which he do not himself vindicate and mr
b
be pleased to say that my lord be always very candid to he and kind in his allowance for the sally of ungovernable youth
upon which my lady say a little tartly yes and for a very good reason i doubt not for who care to condemn himself nay say my lord pleasantly donot put we upon a foot neither for what sally i make before i know your ladyship be but like those of a fox which now and then run away with a straggling pullet when nobody see he whereas those of my brother be like the invasion of a lion break into every man s fold and drive the shepherd as well as the sheep before he ay say my lady but i can look round i and have reason perhaps to think the invade lion have come off little as he deserve it well than the creep fox who with all his cunning sometimes suffer for his pilfering theft o my dear these gentleman be strange creatures what can they think of themselves
for they say there be not one virtuous man in five but i hope for our sex s sake as well as for the world s sake all be not true that evil fame report for you know every man-trespasser must find or make a woman-trespasser and if so what a world be this and how must the innocent suffer from the guilty
yet how much well be it to suffer one s self than to be the cause of another s suffering
i long to hear of you and must shorten my future account or i shall do nothing but write and tire you into the bargain though i cannot my dear father and mother
i be my dear miss always your p b
letter xxxi from miss darnford to mrs
b dear mrs
b every post you more and more oblige we to admire and love you and let i say i will gladly receive your letter upon your own term only when your worthy parent have peruse they see that i have every line of they again
your account of the arrival of your noble guest and their behaviour to you and your to they your conversation and wise determination on the offer title of baronet the just applause confer upon you by all particularly the good countess your breakfast conversation and the narrative of your saucy abominable master though amiable husband all delight we beyond expression
do go on dear excellent lady with your charming journal and let we know all that pass
as to the state of matter with we i have desire my papa to allow i to decline mr
murray s address
the good man love i most violently nay he could not live without i life be no life unless i favour he but yet after a few more of these flight he be try to sit down satisfied without my papa s foolish perverse girl as sir simon call i and to transpose his affection to a worthy object my sister nancy and it would make you smile to see how a little while before he directly apply to she she screw up her mouth to my mamma and truly shewould have none of polly s leaving no not she but no soon do he declare himself in form than the gaudy wretch as he be before with she become a well-dress gentleman the chatter magpie for he talk and laugh much quite conversable and have something agreeable to say upon every subject
once he would make a good master of the buck-hound but now really the more one be in his company the more polite one find he
then on his part he happen to see miss polly first and truly he could have think himself very happy in so agreeable a young lady yet there be always something of majesty what a stately name for ill nature in miss nancy something so awful that while miss polly engage the affection at first sight miss nancy strike a man with reverence insomuch that the one might he love as a woman but the other revere as something more a goddess no doubt
i do but think that when he come to be lift up to her celestial sphere as her fellow constellation what a figure nancy and her ursus major will make together and how will they glitter and shine to the wonder of all beholder
then she must make a bright appearance by far and a more pleasing one too for why
she have three thousand satellite or little star in her train more than poor polly can pretend to
willnot there be a fine twinkling and sparkle think you when the great and less bear-star be join together
but excuse i dear mrs
b this saucy girl have vex i just now by her ill-natured trick and i be even with she have thus vent my spite though she know nothing of the matter
so fancy you see polly darnford abandon by her own fault her papa angry at she her mamma pity she and call her silly girl mr
murray who be a rough lover growl over his mistress as a dog over a bone he fear to lose miss nancy put on her prudish pleasantry snarl out a kind word and break through her sullen gloom for a smile now and then in return and i laugh at both in my sleeve and think i shall soon get leave to attend you in town which will be well than twenty humble servant of mr
murray s cast or if i cannot that i shall have the pleasure of your correspondence here and enjoy unrivalle the favour of my dear parent which this ill-temper girl be always envy i
forgive all this nonsense
i be willing to write something though bad than nothing to shew how desirous i be to oblige you have i a capacity or subject as you have
but nobody can love you well or admire you more of this you may be assure however unequal in all other respect than your polly darnford
i send you up some of your paper for the good couple in kent
pray pay my respect to they and beg theywill let i have them again as soon as they can by your conveyance
our stamford friend desire their kind respect they mention you with delight in every letter
letter xxxii the journal continue thursday friday evening
my dear miss darnford i be return from a very busy day have have no less than fourteen of our neighbour gentleman and lady to dinner the occasion principally to welcome our noble guest into these part mr
b
have as i mention before turn the intended visit into an entertainment after his usual generous manner he and lord davers be go part of the way with they home and lord jackey mount with his favourite colbrand as an escort to the countess and lady davers who be take an airing in the chariot
they offer to take the coach if i would have go but be fatigue i desire to be excuse
so i retire to my closet and miss damford who be seldom out of my thought come into my mind i have a new recruit of spirit which enable i to resume my pen and thus i proceed with my journal
our company be the earl and countess of d who be so fashionable a married couple that the earl make it his boast and his countess bore it like one accustom to such treatment that he have not be in his lady s company an hour abroad before for seven year
you know his lordship s character every body do and there be not a bad as report say in the peerage
sir thomas atkyns a single gentleman not a little finical and ceremonious and a mighty beau though of the tawdry sort and affect foreign air as if he be afraid it would not be judge by any other mark that he have travel
mr
arthur and his lady a moderately happy couple who seem always when together to behave as if upon a compromise that be that each should take it in turn to say free thing of the other though some of their freedom be of so cut a nature that it look as if they intend to divert the company at their own expense
the lady be of a noble family strive to let every one know that she value herself not a little upon that advantage but otherwise have many good quality
mr
brooks and his lady
he be a free joker on serious subject but a good-natured man and say sprightly thing with no ill grace the lady a little reserved and haughty though to-day be free than usual as be observe at table by lady towers who be a maiden lady of family note for her wit and repartee and who say many good thing with so little doubt and really so good a grace that one cannot help be pleased with she
this lady be generally gallante by mr
martin of the grove so call to distinguish he from a rich citizen of that name settle in these part but be covetous and proud be seldom admit among the gentry in their visit or party of pleasure
mr
dormer one of a very courteous demeanour a widower be another who always speak well of his deceased lady and of all the sex for her sake
mr
chapman and his lady a well-behave couple not ashamed to be very tender and observe to each other but without that censurable fondness which sit so ill upon some married folk in company
then there be the dean our good minister whom i name last because i would close with one of the worthy and his daughter who come to supply her mamma s place who be indispose a well-behave prudent young lady
and here be our fourteen guest
the countess of c lord and lady davers mr

and your humble servant make up the rest of the company

a good deal of sprightly wit however fly about between the earl of d lady towers and mr
martin in which that lord suffer as he deserve for he be no match for the lady especially as the presence of the dean be a very visible restraint upon he and mr

saturday morning the countess be a little indisposed
lady davers and i take an airing this morning in the chariot and have a long discourse together
her ladyship be pleased to express great favour and tenderness towards i give i much good advice as to the care she would have i take of myself and tell i that her hop as well as her brother s all centre in my welfare and that the way i be in make her love i well and well
she be pleased to tell i how much she approve of the domestic management and to say that she never see such regularity and method in any family in her life where be the like number of servant every one she say know their duty and do it without speak to in such silence and with so much apparent cheerfulness and delight without the least hurry or confusion that it be her surprise and admiration but kindly would have it that i take too much care upon i
yet say she i donot see but you be always fresh and lively and never seem tired or fatigue and be always dressed and easy so that no company find you unprepared or unfit to receive they come when they will whether it be to breakfast or dinner i tell her ladyship i owe all this and most of the conduct for which she be pleased to praise i to her dear brother who at the beginning of my happiness give i several caution and instruction for my behaviour which have be the rule of my conduct ever since and i hop ever would be to say nothing add i which yet would be very unjust of the assistance i receive from worthy mrs

i be in some pain miss for to-morrow because of the rule we observe of late in our family on sundays and of go through a crowd to church which will afford new scene to our noble visitor either for censure or otherwise but i will soon be censure for do what i think my duty than for the want of it and so will omit nothing that we have be accustom to do

some order surely become the head of family and besides it would be discredit one s own practice if one do not appear at one time what one do at another
for that which be a reason for discontinue a practice for some company would seem to be a reason for lay it aside for ever especially in a family visit and visit as ours
and i remember well a hint give i by my dear friend once on another subject that it be in every one s power to prescribe rule to himself after a while and person to see what be one s way and that one be not to be put out of it
but my only doubt be that to lady who have not be accustom perhaps to the necessary strictness i should make myself censurable as if i aim at too much perfection for however one s duty be one s duty and ought not to be dispense with yet when a person who use to be remiss see so hard a task before they and so many great point to get over all to be no more than tolerably regular it be rather apt to frighten and discourage than to allure and one must proceed as i have read soldier do in a difficult siege inch by inch and be more studious to entrench and fortify themselves as they go on gain upon the enemy than by rush all at once upon an attack of the place be repulse and perhaps oblige with great loss to abandon a hopeful enterprise
and permit i to add that young as i be i have often observe that over-great strictness all at once enjoin and insist upon be not fit for a beginning reformation but for strong christians only and therefore generally do more harm than good
but shall i not be too grave my dear friend excuse i for this be saturday night and as it be a very good method which the ingenious author of the spectator take generally to treat their more serious subject on this day so i think one should when one can consider it as the preparative eve to a still well

the worthy dean at my request and my beloved s permission recommend to i as a sort of family chaplain for sundays a young gentleman of great sobriety and piety and sound principle who have but lately take order have at present no other provision
and this gentleman come and read prayer to we about seven in the morning in the less hall as we call it a retired apartment next the little garden for we have no chapel with we here as in your neighbourhood and this generally with some suitable exhortation or meditation out of some good book which he be so kind as to let i choose now-and-then when i please take up little more than half an hour

no surely for it would render a great prudence on my humble part necessary and a nearer inspection and great frugality on his own and he must be content if he do not as now perhaps lie up every year so long as he live within his income and who will say that the obligation to great prudence and economy be a misfortune
the competency therefore the golden mean be the thing and i have often consider the matter and endeavour to square my action by the result of that consideration
for a person who be not bear to an estate be not satisfied with a competency will probably know no limit to his desire
one whom an acquisition of one or two hundred pound a year will not satisfy will hardly sit down content with any sum
for although he may propose to himself at a distance that such and such an acquisition will be the height of his ambition yet he will as he approach to that advance upon himself far and far and know no bound till the natural one be force upon he and his life and his view end together

letter xxxiii my dear miss darnford i now proceed with my journal which i bring down to tuesday evening and of course i begin with wednesday
towards evening come sir jacob swynford on horseback attend by two servant in livery
i be abroad for i have get leave for a whole afternoon attend by my polly which time i pass in visit no less than four poor sick family whose heart i make glad
but i should be too tedious be i to give you the particular besides i have a brief list of case which when youwill favour i with your company i may shew you for i oblige myself though not desire to keep an account of what i do with no less than two hundred pound a year that mr
b
allow i to expend in act of charity and benevolence
lady davers tell i afterwards that sir jacob carry it mighty stiff and formal when he alight
he strut about the court-yard in his boot with his whip in his hand and though her ladyship go to the great door in order to welcome he he turn short and whistle follow the groom into the stable as if he have be at an inn only instead of take off his hat pull its broad brim over his eye for a compliment

he be about sixty-five year of age a coarse strong big-boned man with large irregular feature he have a haughty supercilious look a swaggering gait and a person not at all bespeak one s favour in behalf of his mind and his mind as you shall hear by and bye not clear up those prepossession in his disfavour with which his person and feature at first strike one
his voice be big and surly his eye little and fiery his mouth large with yellow and blackish tooth what be leave of they be break off to a tolerable regular height look as if they be grind down to his gum by constant use
but with all these imperfection he have an air that set he somewhat above the mere vulgar and make one think half his disadvantage rather owe to his own haughty humour than to nature for he seem to be a perfect tyrant at first sight a man use to prescribe and not to be prescribe to and have the advantage of a shrewd penetrate look but which seem rather acquire than natural

for instance in order to lay claim to the excuse which my conduct if i may suppose it of force enough to do either good or hurt will furnish it be necessary that the object of their wish should be a girl of exquisite beauty and that not only in their own blind and partial judgment but in the opinion of every one who see she friend or foe in order to justify the force which the first attraction have upon he that she be descend of honest and conscientious though poor and obscure parent who have preserve their integrity through great trial and affliction have by their example as well as precept lay deep in the girl s mind the foundation of piety and virtue
it be necessary that to the charm of person this waiting-maid should have an humble teachable mind fine natural part a sprightly yet inoffensive wit a temper so excellent and a judgment so solid as should promise by the love and esteem these quality should attract to herself from her fellow-servant superior and inferior that she would become a high station and be respect in it and that after so good a foundation lay by her parent she should have all the advantage of female education confer upon she the example of an excellent lady improve and build upon so worthy a foundation a capacity surprisingly ready to take in all that be teach she an attention assiduity and diligence almost peculiar to herself at her time of life so as at fifteen or sixteen year of age to be able to vie with any young lady of rank as well in the natural genteelness of her person as in her acquirement and that in nothing but her humility she should manifest any difference between herself and the high-bear
it will be necessary moreover that she should have a mind above temptation that she should resist the offer and menace of one upon whom all her worldly happiness seem to depend the son of a lady to whom she owe the great obligation a person whom she do not hate but greatly fear and whom her grateful heart would have be glad to oblige and who seek to prevail over her virtue by all the inducement that could be think of to attract a young unexperienced virgin at one time or to frighten she at another into his purpose who offer she very high term her circumstance consider as well for herself as for parent she love well than herself whose circumstance be low and distressful yet to all these offer and menace that she should be able to answer in such word as these which will always dwell upon my memory-- i reject your proposal with all my soul

i give it most willingly you may believe

she look almost like a great glutton whom i remember one sir jonathan smith who kill himself with eating he use while he be heap up his plate from one dish to watch the other and follow the knife of every body else with such a greedy eye as if he could swear a robbery against any one who presume to eat as well as he
well letus know when you set out and you shallnot have be a week in london if i can help it but you shall be tell by my tongue as now by my pen how much i be your obliged admirer and friend polly darnford
letter xxxvii my dear friend i now proceed with my journal which i have bring down to thursday night
friday
the two lady resolve as they say to inspect all my proceeding insist upon it that i would take they with i in my benevolent round as they after we return would call it which i generally take once a week among my poor and sick neighbour and find i could not get off i set out with they my lady countess propose mrs
worden to fill up the fourth place in the coach
we talk all the way of charity and the excellence of that duty and my lady davers take notice of the text that it would hide a multitude of fault
the countess say she have once a much well opinion of herself than she find she have reason for within these few day past and indeed mrs
b say she when i get home i shall make a good many people the well for your example and so say lady davers which give i no small inward pleasure and i acknowledge in suitable term the honour they both do i
the coach set we down by the side of a large common about five mile distant from our house and we alight and walk a little way choose not to have the coach come nearer that we might be take as little notice of as possible and they enter with i into two mean cot with great condescension and goodness one belong to a poor widow and five child who have be all down in ague and fever the other to a man and his wife bed-rid with age and infirmity and two honest daughter one a widow with two child the other marry to an husbandman who have also be ill but now by comfortable cordial and good physic be pretty well to what they have be
the two lady be well pleased with my demeanour to the good folk to whom i say that as i should go so soon to london i be willing to see they before i go to wish they well and well and to tell they that i should leave order with mrs
jervis concern they to whom they must make know their want and that mr
barrow would take care of they i be sure and do all that be in the power of physic for the restoration of their health
now you must know miss that i be not so good as the old lady of former day who use to distil cordial water and prepare medicine and dispense they themselves
i know if i be so inclined my dear mr
b
would not have be pleased with it because in the approbation he have kindly give to my present method he have twice or thrice praise i that i donot carry my charity to extreme and make his house a dispensatory
i would not therefore by aim at do too much lose the opportunity of do any good at all in these respect and besides as the vulgar say be one must creep before one go
but this be my method i be upon an agreement with this mr
barrow who be deem a very skilful and honest apothecary and one mr
simmonds a surgeon of like character to attend to all such case and person as i shall recommend mr
barrow to administer physic and cordial as he shall judge proper and even in necessary case to call in a physician
and now and then by look in upon they one s self or send a servant to ask question all be keep right
my lady davers observe a bible a common prayer-book and a whole duty of man in each cot in leathern outside case to keep they clean and a church catechism or two for the child and be please to say it be right and her ladyship ask one of the child a pretty girl who learn she her catechism
and she curtsey d and look at i for i do ask the child question when i come to know how they improve tis as i think say my lady my sister provide for both part
god bless you my dear say she and tap my neck
my lady leave token of their bounty behind they to both family and all the good folk bless and pray for we at parting and as we go out my lady davers with a serious air be pleased to say to i take care of your health my dear sister and god give you when it come a happy hour for how many real mourner would you have if you be to be call early to reap the fruit of your piety god s will must be do my lady say i
the same providence that have so wonderfully put it in my power to do a little good will raise up new friend to the honest heart that rely upon he this i say because some of the good people hear my lady and seem troubled and begin to redouble their prayer for my safety and preservation
we walk thence to our coach and stretch a little far to visit two farmer family about a mile distant from each other
one have the mother of the family with two son just recover the former from a fever the latter from tertian ague and i ask when they see mr
barrow
they tell i with great commendation of he that he have but just leave they
so have congratulate their hopeful way and wish they to take care of themselves and not go too early to business i say i should desire mr
barrow to watch over they for fear of a relapse and should hardly see them again for some time and so i slide in a manner not to be observe a couple of guineas into the good woman s hand for i have a hint give i by mrs
jervis that their illness have make it low with they
we proceed then to the other farm where the case be a married daughter who have a very dangerous lying-in and a wicked husband who have abuse she and run away from she but she be mend apace by good comfortable thing which from time to time i have cause to be send she
her old father have be a little unkind to she before i take notice of she for she marry against his consent and indeed the world go hard with the poor man and he could not do much and besides he have a young daughter who have lose all her limb and be force to be tie in a wicker chair to keep she up in it which have expend much to relieve she be a great pull-back as the good old woman call it
and have be a year in arrear to a harsh landlord who find a good stock upon the ground threaten to distress the poor family and turn they out of all i advance the money upon the stock and the poor man have already pay i half of it for miss i must keep within compass too which be fifty pound at first and be in a fair way to pay i the other half and make as much more for himself
here i find mr
barrow and he give i an account of the success of two other case i have recommend to he and tell i that john smith a poor man who in thatch a barn have tumble down and break his leg and bruise himself all over be in a fair way of recovery
this poor creature have like to have perish by the cruelty of the parish officer who would have pass he away to essex where his settlement be though in a burn fever occasion by his misfortune but hearing of the case i direct mr
simmonds to attend he and to provide for he at my expense and give my word if he die to bury he
i be glad to hear he be in so good a way and tell mr
barrow i hop to see he and mr
simmonds together at mr
b s before i set out for london that we might advise about the case under their direction and that i might acquit myself of some of my obligation to they
you be a good man mr
barrow add i god will bless you for your care and kindness to these poor destitute creature
they all praise you and do nothing but talk of your humanity to they o my good lady say he who can forbear follow such an example as you set
mr
simmonds can testify as well as i for now and then a case require we to visit together that we can hardly hear any complaint from our poor patient let them be ever so ill for the praise and blessing they bestow upon you it be good mr

that enable and encourage i to do what i do
tell they they must bless god and bless he and pray for i and thank you and mr

and here i hope i recite not this with pride though i do with pleasure be a cursory account of my benevolent weekly round as my lady will call it
i know you will not be displeased with it but it will highly delight my worthy parent who in their way do a great deal of discreet good in their neighbourhood for indeed miss a little matter prudently bestow and on true object of compassion whose case be soon at a crisis as be those of most labour people will go a great way and especially if lay out properly for them accord to the exigency of their respective cases for such poor people who live generally low want very seldom any thing but revive cordial at first and good wholesome kitchen physic afterwards and then the wheel of nature be unclog new oil as it be and set right they will go round again with pleasantness and ease for a good while together by virtue of that exercise which their labour give they while the rich and voluptuous be force to undergo great fatigue to keep theirs clean and in order
saturday morning
it be hardly right to trouble either of you my honour correspondent with an affair that have vex i a good deal and indeed should affect i more than any other mistress of a family for reason which will be obvious to you when i tell you the case
and this i cannot forbear do
a pretty genteel young body my polly barlow as i call she have be well recommend and behave with great prudence till this time be the cause

letter xxxix my dear miss darnford i hear that mrs
jewkes be in no good state of health
i be very sorry for it
i pray for her life that she may be a credit if it please god to the penitence she have so lately assume

comfort with that sweetness which be so natural to miss darnford her droop heart and let she know that i have a true concern for she and give it she in charge to take care of herself and spare nothing that will administer either to her health or peace of mind
youwill pardon i that i put you upon an office so unsuitable from a lady in your station to a person in her but not to your piety and charity where a duty so eminent as that of visit the sick and cheer the doubt mind be in the question
i know your condescension will give she great comfort and if she should be hasten to her account what a pleasure will it give such a lady as you to have illuminate a benighted mind when it be totter on the verge of death
i know she will want no spiritual help from good mr
peters but then the kind notice of so generally esteem a young lady will raise she more than can be imagine for there be a tenderness a sympathy in the good person of our sex to one another that while the good of the other seem but to act as in office say those thing which though edify and convincing one be not certain proceed not rather from the fortitude of their mind than the tenderness of their nature mingle with one s very spirit thin the animal mass and run through one s heart in the same lify current i cannot clothe my thought suitably to express what i would give assurance as well as pleasure in the most arduous case and brighten our misty prospect till we see the sun of righteousness rise on the hill of comfort and dispel the heavy fog of doubt and diffidence
this it be make i wish and long as i do for the company of my dear miss darnford
o when shall i see you
when shall i to speak to my present case it be all i long for and pardon my freedom of expression as well as thought when i let you know in this instance how early i experience the ardent longing of one in the way i be in
but i ought not to set my heart upon any thing not in my own power and which may be subject to accident and the control of other
but let whatever intervention happen so i have your will to come i must be rejoice in your kind intention although your power should not prove answerable
but i will say no more than that i be my honoured father and mother your ever dutiful daughter and my dear miss darnford your affectionate and oblige p b
letter xl from miss darnford to mrs
b
my dear mrs
b we be greatly oblige to you for every particular article in your entertaining journal which you have bring soon than we wish to a conclusion
we cannot express how much we admire you for your judicious charity so easy to be practise yet so uncommon in the manner and for your inimitable conduct in the affair of your frail polly and the silly mr
h
your account of the visit of the four rake of your parting with your noble guest mr
h s letter an original indeed have all greatly entertain we as your prerogative hint have amuse we but we defer our opinion of those hint till we have the case more fully explain
but my dear friend be you not in danger of fall into a too thoughtful and gloomy way
by the latter part of your last letter we be afraid you be and my mamma and mrs
jones and mrs
peters enjoin i to write to caution you on that head
but there be the less need of it because your prudence will always suggest to you reason as it do in that very letter that must out-balance your fear
think little and hope much be a good lesson in your case and to a lady of your temper and i hope lady davers will not in vain have give you that caution
after all i dare say your thoughtfulness be but symptomatical and will go off in proper time
but to wave this let i ask you be mr
b s conduct to you as respectful i donot mean fond when you be alone together as in company forgive i--but you have hint two or three time in your letter that he always be most complaisant to you in company and you observe that wisely do he act in this as he thereby do credit with every body to his own choice
i make no doubt that the many charming scene which your genius and fine behaviour furnish out to he must as often as they happen inspire he with joy and even rapture and must make he love you more for your mind than for your person but these rapturous scene last very little long than the present moment
what i want to know be whether in the steady part of life when you be both near the level of we common folk he give up any thing of his own will in compliment to your
whether he act the part of a respectful polite gentleman in his behaviour to you and break not into your retirement in the dress and with the brutal roughness of a fox-hunter make no difference perhaps between the field or his stud i will not say kennel and your chamber or closet policy for his own credit-sake as i mention account to i well for his complaisance to you in public
but his regular and uniform behaviour to you in your retirement when the conversation between you turn upon usual and common subject and you have not oblige he to rise to admiration of you by such scene as those of your two parson sir jacob swynford and the like be what would satisfy my curiosity if you please to give i an instance or two of it
now my dearest mrs
b if you can give i a case partly or nearly thus circumstanced you will highly oblige i first where he have bear with any infirmity of your own and i know of none where you can give he such an opportunity except you get into a vapourish habit by give way to a temper too thoughtful and apprehensive next that in complaisance to your will he recede from his own in any one instance next whether he break not into your retirement unceremoniously and without apology or concern as i hint above
you know my dear mrs
b all i mean by what i have say and if you have any pretty conversation in memory by the recital of which this my bold curiosity may be answer pray oblige i with it and we shall be able to judge by it not only of the in-bear generosity which all that know mr
b
have be willing to attribute to he but of the likelihood of the continuance of both your felicity upon term suitable to the character of a fine lady and fine gentleman and of consequence worthy of the imitation of the most delicate of our own sex
your oblige longing my beloved dear lady for my company i hope will very soon be answer
my papa be so pleased with your sweet earnestness on this occasion that he join with my mamma and both with equal cheerfulness say you should not be many day in london before i
murray and his mistress go on swimmingly and have not yet have one quarrel
the only person he of either sex that ever know nancy so intimately and so long without one
this be all i have to say at present when i have assure you my dear mrs
b how much i be your obliged and affectionate polly darnford
letter xli my dearest miss darnford i be afraid i end my last letter in a gloomy way and i be oblige to you for the kind and friendly notice you take of it
it be owe to a train of thinking which sometimes i get into of late i hope only symptomatically as you say and that the cause and effect will soon vanish together
but what a task my dear friend iwill warrant you think you have set i
i think in the progress of my journal and in my letter i have give so many instance of mr
b s polite tenderness to i that no new one would be require at my hand and when i say he be always most complaisant before company i little expect that such an inference would be draw from my word as would tend to question the uniformity of his behaviour to i when there be no witness to it
but i be glad of an opportunity to clear up all your doubt on this subject
to begin then you first desire an instance where mr
b
have bear with some infirmity of mine next that in complaisance to my will he have recede from his own and lastly whether he break not into my retirement unceremoniously and without apology or concern make no difference between the field or the stud and my chamber or closet
as to the first his bearing with my infirmity he be daily give instance of his goodness to i on this head and i be ashamed to say that of late i give he so much occasion for they as i do but he see my apprehensiveness at time though i endeavour to conceal it and no husband be ever so soothing and so indulgent as mr
b
he give i the good advice as to my malady if i may call it one treat i with redouble tenderness talk to i upon the subject i most delight to dwell upon as of my worthy parent what they be do at this time and at that of our intended journey to london of the diversion of the town of miss darnford s company and when he go abroad send up my good mrs
jervis to i because i should not be alone at other time take i abroad with he bring this neighbour and that neighbour to visit and carry i to visit they talk of our journey to kent and into lincolnshire and to my lady davers s to bath to tunbridge and i cannot tell whither when the apprehend time shall be over in fine my dear miss darnford you cannot imagine one half of his tender goodness and politeness to me then he hardly ever go to any distance but bring some pretty present he think will be grateful to i
when at home he be seldom out of my company delight to teach i french and italian and read i piece of manuscript poetry in several of the modern tongue for he speak they all explain to i every thing i understand not delight to answer all my question and to encourage my inquisitiveness and curiosity try to give i a notion of picture and medal and read i lecture upon they for he have a fine collection of both and every now and then will have it that he have be improve by my question and observation
what say you to these thing my dear
do they come up to your first question
or do they not
or be not what i have say a full answer be i to say no more to all your enquiry
o my dear i be thoroughly convinced that half the misunderstanding among married people be owe to trifle to petty distinction to mere word and little captious folly to over-weening or unguarded petulance and who would forego the solid satisfaction of life for the sake of triumphing in such poor contention if one could triumph
but you next require of i an instance where in complaisance to my will he have recede from his own i donot know what to say to this
when mr
b
be all tenderness and indulgence and require of i nothing that i can have a material objection to ought i not to oblige he
can i have a will that be not his
or would it be excusable if i have all little matter i cheerfully give up great one have not yet occur between we and i hope never will
one point indeed i have some apprehension may happen and that to be plain with you be we have have a debate or two on the subject which i maintain of a mother s duty to nurse her own child and i be sorry to say it he seem more determined than i wish he be against it
i hope it will not proceed so far as to awaken the sleep dragon i mention
prerogative by name but i doubt i cannot give up this point very contentedly
but as to less point have i be a duchess bear i think i would not have contest they with my husband
i could give you many respectful instance too of his recede when he have desire to see what i have be write and i have tell he to whom and beg to be excuse
one such instance i can give since i begin this letter
this be it i put it in my bosom when he come up he see i do so be you write my dear what i must not see i be write to miss darnford sir and she beg you might not at present this augment my curiosity pamela
what can two such lady write that i may not see if you willnot be displease sir i have rather you would not because she desire you may not see her letter nor this my answer till the letter be in her hand then i will not return mr
b
will this instance my dear come up to your demand for one where he recede from his own will in complaisance to mine
but now as to what both our notion and our practice be on the article of my retirement and whether he break in upon they unceremoniously and without apology let the conversation i promise inform you which begin on the follow occasion
mr
b
ride out early one morning within a few day past and do not return till the afternoon an absence i have not be use to of late and breakfasting and dining without he be also a new thing with i i have such an impatience to see he have expect he at dinner that i be force to retire to my closet to try to divert it by writing and the gloomy conclusion of my last be then the subject
he return about four o clock and indeed do not tarry to change his riding-dress as your politeness my dear friend would perhaps have expect but come directly up to i with an impatience to see i equal to my own when he be tell upon enquiry that i be in my closet
i hear his welcome step as he come up stair which generally after a long absence than i expect have such an effect upon my fond heart that it give a responsive throb for every step he take towards i and beat quick and fast as he come nearer
i meet he at my closet door
so my dear love say he how do you fold his kind arm about i and salute i with ardour
whenever i have be but a few hour from you my impatience to see my beloved will not permit i to stand upon the formality of a message to know how you be engage but i break in upon you even in my riding-dress as you see dear sir you be very obliging
but i have no notion of mere formality of this kind how unpolite this my dear in your friend in a married state since tis impossible a virtuous wife can be employ about any thing that her husband may not know and so need not fear surprise i be glad to hear you say this my pamela for i have always think the extraordinary civility and distance of this kind which i have observe among several person of rank altogether unaccountable
for if they be exact by the lady i should suspect she have reserve which she herself believe i could not approve
if not exact but practise of choice by the gentleman it carry with it in my opinion a false air of politeness little less than affrontive to the lady and dishonourable to himself for do it not look as if he suppose and allow that she might be so employ that it be necessary to apprise she of his visit lest he should make discovery not to her credit or his own one would not sir for i think his conclusion too severe make such a harsh supposition as this neither for there be little delicacy and moment of retirement no doubt in which a modest lady would wish to be indulge by the tender husband it may be so in an early matrimony before the lady s confidence in the honour and discretion of the man she have choose have disengage she from her bridal reserve bridal reserve dear sir
permit i to give it as my humble opinion that a wife s behaviour ought to be as pure and circumspect in degree as that of a bride or even of a maiden lady be her confidence in her husband s honour and discretion ever so great
for indeed i think a gross or a careless demeanour little become that modesty which be the peculiar excellency and distinction of our sex you account very well my dear by what you now say for your own over-nice behaviour as i have sometimes think it
but be we not all apt to argue for a practice we make our own because we do make it our own rather than from the reason of the thing i hope sir that be not the present case with i for permit i to say that an over-free or negligent behaviour of a lady in the married state must be a mark of disrespect to her consort and would shew as if she be very little solicitous about what appearance she make in his eye
and must not this beget in he a slight opinion of her sex too as if suppose the gentleman have be a free liver she would convince he there be no other difference in the sex but as they be within or without the pale license by the law or act in defiance of it i understand the force of your argument pamela
but you be go to say something more only sir permit i to add that when in my particular case you enjoin i to appear before you always dress even in the early part of the day it would be wrong if i be less regardful of my behaviour and action than of my appearance i believe you be right my dear if a precise or unnecessary scrupulousness be avoid and where all be unaffected easy and natural as in my pamela
for i have see married lady both in england and france who have keep a husband at a great distance than they have exact from some of his sex who have be more entitled to his resentment than to his wife s intimacy
but to wave a subject in which as i can with pleasure say neither of we have much concern tell i my dearest how you be employ before i come up
here be pen and ink here too be paper but it be as spotless as your mind
to whom be you direct your favour now
may i not know your subject mr
h s letter be a part of it and so i have put it by at his approach and not choose he should see that-- i be write reply i to miss darnford but i think you must not ask i to see what i have write this time
i put it aside that you should not when i hear your welcome step
the subject be our parting with our noble guest and a little of my apprehensiveness on an occasion upon which our sex may write to one another but for some of the reason we have be mention gentleman should not desire to see then i will not my dear love so here my dear be another instance--i could give you an hundred such--of his recede from his own will in complaisance to mine only continue he let i warn you against too much apprehensiveness for your own sake as well as mine for such a mind as my pamela s i cannot permit to be habitually over-clouded
and yet there now hang upon your brow an over-thoughtfulness which you must not indulge indeed sir i be a little too thoughtful from my subject before you come but your presence like the sun have dissipate the mist that hang upon my mind
see you not and i press his hand with my lip they be all go already smile upon he with a delight unfeigned
not quite my dear pamela and therefore if you have no objection i will change my dress and attend you in the chariot for an hour or two whither you please that not one shadow may remain visible in this dear face tenderly salute i
whithersoever you please sir

do these instance come up to your question my dear

letter xlii my dear miss darnford one more letter and i have do for a great while because i hope your presence will put an end to the occasion

when we arrive there we find at the door expect we for they hear the chariot-wheel at a distance my pretty miss goodwin and two other misses who have earn their ride attend by the governess s daughter a discreet young gentlewoman
as soon as i step out the child run into my arm with great eagerness and i as tenderly embrace she and lead she into the parlour ask she abundance of question about her work and her lesson and among the rest if she have merit this distinction of the chaise and dairy-house breakfast or if it be owe to her uncle s favour and to that of her governess

letter xliii my dear father and mother i know you will be pleased to hear that we arrive safely in town last night
we find a stately well-furnish and convenient house and i have my closet or library and my withdraw room all in complete order which mr

letter xliv from mrs
b
to lady davers my dearest lady i must beg pardon for have be in this great town more than a week and not have find an opportunity to tender my devoir to your ladyship
you know dear madam what hurry and fatigue must attend such a journey to one in my way and to an entire new settlement in which an hundred thing must be do and attend to with a preference to other occasion however delightful
yet i must own we find a stately well-order and convenient house but although it be not far from the field and have an airy opening to its back part and its front to a square as it be call yet i be not reconcile to it so entirely as to the beloved mansion we leave

b
have be and be busily employ in order some few alteration to make thing still more commodious

observe with regard to gentleman who build fine house make fine garden and open fine prospect that art should never take place of but be subservient to nature and a gentleman if confine to a situation have well conform his design to that than to do as at chatsworth level a mountain at a monstrous expense which have it be suffer to remain in so wild and romantic a scene as chatsworth afford might have be make one of the great beauty of the place
so i think i have well endeavour to make the good of those natural defect i cannot master than by assume air and dignity in appearance to which i be not bear act neither part tolerably
by this mean instead of be think neither gentlewoman nor rustic as sir jacob hint linsey-wolsey i think be his term too i may be look upon as an original in my way and all original pass well enough you know madam even with judge
now i be upon this subject i can form to myself if your ladyship will excuse i two such polite gentleman as my lawyer mention in my former who with a true london magnanimity and penetration for madam i fancy your london critic will be the severe upon the country girl will put on mighty significant look forget it may be that they have any fault themselves and apprehend that they have nothing to do but to sit in judgment upon other one of they express himself after this manner-- why truly jack the girl be well enough-- considering i cannot say-- then a pinch of snuff perhaps add importance to his air but a man might love she for a month or two these spark talk thus of other lady before i she behave well than i expect from her-- considering again will follow
so i think cry the other and toss his tie behind he with an air partly of contempt and partly of rakery
as you say
jemmy i expect to find an awkward country girl but she top her part iwill assure you nay for that matter behave very tolerably for what she was and be right not to seem desirous to drown the remembrance of her original in her elevation--and i cannot but say for something like it he do say be mighty pretty and passably genteel and thus with their poor praise of mr

but how i run on

letter l from lady davers to mrs
b my dear pamela after i have thank you for your last agreeable letter which have add the earl and lady jenny to the number of your admirer you know lady betty her sister be so before i shall tell you that i now write at their request as well as at those of my lord davers the countess you so dearly love and lady betty for your decision of an odd dispute that on read your letter and talk of your domestic excellency happen among we
lady betty say that notwithstanding any awkwardness you attribute to yourself she cannot but decide by all she have see of your writing and hear from we that yours be the perfect character she ever find in the sex
the countess say that you wrong yourself in suppose you be not every thing that be polite and genteel as well in your behaviour as in your person and that she know not any lady in england who well become her station than you do
why then say lady jenny mrs

must be quite perfect thatbe certain so say the earl so say they all
and lord davers confirm that you be

b
and acquaint she with our subject and as it be impossible for one who can act as she do not to know herself well than any body else can do desire she to acquaint we with some of those secret foible that leave room for she to be still more perfect a good thought say they all
and this be the present occasion of my writing and pray see that you accuse yourself of no more than you know yourself guilty for over-modesty border nearly on pride and too liberal self-accusation be generally but so many trap for acquittal with applause so that whatever other lady might you will not be forgive if you deal with we in a way so poorly artful let your fault therefore be such as you think we can subscribe to from what we have see of you and what we have read of yours and you must try to extenuate they too as you give they lest we should think you above that nature which in the good case be your undoubted talent
i congratulate you and miss damford on her arrival she be a charming young lady but tell she that we shall not allow she to take you at your word and to think that she excel you in any one thing only indeed we think you nice in some point than you need be to as to your present agreeable circumstance
and yet let i tell you that the easy unaffected conjugal purity in word and behaviour between your good man and you be worthy of imitation and what the countess and i have with pleasure contemplate since we leave you an hundred time and admire in you both and it be good policy too child as well as high decorum for it be what will make you ever new and respectful to one another
but you have the honour of it all whose sweet natural and easy modesty in person behaviour and conversation forbid indecency even in thought much more in word to approach you insomuch that no rake can be rake in your presence and yet they hardly know to what they owe their restraint
however as people who see you at this time will take it for grant that you and mr
b
have be very intimate together i should think you need not be ashamed of your appearance because as he rightly observe you have no reason to be ashamed of your husband
excuse my pleasantry my dear and answer our demand upon you as soon as you can which will oblige we all particularly your affectionate sister b

letter li my dearest lady what a task have you impose upon i
and accord to the term you annex to it how shall i acquit myself of it without incur the censure of affectation if i freely accuse myself as i may deserve or of vanity if i do not
indeed madam i have a great many failing and you donot know the pain it cost i to keep they under not so much for fear the world should see they for i bless god i can hope they be not capital as for fear they should become capital if i be to let they grow upon i
and this surely i need not have tell your ladyship and the countess of c who have read my paper and see my behaviour in the kind visit you make to your dear brother and have from both but too much reason to censure i do not your generous and partial favour make you overlook my great failing and pass under a kind name many of my less for surely my good lady you must both of you have observe in what you have read and see that i be naturally of a saucy temper and with all my appearance of meekness and humility can resent and ste too when i think myself provoke
i have also discover in myself on many occasion of some of which i will by-and-by remind your ladyship a malignancy of heart that it be true last but a little while--nor have it need--but for which i have often call myself to account--to very little purpose hitherto
and indeed madam now for a little extenuation as you expect from i i have some difficulty whether i ought to take such pain to subdue myself in some instance in the station to which i be raise that otherwise it would have become i to attempt to do for it be no easy task for one in my circumstance to distinguish between the ought and the ought not to be humble without meanness and decent without arrogance
and if all person think as justly as i flatter myself i do of the inconvenience as well as convenience which attend their being raise to a condition above they they would not imagine all the world be their own when they come to be distinguish as i have be for what with the contempt of superior relation on one side the envy of the world and low reflection arise from it on the other from which no one must hope to be totally exempt and the awkwardness besides with which they support their elevated condition if they have sense to judge of their own imperfection and if the gentleman be not such an one as mine-- and where will such another be found on all these account i say they will be make sensible that whatever they might once think happiness and an high estate be two very different thing
but i shall be too grave when your ladyship and all my kind and noble friend expect perhaps i should give the uncommon subject a pleasanter air yet what must that mind be that be not serious when oblige to recollect and give account of its defect
but i must not only accuse myself it seem i must give proof such as your ladyship can subscribe to of my imperfection
there be so much real kindness in this seem hardship that i will obey you
madam and produce proof in a moment which cannot be controvert
as to my sauciness those paper will give an hundred instance against i as well to your dear brother as to other
indeed to extenuate as you command i as i go along these be mostly when i be apprehensive for my honour they be
and then i have a little tincture of jealousy which sometimes have make i more uneasy than i ought to be as the paper you have not see would have demonstrate particularly in miss godfrey s case and in my conversation with your ladyship in which i have frequently betray my fear of what might happen when in london yet to extenuate again i have examine myself very strictly on this head and really think that i can ascribe a great part of this jealousy to laudable motif no less than to my concern for your dear brother s future happiness in the hope that i may be a humble mean through providence to induce he to abhor those crime of which young gentleman too often be guilty and bring he over to the practice of those virtue in which he will ever have cause to rejoice yet my lady some other part of the charge must stand against i for as i love his person as well as his mind i have pride in my jealousy that would not permit i i verily think to support myself as i ought under trial of a competition in this very tender point
and this oblige i to own that i have a little spark--not a little one perhaps of secret pride and vanity that will arise now and then on the honour do i but which i keep under as much as i can and to this pride let i tell your ladyship i know no one contribute or can contribute more largely than yourself
so you see my dear lady what a naughty heart i have and how far i be from be a faultless creature--i hope i shall be well and well however as i live long and have more grace and more wit for here to recapitulate my fault be in the first place vindictiveness i will not call it downright revenge--and how much room do all these leave for amendment and great perfection
have your ladyship and the countess favour we long in your kind visit i must have so improve by your charming conversation and by that natural ease and dignity which accompany everything your ladyship do and say as to have get over such of these foible as be not root in nature till in time i have be able to do more than emulate those perfection which at present i can only at an awful distance revere as become my dear lady your most humble admirer and oblige servant p b
letter lii from miss darnford to her father and mother
my ever-honoured papa and mamma i arrive safely in london on thursday after a tolerable journey consider deb and i make six in the coach two have be take up on the way after you leave i and none of the six highly agreeable

alban s in their coach and six

we be happy beyond expression
mr
b
be a charming husband so easy so pleased with and so tender of his lady and she so much all that we see she in the country as to humility and affability and improve in every thing else which we hardly think possible she could be--that i never know so happy a matrimony all that prerogative sauciness which we apprehend would so eminently display itself in his behaviour to his wife have she be ever so distinguished by birth and fortune be vanish
i do not think it be in the power of an angel if our sex could have produce one to have make so tender and so fond a husband of mr
b
as he make

may mr
murray and miss nancy make just such a happy pair
you command i my honoured mamma to write to you an account of every thing that please i--i say i would but what a task should i then have i do not think i have undertake to write volumes you must therefore allow i to be more brief than i have intend
in the first place it would take up five or six long letter to do justice to the economy observe in this happy family
you know that mrs
b
have not change one of her servant and only add she polly to they
this be an unexampled thing especially as they be her fellow-servant as we may say but since they have the sense to admire so good an example and be proud to follow it each to his and her power i think it one of her peculiar facility to have continue they and to choose to reform such as be exceptionable rather than dismiss they
their mouth deb tell i be continually full of their lady s praise and prayer and blessing utter with such delight and fervour for the happy pair that it make her eye she say ready to run over to hear they
moreover i think it an extraordinary degree of policy whether design or not to keep they as they be all worthy folk for have she turn they off what have she do but make as many enemy as she have discard servant and as many more as those have friend and acquaintance
and we all know how much the reputation of family lie at the mercy of servant and it be easy to guess to what cause each would have impute his or her dismission
and so she have escape as she ought the censure of pride and make every one instead of reproach she with her descent find those grace in she which turn that very disadvantage to her glory
she be exceedingly affable always speak to they with a smile but yet have such a dignity in her manner that it secure she their respect and reverence and they be ready to fly at a look and seem proud to have her command to execute insomuch that the words-- my lady command so or so from one servant to another be sure to meet with an indisputable obedience be the duty require what it will
if any of they be the least indisposed her care and tenderness for they engage the veneration and gratitude of all the rest who see how kindly they will be treat should they ail any thing themselves
and in all this she be very happy in mrs
jervis who be an excellent second to her admirable lady and be treat by she with as much respect and affection as if she be her mother
you may remember madam that in the account she give we of she benevolent round as lady davers call it she say that as she be go to london she should instruct mrs
jervis about some of her client as i find she call she poor to avoid a word which her delicacy account harsh with regard to they and ostentatious with respect to herself
i ask she how since contrary to she then expectation mrs
jervis be permit to be in town with she she have provide to answer her intention as to those her client whom she have refer to the care of that good woman
she say that mr
barlow her apothecary be a very worthy man and she have give he a plenary power in that particular and likewise desire he to recommend any new and worthy case to she that no deserve person among the destitute sick poor might be unrelieve by reason of her absence
and here in london she have apply herself to dr her parish minister a fine preacher and sound divine who promise on all opportunity to pay his respect to mr
b to recommend to she any poor housekeeper who would be glad to accept of some private benefaction and yet have live creditably till reduce by misfortune be ashamed to apply for public relief and she have several of these already on her benevolent list to some of whom she send coal now at the entrance on the wintry season to some a piece of irish or scottish linen or so many yard of norwich stuff for gown and coat for the girl or yorkshire cloth for the boy and money to some who she be most assure will lay it out with care
and she have moreover mortify as the scot call it one hundred and fifty pound as a fund for loan without interest of five ten or fifteen but not exceed twenty pound to answer some present exigence in some honest family who find the good security they can to repay it in a give time and this fund she purpose as she grow rich she say to increase and estimate pleasantly her worth by this sum say sometimes who would ever have think i should have be worth one hundred and fifty pound so soon
i shall be a rich body in time but in all these thing she enjoin secresy which the doctor have promise
she tell the doctor what mr
adams s office be in her family and hop she say he would give she his sanction to it assure he that she think it her duty to ask it as she be one of his flock and he on that account her principal shepherd which make a spiritual relation between they the requisite of which on her part be not to be dispense with
the good gentleman very cheerfully and applaudingly give his consent and when she tell he how well mr
adams be provide for and that she would apply to he to supply she with a town chaplain when she be deprive of he he wish that the other duty of his function for he have a large parish would permit he to be the happy person himself say that till she be supply to her mind either he or his curate would take care that so laudable a method should be keep up
you will do i the justice madam to believe that i very cheerfully join in my dear friend s sunday duty and i be not a little edify with the good example and the harmony and good-will that this excellent method preserve in the family
