what an example do this dear lady set to all who see she know she and who hear of she how happy they who have the grace to follow it
what a public blessing would such a mind as hers be could it be vest with the robe of royalty and adorn the sovereign dignity
but what be the prince of the earth look at they in every nation and what they have be for age past compare to this lady
who act from the impulse of her own heart unaide in most case by any human example
in short when i contemplate her innumerable excellency and that sweetness of temper and universal benevolence which shine in every thing she say and do i cannot sometimes help look upon she in the light of an angel drop down from heaven and receive into bodily organ to live among man and woman in order to shew what the first of the specie be design to be
and here be the admiration that one see all these duty perform in such an easy and pleasant manner as any body may perform they for they interfere not with any part of the family management but rather aid and inspirit every one in the discharge of all their domestic service and moreover keep their mind in a state of preparation for the more solemn duty of the day and all without the least intermixture of affectation enthusiasm or ostentation
o my dear papa and mamma permit i but to tarry here till i be perfect in all these good lesson and how happy shall i be

every nation as you say have its excellency and our should not quit the manly nervous sense which be the distinction of the english drama
one play of our celebrated shakespeare will give infinitely more pleasure to a sensible mind than a dozen english-italian opera
but my dear in italy they be quite another thing and the sense be not as here sacrifice so much to the sound but that they be both very compatible be pleased sir to give i your observation on this head in writing and then i shall have something to send worthy of lady davers s acceptance i will my dear and he take a pen and write the inclose which i beg your ladyship to return i because i will keep it for my instruction if i should be lead to talk of this subject in company
let my sister know say he that i have give myself no time to re-peruse what i have write
she will do well therefore to correct it and return it to you in italy judge of opera be so far from think the drama or poetical part of their opera nonsense as the unskilled in italian rashly conclude in england that if the libretto as they call it be not approve the opera notwithstanding the excellence of the music will be condemn
for the italians justly determine that the very music of an opera cannot be complete and pleasing if the drama be incongruous as i may call it in its composition because in order to please it must have the necessary contrast of the grave and the light that is the diverting equally blend through the whole
if there be too much of the first let the music be compose ever so masterly in that style it will become heavy and tiresome if the latter prevail it will surfeit with its levity wherefore it be the poet s business to adapt the word for this agreeable mixture for the music be but secondary and subservient to the word and if there be an artful contrast in the drama there will be the same in the music suppose the composer to be a skilful master
now since in england the practice have be to mutilate curtail and patch up a drama in italian in order to introduce favourite air select from different author the contrast have always be break thereby without every one s know the reason and since ignorant mercenary prompter though italians have be employ in hotch-potch and in translate our drama from italian into english how could such opera appear any other than incongruous nonsense permit i dear madam to repeat my assurance that i be and must ever be your obliged sister and servant p b

letter lvii my dearest lady my mind be so wholly engross by thought of a very different nature from those which the diversion of the town and theatre inspire that i beg to be excuse if for the present i say nothing far of those light matter

i hope i shall not be desert in the hour of trial and that this my weakness of mind will not be punish with a spiritual dereliction for suffer myself to be too much attach to those worldly delight and pleasure which no mortal ever enjoy in a more exalted degree than myself
and i beseech you my dear lady let i be always remember in your prayers-- only for a resignation to the divine will a cheerful resignation
i presume not to prescribe to his gracious providence for if one have but that one have every thing that one need to have

i have just be contend with a severe pang that be now go off what effect its return may have god only know
and if this be the last line i shall ever write it will be the more satisfactory to i as with my humble respect to my good lord davers and my dear countess and pray for the continuance of all your health and happiness both here and hereafter i be permit to subscribe myself your ladyship s oblige sister and humble servant p b

have be so good as to return with i hither and i find my baby s case not yet quite desperate--i be easy now i see he in presence of his beloved papa who let i have all my way and approve of my preparative method for myself and he tell i that since i will have it so he will indulge i in my attendance on the child and endeavour to imitate my reliance on god--that be his kind expression--and leave the issue to he
and on my tell he that i fear nothing in the distemper but the loss of his love he say in presence of the doctor and my father and mother press my hand to his lips-- my dear life make yourself easy under this affliction and apprehend nothing for yourself i love you more for your mind than for your face
that and your person will be the same and be that sweet face to be cover with seam and scar i will value you the more for the misfortune and glad i be that i have your picture so well draw in town to satisfy those who have hear of your loveliness what you be and hitherto be
for myself my admiration lie deeply and draw i to the other end of the room whisperingly he say the last uneasiness between we i now begin to think be necessary because it have turn all my delight in you more than ever to the perfection of your mind and so god preserve to i the life of my pamela i care not for my own part what ravage the distemper make here and tap my cheek how generous how noble how comforting be this
when i go from my apartment to go to my child my dear mr
b
meet i at the nursery door and lead i back again
you must not go in again my dearest
they have just be give the child other thing to try to drive out the malady and some pustule seem to promise on his breast i make no doubt my baby be then in extremity and i would have give the world to have shed a few tear but i could not
with the most soothing goodness he lead i to my desk and withdraw to attend the dear baby himself--to see his last gasping poor little lamb i make no doubt
in this suspense my own strange hardness of heart would not give up one tear for the passage from that to my eye seem quite choake up which use to be so open and ready on other occasion affect one too
two day have pass dreadful day of suspense and now bless be god
who have give i hope that our prayer be hear the pustule come kindly out very thick in his breast and on his face but of a good sort they tell me they willnot let i see he indeed they won t what cruel kindness be this
one must believe all they tell one
but my dear lady my spirit be so weak i have such a violent headache and have such a strange shiver disorder all run down my back and i be so hot just now and be so cold at this present--aguishly inclined--i donot know how
that i must leave off the post go away with the assurance that i be and will be to the last hour of my life your ladyship s grateful and oblige sister and servant p b
letter lxxx from mr
b
to lady davers my dear sister i take very kindly your solicitude for the health of my beloved pamela
the last line she write be to you for she take to her bed the moment she lay down her pen
i tell she your kind message and wish for her safety by my lord s gentleman and she beg i would write a line to thank you in her name for your affectionate regard to she
she be in a fine way to do well for with her accustomed prudence she have begin to prepare herself by a proper regimen the moment she know the child s illness be the small-pox
the bad be over with the boy which keep up her spirit and her mother be so excellent a nurse to both and we be so happy likewise in the care of a skilful physician dr
m
who direct and approve of every thing the good dame do that it be a singular providence this malady seize they here and afford no small comfort to the dear creature herself
when i tell you that to all appearance her charming face will not receive any disfigurement by this cruel enemy to beauty i be sure you will congratulate i upon a felicity so desirable but be it to be otherwise if i be capable of slight a person whose principal beauty be much deep than the skin i should deserve to be think the most unworthy and superficial of husband
whatever your notion have be my ever-ready censuring lady davers of your brother on a certain affair i do assure you that i never do and never can love any woman as i love my pamela
it be indeed impossible i can ever love she well than i do and her outward beauty be far from be indifferent to i yet if i know myself i be sure i have justice enough to love she equally and generosity enough to be more tender of she be she to suffer by this distemper
but as her humility and her affection to i would induce she to think herself under great obligation to i for such my tenderness to she be she to lose any the least valuable of her perfection i rejoice that she will have no reason for mortification on that score

i be your affectionate brother and humble servant
letter lxxxi from lady davers in answer to the precede
my dear brother i do most heartily congratulate you on the recovery of master billy and the good way my sister be in
i be the more rejoice as her sweet face be not like to suffer by the malady for be the beauty of the mind what they will those of the person be no small recommendation with some folk i be sure and i begin to be afraid that when it be hardly possible for both conjoin to keep a rove mind constant that one only would not be sufficient
this news give i more pleasure because i be well informed that a certain gay lady be pleased to give herself air upon learning of my sister s illness as that she would not be sorry for it for now she should look upon herself as the prettiest woman in england she mean only i suppose as to outward prettiness brother
you give i the name of a ready censurer
i own i think myself to be not a little interested in all that regard my brother and his honour
but when some people be not readier to censure than other to trespass i know not whether they can with justice be style censorious
but however that be the rod seem to have be hold up as a warning--and that the blow in the irreparable deprivation be not give be a mercy which i hope will be deserve though you never can those very signal one you receive at the divine hand beyond any man i know
for even if i shall not be deem censorious again your very vice have be turn to your felicity as if god would try the nobleness of the heart he have give you by overcome you in answer to my sister s constant prayer as well as mine by mercy rather than by judgment
i might give instance of the truth of this observation in almost all the action and attempt of your past life and take care if you be displeased i will speak it take care thou bold wretch that if this method be ungratefully slight the uplifted arm fall not down with double weight on thy devoted head
i must always love and honour my brother but cannot help speak my mind which after all be the natural result of that very love and honour and which oblige i to style myself your truly affectionate sister b
davers

williams s good fortune
as mr

she be a sly girl

i think since he seem to love she you do right not to hinder the girl s fortune

this be not the case of mrs

letter lxxxvi from lady davers to mrs

in answer to the preceding my dear pamela i be glad miss darnford be likely to be so happy in a husband as sir w g
will certainly make she

i want too to have the foolish fellow marry--for several reason one of which be he be continually tease we to permit he to go up to town and reside there for some month in order that he may see the world as he call it
but we be convince he would feel it as well as see it if we give way to his request for in understanding dress and inconsiderate vanity he be so exactly cut out and sized for a town fop coxcomb or pretty fellow that he will undoubtedly fall into all the vice of those people and perhaps have such expectation as he have will be make the property of rake and sharper
he complain that we use he like a child in a go-cart or a baby with lead-string and that he must not be trust out of our sight

wrightson formerly miss sally godfrey to mrs
b happy deservedly happy dear lady permit these line to kiss your hand from one who though she be a stranger to your person be not so to your character that have reach we here in this remote part of the world where you have as many admirer as have hear of you
but i more particularly be bind to be so by an obligation which i can never discharge but by my daily prayer for you and the blessing i continually implore upon you and your
i can write my whole mind to you though i cannot from the most deplorable infelicity receive from you the wish-for favour of a few line in return write with the same unreservedness so unhappy be i from the effect of an inconsideration and weakness on one hand and temptation on the other which you at a tender age most nobly for your own honour and that of your sex have escape whilst i--but let my tear in these blot speak the rest--as my heart bleed and have constantly bleed ever since at the grievous remembrance--but believe however dear madam that tis shame and sorrow and not pride and impenitence that make i both to speak out to so much purity of life and manner my own odious weakness
nevertheless i ought and i will accuse myself by name
imagine then illustrious lady truly illustrious for virtue infinitely superior to all the advantage of birth and fortune imagine i say that in this letter you see before you the once guilty and therefore i doubt always guilty but ever penitent sarah godfrey the unhappy though fond and tender mother of the poor infant to whom your generous goodness have i hear extend itself so as to make you desirous of take she under your worthy protection god for ever bless you for it
pray an indulgent mother who admire at an awful distance that virtue in you which she could not practise herself
and will you dear lady take under your own immediate protection the poor unguilty infant
will you love she for the sake of she suffering mamma whom you know not for the sake of the gentleman now so dear to you and so worthy of you as i hear with pleasure he be
and will you by the good example in the world give i a moral assurance that she will never sink into the fault the weakness the crime i ought not to scruple to call it so of her poor inconsiderate-but you be her mamma now i will not think of a guilty one therefore
what a joy be it to i in the midst of my heavy reflection on my past misconduct that my beloved sally can boast a virtuous and innocent mamma who have withstand the snare and temptation that have be so fatal--elsewhere and whose example and instruction next to god s grace will be the strong fence to her honour once more i say and on my knee i write it god for ever bless you here and augment your joy hereafter for your generous goodness to my poor and till now motherless infant
i hope she by her duty and obligingness will do all in her little power to make you amend and never give you cause to repent of this your unexampled kindness to she and to i
she cannot i hope except her mother s crime have have an influence upon she too much like that of an original stain be of a sordid or an ungrateful nature
and o my poor sally
if you be and if ever you fail in your duty to your new mamma to whose care and authority i transfer my whole right in you remember that you have no more a mamma in i nor can you be entitle to my blessing or my prayer which i make now on that only condition your implicit obedience to all your new mamma s command and direction
you may have the curiosity madam to wish to know how i live for no doubt you have hear all my sad sad story know then that i be as happy as a poor creature can be who have once so deplorably so inexcusably fall
i have a worthy gentleman for my husband who marry i as a widow whose only child by my former be the care of her papa s friend particularly of good lacy davers and her brother
poor unhappy i
to be under such a sad necessity to disguise the truth mr
wrightson whose name i be unworthily honour by have often entreat i to send for the poor child and to let she be join as his--kill thought that it cannot be with two child i have by him judge my good lady how that very generosity which have i be guiltless would have add to my joy must wound i deep than even ungenerous or unkind usage from he could do
and how heavy that crime must lie upon i which turn my very pleasure to misery and fix all the joy i can know in repentance for my past misdeeds how happy be you madam on the contrary you who have nothing of this sort to pall nothing to mingle with your felicity
who bless in an honour untainted and a conscience that cannot reproach you be enable to enjoy every well deserve comfort as it offer itself and can improve it too by reflection on your past conduct
while mine alas
like a winter frost nip in the bud every rise satisfaction
my husband be rich as well as generous and very tender of i--happy if i could think myself as deserve as he think me my principal comfort as i hint be in my penitence for my past fault and that i have a merciful god for my judge who know that penitence to be sincere
you may guess madam from what i have say in what light i must appear here and if you would favour i with a line or two in answer to the letter you have now in your hand it will be one of the great pleasure i can receive a pleasure next to that which i have receive in know that the gentleman you love well have have the grace to repent of all his evil have early see his error and have thereby i hope free two person from be one day mutual accuser of each other for now i please myself to think that the crime of both may be wash away in the blood of that saviour god whom both have so grievously offend
may that god who have not suffer i to be abandon entirely to my own shame as i deserve continue to shower down upon you those blessing which a virtue like yours may expect from his mercy
may you long be happy in the possession of all you wish
and late very late for the good of thousand i wish this may you receive the reward of your piety your generosity and your filial your social and conjugal virtue
be the prayer of your most unworthy admirer and oblige humble servant sarah wrightson

must not such a lady as this dear madam have as much merit as many even of those who have not have her temptation have not fall
this at least one may aver that next to not commit an error be the resolution to retrieve it all that one may to repent of it and studiously to avoid the repetition
but who besides this excellent mrs
wrightson have so fall and be still so ardently solicit and pursue and flatter perhaps by fond hope that her spoiler would one day do she all the justice he could for who can do complete justice to a woman he have rob of she honour could resolve as she resolve and act as she act
miss goodwin be a sweet child but permit i to say have a little of her papa s spirit hasty yet generous and acknowledge when she be convinced of her fault a little haughty and prouder than i wish she to be but in every thing else deserve the character i give of she to her mamma
she be very fond of fine clothe be a little too lively to the servants told i once when i take notice that softness and mildness of speech become a young lady that they be but servant
and she could say no more than pray and i desire and i wish youwould be so kind to her uncle or to i
i tell she that good servant deserve any civil distinction and that so long as they be ready to oblige in every thing by a kind word it would be very wrong to give they imperative one which could serve for no other end but to convince observer of the haughtiness of one s own temper and look as if one would question their compliance with our will unless we would exact it with an high hand which might cast a slur upon the command we give as if we think it be hardly so reasonable as otherwise to obtain their observation of it
besides my dear say i you donot consider that if you speak as haughtily and commandingly to they on common as on extraordinary occasion you weaken your own authority if even you should be permit to have any and theywill regard you no more in the one case than in the other she take great notice of what i say and when her little proud heart be subdue by reasoning she cannot answer she will sit as if she be study what to say to come off as fly as she can and as the case require i let she go off easily or push the little dear to her last refuge and make she quit her post and yield up her spirit a captive to reason and discretion two excellent commander with whom i tell she i must bring she to be intimately acquaint
yet after all till i can be sure that i can inspire she with the love of virtue for its own sake i will rather try to conduct her spirit to proper end than endeavour totally to subdue it be sensible that our passion be give we for excellent end and that they may by a proper direction be make subservient to the noble purpose
i tell she sometimes there may be a decent pride in humility and that it be very possible for a young lady to behave with so much true dignity as shall command respect by the turn of her eye soon than by asperity of speech that she may depend upon it the person who be always find fault frequently cause they and that it be no glory to be well bear than servant if she be not well behave too
besides i tell her humility be a grace that shin in a high condition but cannot equally in a low one because that be already too much humble perhaps and that though there be a censure lie against be poor and proud yet i would rather forgive pride in a poor body than in a rich for in the rich it be insult and arrogance proceed from their high condition but in the poor it may be a defensative against dishonesty and may shew a natural bravery of mind perhaps if properly direct and manifest on right occasion that the frown of fortune cannot depress
she say she hear every day thing from i which her governess never teach she
that may very well be i tell she because her governess have many young lady to take care of i but one and that i want to make she wise and prudent betime that she may be an example to other misses and that governess and mamma shall say to their misses when will you be like miss goodwin
do you ever hear miss goodwin say a naughty word
would miss goodwin think you have do so or so she throw her arm about my neck on one such occasion as this oh say she what a charming mamma have i get

b
whom now-and-then she say she love as well as if he be her own papa see with pleasure how we go on

there be a pride you know madam in some of our sex that serve to useful purpose be a good defence against improper match and mean action and be not wholly to be subdue for that reason for though it be not virtue yet if it can be virtue s substitute in high rash and inconsiderate mind it may turn to good account
so i will not quite discourage my dear pupil neither till i see what discretion and riper year may add to her distinguish faculty
for as some have no notion of pride separate from imperiousness and arrogance so other know no difference between humility and meanness
there be a golden mean in every thing and if it please god to spare we both i will endeavour to point her passion and such even of those foible which seem too deeply rooted to be soon eradicate to useful purpose choose to imitate physician who in certain chronical illness as i have read in lord bacon rather proceed by palliative than by harsh extirpative which through the resistance give to they by the constitution may create such ferment in it as may destroy that health it be their intention to establish
but whither be i running your ladyship i hope will excuse this parade freedom of my pen for though these notion be well enough with regard to miss goodwin they must be very impertinent to a lady who can so much well instruct miss s tutoress than that vain tutoress can her pupil

letter lxxxviii my dear lady daver i have three marriage to acquaint you with in one letter
in the first place sir w g
have send by the particular desire of my dear friend that he be make one of the happy man in england on the 18 th past and so i have no long my miss darnford to boast of
i have a very good opinion of the gentleman but if he be but half so good a husband as she will make a wife they will be exceedingly happy in one another
mr
williams s marriage to a kinswoman of his noble patron as you have hear be in treaty be the next and there be great reason to believe from the character of both that they will likewise do credit to the state
the third be mr
adams and polly barlow and i wish they for both their sake as happy as either of the former
they be set out to his living highly pleased with one another and i hope will have reason to continue so to be
as to the first i do not indeed think the affair would have be so soon conclude and miss keep it off so long as i understand that her papa be angry with she and indeed as the gentleman s family circumstance and character be such that there could lie no objection against he i think it would have be wrong to have delay it
i should have write to your ladyship before but have be favour with mr
b s company into kent on a visit to my good mother who be indisposed
we tarry there a week and leave both my dear parent to my thankful satisfaction in as good health as ever they be in their life
mrs
judy swynford or miss swynford as she refuse not be call now and then have be with we for this week past and she expect her brother sir jacob to fetch she away in about a week hence
it do not become i to write the least word that may appear disrespectful of any person relate to your ladyship and mr
b
otherwise i should say that the b----s and the s----s be directly the opposite of one another
but yet as she never see your ladyship but once you will forgive i to mention a word or two about she because she be a character that be in a manner new to i
she be a maiden lady as you know and though she will not part with the green leaf from her hand one see by the grey-goose down on her brow and her head that she cannot be less than fifty-five
but so much pain do she take by powder to have never a dark hair in her head because she have one half of they white that i be sorry to see what be a subject for reverence should be deem by the good lady matter of concealment
she be often seemingly reproach herself that she be an old maid and an old woman but it be very discernible that she expect a compliment that she be not so every time she be so free with herself and if nobody make her one she will say something of that sort in her own behalf
she take particular care that of all the public transaction which happen to be talk of her memory will never carry she back above thirty year
and then it is-- about thirty year ago when i be a girl or when i be in hang sleeve and so she make herself for twenty year of her life a very useless and insignificant person
if her tooth which for her age be very good though not over white and which by her care of they she seem to look upon as the last remain of her well day would but fail it might help she to a conviction that would set she ten year forwarder at least
but poor lady she be so young in spite of her wrinkle that i be really concerned for her affectation because it expose she to the remark and ridicule of the gentleman and give one pain for she
surely these lady donot act prudently at all since for every year mrs
judy would take from her age her censurer add two to it and behind her back make she go on towards seventy whereas if she would lay claim to her reverential as i may say and not try to conceal her age she would have many compliment for look so well at her years and many a young body would hope to be the well for her advice and experience who now be afraid of affront she if they suppose she have live much long in the world than themselves
then she look back to the year she own when more flippant lady at the laugh time of her life delight to be frolic she try to sing too although if ever she have a voice she have outlive it and her song be of so antique a date that they would betray she only as she say they be learn she by her grandmother who be a fine lady at the restoration
she will join in a dance and though her limb move not so pliantly as might be expect of a lady no old than she would be think and whose dancing-day be not entirely over yet that be owe to a fall from her horse some year ago which she doubt she shall never recover though she find she grow well and well every year
thus she lose the respect the reverence she might receive be it not for this miserable affectation take pain by ape youth to make herself unworthy of her year and be content to be think less discreet than she might otherwise be deem for fear she should be imagine old if she appear wise
what a sad thing be this madam what a mistaken conduct
we pray to live to old age and it be promise as a blessing and as a reward for the performance of certain duty and yet when we come to it we have rather be think as foolish as youth than to be deem wise and in possession of it
and so we shew how little we deserve what we have be so long coveting and yet covet on for what
why to be more and more ashamed and more and more unworthy of that we covet
how fantastic a character be this well may irreverent unthinke youth despise instead of revere the hoary head which the wearer be so much ashamed of
the lady boast a relationship to you and mr
b
and i think i be very bold
but my reverence for year and the disgust i have to see anybody behave unworthy of they make i take the great liberty which however i shall wish i have not take if it meet not with that allowance which i have always have from your ladyship in what i write
god know whether ever i may enjoy the blessing i so much revere in other
for now my heavy time approach

this be just as i wish it
how often have my heart ache when i have see poor baby roll and swathe ten or a dozen time round then blanket upon blanket mantle upon that its little neck pin down to one posture its head more than it frequently need triple-crown like a young pope with cover upon covering its leg and arm as if to prevent that kindly stretching which we rather ought to promote when it be in health and which be only aim at growth and enlargement the former bundle up the latter pin down and how the poor thing lie on the nurse s lap a miserable little pinioned captive goggle and star with its eye the only organ it have at liberty as if supplicate for freedom to its fettered limb

letter xci i will now my dearest my well beloved correspondent of all begin since the tender age of my dear baby will not permit i to have an eye yet to their well part to tell you what be the little matter to which i be not quite so well reconcile in mr

this excellent author section 52 have justly disallow of slavish and corporal punishment in the education of those we would have to be wise good and ingenuous man add on the other side to flatter child by reward of thing that be pleasant to they be as carefully to be avoid
he that will give his son apple or sugar-plum or what else of this kind he be most delighted with to make he learn his book do but authorize his love of pleasure and cocker up that dangerous propensity which he ought by all mean to subdue and stifle in he
you can never hope to teach he to master it whilst you compound for the check you give his inclination in one place by the satisfaction you propose to it in another
to make a good a wise and a virtuous man tis fit he should learn to cross his appetite and deny his inclination to rich finery or please his palate c this sir be well say but be it not a little too philosophical and abstract not only for the generality of child but for the age he suppose they to be of if one may guess by the apple and the sugar-plum propose for the reward of their well-doing would not this require that memory or reflection in child which in another place be call the concomitant of prudence and age and not of childhood
it be undoubtedly very right to check an unreasonable appetite and that at its first appearance
but if so small and so reasonable an inducement will prevail surely sir it might be comply with
a generous mind take delight to win over other by good usage and mildness rather than by severity and it must be a great pain to such an one to be always inculcate on his child or pupil the doctrine of self-denial by method quite grievous to his own nature
what i would then humbly propose be that the encouragement offer to youth should indeed be innocent one as the gentleman enjoin and not such as would lead to luxury either of food or apparel but i humbly think it necessary that reward proper reward should be propose as incentive to laudable action for be it not by this method that the whole world be influence and govern
do not god himself by reward and punishment make it our interest as well as our duty to obey he
and can we propose ourselves for the government of our child a well example than that of the creator
this fine author seem to think he have be a little of the strict and liable to some exception
i say not this proceed he section 53 that i would have child keep from the convenience or pleasure of life that be not injurious to their health or virtue
on the contrary i would have their life make as pleasant and as agreeable to they as may be in a plentiful enjoyment of whatsoever might innocently delight they and yet he immediately subjoin a very hard and difficult proviso to this indulgence provide say he it be with this caution that they have those enjoyment only as the consequence of the state of esteem and acceptation they be in with their parent and governor i doubt my dear mr
b this be expect such a distinction and discretion in child as they seldom have in their tender year and require capacity not commonly to be meet with so that it be not prescribe to the generality as this excellent author intend
tis i humbly conceive next to impossible that their tender mind should distinguish beyond fact they covet this or that play-thing and the parent or governor take advantage of its desire and annex to the indulgence such or such a task or duty as a condition and shews himself pleased with its compliance with it so the child win its plaything and receive the commendation so necessary to lead on young mind to laudable pursuit
but shall it not be suffer to enjoy the innocent reward of its compliance unless it can give satisfaction that its great delight be not in have the thing covet but in perform the task or obey the injunction impose upon it as a condition of its being oblige
i doubt sir this be a little too strict and not to be expect from child
a servant full-grown would not be able to shew that on condition he comply with such and such term which it be to be suppose by the offer he would not have comply with but for that inducement he should have such and such a reward i say he would hardly be able to shew that he prefer the pleasure of perform the requisite condition to the stipulate reward
nor be it necessary he should for he be not the less a good servant or a virtuous man if he own the condition painful and the reward necessary to his low state in the world and that otherwise he would not undergo any service at all why then should this be exact from a child
let therefore innocent reward be propose and let we be content to lead on the ductile mind of child to a love of their duty by oblige they with such we may tell they what we expect in this case but we ought not i humbly conceive to be too rigorous in exact it for after all the inducement will naturally be the uppermost consideration with the child not as i hint have it be offer to it if the parent himself have not think so
and therefore we can only let the child know his duty in this respect and that he ought to give a preference to that and then rest ourselves content although we should discern that the reward be the chief incentive of it
for this from whatever motive inculcate may beget a habit in the child of do it and then as it improve in year one may hope that reason will take place and enable he from the most solid and durable motif to give a preference to the duty
upon the whole then can we insist upon it that the child should so nicely distinguish away its little innate passion as if we expect it to be bear a philosopher
self-denial be indeed a most excellent doctrine to be inculcate into child and it must be do early but we must not be too severe in our exact it for a duty too rigidly insist upon will make it odious
this mr
locke too observe in another place on the head of too great severity which he illustrate by a familiar comparison offensive circumstance say he ordinarily infect innocent thing which they be join with
and the very sight of a cup wherein any one use to take nauseous physic turn his stomach so that nothing will relish well out of it though the cup be never so clean and well-shape and of the rich material permit i to add that mr
locke write still more rigorously on the subject of reward which i quote to shew i have not misunderstood he but these enjoyment say he should never be offer or bestow on child as the reward of this or that particular performance that they shew an aversion to or to which they would not have apply themselves without that temptation if dear sir the mind of child can be lead on by innocent inducement to the performance of a duty of which they be capable what i have humbly offer be enough i presume to convince one that it may be do
but if ever a particular study be propose to be master or a bias to be overcome that be not an indispensable requisite to his future life of moral to which the child shew an aversion i would not methink have he be too much tempt or compel to conquer or subdue it especially if it appear to be a natural or rivette aversion
for permit i to observe that the education and study of child ought as much as possible to be suit to their capacity and inclination and by these mean we may expect to have always useful and often great man in different profession for that genius which do not prompt to the prosecution of one study may shine in another no less necessary part of science
but if the promise of innocent reward would conquer this aversion yet they should not be apply with this view for the good consequence that can be hop for will be tolerable skill in one thing instead of most excellent in another
nevertheless i must repeat that if as the child grow up and be capable of so much reason that from the love of the inducement one can raise his mind to the love of the duty it should be do by all mean
but my dear mr
b i be afraid that that parent or tutor will meet with but little success who in a child s tender year shall refuse to comply with its foible till he see it value its duty and the pleasure of obey his command beyond the little enjoyment on which his heart be fix
for as i humbly conceive that mind which can be bring to prefer its duty to its appetite will want little of the perfection of the wise philosopher
besides sir permit to i say that i be afraid this perpetual opposition between the passion of the child and the duty to be enforce especially when it see how other child be indulge for if this regimen could be observe by any it would be impossible it should become general while the fond and the inconsiderate parent be so large a part of mankind will cow and dispirit a child and will perhaps produce a necessity of make use of severity to subdue he to this temper of self-denial for if the child refuse the parent must insist and what will be the consequence
must it not introduce a harsh discipline than this gentleman allow of and which i presume to say do never yet do good to any but to slavish and base spirit if to they a discipline which mr
locke every where justly condemn
see here dear sir a speciman of the presumption of your girl what will she come to in time you will perhaps say her next step will be to arraign myself no no dear sir donot think so for my duty my love and my reverence shall be your guard and defend you from every thing saucy in i but the bold approach of my gratitude winch shall always testify for i how much i be your obliged and dutiful servant p b
letter xcii my dearest mr
b i will continue my subject although i have not have an opportunity to know whether you approve of my notion or not by reason of the excursion you have be pleased to allow i to make in your beloved company to the sea-port of this kingdom and to the more noted inland town of essex kent sussex hampshire and dorsetshire which have give i infinite delight and pleasure and enlarge my notion of the wealth and power of the kingdom in which god s goodness have give you so considerable a stake
my next topic will be upon a home education which mr
locke prefer for several weighty reason to a school one provide such a tutor can be procure as he make next to an impossibility to procure
the gentleman have set forth the inconveniencie of both and be himself so discouraged on a review of they that he be ready as he say to throw up his pen
my chief care dear sir on this head be three 1 st the difficulty which as i say mr
locke make almost insuperable to find a qualified tutor
2 ndly the necessity there be accord to mr
locke of keep the youth out of the company of the mean servant who may set he bad example
and 3 rdly those still great difficulty which will arise from the example of his parent if they be not very discreet and circumspect
as to the qualification of the tutor mr
locke suppose that he be to be so learn so discreet so wise in short so perfect a man that i doubt and so do mr
locke such an one can hardly be meet with for this humble and slavish employment
i presume sir to call it so because of the too little regard that be generally pay to these useful man in the family of the great where they be frequently put upon a foot with the uppermost servant and the rather if they happen to be man of modesty
i would say he from child s first begin to talk have some discreet sober nay wise person about they whose care it should be to fashion they right and to keep they from all ill especially the infection of bad company
i think this province require great sobriety temperance tenderness diligence and discretion quality hardly to be find united in person that be to be have for ordinary salary nor easily to be find any where if this sir be the case do not this excellent author recommend a scheme that be render in a manner impracticable from this difficulty
as to these quality be more rarely to be meet with in person that be to be have for ordinary salary i cannot help be of opinion although with mr
locke i think no expence should be spar if that would do that there be as good a chance for find a proper person among the needy scholar if not of a low and sordid turn of mind as among the more affluent because the narrow circumstance of the former which probably become a spur to his own improvement will it be likely at first set out in the world make he be glad to embrace such an offer in a family which have interest enough to prefer he and will quicken his diligence to make he deserve preferment and if such an one want any of that requisite politeness which some would naturally expect from scholar of well fortune might not that be supply to the youth by the conversation of parent relation and visitor in conjunction with those other help which young man of family and large expectation constantly have and which few learn tutor can give he
i say not this to countenance the wretched niggardliness which this gentleman justly censures of those who grudge a handsome consideration to so necessary and painful a labour as that of a tutor which where a deserve man can be meet with cannot be too genteelly reward nor himself too respectfully treat
i only beg to deliver my opinion that a low condition be as likely as any other with a mind not ungenerous to produce a man who have these good quality as well for the reason i have hint at as for other which might be mention
but mr
locke thus proceed to form a young gentleman as he should be tis fit his governor should be well breed understand the way of carriage and measure of civility in all the variety of person time and place and keep his pupil as far as his age require constantly to the observation of they
this be an art not to be learn or teach by books nothe can give it but good company and observation join together and in another place say besides be well-breed the tutor should know the world well the way the humour the folly the cheat the fault of the age he have fall into and particularly of the country he live in these he should be able to shew to his pupil as he find he capable teach he skill in man and their manner pull off the mask which their several calling and pretence cover they with and make his pupil discern what lie at the bottom under such appearance that he may not as unexperienced young man be apt to do if they be unwarned take one thing for another judge by the outside and give himself up to show and the insinuation of a fair carriage or an oblige application teach he to guess at and beware of the design of man he hath to do with neither with too much suspicion nor too much confidence this dear sir be excellently say tis noble theory and if the tutor be a man void of resentment and caprice and will not be govern by partial consideration in his own judgment of person and thing all will be well but if otherwise may he not take advantage of the confidence place in he to the injury of some worthy person and by degree monopolize the young gentleman to himself and govern his passion as absolutely as i have hear some first minister have do those of their prince equally to his own personal disreputation and to the disadvantage of his people
but all this and much more accord to mr
locke be the duty of a tutor and on the finding out such an one depend his scheme of a home education
no wonder then that he himself say when i consider the scruple and caution i here lie in your way methink it look as if i advise you to something which i would have offer at but in effect not do c permit i dear sir in this place to express my fear that it be hardly possible for any one with talent inferior to those of mr
locke himself to come up to the rule he have lay down upon this subject and tis to be question whether even he with all that vast stock of natural reason and solid sense for which as you tell i sir he be so famous have attain to these perfection at his first set out into life
now therefore dear sir you cannot imagine how these difficulty perplex i as to my know how to judge which be good a home or a school education
for hear what this excellent author justly observe on the latter among other thing no less to the purpose i be sure he who be able to be at the charge of a tutor at home may there give his son a more genteel carriage more manly thought and a sense of what be worthy and become with a great proficiency in learning into the bargain and ripen he up soon into a man than any school can do
not that i blame the schoolmaster in this say he or think it to be lay to his charge
the difference be great between two or three pupil in the same house and three or four score boy lodge up and down for let the master s industry and skill be never so great it be impossible he should have fifty or an hundred scholar under his eye any long than they be in the school together but then sir if there be such a difficulty as mr
locke say to meet with a proper tutor for the home education which he thus prefer what a perplexing thing be this
but still accord to this gentleman another difficulty attend a home education and that be what i hint at before in my second article the necessity of keep the youth out of the company of the mean servant who may set he bad example
for thus he say here be another great inconvenience which child receive from the ill example which they meet with from the mean servant
they be wholly if possible to be keep from such conversation for the contagion of these ill precedent both in civility and virtue horribly infect child as often as they come within the reach of it
they frequently learn from unbred or debauched servant such language untowardly trick and vice as otherwise they would be ignorant of all their life
tis a hard matter wholly to prevent this mischief continue he you will have very good luck if you never have a clownish or vicious servant and if from they your child never get any infection then sir my third point which i mention in the beginning of this letter make a still strong objection as it may happen against a home education to wit the example of the parent themselves if they be not very circumspect and discreet
all these difficulty be put together let i dear sir humbly propose it as a matter for your consideration and determination whether there be not a middle way to be find out in a school education that may remedy some of these inconveniencie
for suppose you cannot get a tutor so qualified as mr
locke think he ought to be for your billy as he grow up
suppose there be danger from your mean servant or we his parent should not be able to lay ourselves under the requisite restraint in order to form his mind by our own example which i hope by god s grace however will not be the case--cannot some master be find who shall be so well reward for his care of a few young gentleman as to make it worth his while to be content with those few suppose from five to eight at most whose moral and breeding he may attend to as well as to their learning
the far this master live from the young gentleman s friend the well it may be
we will hope that he be a man of a mild disposition but strict in his discipline and who shall make it a rule not to give correction for small fault or till every other method have be try who carry such a just dignity in his manner without the appearance of tyranny that his look may be of great force than the blow of other and who will rather endeavour to shame than terrify a youth out of his fault
then suppose this gentleman be to allot a particular portion of time for the more learn study and before the youth be tired with they suppose another portion be allot for the write and arithmetic and then to relieve his mind from both suppose the dancing-master should take his part and innocent exercise of mere diversion to fill up the rest at his own choice in which divert by such a rotation of employment all thus render delightful by their successive variety he would hardly wish to pass much time
for the dancing of itself with the dancing-master s instruction if a well-breed man will answer both part that of breeding and that of exercise and thus different study at once be master
moreover the emulation which will be inspire where there be several young gentleman will be of inconceivable use both to tutor and pupil in lessen the trouble of the one and advance the learning of the other which cannot be expect where there be but a single youth to be take care of
such a master will know it to be his interest as well as duty to have a watchful eye over the conduct and behaviour of his servant
his assistant in the different branch of science and education will be person of approve prudence for whom he will think himself answerable since his own reputation as well as livelihood will depend upon their behaviour
the youth will have young gentleman for their companion all under the influence of the same precept and direction and if some choose period be fix as a reward for some excellence where at a little desk raise a step or two above the other seat the excelling youth should be set to read under the master s direction a little portion from the good translation of the greek and roman historian and even from the good english author this might in a very engaging manner initiate they into the knowledge of the history of past time and of their own country and give they a curiosity to pass some of their vacant hour in the same laudable pursuit for dear sir i must still insist that reward and innocent gratification as also little honour and distinction must need be very attractive to the mind of youth
for be not the pretty ride and dairy house breakfasting by which miss goodwin s governess distinguish the little lady who excel in their allotted task a fine encouragement to their ductile minds yes it be to be sure and i have often think of it with pleasure and partake of the delight with which i have suppose their pretty heart must be fill with on that occasion
and why may not such little triumph be in proportion as incentive to child to make they try to master laudable task as the roman triumph of different kind and their mural and civic crown all which i have hear you speak of be to their hero and warrior of old
for mr
dryden well observe that-- man be but child of a large growth our appetite be apt to change as theirs and full as crave too and full as vain permit i
sir to transcribe four or five line more for the beauty of the thought and yet the soul shut up in her dark room view so clear abroad at home see nothing but like a mole in earth busy and blind work all her folly up and cast it outward to the world s open view-- improve the thought methink i can see the dear little miss who have in some eminent task bear away the palm make her public entry as i may call it after her dairy breakfast and pretty airing into the governess s court-yard through a row of her school-fellow draw out on each side to admire she her governess and assistant receive she at the porch their little capitol and lift she out with applause and encomium with a thus shall it be do to the miss whom her governess delighteth to honour i see not why the dear miss in this case as she move through her admire school-fellow may not have her little heart beat with as much delight be as gloriously elated proportionably as that of the great hero in his triumphal car who have return from exploit perhaps much less laudable
but how i ramble yet surely sir you donot expect method or connection from your girl
the education of our sex will not permit that where it be good
we be force to struggle for knowledge like the poor feeble infant in the month who be pin and fetter down upon the nurse s lap and who if its little arm happen by chance to escape its nurse s observation and offer but to expand themselves be immediately take into custody and pinion down to their passive behaviour
so when a poor girl in spite of her narrow education break out into notice her genius be immediately tame by trifle employment lest perhaps she should become the envy of one sex and the equal of the other
but you
sir act more nobly with your pamela for you throw in her way all opportunity of improvement and she have only to regret that she cannot make a well use of they and of consequence render herself more worthy of your generous indulgence
i know not how sir to recover my thread and so must break off with that delight which i always take when i come near the bottom of my letter to your dear self because then i can boast of the honour which i have in be your ever dutiful p b
letter xciii well but my dear mr
b you will perhaps think from my last rambling letter that i be most inclined to a school education for your billy and some year hence if it should please god to spare he to we
yet i cannot say that i be i only lie several thing together in my usual indigested way to take your opinion upon which as it ought will be always decisive with i

in my observation on this head i shall take the liberty in one or two particular a little to differ from an author that i admire exceedingly and that be the present design of my write these letter for i shall hereafter if god spare my life in my little book when you have kindly decide upon the point in which i presume to differ shew you sir my great reverence and esteem for he and can then let you know all my sentiment on this important subject and that more undoubtedly as i shall be more improve by year and your conversation especially sir if i have the honour and happiness of a foreign tour with you of which you give i hope so much be you pleased with the delight i take in these improve excursion which you have now favour i with at different time through more than half the kingdom
well then sir i will proceed to consider a little more particularly the subject of a home education with an eye to those difficulty of which mr

as to the first that of find a qualified tutor we must not expect so much perfection i doubt as he lay down as necessary
what therefore i humbly conceive be good to be do will be to avoid choose a man of bigoted and narrow principle who yet shall not be taint with sceptical or heterodox notion nor a mere scholar or pedant who have travel and yet preserve his moral character untainte and whose behaviour and carriage be easy unaffected unformal and genteel as well acquiredly as naturally so if possible who shall not be dogmatical positive overbearing on one hand nor too yielding suppliant fawn on the other who shall study the child s natural bent in order to direct his study to the point he be most likely to excel in and to preserve the respect due to his own character from every one he must not be a busy body in the family a whisperer a tale-bearer but of a benevolent turn of mind ready to compose difference who shall avoid of all thing that foppishness of dress and appearance which distinguish the petit-maitre and french usher that i have see at some boarding school for coxcomb rather than guide of education for as i have hear you my good tutor often observe the peculiarity of habit where a person aim at something fantastic or out of character be an undoubted sign of a wrong head for such a one be so kind as always to hang out on his sign what sort of furniture he have in his shop to save you the trouble of ask question about he so that one may as easily know by his outward appearance what he be as one can know a widow by her weed
such a person as i have thus negatively describe may be find without very much difficulty perhaps because some of these requisite be personal and other be such as be obvious at first sight to a common penetration or where not so may be find out by inquiry into his general character and behaviour and to the care of such a one dear sir let i suppose your billy be commit and so we acquit ourselves of the first difficulty as well as we can that of the tutor who to become more perfect may form himself as to what he want by mr
locke s excellent rule on that head
but before i quit this subject i beg to remind you of your opinion upon it in a conversation with sir george stuart and his nephew in london in which you seem to prefer a scottish gentleman for a tutor to those of your own nation and still more than to those of france
donot you remember it dear sir
and how much those gentleman be pleased with your facetious freedom with their country and say you make they amend for that in your preference to their learn and travel youth
if you have forget it i will here transcribe it from my record as i call my book of memorandum for every time i be pleased with a conversation and have leisure before it quit my memory i enter it down in as near the very word as i can and now you have make i your correspondent i shall sometimes perhaps give you back some valuable from your own treasure miss darnford and mr
turner and mr
fanshaw be present i well remember
these be your word since the union of the two kingdom we have many person of condition who have take their tutor for their son from scotland which practice to speak impartially have be attend with some advantageous circumstance that should not be overlook
for sir george it must be confess that notwithstanding your narrow and stiff manner of education in scotland a spirit of manly learning a kind of poetic liberty as i may call it have begin to exert itself in that part of the island
the bluster north--forgive i gentleman--seem to have harden the forehead of her hungry son and the keenness with which they set out for preferment in the kindlier south have teach they to know a good deal of the world betime
through the easy term on which learning be generally attain there as it be early inculcated so it may probably take deep root and since tis hardly possible--forgive i dear sirs--they can go to a bad country on this side greenland than some of the northern part of scotland so their education with a view to travel and to better themselves by settlement in other country may perhaps be so many reason to take great pain to qualify themselves for this employment and may make they succeed well in it especially when they have be able to shake off the fetter which be rivette upon they under the narrow influence of a too tyrannical kirk discipline which you sir george have just now so freely censure
to these consideration when we add the necessity which these remote tutor lie under of behave well first because they seldom wish to return to their own country and next because that cannot prefer they if it would and thirdly because it would not if it could if the gentleman be of an enlarged genius and generous way of thinking i say when we add to the premise these consideration they all make a kind of security for their good behaviour while those of our own country have often friend or acquaintance on whose favour they be apt to depend and for that reason give less attention to the duty requisite for this important office
besides as their kind friend aeolus who be accustomed to spread and strengthen the bold muscle of the strong-feature scot have generally blow away that inauspicious bashfulness which hang a much long time commonly on the face of the southern student such a one if he fall not too egregiously into the contrary extreme so as to become insufferable may still be the more eligible person for a tutor as he may teach a young gentleman betime that necessary presence of mind which those who be confine to a private education sometimes want
but after all if a gentleman of this nation be choose for this employment it may be necessary that he should be one who have have as genteel and free an education himself as his country will afford and the native roughness of his climate file off by travel and conversation who have make at least the tour of france and italy and have a taste for the politeness of the former nation but from the boisterousness of a north britain and the fantastic politeness of a frenchman if happily blend such a mixture may result as will furnish out a more complete tutor than either of the two nation singly may be able to produce
but it ought to be remember that this person must have conquer his native brogue as i may call it and be a master of the english pronunciation otherwise his conversation will be disagreeable to an english ear
and permit i to add that as an acquaintance with the muses contribute not a little to soften the manner and give a graceful and delicate turn to the imagination and a kind of polish to severer study it would not be amiss that he should have a taste of poetry although perhaps it be not to be wish he have such strong inclination that way as to make that lively and delectable amusement his predominant passion for we see very few poet whose warm imagination do not run away with their judgment
and yet in order to learn the dead language in their purity it will be necessary to inculcate both the love and the study of the ancient poet which cannot fail of give the youth a taste for poetry in general permit i dear sir to ask you whether you advance this for argument sake as sometimes you love to amuse and entertain your friend in an uncommon way
for i should imagine that our two university which you have shew i and for which i have ever since have a great reverence than i have before be capable of furnish as good tutor as any nation in the world for here the young gentleman seem to i to live both in the world and in the university and we see several gentleman who have not only fine part but polite behaviour and deep learning as you assure i some of whom you entertain and be entertain by in so elegant a manner that no travelled gentleman if i may be allow to judge could excel they
and besides my dear mr
b i know who be reckon one of the politest and well-breed gentleman in england by every body and learn as well as polite and yet have his education in one of those celebrated seat of learn
i wish your billy may never fall short of the gentleman i mean in all these acquirement and he will be a very happy creature i be sure
but how i wander again from my subject
