letter xciv dearest sir i now resume my subject
i have go through the article of the tutor as well as i could and will now observe upon what mr
locke say that child be wholly if possible to be keep from the conversation of the mean servant whom he suppose to be as too frequently they be unbre and debauch to use his own word
now sir i think it be very difficult to keep child from the conversation of servant at all time
the care of personal attendance especially in the child s early age must fall upon servant of one denomination or other who little or much must be conversant with the inferior servant and so be liable to be taint by their conversation and it will be difficult in this case to prevent the taint be communicate to the child
wherefore it will be a surer as well as a more laudable method to insist upon the regular behaviour of the whole family than to expect the child and its immediate attendant or tutor should be the only good one in it
nor be this so difficult to effect as may be imagine
your family afford an eminent instance of it the good have be confirm the remiss have be reform the passionate have be tame and there be not a family in the kingdom i will venture to say to the honour of every individual in it more uniform more regular and free from evil and more regardful of what they say and do than your
and you will allow that though always honest yet they be not always so laudable so exemplarily virtuous as of late which i mention only to shew the practicableness of a reformation even where bad habit have take place--for your pamela sir arrogate not to herself the honour of this change tis owe to the divine grace shin upon heart naturally good for else an example so easy so plain so simple from so young a mistress who moreover have be exalt from their own station could not have be attend with such happy effect
you see dear sir what a master and mistress s example could do with a poor soul so far go as mrs
jewkes
and i dare be confident that if on the hiring of a new servant sobriety of manner and a virtuous conversation be insist upon and a general inoffensiveness in word as well as action be require from they as indispensable condition of their service and that a breach of that kind would be no more pass over than a wilful fraud or an act of dishonesty and if add to these requisite their principal take care to support these injunction by their own example i say then i dare be confident that if such a service do not find they good it would make they so
and why should we not think this a very practicable scheme consider the servant we take be at year of discretion and have the strong tie of interest superadde to the obligation we require of they
and which they must need know let them have what bad habit they will be right for themselves to discharge as well as for we to exact
we all know of how much force the example of superior be to inferior
it be too justly say that the court of prince abound with the most profligate of man insomuch that a man cannot well have a more significantly bad title than that of courtier yet even among these one shall see the force of example as i have hear you sir frequently observe for let but the land be bl with a pious and religious prince who make it a rule with he to countenance and promote man of virtue and probity and to put the case still strong let such a one even succeed to the most libertine reign wherein the manner of the people be wholly deprave yet a wonderful change will be immediately effect
the flagitious liver will be chase away or reform or at least will think it their duty or their interest which be a strong tie with such to appear reform and not a man will seek for the favour or countenance of his prince but by laudable pretence or by worthy action
in the reign of king richard iii as i have read deformity of body be the fashion and the nobility and gentry of the court think it an indispensable requisite of a graceful form to pad for themselves a round shoulder because the king be crook
and can we think human nature so absurdly wicked that it would not much rather have try to imitate a personal perfection than a deformity so shocking in its appearance in people who be naturally straight
tis melancholy to reflect that of all profession of man the mariner who most behold the wonder of almighty power display in the great deep a sight that have strike i with awe and reverence only from a coast prospect and who every moment while at sea have but one frail plank betwixt themselves and inevitable destruction be yet generally speak say to be the most abandon invoker and blasphemer of the name of that god whose mercy they every moment unthankfully although so visibly experience
yet as i once hear at your table sir on a particular occasion we have now a commander in the british navy who to his honour have shew the force of an excellent example support the good precept for on board of his ship not an oath or curse be to be hear while volley of both issue from impious mouth in the same squadron out of his knowledge seem to fill the sail of other ship with guilty breath call aloud for that perdition to overtake they which perhaps his worthy injunction and example in his own might be of weight to suspend
if such then dear sir be the force of a good example what have parent to do who would bring up a child at home under their own eye accord to mr
locke s advice but first to have a strict regard to their conduct
this will not want its due influence on the servant especially if a proper enquiry be first make into their character and a watchful eye have over they to keep they up to those character afterwards
and when they know they must forfeit the favour of a worthy master and their place too which may be think to be the good of place because an uniform character must make all around it easy and happy they will readily observe such rule and direction as shall be prescribe to they--rule and direction which their own conscience will tell they be right to be prescribe and even right for they to follow be they not insist upon by their superior and this conviction must go a great way towards their thorough reformation for a person wholly convince be half reform
and thus the hazard a child will run of be corrupt by converse with the servant will be remove and all mr
locke s other rule be well enforce
i have the boldness sir to make another objection and that be to the distance which mr
locke prescribe to be keep between child and servant for may not this be a means to fill the mind of the former with a contempt of those below they and an arrogance that be not warrant by any rank or condition to their inferior of the same specie
i have before transcribe what mr
locke have enjoin in relation to this distance where he say that the child be by all mean to be keep wholly from the conversation of the mean servant
but how much well advice do the same author give for the behaviour of child to servant in the following word which i humbly think be not so entirely consistent with the former as might be expect from so admirable an author
another way say he section 111 to instil sentiment of humanity and to keep they lively in young folk will be to accustom they to civility in their language and deportment towards their inferior and mean sort of people particularly servant
it be not unusual to observe the child in gentleman s family treat the servant of the house with domineering word name of contempt and an imperious carriage as if they be of another race or specie beneath they
whether ill example the advantage of fortune or their natural vanity inspire this haughtiness it should be prevent or weed out and a gentle courteous affable carriage towards the low rank of man place in the room of it
no part of their superiority will be hereby lose but the distinction increase and their authority strengthen when love in inferior be join to outward respect and the esteem of the person have a share in their submission and domestic will pay a more ready and cheerful service when they find themselves not spurn because fortune have lay they below the level of other at their master s foot these dear sir be certainly the sentiment of a generous and enlarge spirit but i hope i may observe that the great distance mr
locke before enjoin to be keep between child and servant be not very consistent with the above-cite paragraph for if we would prevent this undue contempt of inferior in the temper of child the good way as i humbly presume to think be not to make it so unpardonable a fault for they especially in their early year to be in their company
for can one make the child shun the servant without render they odious or contemptible to they and represent they to the child in such disadvantageous light as must need make the servant vile in their eye and themselves lofty and exalt in their own
and thereby cause they to treat they with domineering word and an imperious carriage as if they be of another race or specie beneath they and so as mr
locke say nurse up their natural pride into an habitual contempt of those beneath they and then as he add where will that probably end but in oppression and cruelty but this matter dear sir i presume to think will all be happily accommodate and reconcile when the servant good behaviour be secure by the example and injunction of the principal
upon the whole then of what mr
locke have enjoin and what i have take the liberty to suggest on this head it shall be my endeavour in that early part of your dear billy s education which you will intrust to i to inculcate betime in his mind the principle of universal benevolence and kindness to other especially to inferior
nor shall i fear that the little dear will be want to himself in assume as he grow up an air of superiority and distance of behaviour equal to his condition or that he will descend too low for his station
for sir there be a pride and self-love natural to human mind that will seldom be keep so low as to make they humbler than they ought to be
i have observe before now instance of this in some of the family we visit between the young masters or misses and those child of low degree who have be bring to play with they or divert they
on the masters and misses side i have always see they lead the play and prescribe the law of it be the diversion what it will while on the other hand their low-rank play-fellow have generally give into their little humour though ever so contrary to their own and the difference of dress and appearance and the notion they have of the more eminent condition of their play-fellow parent have beget in they a kind of awe and respect that perhaps more than sufficiently secure the superiority of the one and the subordination of the other
the advantage of this universal benevolence to a young gentleman as he grow up will be as i humbly conceive so to diffuse itself over his mind as to influence all his action and give a grace to every thing he do or say and make he admire and respect from the good and most durable motif and will be of great advantage to he for his attain a handsome address and behaviour for it will make he conscious that he merit the distinction he will meet with and encourage he still more to merit it than the good rule that can be give he for that purpose
i will therefore teach the little dear courteousness and affability from the properest motif i be able to think of and will instruct he in only one piece of pride that of be above do a mean or low action
i will caution he not to behave in a lordly or insolent manner even to the low servant
i will tell he that that superiority be the most commendable and will be the well maintain which be owe to humanity and kindness and ground on the perfection of the mind rather than on the accidental advantage of fortune and condition that if his conduct be such as it ought to be there will be no occasion to tell a servant that he will be observe and respect that humility as i once tell my miss goodwin be a charming grace and most conspicuously charming in person of distinction for that the poor who be humble by their condition cannot glory in it as the rich may and that it make the low rank of people love and admire the high-bear who can so condescend whereas pride in such be meanness and insult as it owe its boast and its be to accidental advantage which at the same time be seldom of his procure who can be so mean as to be proud that even i would soon forget pride in a low degree than in a high for it may be a security in the first against do a base thing but in the rich it be a base thing itself and an impolitic one too for the more distinction a proud mind grasp at the less it will have and every poor despise person can whisper such a one in the ear when surround with and adorn by all his glitter splendour that he be bear and must die in the same manner with those whom he despise
thus will the doctrine of benevolence and affability implant early in the mind of a young gentleman and duly cultivate as he grow up inspire he with the requisite conduct to command respect from proper motif and while it will make the servant observe a decorum towards he it will oblige they to have a guard upon their word and action in presence of one whose manner of education and training-up would be so great a reproach to they if they be grossly faulty so thus i conceive a mutual benefit will flow to the manner of each and his good behaviour will render he in some measure an instructive monitor to the whole family
but permit i sir to enlarge on the hint i have already give in relation to the example of parent in case a preference be give to the home education
for if this point cannot be secure i should always imagine it be good to put the child to such a school as i formerly mention
but yet the subject might be spar by i in this case as i write with a view only to your family though you will remember that while i follow mr
locke whose work be public i must be consider as direct myself to the generality of the world for sir i have the pleasure to say that your conduct in your family be unexceptionable and the pride to think that mine be no disgrace to it
no one hear a word from your mouth unbecome the character of a polite gentleman and i shall always be very regardful of what fall from mine
your temper sir be equal and kind to all your servant and they love you as well as awfully respect you and well do your beautiful and considerate mind deserve it of they all and they see i be watchful over my own conduct so as not to behave unworthy of your kind example regard i as much as i could wish they should for well do they know that their beloved master will have it so and greatly honour and esteem i himself
your table-talk be such as person of the strict principle may hear and join in your guest and your friend be generally speak person of the genteelest life and of the good manner
so that mr
locke would have advise you of all gentleman have he be live and know you to give your child a home education and assign these and still strong reason for it
but be we to speak to the generality of parent i fear this would be an almost insuperable objection to a home education
for i be sorry to say it when one turn one s eye to the bad precedent give by the head of some family it be hardly to be wonder at that there be so little virtue and religion among man
for can those parent be surprised at the ungraciousness of their child who hardly ever shew they that their own action be govern by reasonable or moral motif
can the gluttonous father expect a self-deny son
with how ill a grace must a man who will often be disguise in liquor preach sobriety
a passionate man patience
an irreligious man piety
how will a parent whose hand be seldom without card or dice in they be observe in lesson against the pernicious vice of gaming
can the profuse father who be squander away the fortune of his child expect to be regard in a lesson of frugality
tis impossible he should except it be that the youth see how pernicious his father s example be should have the grace to make a proper use of it and look upon it as a sea-mark as it be to enable he to shun the dangerous rock on which he see his father split
and even in this well case let it be consider how much shame and disgrace his thoughtless parent ought to take to himself who can admonish his child by nothing but the odiousness of his own vice and how little it be owe to he that his guilt be not double by his son s treading in his step
let such an unhappy parent duly weigh this and think how likely he be to be by his bad example the cause of his child s perdition as well as his own and stand unshocked and unamended if he can
it be then of no avail to wish for discreet servant if the conduct of the parent be faulty
if the fountain-head be pollute how shall the under-current run clear
that master and mistress who would exact from their servant a behaviour which they themselves donot practice will be but ill observe
and that child who discover excess and error in his parent will be find to be less profit by their good precept than prejudice by bad example
excessive fondness this hour violent passion and perhaps execration the next unguarded jest and admiration of fashionable vanity rash censure be perhaps the good that the child see in or hear from those who be most concerned to inculcate good precept into his mind
and where it be so a home education must not surely be choose
have thus as well as my slender ability will permit presume to deliver my opinion upon three great point viz
the qualification of a tutor the necessity of have an eye to the moral of servant and the example of parent all which be take care of will give a preference as i imagine to a home education permit i dear sir to speak a little far to a point that i have already touch upon
it be that of emulation which i humbly conceive to be of great efficacy to lead child on in their duty and study
and how dear sir shall this advantage be procure for a young master who have no school-fellow and who have no example to follow but that of his tutor whom he cannot from the disparity of year and other circumstance without pain because of this disparity think of emulate
and this i conceive be a very great advantage to such a school education as i mention in my former letter where there be no more scholar take in than the master can with ease and pleasure instruct
but one way in my humble opinion be leave to answer this objection and still preserve the reason for the preference which mr
locke give to a home education and that be what i formerly hint to take into your family the child of some honest neighbour of but middling circumstance and like age of your own but who should give apparent indication of his natural promptitude ingenuous temper oblige behaviour and good manner and to let he go hand-in-hand with your in his several study and lesson under the same tutor
the child would be sensible of the benefit as well as of the distinction he receive and consequently of what be expect from he and would double his diligence and exert all his good quality which would inspire the young gentleman with the wish-for emulation and as i imagine would be so promotive of his learning that it would greatly compensate the tutor for his pain with the additional scholar for the young gentleman would be ashamed to be outdo by one of like year and stature with himself
and little reward might be propose to the great proficient in order to heighten the emulation
then sir the generosity of such a method to a gentleman of your fortune and beneficent mind would be its own reward be there no other benefit to be receive from it
moreover such an ingenious youth might by his good moral and industry hereafter be of service in some place of trust in the family or it would be easy for a gentleman of your interest in the world if such a thing offer not to provide for the youth in the navy in some of the public office or among your private friend
if he prove faulty in his moral his dismission would be in your own power and would be punishment enough
but if on the other hand he prove a sober and hopeful youth he would make an excellent companion for your billy in riper year as he would be in a manner a corroborator of his moral for as his circumstance would not support he in any extravagance so they would be a check upon his inclination and this be second by the hope of future preferment from your favour and interest which he could not expect but upon the term of his perseverance in virtue he would find himself under a necessity of set such an example as might be of great benefit to his companion who should be watch as he grow up that he do not if his ample fortune become dangerous to his virtue contribute out of his affluence to draw the other after he into extravagance
and to this end as i humbly conceive the noble doctrine of independence should be early instill into both their mind and upon all occasion inculcate and inforced which would be an inducement for the one to endeavour to improve his fortune by his honest industry lest he never be enable to rise out of a state of dependence and to the other to keep if not to improve his own lest he ever fall into such a servile state and thereby lose the glorious power of confer happiness on the deserving one of the high pleasure that a generous mind can know a pleasure sir which you have oftener experience than thousand of gentleman and which may you still continue to experience for a long and happy succession of year be the prayer of one the most obliged of all other in her own person as well as in the person of her dearest relation and who owe to this glorious beneficence the honour she boast of be your ever affectionate and grateful p b
letter xcv but now my dear mr
b if you will indulge i in a letter or two more preparative to my little book i will take the liberty to touch upon one or two other place wherein i differ from this learn gentleman
but first permit i to observe that if parent be above all thing to avoid give bad example to their child they will be no less careful to shun the practice of such fond father and mother as be willnot to indulge their child in bad habit and give they their head at a time when like wax their tender mind may be mould into what shape they please
this be a point that if it please god i will carefully attend to because it be the foundation on which the superstructure of the whole future man be to be erect
for accord as he be indulge or check in his childish folly a ground be lay for his future happiness or misery and if once they be suffer to become habitual to he it cannot but be expect that they will grow up with he and that they will hardly ever be eradicate
try it say mr
locke speak to this very point in a dog or a horse or any other creature and see whether the ill and resty trick they have learn when young be easily to be mend when they be knit and yet none of these creature be half so wilful and proud or half so desirous to be master of themselves as man and this bring i dear sir to the head of punishment in which as well as in the article of reward which i have touch upon i have a little objection to what mr
locke advance
but permit i however to premise that i be exceedingly pleased with the method lay down by this excellent writer rather to shame the child out of his fault than beat he which latter serve generally for nothing but to harden his mind
obstinacy and tell a lie and commit a wilful fault and then persist in it be i agree with this gentleman the only cause for which the child should be punish with stripe and i admire the reason he give against a too rigorous and severe treatment of child
but i will give mr
locke s word to which i have some objection
it may be doubt say he concern whipping when as the last remedy it come to be necessary at what time and by whom it should be do whether presently upon the commit the fault whilst it be yet fresh and hot
i think it should not be do presently add he lest passion mingle with it and so though it exceed the just proportion yet it lose of its due weight
for even child discern whenever we do thing in a passion i must beg leave dear sir to differ from mr
locke in this point for i think it ought rather to be a rule with parent who shall chastise their child to conquer what would be extreme in their own passion on this occasion for those who cannot do it be very unfit to be the punisher of the wayward passion of their child than to defer the punishment especially if the child know its fault have reach its parent s ear
it be otherwise methink give the child if of an obstinate disposition so much more time to harden its mind and bid defiance to its punishment
just now dear sir your billy be bring into my presence all smile crow to come to i and full of heart-cheer promise and the subject i be upon go to my heart
surely i can never beat your billy dear little life of my life
how can i think thou canst ever deserve it or that i can ever inflict it no my baby that shall be thy papa s task if ever thou art so heinously naughty and whatever he do must be right
pardon my foolish fondness dear sir i will proceed
if then the fault be so atrocious as to deserve whipping and the parent be resolve on this exemplary punishment the child ought not as i imagine to come into one s presence without meet with it or else a fondness too natural to be resist will probably get the upper hand of one s resentment and how shall one be able to whip the dear creature one have cease to be angry with
then after he have once see one without meet his punishment will he not be incline to hope for connivance at his fault unless it should be repeat
and may he not be apt for child s resentment be strong to impute to cruelty a correction when he think the fault have be forget that should always appear to be inflict with reluctance and through motif of love
if from anger at his fault one should go above the due proportion i be sure i might be trust for this let it take its course how barbarously methink i speak he ought to feel the lash first because he deserve it poor little soul
next because it be propose to be exemplary
and lastly because it be not intend to be often use and the very passion or displeasure one express if it be not enormous will shew one be in earnest and create in he a necessary awe and fear to offend again
the end of the correction be to shew he the difference between right and wrong
and as it be proper to take he at his first offer of a full submission and repentance and not before and instantly dispassionate one s self and shew he the difference by act of pardon and kindness which will let he see that one punish he out of necessity rather than choice so one would not be afraid to make he smart so sufficiently that he should not soon forget the severity of the discipline nor the disgrace of it
therebe a cruel mamma for you mr
b
what my practice may be i cannot tell but this theory i presume to think be right
as to the act itself i much approve mr
locke s advice to do it by pause mingle stripe and expostulation together to shame and terrify the more and the rather as the parent by this slow manner of inflict the punishment will less need to be afraid of give too violent a correction for those pause will afford he as well as the child opportunity for consideration and reflection
but as to the person by whom the discipline should be perform i humbly conceive that this excellent author be here also to be object to
if you have a discreet servant say he capable of it and have the place of govern your child for if you have a tutor there be no doubt i think it be good the smart should come immediately from another s hand though by the parent s order who should see it do whereby the parent s authority will be preserve and the child s aversion for the pain it suffer rather be turn on the person that immediately inflict it
for i would have a father seldom strike a child but upon very urgent necessity and as the last remedy tis in such an urgent case that we be suppose that it should be do at all
if there be not a reason strong enough for the father s whip the child himself there cannot be one for his ordering another to do it and stand by to see it do
but i humbly think that if there be a necessity no one can be so fit as the father himself to do it
the child cannot dispute his authority to punish from whom he receive and expect all the good thing of his life he cannot question his love to he and after the smart be over and his obedience secure must believe that so tender so indulgent a father could have no other end in whip he but his good
against he he know he have no remedy but must passively submit and when he be convince he must he will in time conclude that he ought
but to have this severe office perform by a servant though at the father s command and that professedly that the aversion of the child for the pain it suffer should be turn on the person who immediately inflict it be i humbly think the reverse of what ought to be do
and more so if this servant have any direction of the child s education and still much more so if it be his tutor though mr
locke say there be no doubt if there be a tutor that it should be do by he
for dear sir be there no doubt that the tutor should lay himself open to the aversion of the child whose manner he be to form
be not the good method a tutor can take in order to enforce the lesson he would inculcate to try to attract the love and attention of his pupil by the most win way he can possibly think of
and yet be he this very tutor out of all doubt to be the instrument of do an harsh and disgraceful thing and that in the last resort when all other method be find ineffectual and that too because he ought to incur the child s resentment and aversion rather than the father
no surely sir it be not reasonable it should be so quite contrary in my humble notion there can be no doubt but that it should be otherwise
it should methink be enough for a tutor in case of a fault in the child to threaten to complain to his father but yet not to make such a complaint without the child obstinately persist in his error which too should be of a nature to merit such an appeal and this might highly contribute to preserve the parent s authority who on this occasion should never fail of extort a promise of amendment or of instantly punish he with his own hand
and to soften the distaste he might conceive in resentment of too rigid complaining it might not be amiss that his interposition in the child s favour be the fault not too flagrant should be permit to save he once or twice from the impending discipline
tis certain that the passion if i may so call they of affection and aversion be very early discoverable in child insomuch that they will even before they can speak afford we mark for the detection of an hypocritical appearance of love to it before the parent face
for the fondness or averseness of the child to some servant will at any time let one know whether their love to the baby be uniform and the same when one be absent as present
in one case the child will reject with sullenness all the little sycophancy make to it in one s sight while on the other its fondness of the person who generally oblige it be an infallible rule to judge of such an one s sincerity behind one s back
this little observation shews the strength of a child s resentment and its sagacity at the early age in discover who oblige and who disoblige it and hence one may infer how improper a person he be whom we would have a child to love and respect or by whose precept we would have it direct to be the punisher of its fault or to do any harsh or disagreeable office to it
for my own part i beg to declare that if the parent be not to inflict the punishment himself i think it much well it should be give he in the parent s presence by the servant of the low consideration in the family and whose manner and example one would be the least willing of any other he should follow
just as the common executioner who be the low and most flagitious officer of the commonwealth and who frequently deserve as much as the criminal the punishment he be choose to inflict be pitch upon to perform as a mark of great ignominy sentence intend as example to deter other from the commission of heinous crime
the almighty take this method when he be disposed to correct severely his choose people for in that case he generally do it by the hand of the most profligate nation around they as we read in many place of the old testament
but the follow rule i admire in mr
locke when say he for any misdemeanour the father or mother look sour on the child every one else should put on the same coldness to he and nobody give he countenance till forgiveness be ask and a reformation of his fault have set he right again and restore he to his former credit
if this be constantly observe add he i guess there would be little need of blow or chide their own ease or satisfaction would quickly teach child to court commendation and avoid do that which they find every body condemn and they be sure to suffer for without be chid or beat
this would teach they modesty and shame and they would quickly come to have a natural abhorrence for that which they find make they slight and neglect by every body this afford i a pretty hint for if ever your charming billy shall be naughty i will proclaim throughout your worthy family that the little dear be in disgrace
and one shall shun he another decline answer he a third say no master i cannot obey you till your mamma be pleased with you a fourth who shall mind what little master bid they do when they willnot mind what their mamma say to they and when the dear little soul find this he will come in my way and i see pardon i my dear mr
b he have some of his papa s spirit already indeed he have and i will direct myself with double kindness to your beloved davers and to my miss goodwin and not notice the dear creature if i can help it till i can see his papa forgive my boldness banish from his little sullen brow and all his mamma rise to his eye
and when his musical tongue shall be unlocked to own his fault and promise amendment--o then
how shall i clasp he to my bosom
and tear of joy i know will meet his tear of penitence
how these flight dear sir please a body what delight have those mamma which some fashionable dear lady be quite unacquainted with who can make their baby and their first education their entertainment and diversion
to watch the dawning of reason in they to direct their little passion as they shew themselves to this or that particular point of benefit or use and to prepare the sweet virgin soil of their mind to receive the seed of virtue and goodness so early that as they grow up one need only now a little pruning and now a little water to make they the ornament and delight of the garden of this life
and then their pretty way their fond and grateful endearment some new beauty every day rise to observation--o my dearest mr

letter xcvi dear sir mr
locke give a great many very pretty instruction relate to the play-game of child but i humbly presume to object to what he say in one or two place
he would not indulge they in any plaything but what they make themselves or endeavour to make
a smooth pebble a piece of paper the mother s bunch of key or any thing they cannot hurt themselves with he rightly say serve as much to divert little child as those more chargeable and curious toy from the shop which be presently put out of order and break these plaything may certainly do for little one but methink to a person of easy circumstance since the making these toy employ the industrious poor the buy they for the child might be comply with though they be easily break and especially as they be of all price and some less costly and more durable than other
tops gig battledore mr
locke observe which be to be use with labour should indeed be procure they--not for variety but exercise but if they have a top the scourge-stick and leather strap should be leave to their own making and fitting but i may presume to say that whatever be the good mr
locke propose by this it cannot be equal to the mischief child may do themselves in make these plaything
for must they not have implement to work with
and be not a knife or other edge tool without which it be impossible they can make or shape a scourge-stick or any of their plaything a fine instrument in a child s hand
this advice be the reverse of the caution warrant from all antiquity that it be dangerous to meddle with edged tool and i be afraid the tutor must often act the surgeon and follow the indulgence with a styptic and plaister and the young gentleman s hand might be so often bind up as to be one way to cure he of his earnest desire to play but i can hardly imagine any other good that it can do he for i doubt the excellent consequence propose by our author from this doctrine such as to teach the child moderation in his desire application industry thought contrivance and good husbandry quality that as he say will be useful to he when he be a man be too remote to be ingraft upon such beginning although it must be confess that as mr
locke wisely observe good habit and industry cannot be too early inculcated
but then sir may i ask be not the very play and sport to which child accustom themselves whether they make their own plaything or not equivalent to the work or labour of grown person
yes sir i will venture to say they be and more than equivalent to the exercise and labour of many
mr
locke advise that the child s plaything should be as few as possible which i entirely approve that they should be in his tutor s power who be to give he but one at once
but since it be the nature of the human mind to court most what be prohibit and to set light by what be in its own power i be half doubtful only that mr

and if when he have do with any plaything he be oblige to put it into its allotted place and be accustom to keep account of the number and place of they severally this would teach he order and at the same time instruct he to keep a proper account of they and to avoid be a squanderer or waster and if he should omit to put his plaything in their place or be careless of they the taking they away for a time or threaten to give they to other would make he the more heedful
mr
locke say that he have know a child so distracted with the number and variety of his plaything that he tire his maid every day to look they over and be so accustomed to abundance that he never think he have enough but be always ask what more
what new thing shall i have a good introduction add he ironically to moderate desire and the ready way to make a content happy man all that i shall offer to this be that few man be so philosophical as one would wish they to be much less child

what be faulty in it which he call sheepishness should indeed be shake off as soon as possible because it be an enemy to merit in its advancement in the world but sir be i to choose a companion for your billy as he grow up i should not think the bad of the youth who not have have the opportunity of know man or see the world have this defect

your ever dutiful c letter xcix my dear lady g i receive your last letter at paris as we be dispose every thing for our return to england after an absence of near two year in which as i have inform you from time to time i have be a great traveller into holland the netherlands through the most considerable province of france into italy and in our return to paris again the principal place of our residence through several part of germany
i tell you of the favour and civility we receive at florence from the then countess dowager of---- who with her humble servant lord c---- that have so assiduously attend she for so many month in italy accompany we from florence to inspruck
her ladyship make that worthy lord happy in about a month after she part from we and the noble pair give we an opportunity at paris in their way to england to return some of the civility which we receive from they in italy and they be now arrive at her ladyship s seat on the forest
her lord be exceedingly fond of she as he well may for she be one of the most charming lady in england and behave to he with so much prudence and respect that they be as happy in each other as can be wish
and let i just add that both in italy and at paris mr
b s demeanour and her ladyship s to one another be so nobly open and unaffectedly polite as well as highly discreet that neither lord c
who have once be jealous of mr
b
nor the other party who have have a tincture of the same yellow evil as you know because of the countess have so much as a shadow of uneasiness remain on the occasion
lord davers have have his health which have begin to decline in england so well that there be no persuade lady davers to return before now although i beg and pray i might not have another little frenchman for fear they should as they grow up forget as i pleasantly use to say the obligation which their parentage lay they under to dearer england
and now my dear friend i have shut up my ramble for my whole life for three little english folk and one little frenchman but a charming baby as well as the rest charley by name and a near prospect of a further increase you will say be family enough to employ all my care at home
i have tell you from time to time although i could not write to you so often as i would because of our be constantly in motion what be most worthy of your knowledge relate to every particular and how happy we all have be in one another
and i have the pleasure to confirm to you what i have often write that mr
b
and my lord and lady davers be all that i could wish and hope for with regard to their first duty
we be indeed a happy family unite by the good and most solid tie
miss goodwin be a charming young lady i cannot express how much i love she
she be a perfect mistress of the french language and speak italian very prettily
and as to myself i have improve so well under my dear tutor s lesson together with the opportunity of converse with the politest and most learn gentry of different nation that i will discourse with you in two or three language if you please when i have the happiness to see you
therebe a learned boaster for you my dear friend
if the knowledge of different language make one learned but i shall bring you an heart as entirely english as ever for all that
we land on thursday last at dover and direct our course to the dear farm-house and you can well imagine than i express our meeting with my dear father and mother and my beloved davers and pamela who be charming babies but be not this the language of every fond mamma
miss goodwin be highly delighted now with my sweet little pamela and say she shall be her sister indeed
for madam say she miss be a beauty and we see no french beauty like master davers and miss beauty
my dear say i what be beauty if she be not a good girl
beauty be but a specious and as it may happen a dangerous recommendation a mere skin-deep perfection and if as she grow up she be not as good as miss goodwin she shall be none of my girl what add to my pleasure my dear friend be to see they both so well get over the small-pox
it have be as happy for they as it be for their mamma and her billy that they have it under so skilful and kind a manager in that distemper as my dear mother
i wish if it please god it be as happily over with my little pretty frenchman
every body be surprised to see what the past two year have do for miss goodwin and my billy o my dear friend they be both of they almost--nay quite i think for their year all that i wish they to be
in order to make they keep their french which miss so well speak and billy so prettily prattle i oblige they when they be in the nursery to speak nothing else but at table except on particular occasion when french may be speak they be to speak in english that be when they do speak for i tell they that little master must only ask question for information and say-- yes or-- no till their papa or mamma permit they to speak nor little lady neither till they be sixteen for-- my dear love cry i you would not speak before you know how and knowledge be obtain by hear and not by speak and set my billy on my lap in miss s presence-- here say i take an ear in the finger of each hand be two ear my billy and then point to his mouth but one tongue my love so you must be sure to mind that you hear twice as much as you speak even when you grow a big master than you be now you have so many pretty way to learn one madam say miss now and then that it be impossible we should not regard what you say to we several french tutor when we be abroad be recommend to mr

but there be one english gentleman now on his travel with young mr

with whom mr

have agree and in the mean time my good friend be pleased to compliment i that the child will not suffer for want of a tutor while i can take the pain i do which he will have to be too much for i especially that now on our return my davers and my pamela be add to my care
but what mother can take too much pain to cultivate the mind of her children if my dear lady g it be not for these frequent lying-in but this be the time of life though little do i think so early i should have so many careful blessing
i have as great credit as pleasure from my little family
all our neighbour here admire we more and more
youwill excuse my seeming for it be but seem vanity i hope i know well than to have it real-- never say mrs

and she make i a thousand compliment upon lord and lady davers s account who she would fain persuade i owe a great deal of improvement my lord in his conversation and my lady in her temper to live in the same house with we
my lady davers be exceed kind and good to i be always magnify i to every body and say she know not how to live from i and that i have be a means of save half a hundred soul as well as she dear brother s

we have great difficulty with he abroad his aunt and i endeavour to give he a serious and religious turn we have like to have turn he into a roman catholic
for he be much pleased with the shewy part of that religion and the fine picture and decoration in the church of italy and have get into company with a dominican at padua a franciscan at milan and a jesuit at paris they lie so hard at he in their turn that we have like to have lose he to each assailant so be force to let he take his own course for his aunt would have it that he have no other defence from the attack of person to make he embrace a faulty religion than to permit he to continue as he be that be to say to have none at all
so she suspend attempt to proselyte the thoughtless creature till he come to england
i wish her success here but i doubt he will not be a credit to any religion for a great while
and as he be very desirous to go to london it will be find when there that any flutter coxcomb will do more to make he one of that class in an hour than his aunt s lesson to make he a good man in a twelvemonth
where much be give much be require the contrary of this i doubt be all poor mr

have to trust to

so now he be a lord indeed
he flutter and start about most strangely i warrant and be wholly employ in give direction as to his mourning equipage and now there will be no hold he in i doubt except his new title have so much virtue in it as to make he a wise and well man
he will now have a seat in the house of peers of great britain but i hope for the nation s sake he will not find many more like himself there for to i that be one of the most venerable assembly in the world and it appear the more so since i have be abroad for an english gentleman be respect if he be any thing of a man above a foreign nobleman and an english nobleman above some petty sovereign
if our travel gentry duly consider this distinction in their favour they would for the honour of their country as well as for their own credit behave in a well manner in their foreign tour than i be sorry to say some of they do
but what can one expect from the unlicked cub pardon the term send abroad with only stature to make they look like man and equipage to attract respect without one other qualification to enforce it
here let i close this with a few tear to the memory of my dear mrs
jervis my other mother my friend my adviser my protectress in my single state and my faithful second and partaker in the comfort of my high life and well fortune
what would i have give to have be present as it seem she so earnestly wish to close her die eye
i should have do it with the piety and the concern of a truly affectionate daughter
but that melancholy happiness be deny to we both for as i tell you in the letter on the occasion the dear good woman who be now in the possession of her blessed reward and rejoice in god s mercy be no more when the news reach i so far off as heidelburgh of she last illness and wish
i cannot forbear every time i enter she parlour where i use to see with so much delight the good woman sit always employ in some useful or pious work shed a tear to her memory and in my sabbath duty miss she i miss half a dozen friend methink and i sigh in remembrance of she and can only recover that cheerful frame which the performance of those duty always give i by reflect that she be now reap the reward of that sincere piety which use to edify and encourage we all
the servant we bring home and those we leave behind melt in tear at the name of mrs

for the loss of so excellent a friend and relation be loss enough to all who know she and claim kindre with she
poor worthy jonathan too tis almost a misery to have so soft so susceptible an heart as i have or to have such good servant and friend as one cannot lose without such emotion as i feel for the loss of they his silver hair which i have behold with so much delight and think i have a father in presence when i see they adorn so honest and comely a face be now lay low forgive i he be not a common servant neither be any of our so but jonathan excel all that excel in his class i am tell that these two worthy folk die within two day of one another on which occasion i could not help say to myself in the word of david over saul and his son jonathan the name-sake of our worthy butler-- they be lovely and pleasant in their life and in their death they be not divide i might have continue on in the word of the royal lamenter for surely never do one fellow-servant love another in my maiden state nor servant love a mistress in my exalt condition well than jonathan love i

my lord daver for iff you will not call i neffew i have no reason to call you unkell surely you forgett who it be you hold up your kane to i have as little reason to valew you displeassure as you have i for i be god be thank a lord and a pere of the realme as well as you and as to youre nott ownee i nor your brother b

some thinge i wish have be otherwise--perhapps i do what then must you my lord make more mischiefe and adde to my plague iff i have any is this your unkelship
butt i shallnot want youre advice
i have as good an estate as you have and be as much a lord as yourselfe why the devill then be i to be treat as i am why the plague--but i willnot sware neither
i desire not to see you any more than you doe i i can tell you thatt
and iff we ever meet under one roofe with my likeing it must be at the house of peeres where i shall be upon a parr with you in every thing thatbe my cumfurte
as to lady davers i desire not to see her ladyship for she be always plaguy nimbel with her finger but lett my false stepp be what itt will i have in other respecte marry d a lady who be as well descend as herseife and no disparagement neither so have nott thatt to answer for to her pride and who have as good a spiritt too if they be to come face to face or i be mistaken nor will shee take affmnte from any one
so my lord leave mee to make the good of my matter as i will of youre

i will only say i be sorry for it on his own account but more for that of lord and lady davers who take the matter very heavily and wish he have marry the lowest bear creature in england so she have be honest and virtuous rather than do as he have do
but i suppose the poor gentleman be resolve to shun at all adventure mr
b s fault and keep up to the pride of descent and family and so married the only creature as i hope since it cannot be help that be so great a disgrace to both for i presume to flatter myself for the sake of my sex that among the poor wretch who be sink so low as the town-woman be there be very few of birth or education but such principally as have have their necessity or their ignorance take advantage of by base man since birth and education must need set the most unhappy of the sex above so sordid and so abandon a guilt as the hourly wickedness of such a course of life subject they to

letter ci my dear lady g the remark your cousin fielding say i have make on the subject of young gentleman s travelling and which you request i to communicate to you be part of a little book upon education which i write for mr
b s correction and amendment on his put mr
locke s treatise on that subject into my hand and require my observation upon it
i cannot flatter myself they will answer your expectation for i be sensible they must be unworthy even of the opportunity i have have in the excursion in which i have be indulge by the good of man
but your request be so many law to i and i will give you a short abstract of what i read miss fielding who have so greatly overrate it to you
the gentleman s book contain many excellent rule on education but this of travel i will only refer you to at present
you will there see his objection against the age at which young gentleman be send abroad from sixteen to twenty-one the time in all their life he say at which young gentleman be the least suited to these improvement and in which they have the least fence and guard against their passion
the age he propose be from seven to fourteen because of the advantage they will then have to master foreign language and to form their tongue to the true pronunciation as well as that they will be more easily direct by their tutor or governor
or else he propose that more sedate time of life when the gentleman be able to travel without a tutor and to make his own observation and when he be thoroughly acquaint with the law and fashion the natural and moral advantage and defect of his own country by which mean as mr
locke wisely observe the traveller will have something to exchange with those abroad from whose conversation he hop to reap any knowledge
and he support his opinion by excellent reason to which i refer you
what i have write in my little book not yet quite finish on this head relate principally to home travelling which mr
b
be always resolve his son should undertake before they enter upon a foreign tour
i have there observe that england abound with curiosity both of art and nature worth the notice of a diligent inquirer and equal with some of those we admire in foreign part and that if the youth be not send abroad at mr
locke s early time from seven to fourteen which i can hardly think will be worth while merely for the sake of attain a perfection in the language he may with good advantage begin at fourteen or fifteen the tour of great britain now-and-then by excursion in the summer month between his other study and as a diversion to he
this i should wish might be enter upon in his papa s company as well as his tutor s if it could conveniently be do who thus initiate both the governed and governor in the method he would have observe by both will obtain no small satisfaction and amusement to himself
for the father would by this mean be an eye-witness of the behaviour of the one and the other and have a speciman how fit the young man be to be trust or the tutor to be depend upon when they go abroad and be out of his sight as they would of what be expect from they by the father
and hence a thousand benefit may arise to the young gentleman from the occasional observation and reflection of his father with regard to expence company conversation hour and such like
if the father could not himself accompany his son he might appoint the stage the young gentleman should take and enjoin both tutor and son to give at every stage an account of whatever they observe curious and remarkable not omit the minute occurrence
by this mean and the probability that he might hear of they and their proceeding from his friend acquaintance and relation who might fall in with they they would have a great regard to their conduct and so much the more if the young gentleman be to keep an account of his expence which upon his return he might lay before his father
by see thus the different custom manner and economy of different person and family for in so mixed a nation as ours be there be as great a variety of that sort to be meet with as in most and from their different treatment at their several stage a great deal of the world may be learn by the young gentleman
he would be prepared to go abroad with more delight to himself as well as more experience and great reputation to his family and country
in such excursion as these the tutor would see his temper and inclination and might notice to the father any thing amiss that it might be set right while the youth be yet in his reach and more under his inspection than he would be in a foreign country and his observation on his return as well as in his letter would shew how fit he be to be trust and how likely to improve when at a great distance
after england and wales as well the inland part as the sea-coast let they if they behave accord to expectation take a journey into scotland and ireland and visit the principal island as guernsey jersey c
the youth continue to write down his observation all the way and keep a journal of occurrence and let he employ the little time he will be on board of ship in these small trip from island to island or coastwise in observe upon the noble art of navigation of the theory of which it will not be amiss that he have some notion as well as of the curious structure of a ship its tackle and furniture a knowledge very far from be insignificant to a gentleman who be an islander and have a stake in the great maritime kingdom in the world and hence he will be teach to love and value that most useful and brave set of man the british sailor who be the natural defence and glory of the realm
hereby he will confirm his theory in the geography of the british dominion in europe he will be apprise of the situation convenience interest and constitution of his own country and will be able to lay a ground-work for the future government of his thought and action if the interest he bear in his native country should call he to the public service in either house of parliament
with this foundation how excellently would he be qualified to go abroad
and how properly then would he add to the knowledge he have attain of his own country that of the different custom manner and form of government of other
how would he be able to form comparison and to make all his inquiry appear pertinent and manly
all the occasion of that ignorant wonder which render a novice the jest of all about he would be take away
he would be able to ask question and to judge without lead string
nor would he think he have see a country and answer the end of his father s expence and his own improvement by run through a kingdom and know nothing of it but the inn and stage at which he stop to eat and drink
for on the contrary he would make the good acquaintance and contract worthy friendship with such as would court and reverence he as one of the rise genius of his country
whereas most of the young gentleman who be send abroad raw and unprepared as if to wonder at every thing they see and to be laugh at by all that see they do but expose themselves and their country
and if at their return by interest of friend by alliance or marriage they should happen to be promote to place of honour or profit their unmerite preferment will only serve to make those foreigner who be eye-witness of their weakness and folly when among they conclude greatly in disfavour of the whole nation or at least of the prince and his administration who could find no fitter subject to distinguish
this my dear friend be a brief extract from my observation on the head of qualify young gentleman to travel with honour and improvement
i doubt youwill be apt to think i not a little out of my element but since you would have it i claim the allowance of a friend to which my ready compliance with your command the rather entitle i
i be very sorry mr
and mrs
murray be so unhappy in each other
be he a generous man the heavy loss the poor lady have sustain as well as her sister my beloved friend in so excellent a mother and so kind a father would make he bear with she infirmitie a little
but really i have see on twenty occasion that notwithstanding all the fine thing gentleman say to lady before marriage if the latter do not improve upon their husband hand their impute grace when single will not protect they from indifference and probably from bad while the gentleman perhaps think he only of the two be entitle to go backward in act of kindness and complaisance
a strange and shocking difference which too many lady experience who from fond lover prostrate at their foot find surly husband trample upon their neck
you my dear friend be happy in your day of courtship and be no less so in your state of wedlock
and may you continue to be so to a good old age pray your affectionate and faithful friend p b
letter cii my dear lady g i will cheerfully cause to be transcribe for you the conversation you desire between myself mrs

i be only sorry you should be concern about the suppose trouble you give i by have mislay my former relation of it
for besides oblige my dear lady g the hope of do service by it to a family so worthy in a case so nearly affect its honour as to make two headstrong young lady recollect what belong to their sex and their character and what their filial duty require of they afford i high pleasure and if it shall be attend with the wished effect it will add to my happiness
i say cause to be transcribe because i hope to answer a double end by it for on reconsideration i set miss goodwin to transcribe it who write a pretty hand and be not a little fond of the task nor indeed of any task i set she and will be more affected as she perform it than she could be by read it only although she be a very good girl at present and give i hope that she will continue to be so
i will inclose it when do that it may be read to the party without this introduction if you think fit

my dear lady g the paper you have mislay as to the conversation between i and the young lady relation of mrs
towers and lady anne arthur in presence of these two last-name lady mrs
brooks and the worthy dean and miss l
of which in order to perfect your kind collection of my communication you request another copy contain as follow
i first state that i have see these three lady twice or thrice before as visitor at their kinswoman s house so that they and i be not altogether stranger to one another and my two neighbour acquaint i with their respective taste and disposition and their history preparatory to this visit to the following effect that miss stapylton be over-run with the love of poetry and romance and delight in flowery language and metaphorical flourish be about eighteen want not either sense or politeness and have read herself into a vein more amorous that be mrs
towers s word than discreet
have extraordinary notion of a first sight love and give herself great liberty with a pair of fine eye in hope to make sudden conquest in pursuance of that notion than be pretty in her sex and age which make those who know she not conclude she bold and forward and be more than suspect with a mind thus prepare for instantaneous impression to have experience the argument to her own disadvantage and to be strike by before she have stricken a gentleman whom her friend think not at all worthy of she and to whom she be make some indiscreet advance under the name of philoclea to philoxenus in a letter which she entrust to a servant of the family who discover her design prevent her indiscretion for that time
that in other respect she have no mean accomplishment will have a fine fortune be genteel in her person though with some visible affectation dance well sing well and play prettily on several instrument be fond of reading but affect the action and air and attitude of a tragedian and be too apt to give an emphasis in the wrong place in order to make an author mean significantly even where the occasion be common and in a mere historical fact that require as much simplicity in the reader s accent as in the writer s style
no wonder then that when she read a play she will put herself into a sweat as mrs
towers say distort very agreeable feature and make a multitude of wry mouth with one very pretty one in order to convince her hearer what a near neighbour her heart be to her lip
miss cope be a young lady of nineteen lovely in her person with a handsome fortune in possession and great prospect
have a soft and gentle turn of mind which dispose she to be easily impose upon
be address by a libertine of quality whose courtship while permit be imperiousness and whose tenderness insult have find the young lady too susceptible of impression open and unreserved and even value he the more as it seem for treat she with ungenerous contempt for that she be always make excuse for slight ill manner and even rudeness which no other young lady would forgive
that this docility on her side and this insolence on his and an over-free and even indecent degree of romp as it be call with she which once her mamma surprise they in make her papa forbid his visit and she receive they

if therefore a man be his birth or quality what it will the high the bad presume to wound a lady s ear with indecent word if he endeavour in his expression or sentiment to convey gross or impure idea to her mind if he be continually press for her confidence in his honour if he request favour which a lady ought to refuse if he can be regardless of his conduct or behaviour to she if he can use boisterous or rude freedom either to she person or dress here poor miss cope by her blush bear witness to her case if he avoid speak of marriage when he have a fair opportunity of do it-- here miss l

the distance of time since this conversation pass enable i to add what i could not do when i write the account of it which you have mislay and which take briefly as follow miss stapylton be as good as her word and write down all she could recollect of the conversation and i have already send she the letter she desire contain my observation upon the flighty style she so much admire it have such an effect upon she as to turn the course of her reading and study to weighty and more solid subject and avoid the gentleman she have begin to favour give way to her parent recommendation and be happily married to sir jonathan barnes
miss cope come to i a week after with the leave of both her parent and tarry with i three day in which time she open all her heart to i and return in such a disposition and with such resolution that she never would see her peer again nor receive letter from he which she own to i she have do clandestinely before and she be now the happy lady of sir michael beaumont who make she the good of husband and permit she to follow her charitable inclination accord to a scheme which she consult i upon
