letter ciii my dear lady g you desire to have a little speciman of my nursery tale and story with which as miss fenwick tell you on her return to lincolnshire i entertain my miss goodwin and my little boy
but you make i too high a compliment when you tell i it be for your own instruction and example
yet you know my dear lady g be your motif what they will i must obey you although be other to see it i might expose myself to the smile and contempt of judge less prejudiced in my favour
so i will begin without any further apology and as near as i can give you those very story with which miss fenwick be so pleased and of which she have make so favourable a report
let i acquaint you then that my method be to give character of person i have know in one part or other of my life in feign name whose conduct may serve for imitation or warn to my dear attentive miss and sometimes i give instance of good boy and naughty boy for the sake of my billy and my davers and they be continually come about i dear madam a pretty story now cry miss and dear mamma tell i of good boy and of naughty boy cry billy
miss be a surprising child of her age and be very familiar with many of the good character in the spectators and have a smattering of latin and more than a smattering of italian and be a perfect mistress of french be seldom at a loss for a derivation of such word as be not of english original
and so i shall give you a story in feign name with which she be so delighted that she have write it down
but i will first trespass on your patience with one of my childish tale
every day once or twice i cause miss goodwin who play and sing very prettily to give a tune or two to i my billy and my davers who as well as my pamela love and learn to touch the key young as the latter be and she will have a sweet finger i can observe that and a charming ear and her voice be music itself o the fond fond mother i know you will say on read this
then madam we all proceed hand-in-hand together to the nursery to my charley and jemmy and in this happy retirement so much my delight in the absence of my well beloved imagine you see i seat surround with the joy and the hope of my future prospect as well as my present comfort
miss goodwin imagine you see on my right hand sit on a velvet stool because she be eld and a miss billy on my left in a little cane elbow-chair because he be eld and a good boy my davers and my sparkle-ey d pamela with my charley between they on little silken cushion at my foot hand-in-hand their pleased eye look up to my more delighted one and my sweet-natured promising jemmy in my lap the nurse and the cradle just behind we and the nursery maid delightedly pursue some useful needle-work for the dear charmer of my heart-all as hush and as still as silence itself as the pretty creature generally be when their little watchful eye see my lip begin to open for they take neat notice already of my rule of two ear to one tongue insomuch that if billy or davers be either of they for break the mum as they call it they be immediately hush at any time if i put my finger to my lip or if miss point hers to her ear even to the breaking of a word in two as it be and yet all my boy be as lively as so many bird while my pamela be cheerful easy soft gentle always smile but modest and harmless as a dove
i begin with a story of two little boy and two little girl the child of a fine gentleman and a fine lady who love they dearly that they be all so good and love one another so well that every body who see they admire they and talk of they far and near that they would part with any thing to the another love the poor speak kindly to the servant do every thing they be bid to do be not proud know no strife but who should learn their book good and be the prettiest scholar that the servant love they and would do any thing they desire that they be not proud of fine clothe let not their head run upon their plaything when they should mind their book say grace before they eat their prayer before they go to bed and as soon as they rise be always clean and neat would not tell a fib for the world and be above do any thing that require one that god bless they more and more and bless their papa and mamma and their uncle and aunt and cousin for their sake
and there be a happy family my dear loves no one idle all prettily employ the master at their book the misses at their book too or at their needle except at their play-hour when they be never rude nor noisy nor mischievous nor quarrelsome and no such word be ever hear from their mouth as why maynot i have this or that as well as billy or bobby or why should sally have this or that any more than i but it be as my mamma please my mamma know well and a bow and a smile and no surliness or scowl brow to be see if they be deny any thing for well do they know that their papa and mamma love they so dearly that they would refuse they nothing that be for their good and they be sure when they be refuse they ask for something that would have do they hurt have it be grant
never be such good boy and girl as these i and they grow up and the master become fine scholar and fine gentleman and every body honour they and the misses become fine lady and fine housewife and this gentleman when they grow to be woman seek to marry one of the misses and that gentleman the other and happy be he that could be admit into their company i so that they have nothing to do but to pick and choose out of the good gentleman in the country while the great lady for birth and the most remarkable for virtue which my dear be well than either birth or fortune think themselves honour by the address of the two brother
and they marry and make good papa and mamma and be so many blessing to the age in which they live
there my dear love be happy son and daughter for good masters seldom fail to make good gentleman and good misses good lady and god bless they with as good child as they be to their parent and so the blessing go round who would not but be good well but mamma we will all be good won t we master davers cry my billy
yes brother billy
but what will become of the naughty boy
tell we mamma about the naughty boy why there be a poor poor widow woman who have three naughty son and one naughty daughter and they would do nothing that their mamma bid they do be always quarrel scratch and fighting would not say their prayer would not learn their book so that the little boy use to laugh at they and point at they as they go along for blockhead and nobody love they or take notice of they except to beat and thump they about for their naughty way and their undutifulness to their poor mother who work hard to maintain they
as they grow up they grow bad and bad and more and more stupid and ignorant so that they impoverish their poor mother and at last break her heart poor poor widow woman and her neighbour join together to bury the poor widow woman for these sad ungracious child make away with what little she have leave while she be ill before her heart be quite break and this help to break it the soon for have she live she see she must have want bread and have no comfort with such wicked child poor poor widow woman say my billy with tear and my little dove shed tear too and davers be move and miss wipe her fine eye
but what become of the naughty boy and the naughty girl mamma become of they
why one son be force to go to sea and there he be drown another turn thief for he would not work and he come to an untimely end the third be idle and ignorant and nobody who know how he use his poor mother would employ he and so he be force to go into a far country and beg his bread
and the naughty girl have never love work pin away in sloth and filthiness and at last break her arm and die of a fever lamenting too late that she have be so wicked a daughter to so good a mother and so there be a sad end to all the four ungracious child who never would mind what their poor mother say to they and god punish their naughtiness as you see while the good child i mention before be the glory of their family and the delight of every body that know they who would not be good be the inference and the repetition from billy with his hand clapt together poor widow woman give i much pleasure
so my childish story end with a kiss of each pretty dear and their thank for my story and then come on miss s request for a woman s story as she call it
i dismiss my baby to their play and take miss s hand she stand before i all attention begin in a more womanly strain to she for she be very fond of be think a woman and indeed be a prudent sensible dear comprehend any thing instantly and make very pretty reflection upon what she hear or read as you will observe in what follow there be nothing my dear miss goodwin that young lady should be so watchful over as their reputation tis a tender flower that the least frost will nip the least cold wind will blast and when once blast it will never flourish again but wither to the very root
but this i have tell you so often i need not repeat what i have say
so to my story
there be four pretty lady live in one genteel neighbourhood daughter of four several family but all companion and visitor and yet all of very different inclination
coquetilla we will call one prudiana another profusiana the third and prudentia the fourth their several name denote their respective quality
coquetilla be the only daughter of a worthy baronet by a lady very gay but rather indiscreet than unvirtuous who take not the requisite care of her daughter s education but let she be over-run with the love of fashion dress and equipage and when in london ball opera play the park the ring the withdrawing-room take up her whole attention
she admire nobody but herself flutter about laugh at and despise a crowd of man-follower whom she attract by gay thoughtless freedom of behaviour too nearly treading on the skirt of immodesty yet make she not one worthy conquest exciting on the contrary in all sober mind that contempt of herself which she so profusely would be think to pour down upon the rest of the world
after she have several year flutter about the dangerous light like some silly fly she at last sing the wing of her reputation for be despise by every worthy heart she become too easy and cheap a prey to a man the most unworthy of all her follower who have resolution and confidence enough to break through those few cobweb reserve in which she have encircle her precarious virtue and which be no long of force to preserve her honour when she meet with a man more bold and more enterprising than herself and who be as design as she be thoughtless
and what then become of coquetilla why she be force to pass over sea to ireland where nobody know she and to bury herself in a dull obscurity to go by another name and at last unable to support a life so unsuitable to the natural gaiety of her temper she pin herself into a consumption and die unpitied and unlamented among stranger have not one friend but whom she buy with her money poor lady coquetilla say miss goodwin what a sad thing it be to have a wrong education and how happy be i who have so good a lady to supply the place of a dear distant mamma but be please madam to proceed to the next prudiana my dear be the daughter of a gentleman who be a widower and have while the young lady be an infant bury her mamma
he be a good sort of man but have but one lesson to teach to prudiana and that be to avoid all sort of conversation with the man but never give she the right turn of mind nor instill into it that sense of her religious duty which would have be her good guard in all temptation
for provide she keep out of the sight and conversation of the gentleman and avoid the company of those lady who more freely converse with the other sex it be all her papa desire of she
this give she a haughty sullen and reserved turn make she stiff formal and affect
she have sense enough to discover early the fault of coquetilla and in dislike to they fall the more easily into that contrary extreme which a recluse education and her papa s caution naturally lead she
so that pride reserve affectation and censoriousness make up the essential of her character and she become more unamiable even than coquetilla and as the other be too accessible prudiana be quite unapproachable by gentleman and unfit for any conversation but that of her servant be also desert by those of her own sex by whom she might have improve on account of her censorious disposition
and what be the consequence
why this every worthy person of both sex despise she and she be use to see nobody but servant at last throw herself upon one of that class in an evil hour she find something that be take to her low taste in the person of her papa s valet a wretch so infinitely beneath she but a gay coxcomb of a servant that every body attribute to she the scandal of make the first advance for otherwise it be presume he durst not have look up to his master s daughter
so here end all her pride
all her reserve come to this
her censoriousness of other redouble people s contempt upon herself and make nobody pity she
she be finally turn out of door without a penny of fortune the fellow be force to set up a barber s shop in a country town for all he know be to shave and dress a peruke and her papa would never look upon her more so that prudiana become the outcast of her family and the scorn of all that know she and be force to mingle in conversation and company with the wretch of her husband s degree poor miserable prudiana say miss-- what a sad sad fall be her
and all owe to the want of a proper education too and to the loss of such a mamma as i have an aunt and so wise a papa as i have an uncle how could she papa i wonder restrain her person as he do like a poor nun and make she unacquainted with the generous restraint of the mind
i be sure my dear good aunt it will be owe to you that i shall never be a coquetilla nor a prudiana neither
your table be always surround with the good of company with worthy gentleman as well as lady and you instruct i to judge of both and of every new guest in such a manner as make i esteem they all and censure nobody but yet to see fault in some to avoid and grace in other to imitate but in nobody but yourself and my uncle any thing so like perfection as shall attract one s admiration to one s own ruin you be young yet my love and must always doubt your own strength and pray to god more and more as your year advance to give you more and more prudence and watchfulness over your conduct
but yet my dear you must think justly of yourself too for let the young gentleman be ever so learned and discreet your education entitle you to think as well of yourself as of they for donot you see the lady who be so kind as to visit we that have not be abroad as you have be when they be young yet make as good figure in conversation say as good thing as any of the gentleman
for my dear all that the gentleman know more than the lady except here and there such a one as your dear uncle with all their learn education be only that they have be discipline perhaps into an observation of a few accuracy in speech which if they know no more rather distinguish the pedant than the gentleman such as the avoiding of a false concord as they call it and which you know how to do as well as the good not to put a be for a be an be for an be and to be able to speak in mood and tense and such like valuable part of education so that my dear you can have no reason to look upon that sex in so high a light as to depreciate your own and yet you must not be proud nor conceited neither but make this one rule your guide in your maiden state think yourself above the gentleman and theywill think you so too and address you with reverence and respect if they see there be neither pride nor arrogance in your behaviour but a consciousness of merit a true dignity such as become virgin modesty and untainted purity of mind and manner like that of an angel among man for so young lady should look upon themselves to be and will then be treat as such by the other sex
in your married state which be a kind of state of humiliation for a lady you must think yourself subordinate to your husband for so it have please god to make the wife
you must have no will of your own in petty thing and if you marry a gentleman of sense and honour such a one as your uncle he will look upon you as his equal and will exalt you the more for your abase yourself
in short my dear he will act by you just as your dear uncle do by i and then what a happy creature will you be so i shall madam
to be sure i shall but i know i shall be happy whenever i marry because i have such wise director and such an example before i and if it please god i will never think of any man in pursuance of your constant advice to young lady at the tea-table who be not a man of sense and a virtuous gentleman
but now dear madam for your next character
there be two more yet to come thatbe my pleasure
i wish there be ten why the next be profusiana you may remember my love
profusiana take another course to she ruin
she fall into some of coquetilla s foible but pursue they for another end and in another manner
strike with the grandeur and magnificence of what weak people call the upper life she give herself up to the circus to ball to opera to masquerade and assembly affect to shine at the head of all company at tunbridge at bath and every place of public resort play high be always receive and pay visit give ball and make treat and entertainment and be so much above the conduct which mostly recommend a young lady to the esteem of the deserving of the other sex that no gentleman who prefer solid happiness can think of address she though she be a fine person and have many outward grace of behaviour
she become the favourite toast of the place she frequent be proud of that distinction give the fashion and delight in the pride that she can make ape in imitation whenever she please
but yet endeavour to avoid be think proud make herself cheap and be the subject of the attempt of every coxcomb of eminence and with much ado preserve her virtue though not her character
what all this while be poor profusiana do
she would be glad perhaps of a suitable proposal and would it may be give up some of her gaiety and extravagance for profusiana have wit and be not totally destitute of reason when she suffer herself to think
but her conduct procure her not one solid friendship and she have not in a twelvemonth among a thousand profession of service one devoir that she can attend to or a friend that she can depend upon
all the woman she see if she excel they hate she the gay part of the man with whom she accompany most be all in a plot against her honour
even the gentleman whose conduct in the general be govern by principle of virtue come down to these public place to partake of the innocent freedom allow there and oftentime give themselves air of gallantry and never have it in their thought to commence a treaty of marriage with an acquaintance begin upon that gay spot
what solid friendship and satisfaction then be profusiana exclude from
her name indeed be write in every public window and prostitute as i may call it at the pleasure of every profligate or sot who wear a diamond to engrave it and that it may be with most vile and barbarous imputation and freedom of word add by rake who very probably never exchange a syllable with she
the wounded tree be perhaps also teach to wear the initial of her name link not unlikely and widen as they grow with those of a scoundrel
but all this while she make not the least impression upon one noble heart and at last perhaps have run on to the end of an uninterrupted race of folly she be cheat into the arm of some vile fortune-hunter who quickly lavish away the remain of that fortune which her extravagance have leave and then after the bad usage abandon she with contempt she sink into an obscurity that cut short the thread of her life and leave no remembrance but on the brittle glass and still more faithless bark that ever she have a be alas alas
what a butterfly of a day say miss an expression she remember of lady towers be poor profusiana what a sad thing to be so dazzle by worldly grandeur and to have so many admirer and not one real friend very true my dear and how carefully ought a person of a gay and lively temper to watch over it i and what a rock may public place be to a lady s reputation if she be not doubly vigilant in her conduct when she be expose to the censure and observation of malignant crowd of people many of the bad of whom spare the least those who be most unlike themselves but then madam say miss would profusiana venture to play at public place
will lady game madam
i have hear you say that lord and sharper but just out of livery in gaming be upon a foot in every thing save that one have nothing to lose and the other much besides his reputation
and will lady so disgrace their character and their sex as to pursue this pernicious diversion in public yes my dear they will too often the morebe the pity
and donot you remember when we be at bath in what a hurry i once pass by some knot of genteel people and you ask what those be do
i tell you whisperingly they be game and loath i be that my miss goodwin should stop to see some sight to which till she arrive at the year of discretion it be not proper to familiarize her eye in some sort act like the ancient romans who would not assign punishment to certain atrocious crime because they have such an high idea of human nature as to suppose it incapable of commit they so i be not for have you while a little girl see those thing which i know would give no credit to our sex and which i think when you grow old should be new and shocking to you but now you be so much a woman in discretion i may tell you any thing she kiss my hand and make i a fine curtsey-and tell i that now she long to hear of prudentia s conduct
her name madam say she promise well thing than those of her three companion and so it have need for how sad be it to think that out of four lady of distinction three of they should be naughty and of course unhappy these two word of course my dear say i be very prettily put in let i kiss you for it since every one that be naughty first or last must be certainly unhappy
far otherwise than what i have relate be it with the amiable prudentia
like the industrious bee she make up her honey-hoard from every flower bitter as well as sweet for every character be of use to she by which she can improve her own
she have the happiness of an aunt who love she as i do you and of an uncle who doate on she as yours do for alas
poor prudentia lose her papa and mamma almost in her infancy in one week but be so happy in her uncle and aunt s care as not to miss they in her education and but just to remember their person
by reading by observation and by attention she daily add new advantage to those which her education give she
she see and pity the flutter freedom and dangerous night of coquetilla
the sullen pride the affectation and stiff reserve which prudiana assume she penetrate and make it her study to avoid
and the gay hazardous conduct extravagant temper and love of tinselled grandeur which be the blemish of profusiana s character she dread and shun
she fortify herself with the excellent example of the past and present age and know how to avoid the fault of the faulty and to imitate the grace of the most perfect
she take into her scheme of that future happiness which she hop to make she own what be the true excellency of her sex and endeavour to appropriate to herself the domestic virtue which shall one day make she the crown of some worthy gentleman s earthly happiness and which of course as you prettily say my dear will secure and heighten her own
that noble frankness of disposition that sweet and unaffected openness and simplicity which shin in all her action and behaviour commend she to the esteem and reverence of all mankind as her humility and affability and a temper uncensorious and ever make the good of what she say of the absent person of either sex do to the love of every lady
her name indeed be not prostitute on window nor carve on the bark of tree in public place but it smell sweet to every nostril dwell on every tongue and be engraven on every heart
she meet with no address but from man of honour and probity the flutter coxcomb the inveigling parasite the insidious deceiver the mercenary fortune-hunter spread no snare for a heart guard by discretion and prudence as hers be
they see that all her amiable virtue be the happy result of an uniform judgment and the effect of her own wisdom found in an education to which she do the high credit
and at last after several worthy offer enough to perplex a lady s choice she bless some one happy gentleman more distinguished than the rest for learn good sense and true politeness which be but another word for virtue and honour and shin to her last hour in all the duty of domestic life as an excellent wife mother mistress friend and christian and so confirm all the expectation of which her maiden life have give such strong and such edify presage then fold my dear miss in my arm and kiss she tear of pleasure stand in her pretty eye who would not say i shun the example of the coquetilla s the prudiana s and the profusiana s of this world and choose to imitate the character of prudentia the happy and the happy-make prudentia o madam
madam say the dear creature smother i with her rapturous kiss prudentia be you is you indeed it can be nobody else o teach i good god
to follow your example and i shall be a second prudentia--indeed i shall god send you may my beloved miss
and may he bless you more if possible than prudentia be bless and so my dear lady g you have some of my nursery tale with which rely on your kind allowance and friendship i conclude myself your affectionate and faithful p b
conclusion the editor think proper to conclude in this place that he may not be think to deserve a suspicion that the extent of the work be to be measure by the patience of its reader
but he think it necessary in order to elucidate the whole to subjoin a note of the following fact
mr
b
after the affair which take date at the masquerade and conclude so happily continue to be one of the good and most exemplary of man an honour to his country both in his public and private capacity have at the instance of some of his friend in very elevated station accept of an honourable employment abroad in the service of the state which he discharge in such a manner as might be expect from his qualification and knowledge of the world and on his return after an absence of three year resist all the temptation of ambition devote himself to private duty and join with his excellent lady in every pious wish of her heart adorn the married life with all the warmth of an elegant tenderness belove by his tenant respect by his neighbour revere by his child and almost adore by the poor in every county where his estate give he interest as well for his own bountiful temper as for the charity he permit to be dispense with so liberal a hand by his lady
she make he the father of seven fine child five son and two daughter all adorn and accomplish by nature to be the joy and delight of such parent be educate in every respect by the rule of their inimitable mother lay down in that book which she mention to have be write by she for the revisal and correction of her consort the content of which may be gather from her remark upon mr
locke s treatise on education in her letter to mr
b and in those to lady g
miss goodwin at the age of eighteen be married to a young gentleman of fine part and great sobriety and virtue and both she and he in every material part of their conduct and in their behaviour to one another emulate the good example set they by mr
and mrs
b
lord davers die two year before this marriage his lady go to reside at the hall in lincolnshire the place of her birth that she might enjoy the company and conversation of her excellent sister who for conveniency of the chapel and advantage of room and situation have prevail upon mr
b
to make it the chief place of his residence and there the noble lady live long in the strict friendship with the happy pair an honourable relict of her affectionate lord
the worthy mr
andrews and his wife live together in the sweet tranquillity set forth in their letter for the space of twelve year at the kentish farm the good old gentlewoman die first full of year and comfort her dutiful daughter perform the last pious office to so beloved and so love a parent her husband survive she about a year only
lady g miss darnford that be after a happy marriage of several year die in child-bed of her fourth child to the inexpressible concern of her affectionate consort and of her dear friend mrs
b
lord h after have suffer great dishonour by the ill course of his wife and great devastation in his estate through her former debt and continue extravagance intimidate and dispirit by her perpetual insult and those of her gaming brother who with his bullying friend terrify he into their measure throw himself upon the protection of mr
b
who by his spirit and prudence save he from utter ruin punish his wife s accomplice and oblige she to accept a separate maintenance and then take his affair into his own management in due course of time entirely re-establish they and after some year his wife die he become wise by his past suffering and marry a second of lady davers s recommendation who by her prudence and virtue make he happy for the remainder of his day
mr
longman live to a great age in the worthy family much esteem by every one have train up a diligent youth whom he have recommend to ease he in his business and who answer expectation succeed he in it after his death
he die rich out of his great love and gratitude to the family in whose service he have acquire most of his fortune and in disgust to his near relation who have perversely disoblige he he bequeath to three of they one hundred pound a-piece and leave all the rest to his honoured principal mr
b who as soon as he come to know it be at that time abroad direct his lady to call together the relation of the old gentleman and after touch they to the heart with a just and effectual reproof and find they fill with due sense of their demerit which have be the cause of their suffering then to divide the whole which have be leave he among they in great proportion as they be more nearly related an action worthy prayer and blessing not only of the benefit but all who hear of it
for it be easy to imagine how cheerfully and how gracefully his benevolent lady discharge a command so well suit to her natural generosity
the end
